user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.55) was wrong on realized data: the latest observation shows fed_assets at 4,461,117, and the 4-week change is +5,456$M — a rise, not the runoff I expected. The 13-week change is still slightly negative (-4,167$M, -0.09%), and assets remain below the 52w high by -23,375$M, so on a longer window there has been mild drift down, but the most recent, most policy-relevant momentum is flat-to-up. Critically, the policy language has now firmed: the Committee moved from "expects to begin... relatively soon" to a concrete commitment — "In [date] the Committee will initiate the balance sheet normalization program." This is a clear announcement of runoff starting, but the reinvestment language for the prior period was still full reinvestment (no runoff yet at the time of measurement). Since the runoff caps start small and phase in gradually, and the most recent weekly prints still show noisy but net-positive short-term movement, I read this as: balance sheet is at an inflection point — a firm plan to shrink is now locked in, but the actual month-ahead change is likely to be only modestly negative to flat as the small initial caps bite. I'm revising toward a mild-down lean, much lower conviction than before given the recent flat/up prints.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent reading showing fed_assets clearly declining month-over-month in line with announced runoff caps would confirm; continued flat/rising prints would falsify.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.35) was wrong to be neutral: the spread has clearly rolled over. After rising to a local peak of 0.83pp for three readings, it dropped to 0.79 and then 0.78pp in the last two observations — a genuine reversal, not noise. My own stated falsifier (a drop back below 0.82pp with slope_change_21d turning negative) has now been triggered: slope_change_21d is -0.08pp, slope_change_63d is -0.14pp, and slope_change_252d is -0.20pp, showing flattening momentum consistent across all horizons. The spread sits just 0.03pp above its 12-month low and only 5% up its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.05), meaning it is back near the most inverted-adjacent level of the year. The curve remains far from flat in absolute terms (0.78pp), so there's no inversion risk near-term, but the trend is unambiguously toward flattening. Policy language shows no material change (only a cosmetic "Consequently" insertion), so this move is being driven by market pricing rather than a shift in forward guidance. With multi-horizon momentum aligned and the level pinned near 12-month lows, I now lean toward continued flattening into month-end rather than stabilization.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.82pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would indicate the flattening move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has drifted to its 52-week low (-0.59), continuing a slow easing trend rather than reversing. The last two prints are the lowest in the window, and the 4-week change (-0.03) shows modest continued easing momentum, while the 13-week change (-0.01) confirms this is a gentle grind lower rather than a sharp move. My prior flat call was reasonable given the tiny magnitudes, but with hindsight the index has now made a new low and the trend, while shallow, is consistently negative over the past several observations (from -0.56 down to -0.59). There is no sign of the tightening turn I flagged as a falsifier (no print above -0.53, no positive 4-week change). Conditions remain well below the zero average, meaning financial conditions are looser than historical norms, and the marginal direction continues to point toward further easing rather than stabilization. Given the small magnitude of weekly moves, I don't want to overstate conviction, but the consistent directionality across multiple weeks now outweighs the case for flat. I am shifting toward a mild "down" call (continued easing) for the next month, holding conviction moderate given the small absolute changes involved.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print clearly above -0.55, or a 4-week change turning positive (>0.02), would signal a tightening reversal.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call is validated by the newest print: breakeven_10y ticked from 1.87 to 1.88, holding near the top of its recent range rather than reverting toward 1.80 as the falsifier condition specified. The series has moved from 1.84 to 1.88 over the last ten observations, a modest but persistent grind higher, with be_change_21d (+0.04pp), be_change_63d (+0.09pp), and be_change_252d (+0.16pp) all positive and consistent in sign — a genuine multi-horizon uptrend rather than noise. Range position at 0.52 shows breakevens are roughly mid-range over the past year, having risen well off the 12m low (+0.22pp above it) but still 0.20pp below the 12m high, leaving room to continue upward. Breakevens remain 0.12pp below the 2% objective, so there is no overshoot pressure, but the trajectory is toward, not away from, target. Policy language remains unchanged and describes market-based measures as "low," which is consistent with the current sub-target reading and doesn't contradict the drift higher. Given the last two prints are flat-to-slightly-up (1.89, 1.88, 1.87, 1.88) after a stronger earlier climb, I temper conviction slightly — the pace of increase has decelerated even as direction holds.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward 1.83 or below, sustained over multiple prints, would signal the uptrend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call (0.45 conviction) was largely wrong: the newest headline print fell to 0.08% m/m, well below the 0.15% threshold I set, and headline yoy actually dipped from 2.18% to 2.02%, giving up most of the prior jump. That confirms the hurricane-driven gasoline spike (explicitly flagged in the policy language) was transitory, as the statement itself now attributes the temporary boost to storm effects. Core PCE continues to soften — 1.42% yoy, down from 1.87% two quarters ago, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.15pp and core_3m_annualized at just 1.22%, both signaling persistent disinflationary momentum in the underlying trend. Headline_3m_annualized at 3.96% looks hot only because of the one large 0.51% print now rolling toward the edge of the 3-month window; the very next observation (0.08%) suggests that annualized rate will decelerate sharply as new data replaces the storm-affected month. With core running 0.58pp below target and still drifting down, and headline's temporary energy impulse fading, I expect headline yoy to retreat further or hold near 2.0% rather than extend upward. Weighting core's persistence signal over the noisy headline energy pass-through, I lean toward deceleration, though with only moderate conviction given headline's mom_gap_vs_outgoing still shows some volatility.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in headline m/m back above 0.3% or core PCE yoy stabilizing/rising would indicate the disinflation trend has stalled and energy effects are recurring, not transitory.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, core_3m_annualized, headline_3m_annualized, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 4.20%, a new 12-month low, confirming the tightening trend I flagged as a trigger last time rather than the flat call I actually made. The series shows a steady downtrend: 3m change -0.10pp, 6m change -0.20pp, 12m change -0.70pp, with the unemployment rate now sitting right at its 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00) and only 0.20pp above the assumed natural rate of 4%. There is no Sahm-rule style acceleration in loosening — quite the opposite, the trend is a slow grind lower. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed's updated statement describes job gains as "solid" and continuing labor market strengthening, consistent with the falling unemployment rate. Given the persistent negative momentum, the small gap to the natural rate, and no evidence of any inflection or stalling in the last few prints (4.4→4.3→4.4→4.3→4.2), the more likely near-term outcome is another flat-to-down print rather than a reversal upward. I'll lean down, though modestly, since the gap to natural-rate floor is narrowing and mean-reversion risk rises as the rate approaches structural lows.
Would change its mind if: A print back up to 4.4% or higher would signal the downtrend has stalled or reversed into loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y sits at 2.38%, near the top of its 12-month range (0.71 range position) after climbing from 2.34 ten observations ago. Momentum has moderated: the 21d change is only +0.05pp and the last three prints (2.46→2.42→2.37→2.38) show a mild pullback/stabilization after peaking at 2.46. Still, the 63d (+0.12) and 252d (+0.53) changes confirm the broader trend of the last year has been higher yields, and the level remains well above the 12m low (+0.59pp above it) with 30s10s slope steepish at +0.50pp, consistent with a still-elevated term premium rather than one that's collapsing. My prior "up" call was directionally correct over the year but the most recent short-term data show the ascent losing steam and essentially flattening over the past two prints. The unchanged reinvestment/roll-over policy language provides no fresh impulse either way. Given the loss of near-term momentum but persistent high range position and positive longer-term drift, I lean toward continued mild upward pressure but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the last two observations are essentially flat-to-slightly-up rather than a clean continuation.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back below 2.30 over several observations, or a clear downside break, would flip this to down; renewed acceleration above 2.46 would raise conviction on up.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Six drivers bear on the level; curve_slope is out of scope for this seat but its flattening read (down 0.55, fresh) confirms the front-end anchor argument. The net picture: inflation (realized CPI/core PCE) points down with moderate conviction and is fresh-ish (16 days), while inflation_expectations (market breakevens, fresh, this meeting) points up but only mildly and with deceleration noted in the last two prints. These two partially offset — realized inflation is softening while market compensation grinds marginally higher off a low base; I weight realized CPI/core PCE slightly more since it's the more direct input to Fed reaction function and the panel's own gap-check flags that inflation's call would be reinforced by expectations moving down together, which they are not doing, so I temper the inflation call rather than let it dominate. Labor tightening is easing (unemployment falling toward natural rate) — a small down-pull on yields via reduced wage pressure, though this is a labor-market read more than a rates read; I give it modest weight. Financial conditions are easing (looser, NFCI at 52w lows) — modest downward pull on required yield compensation, fresh and consistent across horizons. Term premium is the freshest, most yield-direct read: 10Y near top of 12m range, longer-horizon momentum (63d, 252d) clearly positive, but near-term (21d) losing steam — I read this as mildly up but decelerating, so limited conviction. Balance sheet is genuinely at an inflection: prior strong-down call reversed to flat-to-mild-down given policy commitment to runoff but current prints still net positive — this is the most compromised, self-revised driver and I discount it toward near-neutral. On net, the pulls roughly balance: term premium's persistent uptrend and inflation-expectations' modest rise are offset by easing financial conditions, softening realized inflation, and loosening labor tightness. I lean toward a slight net-down to flat level bias, favoring the fresher, more consistent multi-horizon signals (term premium's longer window, financial conditions, curve flattening) over the noisier or reversing ones (balance sheet, headline inflation's volatility).

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,450,615) is up slightly from the prior observation (4,448,289) but sits well within the recent choppy range that has prevailed over the last several weeks (roughly 4,448k-4,470k). Looking at the trend measures rather than the single last tick: assets_change_4w is -10,502M, assets_change_13w is -13,222M, and assets_change_52w is -15,167M, all negative, confirming a gradual runoff trajectory rather than expansion. The 13-week average (4,458,097) is above the latest print, and the series remains ~34k below its 52-week high, consistent with ongoing balance sheet normalization. My prior call of 'down' was directionally correct over the 4-week and 13-week windows despite some noisy week-to-week upticks (the series is not monotonic, consistent with settlement timing/reinvestment noise within a capped runoff). The policy language confirms the normalization program is 'proceeding,' with no signal of tapering the pace or reinvesting more. This supports continued gradual runoff into month-end, though the pace is modest and week-to-week noise could produce a flat or even slightly positive print. I maintain a lean toward continued decline, moderate conviction given the noisy short-term action.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets above the 13w average, or explicit statement language signaling pause/end of runoff, would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print shows slope_2s10s jumping from 0.59 to 0.64pp, a 0.05pp uptick after several months of grinding flatter. My prior "down" call was directionally right through most of the series (0.65→0.58 over the year, slope_change_252d -0.57pp, slope_change_21d and 63d still negative at -0.14/-0.18pp), but the newest observation is a reversal off the cycle low, not a continuation. The specific rebound threshold I set (above 0.82pp) wasn't met, so the long flattening trend is still intact by that test, but the direction of the most recent move is up, not down. The spread remains deeply compressed — only 0.06pp above its 12-month low and at just 8% of its 12-month range (0.58-1.34pp) — so this is a shallow bounce within a still-flat, historically compressed curve, not evidence of a durable steepening. Given the tension between the still-negative 21d/63d trends and the fresh single-observation uptick, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for month-end: the flattening pressure looks exhausted near this floor, but one data point doesn't confirm reversal.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to 0.58-0.59pp with slope_change_21d re-steepening negative would confirm the flattening trend resumed and the bounce was noise.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_above_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its slow, steady decline, now at -0.61, its 52-week low and well below the zero average, indicating looser-than-average financial conditions. My previous "down" call at -0.35 conviction was correct: the index has continued easing since then, moving from roughly -0.59/-0.60 down to -0.61. Momentum remains modestly negative (4-week change -0.01, 13-week change -0.04), showing consistent but decelerating easing rather than a sharp move. The range position at 0.00 confirms we're sitting at the bottom of the observed 52-week range, with no sign of a bounce. There is no policy language accompanying this release to suggest a shift in driver behind the move, so I'm relying purely on the mechanical trend. The pace of decline has been slowing (each weekly step is only about -0.01), suggesting the easing trend, while intact, may be approaching a plateau. Given the consistency of the trend and lack of any reversal signal (no reading above -0.55, no positive 4-week change), I maintain a "down" call, but conviction is capped by the shrinking magnitude of week-over-week moves, which raises the possibility of the series flattening near current levels within the next month.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print clearly above -0.58, or a 4-week change turning positive (>0.02), would signal a tightening reversal or flattening.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print ticked up to 1.86 from a flat cluster around 1.83-1.85, matching the prior high in this window. My previous "up" call was directionally right in the very latest reading but the path since then was mostly sideways-to-slightly-down (21d change -0.02pp) before the final uptick, so conviction should stay modest rather than firm. The 63-day change of +0.10pp shows a genuine, if shallow, upward drift over the medium term, while the 252-day change of 0.00pp indicates no durable trend over a full year — this remains range-bound behavior. Breakeven sits at 1.86%, still -0.14pp below the 2% objective, and at 0.48 of its 12-month range (near the midpoint), not breaking out. Policy language is unchanged and explicitly notes market-based inflation compensation remains low with little-changed survey expectations, reinforcing a low-conviction, range-bound read rather than a decisive move. The bounce to 1.86 could be noise given the tight 1.83-1.86 band of the last 10 prints. I lean toward modest further firming given the two-month drift and the fresh high, but the flat annual change and unchanged policy tone temper conviction.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back to 1.83-1.84 sustained over multiple prints, or a drop in be_change_63d toward zero/negative, would signal the drift has stalled.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was wrong: headline CPI yoy rose from 2.02 to 2.17, and the m/m print (0.27) came in above my stated flip trigger of 0.3%... actually just under it, but combined with the prior month's 0.51 print, the 3m annualized headline pace is 3.47%, well above target and above the 12-month trend. The hurricane-related gasoline effects the Committee flags as "temporary" are the direct cause of this headline pop, which argues for fading it somewhat. Core PCE, the better persistence read, ticked up from 1.42 to 1.54 — a genuine uptick after a multi-month slide to a 12-month low, but still 0.46pp below target and the 3m annualized core pace (1.92%) is actually below the top-line core yoy level, suggesting the acceleration is not yet broad-based. Policy language explicitly attributes the headline rise to storm-related gasoline and reiterates core "remained soft," expecting near-term readings to stay somewhat below 2% before stabilizing. Given the headline base effect is real but the Committee itself calls it temporary and non-recurring, I expect headline yoy to tick up further next month as the full hurricane gasoline effect rolls through, before fading. Net: modest further upside in headline near-term.
Would change its mind if: A drop in headline m/m back near/below 0.1% or reversal of core PCE yoy back toward 1.4% would indicate the gasoline bump is fading and disinflation resumed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 4.20%, unchanged from the prior month and matching the 12-month low. My previous call for a further decline ("down") did not materialize this round—the rate held flat rather than falling further, though it did not reverse upward either, so the call was directionally consistent with continued tightening but wrong on the specific down move this period. Looking at the broader trend: the rate has fallen from 4.70% to 4.20% over the trailing year (-0.50pp on 12m), with -0.20pp over both 3m and 6m windows, and the Sahm-gap is negligible (+0.03pp), confirming no loosening signal whatsoever. The range position sits at 0.00 (right at the 12-month low), and distance from natural rate (~4%) is a modest +0.20pp, meaning the market is tight and getting tighter, not looser. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed describes labor market "continued to strengthen," job gains "solid," and notes the unemployment rate "declined further" despite hurricane disruptions to payrolls. With the rate stabilizing at cycle lows and no evidence of reversal, the most likely near-term outcome is another flat-to-slightly-lower print rather than a reversal upward.
Would change its mind if: A print back up to 4.4% or higher, or two consecutive months of increases, would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has ticked up to 2.42 from 2.37, continuing the modest uptrend flagged last time. Over 63 days the yield is up 0.27pp and over 252 days up 0.12pp, both positive, though the 21-day change is a small +0.04pp — momentum is present but not accelerating sharply. The yield sits at 0.65 of its 12-month range, comfortably in the upper-middle zone, well above the 12m low (2.05) and closer to but still below the 12m high (2.62). The 10s30s slope at 0.41pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal a big steepening/flattening shift in long-end premium specifically. My prior call of "up" at 0.30 conviction is validated by this print: the move from 2.37 to 2.42 is a continuation, not a reversal, and no sustained break below 2.30 occurred. Policy language on inflation expectations is unchanged and low, suggesting the move is not being driven by inflation risk premium repricing but likely reflects supply/term-premium dynamics consistent with gradual upward drift. I maintain a modest up lean, slightly firmer than before given the confirmed continuation, but not high conviction since the recent 21-day pace is tepid and range position isn't extreme.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back below 2.30 over several observations would flip this to down; a break above 2.46 would raise conviction on up further.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Reconciling the panel for a level (duration) call, I exclude curve_slope from the direct read since curve shape is out of scope, though its "flattening exhausted, shallow bounce up" read is a mild cross-check against aggressive bull-steepening narratives.

The core drivers pushing yields higher are inflation (CPI 3m annualized 3.47%, core PCE ticking up off lows, moderate conviction 0.45) and term_premium (10y continuing a confirmed uptrend, 2.37→2.42, moderate conviction 0.35). Inflation_expectations corroborates with a fresh breakeven high (1.86), though low conviction and range-bound. Labor_tightness is flat but at cycle-tight levels with no loosening signal — this is consistent with sustained modest upward pressure on yields via a still-tight labor market keeping the Fed cautious, not a driver of decline.

Working against higher yields: financial_conditions is easing (NFCI at 52w low, -0.61) which normally supports risk-on and can pull term premium down via looser conditions, and balance_sheet continues gradual runoff (down, moderate conviction) which mechanically should support higher long-end yields via reduced Fed demand — actually runoff is consistent with upward yield pressure, not down. Re-examining: balance_sheet "down" refers to the level of assets falling (QT continuing), which is a modest bearish-for-bonds (yield-up) force via reduced official-sector demand, not yield-down. So balance_sheet analyst's own directional framing is on the asset level, but its market implication actually aligns with upward yield pressure, reinforcing term_premium's read given the flagged gap (term_premium lacks balance_sheet input on QT pace/issuance).

Netting: inflation, term_premium, inflation_expectations, and QT-driven supply pressure all point modestly higher; financial_conditions loosening is the main countervailing force but is decelerating and mechanical/no-fresh-catalyst. On balance, I read a modest net upward drift in nominal yields over the one-month horizon, driven primarily by inflation and term premium/supply dynamics, tempered by easier financial conditions capping the magnitude.

discounted.why: Out of scope for a level call; shape/steepening view belongs to another pod.
Low conviction, range-bound breakevens; redundant with sturdier inflation driver.

arbitrated.falsifier: A sustained break of 10y back below 2.30 alongside a CPI reversal toward sub-0.1% m/m and core PCE re-easing toward 1.4% would flip this to a down call.

risks.text: Fed characterizes CPI pop as temporary/hurricane-driven; a fade could reverse the inflation-led yield lift quickly.
NFCI easing trend, if it accelerates rather than plateaus, could pull yields down via looser financial conditions.
Term premium move is tepid on 21d basis; a shift in positioning could stall the uptrend before month-end.
Balance sheet read is inferred indirectly since term_premium analyst lacks QT/supply input directly.

why.inflation: Fresh CPI/core PCE uptick, 3m annualized well above target; primary upward pressure despite temporary-effects caveat.
why.term_premium: Confirmed continuation of 10y uptrend; supply/term-premium dynamics support gradual yield rise, reinforced by unmodeled QT-supply link.
why.labor_tightness: Flat at cycle-tight levels, no loosening signal; mild supportive lean for yields staying firm, not a reversal driver.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI at 52w low and still easing, a countervailing force capping yield upside, though pace of easing is decelerating.
why.balance_sheet: Continued QT/runoff reduces official demand for duration, a modest upward yield force despite the analyst's own 'down' framing on asset levels.

Given the tension between a still-negative 13w/52w trend and a flattening-to-mildly-rising most recent few weeks, I lean toward flat rather than a confident continuation of runoff for the next month-end. The month-end print could easily land close to the current level with noise dominating the announced-but-modest pace of caps.

Call: down, conviction 0.55

The prior view called flat expecting a possible re-steepening bounce, but the data shows the opposite: 21d change -0.08pp, 63d -0.35pp, 252d -0.74pp — a persistent, accelerating-into-the-latest-print flattening trend, and the last three prints (0.53, 0.52, 0.51) are a steady grind down with no sign of stabilizing. The Fed's policy language shift to an actual rate hike (from 1-1.25% to 1.25-1.5%) with continued gradual-hike guidance and "labor market conditions will remain strong" reinforces front-end pressure (dgs2 has risen to 1.89%) while the long end (dgs10 2.40%) is comparatively anchored, consistent with continued flattening. With the spread already at its 12-month low and every trend window pointing the same direction, the flattening trend looks intact rather than reversing. I was wrong last time to call flat; the bounce was noise and the underlying flattening resumed as the falsifier condition specified.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back toward 0.58-0.60pp with slope_change_21d turning positive would signal the flattening trend has stalled or reversed.

Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI index has continued its slow easing trend, now printing -0.61 for four consecutive weeks, at the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.00, at the 52w low). The 4-week change is essentially flat (-0.01) and the 13-week change is only modestly negative (-0.05), showing the pace of easing has decelerated sharply and flattened out at this new low level. My prior call for further easing ('down') was directionally correct as the index did drift from -0.60 to -0.61, but the momentum has now clearly stalled — the last four readings are identical, indicating conditions have plateaued rather than continuing to loosen. With no new policy language addressing this driver, and the series essentially flat-lining at its most accommodative level in over a year, the most defensible call for the next month is flat, though there's a slight residual easing bias given the multi-month downward trend and readings still sitting at a fresh 52w low with no signs of reversal. Conviction is modest given the flattening momentum outweighs the longer-term trend.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print clearly above -0.59, or 4-week change turning positive (>0.02), would signal a tightening reversal; a fresh push below -0.61 would confirm renewed easing.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: breakeven_10y continued its steady climb from 1.88 to 1.96 over the ten prints, extending the drift I flagged last time. The most recent two observations are flat at 1.96, and the 21-day change (+0.12pp) still outpaces the 63-day change (+0.09pp), which itself outpaces the 252-day change (+0.01pp) — a sign that the recent grind higher is a fresh, short-term phenomenon rather than a continuation of a long stale trend. Level is now within 0.04pp of the 2% objective, and range position at 0.71 (near the 12-month high of 2.08, well above the 12-month low of 1.66) suggests the market is pricing inflation compensation back toward target after a period of subdued readings. Policy language remains unchanged/stale, describing inflation compensation as "low," which is now somewhat lagging what the market has actually done — this is old context, not a fresh signal against the move. The two consecutive flat prints at 1.96 raise the question of whether momentum is stalling, but with no reversal and 21d/63d changes still positive, I read this as a pause within an uptrend rather than exhaustion. I'll maintain a modestly positive call, tempered by the plateau in the last two prints.
Would change its mind if: A reversal below 1.93-1.94 sustained over multiple prints, or be_change_21d turning negative, would signal the uptrend has ended.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked down to 2.13 from 2.17, with mom 0.21 easing from an elevated 0.27 print but still above the 3m average of 0.18 — so the "up" call was roughly right on momentum but the y/y print itself dipped slightly rather than rising, a mixed outcome. Core PCE y/y actually rose to 1.55 from 1.54, consistent with the disinflation trend stalling rather than reversing further. The gasoline/hurricane bump the Committee flagged appears to be fading per their own language shift ("have affected" past tense vs "will continue to affect"), which argues for headline convergence back toward core's softer trend. Core_3m_annualized at 1.68% remains below the trailing 12m core pace, and core sits 0.45pp below target — genuine disinflationary undertow persists. Headline_yoy_change_3m is slightly negative (-0.05pp) showing no clear reacceleration in the yoy trend despite the volatile monthly prints. With mom_gap_vs_outgoing negative (-0.22pp), the most recent month's pace decelerated versus the prior month. On balance, energy base effects are fading and core inflation remains subdued, suggesting headline y/y is more likely to hold flat or drift marginally lower next release rather than accelerate. I'm revising down from my prior "up" call given the softer mom and stalled core.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.3% or core PCE yoy breaking above 1.6% would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this flat/down lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.10%, a new 12-month low, extending the downtrend rather than stalling it as I had previously flagged as a risk. My prior "flat" call was too cautious — the series shows a clear, persistent decline (4.70 to 4.10 over the trailing year), with the most recent move (4.20, 4.20, 4.10) confirming continued tightening rather than a pause. The 3m and 6m changes are both -0.20pp, and the 12m change is -0.60pp, all pointing the same direction with no countervailing uptick. Sahm-gap is trivial at +0.07pp, meaning there is no recession-signal risk building here — this is a low, still-falling rate. Distance from the ~4% natural rate is just 0.10pp, so the labor market is now essentially at or slightly above full employment territory, but momentum remains downward, not upward. Policy language reinforces this, describing "continued strengthening" of the labor market despite hurricane disruptions, with unemployment "declining further." Given the level is already near-natural and momentum is intact, I lean toward another modest decline or flat print, but with the rate this close to natural, further big drops are less likely — a flattening near the low is plausible next.
Would change its mind if: A print back up to 4.3% or higher, or two consecutive monthly increases, would signal the tightening trend has reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

Call: flat, conviction 0.25

Report:
  The 10y yield has pulled back from the recent local peak of 2.49 to 2.40 over the last few observations, after rising from 2.35 to 2.49 across the prior stretch. Net, the 21d change is still +0.03pp and 63d is +0.09pp (both mildly positive), but the most recent three prints (2.47, 2.42, 2.43, 2.40) show a modest downward drift from the high, not a continuation of the uptrend I flagged last time. My previous "up" call partially played out (yield did push to 2.49, above my 2.46 trigger) but has since faded back toward the middle of the range rather than extending. The yield sits at 0.61 of its 12-month range — still upper-middle, not extreme — and the 252d change is -0.11pp, reminding us the longer-run trend is still down from a year ago. The 30s10s slope at +0.34pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal a strong steepening/flattening impulse from the long end specifically. Given the recent stall/reversal after touching range highs, I see less basis for continued "up" conviction; the data now look more consistent with a flat-to-slightly-lower drift near-term, absent a clear catalyst. I'm scaling back conviction and shifting toward flat/mild-down given the loss of upward momentum.
Would change its mind if: A push back above 2.49 (12m high) over several observations would revive the up case; a drop below 2.35 would confirm a down trend.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,441,317) sits essentially at the 13-week average (4,446,784.69) and only modestly below the prior observation (4,439,145 → 4,441,317), i.e. a small uptick after several weeks of choppy decline. Looking at the last 13 observations, the series has been oscillating in a roughly 4,437,000-4,458,000 band with no clean monotonic trend — a bounce, then a fade, then another bounce. The 4-week change (-7,363) and 13-week change (-19,800) both still point down, consistent with continued slow runoff rather than any resumption of expansion, but the magnitude is small relative to typical monthly cap-driven drawdowns and the most recent two prints actually ticked up. My prior flat call was reasonable: the series has stayed in a tight range without a clear renewed multi-week decline. Now, given the still-negative 4w/13w changes but a recent tick higher and no statement language reaffirming aggressive runoff, I lean toward a mild continuation of gradual runoff over the next month, but conviction is limited by the choppiness and lack of a clean trend. The absence of any statement commentary is itself notable and slightly reduces confidence in an aggressive pace call.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average and 52w trend, or statement language signaling slowed/paused runoff, would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down/flattening) was wrong for this most recent print: the spread rebounded to 0.58 from a low of 0.53, and slope_change_21d flipped positive (+0.07pp), exactly the reversal condition I had specified. The last three readings (0.59, 0.60, 0.58) show a modest bounce off the recent trough. That said, the broader trend remains one of flattening: slope_change_63d (-0.21) and especially slope_change_252d (-0.69) are both firmly negative, and the spread sits at just 0.10 of its 12-month range (0.50-1.28), meaning it's still very close to its lows and far from its highs. The curve remains deeply compressed relative to the past year even after the recent uptick. Policy language shifted to holding rates steady this meeting after a hike last meeting, with continued signaling of "further gradual increases" ahead — this keeps front-end pressure alive, which tends to work against sustained steepening. Given the tension between a genuine short-term bounce and a still-dominant multi-quarter flattening trend near cycle lows, I read this as a stalled flattening rather than a confirmed reversal into a new steepening trend. Direction is uncertain enough to call it flat with modest conviction, leaning down given the range position.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.53pp would confirm renewed flattening; a push above 0.65-0.70pp with continued positive 21d/63d changes would confirm genuine steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has continued its extremely gradual grind lower, from -0.60 to -0.62 over 13 weeks, with the most recent four readings all at -0.62, matching the 52-week low. The 4-week change is essentially zero (-0.00), and the 13-week change is a modest -0.02, indicating conditions are still loosening but at a decelerating pace — momentum is flattening even as the level sits near the loosest point in the observed range (range_position 0.03, right at the floor). My prior "flat" call was reasonably close: the index barely moved over the last period, consistent with the near-zero 4-week change. Given the index is pinned at its 52-week low with no upward pressure and no policy commentary addressing this driver, the most defensible read is continued mild easing bias rather than a reversal, since there is no evidence of tightening (no print above -0.59, no positive 4-week change). However, the magnitude of further easing looks small — the series has essentially plateaued at -0.61/-0.62 for many weeks. I lean slightly toward continued easing (down) but with low-to-moderate conviction given the flattening trend suggests we may be near a floor.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print clearly above -0.59, or 4-week change turning positive (>0.02), would signal a tightening reversal; continued prints at -0.62 confirm stagnation.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is confirmed. Breakeven_10y has continued its steady climb, printing at the top of its 12-month range (2.11%, be_range_position=1.00) with no sign of reversal. The 21-day (+0.15pp) and 63-day (+0.24pp) changes are both solidly positive and accelerating relative to the 252-day change (+0.04pp), indicating the uptrend is a recent, strengthening phenomenon rather than legacy drift. The series itself shows a clean monotonic grind higher across the last ten prints with no down-ticks. Policy language corroborates this from the Fed's own read: the statement was explicitly revised to note that "market-based measures of inflation compensation have increased in recent months," a direct acknowledgment of the same move visible in the data. Distance from the 2% target is now +0.11pp, meaning breakevens have pushed modestly above objective, reinforcing the sense that the move is a genuine shift in inflation compensation rather than noise. With the series at cycle highs, momentum intact, and policy commentary confirming the trend, I maintain a directional call, with slightly higher conviction than before given the confirmation of persistence and the more forceful language change.
Would change its mind if: A print back below ~2.05-2.06 sustained over multiple releases, or be_change_21d turning negative, would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline mom came in at 0.43%, well above my 0.3% trigger, and headline yoy ticked up to 2.15% from 2.13%. My prior "flat" call was too cautious — the momentum signal I flagged as a reversal condition fired. Core PCE yoy also rose to 1.58% from 1.55%, its second consecutive increase after bottoming at 1.42%. The recent trend: headline mom_3m_avg is 0.30%, well above the outgoing 0.16% print, and headline_3m_annualized (3.67%) sits notably above the yoy print (2.15%), signaling building pipeline pressure — likely gasoline/hurricane-related per the policy language, which explicitly flags gasoline as boosting headline while core/ex-food-energy remains "soft." Core_3m_annualized (1.78%) is still below the yoy level, suggesting core is not yet in a strong acceleration phase, but it has stopped declining and turned up two months running. Headline is being pushed by energy (temporary, per hurricane commentary) while core is only gently firming. Given the strong recent mom prints (0.51, 0.27, 0.21, 0.43) and reversal of the core downtrend, I lean toward continued modest upward drift in headline yoy next month, but tempered by the transitory nature of the energy component flagged in the statement.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.1% or core PCE yoy falling back below 1.5% would signal the energy-driven pop was transitory and reverse this up lean.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

  The prior call (further decline) was correct: unemployment printed 4.00%, a new 12-month low, down from 4.10% the month before. The trend remains firmly one-directional — 3m change -0.20pp, 6m -0.30pp, 12m -0.70pp — with no reversals or upticks anywhere in the last several prints. The series sits exactly at its 12-month low (unrate_above_12m_low = 0.00) and range position is at the floor (0.0), meaning there is no slack building and no sign of the loosening the Sahm-rule spirit warns about (sahm_gap is trivially positive at only 0.10pp, driven mechanically by the 3m average sitting slightly above the current low, not by any turn upward). Fed language confirms this, upgrading from "strengthen" to explicit tightening acknowledgment alongside a rate hike, consistent with a labor market perceived as at or beyond full employment (4.00% is now at/below the ~4% natural-rate anchor). With such a persistent grinding decline and no counter-signal in the data, the base case is continuation or at worst stabilization near the floor. A reversal higher would require a fresh shock; nothing here points to one. I lean toward another flat-to-down print, though the room to fall further from an already-low level tempers conviction on continued decline versus flat.
Would change its mind if: A print rising to 4.2% or higher, or two consecutive monthly increases, would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: the 10y has pushed decisively higher since, and the latest print (2.72) sits essentially at the top of the 12-month range (range_position 0.99, 0.67pp above the 12m low). Momentum is consistently positive across horizons — +0.32pp over 21d, +0.35pp over 63d, +0.23pp over 252d — indicating a sustained, not just transient, rise in long-end yields. The 10s30s slope at +0.23pp shows the long end still steepening modestly relative to 30s, consistent with term-premium building rather than pure duration-buying compression. Policy language now explicitly notes market-based inflation compensation has risen in recent months, which if anything corroborates upward pressure on nominal long yields (compensation for expected price erosion, distinct from the level call itself, but consistent with a rising term premium narrative). With yields sitting at the 12-month high and clear multi-horizon upward momentum, the balance of evidence favors continued firmness or further increase into month-end rather than a reversal. I'm revising up from my prior flat stance, having misread the trend last time — the range-position and multi-window momentum are unambiguous signals here.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~2.60 (mid-range) over several observations, or a reversal in 21d/63d momentum, would revive a flat/down case.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,411,660) continues the downward drift, confirming the prior "down" call. The series is noisy week to week (e.g., 4,419,225 → 4,420,745 → 4,434,863 → 4,411,660), but the trend measures are unambiguous: 4-week change -29,657M, 13-week change -38,955M, 52-week change -57,042M, and the level sits 72,832M below the 52-week high with a -0.88% 13-week pct change. There is no sign of a rise back above the 13-week average sustained for multiple weeks — the one uptick to 4,434,863 was immediately reversed. No statement language is available this cycle to signal a pause or taper in runoff, so I have no qualitative confirmation either way, but the absence of any dovish/pause language means the default assumption of continued runoff at the prevailing pace stands. Given the consistent negative slope across all horizons (4w, 13w, 52w) and no offsetting evidence, I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, continuing the QT trajectory. Conviction is moderate-clear given the consistency of the multi-horizon negative changes, tempered by the single-week noise in the series and lack of fresh policy language this period.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average and 52w trend, or statement language signaling slowed/paused runoff, would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call is roughly validated: spread has oscillated in a narrow 0.62-0.68pp band over the last several readings, ending at 0.62pp, essentially where it sat two prints ago. The 21d (+0.03pp) and 63d (+0.04pp) changes are both marginally positive but tiny, well within noise, while the 252d change (-0.54pp) shows the dominant trend over the past year has been substantial flattening — confirmed by slope_range_position of just 0.16, meaning the curve sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (0.50-1.23pp) and is 0.62pp from flat/inversion. The latest print (0.62) actually ticked down from 0.63/0.64, the opposite of steepening continuation needed to confirm my prior falsifier threshold of a push above 0.65-0.70. So neither falsifier condition (break below 0.53 or sustained push above 0.65-0.70) has fired cleanly, but the tick lower keeps the flattening bias alive. Policy language shift from "further" tightening language toward a maintained/pause framing softens the pace of front-end pressure, which could stabilize the curve rather than force continued flattening. Net: still range-bound, no clear directional impulse, with a very slight bias toward continued mild flattening given proximity to 12-month lows and the negative 1yr trend outweighing the flat short-term momentum.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 0.53pp confirms flattening resumption; a sustained push above 0.68-0.70pp with positive 21d/63d momentum confirms steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/easing continuation) was wrong: the series has clearly reversed off its lows. After sitting flat around -0.61/-0.62 for most of the window, the last three prints stepped up to -0.59, -0.58, -0.56 — a consistent, monotonic rise. The 4-week change is now +0.06 (above my +0.02 reversal threshold) and even the 13-week change has turned positive (+0.04), confirming this isn't just noise from one print. Range position has moved to 0.44 of the 52-week band, up from near the low end, and the series is now 0.06 above its 52-week low. Conditions remain loose in absolute terms (-0.56, still well below zero), so this is a move from very loose toward merely loose, not a shift to tight. But the momentum is unambiguous over the last three observations: NFCI is rising, meaning financial conditions are tightening at the margin. With no offsetting statement language and a clean three-week monotonic climb, I'm calling this up (tightening) for month-end, though I temper conviction since the absolute level is still deeply negative and three prints is a modest run.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below -0.59, or 4-week change falling back near zero/negative, would signal the tightening move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: breakeven_10y has continued to grind higher, now sitting at 2.12 after touching 2.13, near the top of its 12-month range (be_range_position 0.96, be_above_12m_low +0.46pp). The medium-term trend remains firmly up: be_change_63d +0.29pp and be_change_252d +0.11pp both positive, and the Fed's own language confirms this, upgrading its description from inflation compensation "remains low" to "have increased in recent months but remain low" — an explicit acknowledgment of the rise in market-based measures. However, the most recent short-term signal is softening: be_change_21d has slowed to +0.03pp, and the last two prints (2.13, 2.13, 2.12) show flattening/slight pullback after a steady climb from 2.09. Breakevens are close to a plateau just under the 2.14 high, with distance from the 2% target still modest at +0.12pp — not stretched, but consistent with a leveling-off. Given the deceleration in momentum even as the broader trend and policy language stay supportive, I lean toward continued mild upward drift but with less conviction than before, allowing for a flat month given the recent stall.
Would change its mind if: A print below ~2.09-2.10 sustained, or be_change_21d turning negative, would confirm the plateau has become a reversal.
Leaned on: be_change_63d, be_change_21d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 13 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirmed the prior "up" call: headline CPI yoy rose to 2.26% from 2.15%, with mom of 0.27%, and core PCE yoy ticked up to 1.63% from 1.58%. Neither of my stated reversal conditions (mom below 0.1%, core PCE back below 1.5%) triggered, so the up lean was validated.
  
  Looking forward, momentum remains firmly upward: headline_3m_annualized (3.68%) is running well above the current yoy rate, and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.30%) exceeds the outgoing month's print that will roll off (-0.05%), producing a positive mom_gap_vs_outgoing of +0.35pp. That base-effect arithmetic mechanically pushes yoy higher next month unless mom decelerates sharply. Core PCE is also trending up for three straight months (1.54→1.55→1.58→1.63), narrowing the distance from target to -0.37pp, suggesting the acceleration is not purely energy/food noise but has some underlying persistence. Policy language corroborates this, shifting from inflation "running below 2 percent" and "expected to move up" — consistent with the observed acceleration. Yoy_range_position at 0.78 shows we're near the top of the 12-month range, historically a point where deceleration risk rises, but current momentum indicators dominate near-term.
  
  I lean up again, with slightly higher conviction given confirmed follow-through and core corroboration.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.1% or core PCE yoy reversing back toward 1.5% would signal the acceleration has stalled and reverse this up lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, headline_mom_3m_avg, core_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 20 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print ticked up to 4.10% from a 12-month low of 4.00%, giving one month of reversal after a long downtrend (12m change -0.50pp, 6m -0.30pp, 3m -0.10pp). My prior "down" call was wrong for this print: the rate rose rather than continuing to fall. That said, the move is small (+0.10pp off the low), the Sahm gap is trivial (+0.07pp), and the 3-month average (4.07%) is still below the most recent print, meaning the broader trend remains one of a historically tight labor market near/at the natural rate (~4.0-4.1% vs 4% natural, distance only 0.10pp). One month's uptick is not yet two consecutive increases or a clear trend break — it could be noise around a cyclical floor. Policy language continues to describe the labor market as strengthening/staying strong, with no acknowledgment of loosening. Given the level is already near the natural rate and the recent uptick is modest and singular, I don't see strong evidence for continued loosening, but the clean downtrend has stalled. This looks like a flat-to-slightly-up regime now rather than continued tightening.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive monthly increase to 4.2% or higher would confirm a genuine reversal/loosening trend rather than noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 2.87, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.92, +0.82pp above the 12m low of 2.05%). Momentum remains positive across horizons: +0.17pp over 21d, +0.55pp over 63d, +0.51pp over 252d, confirming a sustained uptrend rather than a blip. The last ten prints show a modest pullback from the 2.94 peak but yields are still oscillating in the high 2.80s-2.90s, well above range mid-point — no reversal signal per my prior falsifier condition. The 10s30s slope at +0.26pp is positive but not steep, consistent with a long end that has repriced higher without extreme term-premium blowout. Policy language shift — noting market-based inflation compensation "has increased in recent months" — supports somewhat firmer long-end pricing, consistent with the observed yield rise, and is corroborating rather than contradicting the measurement-based call. My prior up call at 0.55 was directionally correct: yields have stayed elevated near range highs with intact positive momentum. I maintain an up view, though conviction is tempered since the 21d change decelerated from the 63d pace and the last two prints (2.90, 2.87) show a slight cooling from the cycle high of 2.94, suggesting the move may be losing some steam.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~2.60 (mid-range) sustained over several observations, or a clear negative reversal in 21d/63d momentum, would shift this to flat/down.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The level case for Treasury yields is net higher, built on convergent, fresh signals from term premium, inflation, and financial conditions, with balance sheet runoff as a secondary structural tailwind. Inflation (formed 13 days ago, still fairly fresh) shows confirmed acceleration in both headline CPI and core PCE with mechanical base-effect momentum pointing further up next print — this is the most fundamentally-grounded upward driver. Term premium corroborates directly: 10y at cycle highs, positive multi-horizon momentum, though decelerating slightly. Financial conditions just reversed a long easing trend with a clean monotonic three-week tightening move — fresh and unambiguous in direction even if the absolute level stays loose; tightening financial conditions historically travel with higher, not lower, yields as the causal read here is Fed-driven repricing rather than risk-off flight-to-quality (the latter would argue the opposite, which is a real risk flagged below). Balance sheet continues its QT drift, a modest structural support for higher term premium/duration supply, consistent with the term_premium analyst's own noted gap (missing balance sheet input) — this cross-read reinforces rather than contradicts the up case. Labor tightness is flat/stalling-near-natural-rate, giving no directional push either way, and curve_slope is a curve-shape read I set aside per my mandate. Inflation expectations lean up but momentum is decelerating (21d slowing to +0.03), so I discount its incremental force somewhat versus the harder inflation-print evidence. Overall: multiple independent, non-stale drivers align on "up," with only curve shape and labor giving no signal — a reasonably convergent, not unanimous, level call.

Term premium momentum is already decelerating; a further stall would remove the strongest single up-driver.
Inflation expectations plateauing near 12-month highs could cap further breakeven-driven yield rises.

trade.rationale: Convergent up-signals from inflation, term premium, financial conditions, and QT support higher yields broadly; tilt toward DGS10 since term premium/inflation evidence is longer-horizon and cleaner.

why.financial_conditions: Clean 3-week monotonic tightening reversal off lows; fresh and unambiguous direction even though absolute level still loose.

why.labor_tightness: Flat near natural rate after single uptick; no clear directional push, treated as neutral for the level view.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.45) was directionally correct: latest reading 4,392,198 is below the previous 4,401,222 and well below the 13w average (4,418,993). Total assets have been on a bumpy but net-declining path — 13w change -56,482M, 52w change -77,420M, and now sitting 92,294M below the 52w high. The last few prints show some noise (up-down-up-down) but the most recent move is a fresh leg down to a new low in this window, consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff at a modest pace. The 4w change (-1,203M) is small in isolation, but it follows a decline from 4,407,258, meaning the last two data points reversed a brief uptick and resumed the downward trend. There is no statement language shown to indicate any change to the pace or continuation of runoff, so I have no evidence of a pause or reinvestment shift. Absent any policy signal, the measurement trend remains the dominant input, and it continues to point to gradual QT rather than stabilization. I maintain a "down" call, though conviction is only moderate given the choppiness of recent weekly prints and the very small 4-week change.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average with statement language confirming slowed/paused runoff would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The spread has printed a fresh 12-month low, closing the series at 0.47pp after a steady grind down from 0.58pp four observations back. My prior "flat" call was wrong: the falsifier condition I set (a clear break below 0.53pp) has been met decisively, and momentum confirms it — slope_change_21d is -0.15pp and slope_change_63d is -0.06pp, both negative, with the 252-day change even more negative at -0.65pp, showing this is a persistent multi-horizon flattening trend, not noise. slope_range_position sits at 0.00, meaning the curve is at the very bottom of its 12-month range with no cushion above the low. The Fed just hiked the funds rate (target raised to 1-1/2 to 1-3/4%) while retaining "further gradual adjustments" language — a front-end-driven flattening consistent with dgs2 rising alongside dgs10 but the short end moving faster relative to compression at the long end. The 10s30s slope at 0.23pp is still positive but well off wide levels, corroborating broad curve compression rather than an isolated 2s10s quirk. With the spread already at cycle lows, momentum negative across all windows, and continued gradual-hike guidance from the Committee, the path of least resistance is further flattening into month-end, though the magnitude of further downside may be modest given how compressed the curve already is.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55-0.58pp with positive 21d momentum would signal stabilization or reversal of the flattening trend.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s, dgs2, dgs10

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady, monotonic climb from -0.62 toward -0.50, with the latest print at -0.50 — the highest (tightest) value in the 52-week window and essentially at the 52-week high. The prior call (up, 0.55) was correct: the 4-week change has stayed positive throughout and the trend has not stalled or reversed, so no falsifier condition was triggered. Momentum is consistent rather than accelerating: 4-week change (+0.05) and 13-week change (+0.11) show a steady, gradual pace of tightening, not a sharp move. Conditions remain historically loose in absolute terms (index still well below zero at -0.50), but the trajectory is unambiguous — a persistent, unbroken drift toward tighter conditions over the past quarter, with range_position at 0.89 indicating we are near the top of the recent trading range. Absent any reversal signal or deceleration in the latest prints (the last few increments are actually the smallest of the series: +0.02, +0.01, +0.01), there is a slight hint the pace of tightening may be decelerating, but the direction itself remains intact. I maintain a modestly confident up call for next month-end.
Would change its mind if: A print flat or below -0.51, or 4-week change turning zero/negative, would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_high

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call has been contradicted: breakeven_10y has drifted from 2.08 down to 2.05 over the last two prints, with be_change_21d now -0.07pp — exactly the reversal signal I flagged as a falsifier. Longer-window measures (63d +0.11, 252d +0.08) still show the broader uptrend intact, and the series sits at 0.81 of its 12-month range, well above the low, so this reads as a mild pullback within an otherwise elevated range rather than a structural break. Still, the most recent two observations (2.05, 2.05) are the lowest in the sample window and the short-term momentum has clearly turned negative. Policy language is unchanged/stale, describing breakevens as "increased in recent months but remain low," which doesn't help distinguish continuation from reversal at this margin. Distance from the 2% target is only +0.05pp, meaning expectations are barely above objective, and the recent softening reduces the case for the Committee treating this as expectations drift upward. Given the recent 21-day decline outweighs longer-term momentum for a one-month-ahead call, I lean toward continued softness or flat-to-down, but the range position and longer trend argue against a strong reversal call. I'm revising down from my prior up-bias, now leaning flat-to-slightly-down.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.08-2.09 sustained over multiple prints would reassert the uptrend; continued prints at or below 2.05 would confirm the reversal.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call largely played out over the past several prints: headline CPI yoy rose from ~2.13 to 2.33 over the last three readings, and core PCE yoy ticked up to 1.64, its highest in the window. However, the newest single month is a caution flag: headline_mom just printed +0.02%, well below the 3m average of 0.24% and below my own stated 0.1% falsifier threshold. This is the softest monthly print in the entire 13-month series (tied with -0.05 and -0.08 as the lowest, but those were early in the window when yoy was falling). The 3m annualized rates (headline 2.89%, core 2.66%) are still running above the yoy print, meaning underlying momentum has been outpacing the yoy figure — but the most recent month's near-zero print suggests that momentum may be fading right at the margin. Policy language confirms inflation has "continued to run below 2 percent" but is "expected to move up this year," consistent with a firming-but-not-yet-at-target trend. Given the mixed signal — strong 3-month trend and rising yoy, but a very weak latest month — I moderate my conviction. The yoy trend still points up given the 3m annualized cushion above the current yoy print, but the outgoing month's weakness tempers confidence in continued acceleration next release.
Would change its mind if: A second consecutive sub-0.1% headline mom print, or core PCE yoy falling back below 1.55%, would confirm stalling and flip this to flat/down.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate ticked back down to 4.00% from 4.10%, matching the 12-month low reached two prints ago. My prior "flat" call was too cautious given the data actually resumed its downtrend rather than stalling — the brief bounce to 4.10%/4.20% proved to be noise, not a reversal. Over 3/6/12 months the trend is clearly downward (-0.10/-0.30/-0.40 pp), the series sits at its 12-month low with zero Sahm-rule gap, and range position is at 0 (bottom of the 12-month range). Policy language reinforces this: the Fed characterizes the labor market as having "continued to strengthen," job gains as "solid," and conditions as "strong," with no hedging language suggesting concern about loosening. There is no evidence of a rapid rise off a trailing low — quite the opposite, the market is pinned at the tightest level observed. Given the level is already at/below the natural rate anchor (4%) and momentum is flat-to-tightening, the most likely near-term outcome is unemployment holding near 4.0% or ticking marginally in either direction within noise. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down, but with the rate already at cycle lows, further declines have less room, so I keep conviction modest.
Would change its mind if: A print rising to 4.2% or higher for two consecutive months would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted down over the last several observations (2.89→2.74), with dgs10_change_21d now -0.13pp, reversing some of the prior uptrend. However, the longer-window momentum (63d/252d +0.32pp) remains solidly positive, and the yield still sits at 0.78 of its 12-month range — closer to the highs than the lows, well above the 2.05% low. The 10s30s slope (+0.23pp) is modest and not signaling a sharp long-end selloff or a flight-to-quality steepening. My prior call of 'up' was partially wrong for the most recent month: the near-term move was down, not up, so the short-horizon momentum failed to confirm the longer trend. Given the mixed signal — recent pullback in yields but still-elevated range position and positive medium-term momentum — I see this as a genuine tug-of-war rather than a clear directional call. The recent 21d softening is the freshest and most relevant signal for a one-month-ahead call, but it's not decisive enough (only -0.13pp, no sustained break below range) to call outright down. I'll shift to a slight lean down from my prior up-lean, reflecting the recent reversal while acknowledging the level still sits high in-range.
Would change its mind if: A resumption of the 63d uptrend (yields back above ~2.85 sustained) or a clear steepening of 30s10s would flip this back to up.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  balance_sheet lacks curve_slope — Reinvestment caps and Treasury issuance mix can affect pace of SOMA runoff.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Realized CPI surprises often drive short-term breakeven moves and would clarify if the dip is noise or trend.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — Oil/commodity price shifts embedded in financial conditions often explain breakeven swings independent of policy.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of QT/runoff and Treasury issuance guidance directly affects long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-on/risk-off tone would clarify if the recent dip reflects flight-to-quality.

arbitrated.notes: The panel is mixed but the more directly rate-relevant, freshest reads skew modestly toward higher yields, tempered by a genuine tug-of-war in term premium and a cooling inflation-expectations signal. Financial conditions show an unambiguous, monotonic tightening trend at a 52-week high — a live, non-stale signal that historically correlates with rising nominal yields as the Fed continues gradual hikes. Inflation itself is still "up" on trend (yoy rising, 3m annualized above yoy) even though the latest single print was soft — the trend, not the one noisy month, is the better one-month-ahead read. Labor tightness is flat but pinned at cycle-low unemployment with no loosening signal, consistent with continued gradual policy firming rather than any dovish pivot — a mild supportive input for the up case rather than a drag. Against this, term premium itself is genuinely torn: 21d momentum down, but 63d/252d momentum and range position still elevated — I read this as a real split, not a signal to fight the other drivers, so I size it down rather than let it flip the call. Inflation-expectations softening is real but explicitly flagged as lacking the inflation driver's fresher CPI read, which argues up — so I lean on inflation over inflation-expectations per the gap-override rule. Balance sheet continues gradual runoff (modest, per its own admission, choppy) — a modest independent upward pressure on term premium/long-end yields via supply, but I keep conviction low given noise. Curve slope is explicitly out of scope for this pod. Netting these: modest, not high-conviction, lean toward higher yields across the curve, driven mainly by financial conditions tightening and firming inflation trend, partially offset by term premium's internal split and expectations softening.

why.labor_tightness: Pinned at cycle lows with no loosening evidence; mildly supports continued gradual tightening bias, low conviction given flat call.
