user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,372,886) extends the decline from the prior observation (4,385,903), confirming the previous call was correct. Looking at the full series, total assets have fallen fairly steadily from ~4,434,863 down to 4,372,886, a drop of roughly 68,431 over 13 weeks (-1.54%) and about 19,312 over the most recent 4 weeks. The pace of decline in the last few prints (4,386,104 → 4,383,684 → 4,385,903 → 4,372,886) shows a small wobble but the net trend remains downward, consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff. There is no statement language shown to indicate any change in reinvestment policy or a pause in caps, so the default assumption is continuation of the existing runoff pace. The asset level remains well below the 52-week high (-104,017), and the 4w change is negative, reinforcing the runoff read. Nothing here suggests deceleration or a pause; the trend is a steady, moderate pace of contraction. Absent any new policy language, the most probable path over the next month is a continued modest decline in total assets, in line with the established QT trajectory.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average with statement language confirming slowed/paused runoff would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: slope fell from 0.51 to 0.47 to 0.46 over the last two prints, confirming continued flattening. The spread now sits at 0.46pp, just 0.05pp above its 12-month low (0.41) and in the bottom 8% of its 12-month range (range_position 0.08) — a compressed, historically flat curve. Momentum remains negative across horizons: 21d change -0.01pp (roughly flat short-term), but 63d -0.13pp and 252d -0.59pp show the dominant multi-month trend is firmly toward flattening. The rate hike (fed funds raised to 1-1/2 to 1-3/4%) with continued "gradual adjustments" language pushed the front end (dgs2 +2.49%) while the long end (dgs10 +2.95%) rose less on a relative basis, consistent with further flattening pressure as policy tightens against a well-anchored long end. The 10s30s slope of 0.16pp confirms the long end itself is also fairly compressed, offering no signal of steepening pressure from further out the curve. With the spread near cycle lows and no rebound above the 0.55-0.58pp threshold I flagged previously, the falsifier for reversal has not been met. I lean toward continued modest flattening or at best stabilization near current depressed levels, but the deceleration in 21d momentum (-0.01 vs -0.13, -0.59) tempers conviction that the pace of flattening will accelerate further.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.55-0.58pp with positive 21d and 63d momentum would signal stabilization or reversal of the flattening trend.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s, dgs2, dgs10

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print of -0.53 is a fall from -0.51 the prior week, and the 4-week change is now -0.03, i.e. easing, not tightening. My prior "up" call at 0.50 conviction was wrong on this most recent data — the trend that had been building over roughly the last 8-9 weeks (from -0.50 toward tighter readings) has stalled and reversed over the last two prints. Looking at the full 13-week window, conditions did tighten overall (nfci_change_13w +0.09, and nfci_range_position at 0.77 shows we're near the tighter end of the recent 52-week range), but the most recent momentum is now pointing the other way, back toward looser conditions. NFCI remains deeply negative (-0.53, distance from zero -0.53), meaning conditions are still far looser than the historical average — there's no tightening regime here, just a mild multi-week wobble within a persistently easy financial-conditions environment. Given the reversal in the 4-week change and the last two consecutive declines, I now lean toward continued easing (lower NFCI) into month end, but conviction is limited since the longer 13-week trend still shows net tightening and the move is small in magnitude.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.51 or 4-week change turning positive again would signal renewed tightening rather than easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was wrong: the last ten prints show breakeven_10y stabilizing at 2.17-2.18, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.98), not reversing lower. The series has essentially plateaued over the last several observations (2.17,2.17,2.18,2.17,2.17) after a run-up (21d +0.12, 63d +0.08, 252d +0.26), suggesting the uptrend has paused rather than reversed. Breakevens sit 0.51pp above the 12-month low and only 0.01pp below the 12-month high, and 0.17pp above the 2% objective — modestly elevated but not stretched. Policy language confirms market-based inflation compensation "has increased in recent months but remains low," consistent with an uptrend that has now flattened at elevated levels. With no fresh catalyst in the data and the last five prints essentially flat within a 0.01pp band, I don't see strong evidence for either a fresh leg higher or a reversal lower. Given the recent stall at cycle highs, the most defensible call is flat, with a slight upward lean since the multi-month trend remains intact and momentum hasn't broken down.
Would change its mind if: A break below 2.14 sustained over multiple prints would confirm reversal; a fresh push above 2.20 would reassert the uptrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call played out: headline CPI y/y rose from 2.33 to 2.47, and core PCE y/y jumped from 1.64 to 1.91 — a notably large 0.27pp single-month move. Momentum confirms this isn't noise: headline_3m_annualized (2.22%) and especially core_3m_annualized (2.96%) both run above the recent y/y pace, and both headline_yoy_change_3m and core_yoy_change_3m are firmly positive (+0.32/+0.33pp). The latest headline_mom of 0.26% is above the 3m average of 0.18%, reversing the prior month's soft 0.02% print — so the deceleration signal I was watching for did not materialize; instead the trend re-accelerated. Yoy_range_position sits at 1.00, meaning headline is at the top of its 12-month range. Core PCE, which I weight more heavily for persistence, closed nearly all the gap to target (core_distance_from_target just -0.09pp) after running persistently below 2% for the whole sample — this is a meaningful shift in trend, not just a one-off. Policy language also shifted hawkish, dropping "this year" for "coming months" on the inflation-firming expectation, consistent with data momentum. Given both level (near target) and momentum (accelerating on 3m annualized basis) point the same direction, I'm raising conviction from last time.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.1% combined with core PCE yoy retreating back under 1.7% would signal the recent jump was a one-off, not a trend.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, core_distance_from_target, yoy_range_position, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The last observation held at 4.00%, unchanged from the prior month, and the previous "flat" call was correct — no loosening materialized. The unemployment rate has been on a gentle downtrend: -0.40pp over 12 months, -0.20pp over 6 months, and flat over the most recent 3 months (4.10, 4.00, 4.00). The 3-month average (4.03%) sits right at the current level, and the rate is at its 12-month low with a Sahm gap of only +0.03pp — far below the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn toward loosening. The range position of 0.00 confirms we are at the bottom of the observed band, and distance from a ~4% natural rate is essentially zero, indicating a historically tight but not deteriorating labor market. Policy language reinforces this: the Fed characterizes the labor market as continuing to strengthen, with unemployment staying low and conditions expected to remain strong, alongside a rate hike consistent with confidence in labor market health. With three consecutive months pinned at 4.0-4.1% and no acceleration in either direction, the most reasonable expectation for next month is another flat-to-marginal print near 4.0%, not a fresh decline (little room left) nor a rise (no momentum signal present).
Would change its mind if: A print rising to 4.2% or higher, or two consecutive months above 4.1%, would signal an emerging loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.30) was wrong: the 10y has not resumed a broad decline. After peaking near 3.03, it has pulled back only modestly to 2.95, still well within an uptrend that took it from 2.82 to over 3.00 over the past 63 trading days. The 21d and 63d changes remain solidly positive (+0.21, +0.25 pp), and the 252d change (+0.65 pp) confirms a strong multi-month rise. The yield sits at 0.92 of its 12-month range (near the top), a full 0.90 pp above the 12m low, indicating the long end remains near cycle highs rather than reversing. The recent two-print pullback (3.03→3.00→2.96→2.95) looks like a modest consolidation after a sharp run-up, not a trend reversal. arbitrated.notes: Building the level view for nominal Treasury yields (DGS2/DGS10) over roughly a one-month horizon. The two most decision-relevant, freshest, and highest-conviction reads point the same way: inflation is firmly up (0.55, fresh 15-day data with accelerating 3m-annualized core PCE near target and hawkish policy language shift), and term_premium is up (0.35, 10y near top of 12m range, multi-month uptrend intact after only a shallow pullback). These two reinforce each other on the long end and give a coherent upward-pressure story for nominal yields. Labor_tightness is flat with decent conviction (0.55) — a genuinely tight but stable labor market neither adds nor subtracts from the level call; it's a background support for "no imminent Fed easing" but not itself a directional yield driver here. Balance_sheet's down call (runoff continuing) is a modest yield-supportive (higher-yield) factor via reduced Fed demand/duration absorption, though its own analyst frames it as an asset-level call, not a rates call directly — I read continued QT as mildly consistent with upward yield pressure, so it corroborates rather than opposes the inflation/term-premium read. Financial_conditions is down (easier) but the analyst flags this as a reversal of an 8-9 week tightening trend with only two prints of new evidence — I discount this as noisy/early-stage rather than a strong offsetting force; easier conditions typically don't compress yields as directly as inflation/term-premium do. Curve_slope and inflation_expectations are explicitly out of scope for this level seat (curve shape, and near-flat breakeven call with an explicitly flagged gap versus CPI данные I already have). Net: inflation and term premium both leaning up, tight labor removing any dovish offset, modest QT support — I lean toward higher nominal yields, moderate conviction given the FCI wobble and shallow-pullback caveat on term premium.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,337,301) is essentially flat versus the prior observation (4,337,609), but this follows a clear, persistent multi-week downtrend: assets_change_4w is -35,585M and assets_change_13w is -74,359M (-1.69%), with the series sitting well below its 13w average (4,377,538) and 52w high (4,476,903, a gap of -139,602M). My prior call of "down" was correct — the balance sheet continued to run off through this window, consistent with ongoing QT at a steady pace. The near-zero week-over-week move is not evidence of a pause; it's noise within a still-declining trend, and single-week flattening has appeared before within this same runoff episode without reversing the broader trajectory. With no statement language available to signal any change in caps, reinvestment policy, or a slowdown/pause, I have no "why" input to override the "what" — and the "what" still points to gradual, continued runoff. I'll maintain a down call, though I trim conviction slightly given the very flat last data point, which introduces some near-term ambiguity about whether the pace is decelerating.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff, would flip this to flat/up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/flattening) played out: slope fell from 0.48 toward the low 0.42, before a small tick to 0.43 at the newest print. The broader trend remains firmly flattening — slope_change_252d is -0.50pp and slope_change_63d is -0.16pp, both negative, though the 21d change (-0.04pp) shows the pace of flattening has slowed sharply versus prior months. The spread now sits at only 0.02pp above its 12-month low and at just 3% of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.03), meaning it is about as flat/inverted as it has been all year, with very little room left before outright inversion (distance from flat only 0.43pp). The last two prints (0.42, 0.43) show a marginal uptick, but this is too small against a full-range width of 0.59pp to call a reversal — it looks like stabilization near the lows rather than a turn. Policy language shows the Committee held rates steady after a hike, with continued gradual-tightening guidance, which historically keeps pressure on the front end and does not obviously argue for reversal at the long end. I lean toward continued flattening or at best rangebound-near-lows, not a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 0.50-0.55pp with the 21d and 63d changes both turning clearly positive would signal a genuine steepening reversal.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_12m_low, slope_range_position, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear recent easing trend after a mid-series pause. From the trough of tightening around -0.50 (mid-series), the index has moved steadily more negative over the last five prints (-0.50, -0.51, -0.53, -0.54, -0.55, -0.56, -0.57), confirming the down call from last release was correct — conditions continued to loosen rather than reverse. The 4-week change of -0.04 confirms current momentum is still easing, and the index sits well below zero (-0.57), meaning financial conditions remain looser than historical average and are now at the loose end of the observed 52-week range (range_position 0.45, near the 52w low of -0.62). The 13-week change is roughly flat (-0.00), showing that over a longer window the series round-tripped from tightening back to easing, but the most recent trajectory (last 5-6 weeks) is unambiguously toward further easing with consistent step-wise declines. No offsetting information suggests a reversal. Absent any bounce back above -0.51 (which was my prior falsifier), the momentum evidence supports continued easing into month-end. I maintain a moderate conviction on further easing, tempered by the flat 13-week change signaling this could be nearing a local trough.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.54 or a positive 4-week change would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed toward tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by data: breakeven_10y fell from 2.14 to a low of 2.04 before a small bounce to 2.06-2.07, confirming the multi-print break below 2.14 that I flagged as my reversal trigger. The 21-day change is now -0.09pp, a meaningful decline, though the 63-day change is only -0.03pp, suggesting the drop was concentrated recently and has begun to stabilize with two consecutive upticks off the low. Still, breakeven sits well off its 12-month high (2.18) and range_position of 0.79 shows it remains historically elevated within its 12-month range despite the pullback. The policy language shift — dropping "have increased in recent months" and now stating market-based measures "remain low" — corroborates the downshift in market pricing, removing the prior acknowledgment of an uptrend. Distance from target is still modestly positive (+0.07pp), so expectations aren't collapsing, just cooling. Given the recent two-tick bounce (2.04→2.06→2.07) after the sharp decline, I lean toward continued stabilization or mild further softness rather than a resumption of the prior uptrend, but the bounce introduces some uncertainty about whether the decline is over.
Would change its mind if: A sustained push back above 2.14 would signal the decline was noise; a fresh drop below 2.00 would confirm renewed downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was validated: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.78% from 2.47%, the highest print in the 13-month window, driven by a solid 0.23% m/m reading. My falsifier condition (mom below 0.1% and core PCE back under 1.7%) did not occur — core PCE actually held near 1.88-1.91%, well above the earlier 1.4-1.6% trough. Momentum indicators confirm persistence: headline 3m annualized (2.04%) and core 3m annualized (2.38%) both sit at or above target, and the yoy_range_position of 1.00 shows headline sitting at its 12-month high with a full +1.14pp climb off the trough. However, the pace of increase in the incoming yoy print is decelerating slightly — outgoing_mom (0.07) is below the 3m average (0.17), and headline_yoy_change_3m (+0.52pp) is a smaller step than earlier stretches. Policy language now confirms inflation has "moved close to 2 percent" (upgraded from "continued to run below 2 percent"), and forward guidance shifts to expecting it to "run near" the symmetric objective rather than "move up." This suggests the acceleration phase may be nearing its peak, but the last print itself still points to continued near-term upward pressure given elevated 3m-annualized core and headline rates. I lean toward another modest increase or plateau near current highs, not a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.10% paired with core_pce_yoy dropping back under 1.7% would signal the recent run-up has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.55) was wrong: unemployment fell to 3.80%, a fresh 12-month low, not a rise to 4.2%+ as my falsifier contemplated. The trend is unambiguous and accelerating tightening: 3m change -0.30pp, 6m -0.40pp, 12m -0.60pp, with the most recent print itself down 0.20pp from the prior two months' plateau at 4.00. The level is now 0.20pp below my 4% natural-rate anchor, and the range position sits at the very bottom (0.0), meaning the labor market is tighter than at any point in the trailing year. Sahm-gap is trivially positive (0.13pp) reflecting the low being simultaneous with the print itself, not any lagging cushion — there is no loosening signal in the Sahm sense. Policy language is stable, describing continued strengthening and job gains as strong, unemployment stayed low — consistent with the data, no hint of concern about labor slack. Given a rate already below assumed full-employment level and still falling, momentum favors either another dip or at most stabilization near this floor; a reversal higher isn't supported by any measurement here. I lean toward a further slight decline or flat-at-low-level, but genuine downside room is limited near 3.8%, so I won't go to extreme conviction.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back to 4.0% or higher, or two consecutive months above 3.9%, would indicate the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10yr has drifted lower over the past month: from 3.01 down to 2.83, with the 21d change -0.14pp, though the 63d change is flat (+0.02pp) and the 252d change still shows a large +0.62pp rise, so the longer trend remains up even as the recent move has reversed. My prior call (up, 0.35) was wrong for the most recent leg — yields broke below my 2.85 threshold (last print 2.83, prior 2.77), and the 30s10s slope at +0.17pp has compressed rather than inverted, a partial but not full confirmation of my stated falsifier. The 12-month range position at 0.74 still shows the yield sitting in the upper part of its range, so this isn't a low-yield extreme, just a pullback from cycle highs. The removal of "increased in recent months" from the inflation-compensation language suggests less upward pressure from term-premium-related inflation compensation building. On balance, the recent trend and the softened language argue for continued softness in the long end over the next month, but the still-elevated range position and modest slope keep me from high conviction. I'll shift to a modest down lean.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above ~2.95 with 30s10s steepening back toward 0.25+ would signal the pullback reversing higher again.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  balance_sheet lacks financial_conditions — Statement language on caps/reinvestment would confirm whether runoff pace is intentionally steady or being adjusted.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel is split on the rate axis, which itself is informative: term_premium (down, 0.35), financial_conditions (down, 0.45), and balance_sheet (down, 0.45) all point toward lower/looser conditions and a softer long end, while inflation (up, 0.45) and labor_tightness (down in unemployment = tighter labor, 0.55) point toward upward pressure on yields from the real economy and price side. Curve_slope is out of scope for level but its flattening bias (down conviction on slope) is consistent with front-end pressure persisting even as the long end softens — this is a curve signal, not one I use for level, but it corroborates that the two sides of the level debate (inflation/labor pulling up vs financial conditions/balance sheet pulling down) are roughly fighting to a standstill rather than one clearly dominating.

The strongest, freshest, most directly yield-linked signal is term_premium's own read: DGS10 has already fallen from 3.01 to 2.83 over the past month, with easing financial conditions (NFCI at loose end of range, still trending easier) providing a plausible macro backdrop for that move, and continued balance-sheet runoff not obviously reversing it. Inflation's "up" call is real but is described by its own author as likely near a plateau (deceleration in the pace of increase, dovish-leaning language shift), and inflation_expectations (breakevens) has actually rolled over, undercutting the strength of the inflation-up thesis for forward-looking yield pricing. Labor tightness is a hawkish counterweight but is a lagging/coincident indicator with no direct crossover to financial conditions or term premium in the gap analysis, limiting how much weight I give it against directly-observed yield behavior.

Net: modest bias toward lower yields, driven primarily by the realized term-premium decline and confirmatory financial-conditions easing, but conviction is capped because inflation and labor are pulling the other way with real (if plateauing) force.

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior call (up, 0.45) was directionally correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.81 from 2.78, extending an uptrend that has taken it from 1.73 to 2.81 over the past year. However, the momentum underlying that print has clearly decelerated — the newest headline_mom is just 0.09%, well below the 3m average of 0.19% and below the 0.10% threshold I flagged as a reversal signal. The 3-month annualized rates for both headline (2.33%) and core PCE (2.32%) are now sitting almost exactly at target, a marked cooling from the mom prints of 0.43/0.27/0.26 seen a few months back. Core PCE y/y did continue climbing to 1.98, not falling under 1.7 as my falsifier specified, so the disinflation signal is partial, not confirmed. Put together: the y/y comparison next month faces an easy base (outgoing_mom of just 0.03 rolling off) which mechanically makes a rise in headline y/y likely even if monthly momentum is soft — a low print rolling off raises y/y readings. So despite decelerating mom momentum, the yoy print is more likely to tick up again due to base effects, though with less conviction than before given the clear monthly deceleration.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print below 0.10% combined with a further drop in core_pce_yoy below 1.9% would confirm the deceleration and flip me to flat/down.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down/tightening further) was wrong: unemployment rose from 3.80% back to 4.00%, exactly the reversal I flagged as a falsifier. The latest print breaks the tightening trend. Looking at the full series, the rate has been oscillating in a narrow 3.8-4.4% band, with the last two prints (3.80, 4.00) showing a 0.20pp bounce off the 12-month low. The 3m change is flat (0.00pp), 6m change is -0.10pp, and 12m change is -0.30pp — so the longer trend is still one of gradual loosening-of-tightness (i.e., unemployment drifting down over 12 months), but the most recent single-month move is a meaningful uptick from the cycle low. The Sahm gap (+0.13pp) is well below the 0.5pp recession-signal threshold, so this is not a sharp deterioration, just a pause or minor reversal in an otherwise tight labor market near the natural rate (distance from natural ~0.00pp). Policy language is essentially unchanged, still describing continued strengthening and low unemployment, suggesting the Fed does not view this uptick as a regime change. Given the bounce off the low and lack of a clear renewed downtrend, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for the next print, but without strong conviction since the level remains historically tight.
Would change its mind if: A print back down to 3.8-3.9% would confirm the bounce was noise; a second consecutive rise to 4.1%+ would confirm a genuine loosening trend.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_above_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted down modestly over the last few prints (2.85 vs recent 2.90ish range), but the broader trend over 63d and 252d is still up (+0.12pp and +0.63pp respectively), and the level sits at 0.75 in its 12-month range — closer to the top than bottom. My prior "down" call was only partially right: the very recent short-term move (21d +0.02pp) is essentially flat/slightly up, not a continuation of a clear decline, and the yield never approached the 2.95 rebound threshold I flagged, but neither did it fall meaningfully further. The 30s10s slope (0.13pp) is flatter than the 0.25+ level that would signal renewed steepening pressure, suggesting long-end premium is not aggressively re-pricing higher right now, but it's also not collapsing. Given the yield is hovering in a tight band (2.83-2.93) over the last 10 observations with no clear directional break, and sits high in its 12-month range, I see this as more consolidation than a fresh leg down. The removal of specific language on market-based inflation compensation being "low" is a policy-language change I don't weigh directly, but the overall term premium picture looks range-bound with a slight upward bias given the elevated range position.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 2.80 with 30s10s flattening further would confirm renewed decline; a rebound above 2.95 with steepening would confirm upside reversal.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Runoff pace changes directly affect long-end supply/demand and premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broad risk-off/on shifts drive flight-to-quality flows into long bonds.

Synthesizing across the panel: I exclude curve_slope and inflation_expectations from the level call since those are explicitly other pods' seats (curve shape, real/nominal split). Of the drivers relevant to the outright level, the picture is mixed but leans mildly toward higher yields with weak overall conviction, so I size down rather than pick a strong side.

Balance sheet is the freshest and most mechanically confirmed read: runoff continues steadily across 4w/13w/52w windows with no reversal signal, and this is a straightforward decline in Fed asset holdings — reduced duration absorption, a modestly bearish (higher-yield) impulse for term premium, though its direct yield transmission is diffuse and slow-moving over a month horizon.

Financial conditions has just turned, with three consecutive tightening prints off the low — a real if small inflection toward tighter conditions, which is consistent with modest upward yield pressure.

Inflation is up but decelerating in momentum; the y/y print is likely to tick up mechanically on base effects even as underlying momentum cools. This gives a mild upward pull on yields but with justified low conviction given the acknowledged deceleration.

Term premium itself reports flat/range-bound with a slight upward bias (high in 12-month range), but explicitly lacks visibility into balance_sheet and financial_conditions — two drivers I have fresher, moving reads on. Both of those unseen inputs point modestly toward higher long-end yields (less easy money, less balance sheet support), which should nudge term premium's own flat call slightly higher than its self-assessment.

Labor tightness is genuinely flat/mixed — a bounce off cycle lows but not a clear trend change; weak signal either way, so it contributes little conviction to the level call.

Net: modest upward bias to yields, driven by balance sheet runoff, a financial-conditions inflection toward tightening, and inflation's base-effect uptick, partially offset by decelerating inflation momentum and an ambiguous labor read. Conviction is limited by disagreement and staleness (inflation and labor readings are ~2-3 weeks old).

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,277,681) extended the decline from the prior reading (4,291,615), confirming the previous "down" call was correct. Total assets have fallen in 4 of the last 5 weeks, with the 4-week change at -27,810M and the 13-week change at -95,205M (-2.18%), consistent with steady balance-sheet runoff rather than any pause. The 52-week change of -187,603M shows the trend is durable, not a short-term wobble, and assets remain -193,493M below the 52-week high with no sign of rebound. There is no statement language accompanying this release to indicate any change in reinvestment policy or a slowing/pausing of the runoff cap, so I have no "why" evidence suggesting deceleration — only the quantitative pace, which looks broadly similar to recent months (roughly -25 to -30bn per 4 weeks). Absent any indication of taper in the pace of decline or statement confirmation of a slowdown, the mechanical trend of continued balance-sheet reduction should persist into next month-end. I maintain a down call with similar conviction as before, since nothing here falsifies the prior thesis — the run lower continued exactly as expected, and no evidence points to reversal or deceleration.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (0.29) is essentially flat versus prior prints (0.32, 0.29) and sits within the tight recent band of 0.28-0.32. My prior "down" call was directionally correct over the medium term: slope_change_63d (-0.18) and slope_change_252d (-0.67) confirm a sustained flattening trend over the past year, and the spread remains near the bottom of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.07, only 0.05pp above the 12m low of 0.24). However, the very short-term momentum has stalled — slope_change_21d is only -0.04pp, and the last few prints (0.28, 0.29, 0.29, 0.32, 0.29) show sideways chop rather than continued decisive flattening. The Fed statement language shifted to an actual hike (to 1-3/4 to 2%) with continued "further gradual increases" guidance, consistent with front-end pressure that has driven the long flattening trend, but this is already reflected in the year-long move and doesn't obviously accelerate flattening from here. Given the curve is compressed near cycle lows with little room left before flat/inversion, and near-term momentum has flattened out rather than continuing to fall, I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call for month-end, with reduced conviction versus the prior sharp downward call since the 21d trend no longer supports strong continuation.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 0.24pp (new 12m low) or above 0.40pp with 21d/63d changes turning strongly directional would revise this view.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call has been falsified: the latest print is -0.56, matching the 52-week-low-adjacent trough seen a few observations back, and the 4-week change is -0.01, i.e. negative — exactly the condition I flagged as a reason to abandon the tightening thesis. Looking at the full series, conditions oscillated in a tight -0.54 to -0.57 band with no clear trend break; the last three prints (-0.54, -0.55, -0.56) show a slight re-easing drift after briefly firming toward -0.54. The 13-week change is -0.03, still net easing over the medium window, and the index remains well below zero (distance from zero -0.56), meaning conditions are substantially looser than historical average. Range position at 0.55 shows the index is roughly mid-range of its own 52-week band, not at an extreme in either direction. Given the tiny magnitude of moves (all changes are hundredths of an index point) and the reversal of the incipient tightening signal, I see no strong momentum in either direction. The data supports a modest lean toward continued easing/drift lower rather than tightening, but the moves are too small and noisy to call with confidence.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-week rise back above -0.54 with positive 4-week change would signal renewed tightening; a drop toward the 52w low -0.62 would confirm easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous flat call held: breakeven_10y has traded in a tight 2.09–2.13 band over the last ten readings, ending at 2.12, essentially unchanged from where it started. Neither of my stated triggers (sustained push above 2.14 or break below 2.05) was hit. The 21-day change is a negligible +0.01pp and the 63-day change is a small -0.04pp, both consistent with sideways consolidation rather than a new trend. The longer 252-day change of +0.29pp shows the level has drifted up over the past year, and range_position at 0.86 confirms breakevens sit near the top of their 12-month range, comfortably above the 12-month low. Distance from the 2% objective is a modest +0.12pp, not stretched. Policy language reinforces this: "market-based measures of inflation compensation remain low" was replaced with the more neutral "indicators of longer-term inflation expectations are little changed, on balance" — a modest hawkish-leaning edit acknowledging expectations have firmed somewhat, but not signaling any fresh momentum. With price action pinned in a narrow band near cycle highs and language shifting only marginally, I don't see a catalyst for a directional move over the next month. This still reads as a market in equilibrium slightly above target, not accelerating.
Would change its mind if: A sustained push above 2.14 would signal renewed uptrend; a break below 2.08 would confirm a new downtrend. Either would overturn flat.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked up again to 2.85% from 2.81%, extending the up-trend I called (correctly) last time. But momentum has clearly cooled under the surface: the outgoing month-over-month print was only 0.08%, well below the 3m average of 0.13% and far below the 0.38-0.51% prints seen earlier in the window. The 3-month annualized headline rate is just 1.59%, and core 3m annualized is 1.80% — both below the y/y readings, signaling the recent trend is decelerating even though the y/y comparison still looks firm due to base effects. Core PCE y/y actually slipped from 1.98% to 1.94%, missing my prior threshold for confirmation only marginally (I'd flagged sub-1.9%). Combined with core sitting essentially at the 2% target (-0.06pp) and policy language reiterating expectations are "little changed" and inflation running "near" 2% symmetric objective, there's no evidence of building pressure. The y/y series is near a 12-month high, but the underlying monthly pulse (0.08%, 0.09% in the last two prints) suggests the y/y ascent is losing steam and next month's headline print is more likely to flatten or dip given soft recent monthly comparisons rolling off higher year-ago base effects.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print rebounding above 0.25% combined with core_pce_yoy resuming its climb above 2.0% would revive the acceleration case.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell back to 3.80%, matching the 12-month low, after the prior tick up to 4.00%. This confirms my prior expectation that the bounce to 4.00% was noise rather than the start of a loosening trend. The series remains firmly on a gradual downtrend: -0.20pp over 3 and 6 months, -0.50pp over 12 months, and the level is now 0.20pp below the ~4% natural rate estimate. The Sahm-style gap is essentially zero (0.07pp), with unrate sitting at its 12-month low, so there is no evidence of an accelerating loosening. Policy language reinforces this: the updated FOMC statement upgrades from "unemployment rate has stayed low" to "unemployment rate has declined," alongside upgraded growth language ("moderate" to "solid") and a rate hike — consistent with a still-tightening labor market. Given the level is now at the low end of its recent range and momentum has resumed downward, I lean toward continued tightness or at most a flat-to-slightly-lower unemployment rate next month. A repeat print at 3.8% or a further decline would confirm; only a jump back to 4.0%+ would revive the loosening concern.
Would change its mind if: A print rising to 4.0-4.1%+ would signal renewed loosening; continued readings at/below 3.8% confirm sustained tightness.

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has rebounded from the recent low (2.84) to 2.96, printing new local highs (2.98) within the series and sitting at 0.86 of its 12-month range — near the top. The prior flat call was too cautious: the 21-day change of +0.11pp and the move above 2.95 confirms the upside reversal condition I set previously. The 63-day change is essentially flat (-0.01pp), showing the move has stalled somewhat over the last few weeks, but the yield is still holding near the top of its range rather than retreating. The 30s10s slope (+0.12pp) is modestly positive but not aggressively steepening, suggesting the long-end premium move is real but not accelerating sharply. Level and range position argue for continued modest upward pressure or at least sustained elevation rather than reversion. Given the yield already sits near 12-month highs, further large moves up may be harder, but momentum and range position favor continued mild upside rather than a reversal lower. I'm shifting from flat to a modest up lean, though conviction stays moderate given the stalled 63-day change.
Would change its mind if: A break back below 2.90 with 30s10s flattening further would signal the upside move has failed and reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  balance_sheet lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify if reserve scarcity is prompting officials to consider slowing runoff pace soon.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of runoff/issuance dynamics would clarify whether supply pressure is building or easing.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk appetite context would help distinguish flight-to-quality from reflation-driven yield moves.

arbitrated.notes: The level call for nominal Treasury yields over the next month is mildly upward-biased but low-conviction, reflecting a genuinely split panel. The strongest, freshest signal is term_premium: 10y yields have broken to new range highs (2.96-2.98, range position 0.86) with a real 21d move (+0.11), consistent with the Fed's hike and "further gradual increases" language feeding through the long end. That is a live, non-stale, directly-observed yield signal and deserves the most weight in a duration call. Balance-sheet runoff continuing at a steady ~-25 to -30bn/4wk pace is also a genuine, high-conviction, mechanical driver that argues for higher yields via reduced official-sector demand/duration absorption, and it is corroborated by term_premium's own note that it lacks balance-sheet context — the runoff continuing is exactly the missing piece that would explain the yield backup, so I treat these two as mutually reinforcing rather than independent bulls. Inflation and inflation expectations are flat-to-cooling (headline CPI decelerating on a 3m annualized basis, breakevens pinned in a tight band) — these argue against a big upward move and cap conviction, but don't argue for lower yields either. Labor tightness is a moderate down-conviction (tightening labor argues for higher rates, but conviction is only 0.35 and it's a somewhat stale/23-day-old call). Financial conditions are flat/noisy with tiny magnitude changes — essentially no signal, and stale relative to term_premium's fresher print. Curve slope is explicitly out of scope for this seat. Netting it out: a real, fresh long-end move higher plus continuing balance-sheet drain outweigh flat inflation and modest labor tightening, producing a mild-conviction "yields drift higher" call, concentrated more in the long end where the term-premium evidence is direct, with a smaller complementary lean in the front end from labor tightness and Fed hike language.

risks.text: A dovish Fed pivot or runoff-pace slowdown would remove both supporting drivers at once.
Term premium's 63d change is flat, suggesting the recent move may already be stalling.
Panel is split (flat inflation, flat conditions) — level view has limited breadth of support.

trade.rationale: Long-end led backup (term premium, balance-sheet supply) plus front-end tightening/hike support justify a modest duration-short bias, weighted toward the 10y where the fresher signal lives.

why.term_premium: Fresh 10y breakout to range highs, real 21d move; direct, live yield-level evidence, most weight given here.
why.balance_sheet: Steady, non-stale runoff continues mechanically; reinforces term_premium's missing supply-pressure context toward higher yields.
why.labor_tightness: Tightening labor and hawkish Fed language support higher yields, but call is 23 days stale, discounted somewhat.
why.inflation: Headline flat/decelerating on 3m annualized basis; no directional push on yields, offsets nothing but adds no lift.
why.financial_conditions: Tiny, noisy moves within a stable band; no usable level signal, discounted to abstention.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call is confirmed by the latest print: total assets fell to 4,218,914M from 4,228,818M, continuing an unbroken decline across all 13 observations shown. The 4-week change (-36,739M) and 13-week change (-108,605M) both show runoff continuing at a steady, roughly consistent pace (~$25-35B/month), consistent with balance-sheet normalization proceeding under an announced cap rather than accelerating or pausing. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-252,260M) with no signs of reversal — every recent weekly print is at or near cycle lows. There is no statement language this period to indicate any change in reinvestment policy or cap adjustment, so I extrapolate continuation of the existing runoff trajectory. The pace looks marginally faster than typical recent months (4w change of -36.7B is toward the upper end of what's been seen), suggesting no slowdown is materializing. With no evidence of a pause, no rebound above the 13w average, and no statement confirming any change, the mechanical trend strongly favors continued runoff into month-end. I maintain a firm down call, slightly higher conviction than before given the consistency of the decline and absence of any countervailing signal.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call of flat (0.35 conviction) is now wrong on the latest print: slope rose to 0.24pp, tying the recent high in this window and breaking the small down-drift that had been building (0.22→0.18 trough mid-series). The last three readings (0.21, 0.22, 0.22, 0.24) show a modest steepening bounce off the 0.18 low. However, slope_range_position at 0.09 confirms the spread is still sitting near the bottom of its 12-month range (low 0.18, high 0.86), and slope_change_252d (-0.58pp) and slope_change_63d (-0.18pp) both show the dominant trend over the year has been flattening. The 21d change is only -0.08pp, meaning the very recent move is actually a tick toward steepening despite the longer trend. This is a small, tentative bounce within a still-flat, historically compressed curve, not a trend reversal. The 10s30s slope at 0.16pp is unremarkable context. Given the mixed signal — near-term uptick versus dominant multi-month flattening bias and a curve pinned near cycle lows — I lean toward a slight steepening continuation but with low-moderate conviction, since one data point isn't enough to call a clear break.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.20pp would confirm resumed flattening; a push above 0.30pp with positive 21d/63d changes would confirm steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has extended its easing trend, printing -0.59 at the latest reading versus -0.54 four weeks prior, confirming the "down" call from the previous release. The index has been grinding steadily lower over the last several observations (-0.54 to -0.59), with the 4-week and 13-week changes both at -0.03, indicating consistent, modest easing momentum rather than a one-off move. The level sits well below zero (-0.59), meaning conditions remain looser than the historical average, and the series is now closer to its 52-week low (-0.62) than its 52-week high (-0.50), with range position at just 0.22 — firmly in the loose end of its recent range. There is no sign of stabilization or reversal in the last two prints (-0.58, -0.59), suggesting the easing trend has legs into month end. Absent any offsetting policy language or shock, the path of least resistance is a continuation of this gradual easing, though the pace is slow enough that a plateau near -0.59/-0.60 is plausible. I am maintaining a directional call of further easing but only modestly increasing conviction given the small magnitude of weekly moves.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above -0.55 with positive 4-week change would signal reversal toward tightening; stabilization at -0.59 for multiple weeks would suggest flat.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven printed 2.10 then 2.08 in the last two readings, pulling back from the recent local peak of 2.12. My prior flat call anticipated a range around 2.08-2.14, and the latest print (2.08) sits at the lower edge of that band, effectively testing the downside trigger I had flagged. The 21-day and 63-day changes are both -0.04pp, consistent, modest declines rather than a sharp break, but the two-observation slide from 2.12 to 2.08 is a real move, not noise. Against that, the 252-day change is still solidly positive (+0.32pp) and breakevens remain 0.76 of the way up the 12-month range, well above the 12-month low and 8bp above the 2% objective — so this is a pullback within an uptrend, not a reversal. Policy language describes expectations as "little changed, on balance," which supports treating this as noise around a plateau rather than a new regime. Given the recent two-point slide right to my downside trigger level, I lean modestly toward further softening or at least a flat-to-down bias rather than resuming the climb, but the longer-term trend and range position argue against a confident down call.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.11-2.12 would negate the recent pullback and restore the uptrend; a further drop below 2.06 would confirm a genuine downtrend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call was largely correct in direction terms but headline actually declined notably (2.85→2.64, a -0.21pp drop) while my stated trigger did not fire: outgoing mom was only 0.18%, below the 0.25% threshold, and core PCE ticked up modestly to 2.00% (right at target, not clearly "resuming a climb above 2.0%"). So the acceleration case did not revive; if anything headline cooled. Momentum measures confirm this: headline_3m_annualized (1.40%) and core_3m_annualized (1.60%) both run well below the current yoy readings, implying disinflationary pressure is building beneath the surface. headline_mom_3m_avg (0.12%) is soft versus the outgoing 0.51% print, so the yoy figure should keep drifting down as high year-ago comparisons roll off. Core PCE sits exactly at the 2% target with yoy_change_3m only +0.11pp — not a strong acceleration signal. Policy language reinforces this: the Committee's shift from "have moved close to 2 percent" to "remain near 2 percent" is neutral, non-alarming language, consistent with inflation having arrived at target rather than overshooting. Given the sharp deceleration in the latest headline print and soft 3m annualized trends, I lean toward continued softening or flatness in the next release, not a rebound.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% combined with core_pce_yoy breaking clearly above 2.0% (e.g., 2.1%+) would signal renewed acceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 3.8% for the third time in the last five readings, matching the 12-month low and sitting 0.2pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor. My prior call for further declines ("down") was directionally right in spirit but the actual print was flat vs the prior month (3.8%→3.8%), not a fresh decline. The 3m change is exactly 0.00pp, the 6m and 12m changes remain negative (-0.30, -0.60pp), and the Sahm-gap is a negligible +0.07pp — well below any threshold indicating loosening. Range position is at 0 (sitting at the 12-month low), showing no recent upward drift at all. This is a labor market that tightened steadily over the past year and has now stabilized at a low level rather than continuing to fall or reversing upward. With no upward momentum evident and the level still comfortably below natural-rate, the most defensible call for the next print is flat-to-slightly-down, but given the plateau at 3.8% for two of the last three readings, "flat" better captures the exhausted momentum than "down." I'll shade toward flat with a slight tightening bias given the low level and lack of any loosening signal.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.0% would confirm loosening; continued readings at 3.8% or lower with no upward drift confirm the plateau/tightness view.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_3m_avg, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has been essentially flat over the last several prints (2.82→2.86 range), with modest recent softening: -0.12pp over 21d and -0.08pp over 63d, even as the 252d change remains strongly positive (+0.71pp), reflecting a large prior move up. My prior 'up' call at 0.40 conviction has not been validated in the near term — the level has actually drifted down/sideways from the recent 2.89 high, consistent with my stated falsifier condition (break back below 2.90). The yield sits at 0.76 of its 12-month range, still elevated historically, but momentum over the last month is flat-to-slightly-down rather than continuing to climb. The 30s10s slope at +0.16pp is modest and not signaling aggressive further long-end cheapening; 10s2s at +0.24pp is not my remit but doesn't suggest a steepening shock at the long end specifically. With inflation expectations described as little changed, there's no strong fresh catalyst for renewed upward pressure on term premium. Given the stall in the uptrend and slight pullback, I'm revising toward a flatter, more neutral stance rather than continuing to press the 'up' call — the recent evidence doesn't support high conviction in either direction.
Would change its mind if: A decisive break above 2.90-2.95 with steepening 30s10s would revive the upward term-premium call; a drop below 2.75 would confirm a down call.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of QT/runoff and Treasury issuance plans directly affect long-end supply pressure and term premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment/flight-to-quality flows would clarify if recent softness is safe-haven driven.

On the disinflationary side, inflation (formed 16d ago, -0.40) shows a real deceleration in headline with soft 3m annualized momentum, and inflation_expectations (fresh, -0.35) confirms breakevens pulling back from local highs, though both stop short of confirming a trend break — this is a genuine but moderate downward pull on yields via the inflation channel. Financial_conditions is easing further (-0.40, fresh) which is modestly yield-supportive (looser conditions typically coincide with lower risk-free yields via easier financing, or reflects diminished term premium demand pressure) but the magnitude is small and grinding, not a regime shift. Term_premium itself de-escalated to flat/neutral (0.25) and explicitly lacks balance_sheet and financial_conditions inputs, so its own 'flat' read is under-informed on the very inputs that matter most for long-end level; I discount its standalone call and instead let balance_sheet and financial_conditions inform the long-end directly, per the gap flag. Netting it out: disinflationary pressure (inflation, inflation_expectations) and easier financial conditions lean yields lower, while continued balance-sheet runoff leans yields higher on the long end. These roughly offset, leaving a mild net downward lean, driven primarily by the two independently-confirming inflation signals, tempered heavily by the runoff dynamic and a genuinely split panel. Curve_slope is out of scope (curve pod's seat), and labor is neutral.

Genuinely flat/plateaued; no directional information for level view.

Inflation deceleration could stall or reverse if energy/base effects fade, undermining the disinflation case.

why.inflation: Deceleration in headline CPI and soft 3m annualized momentum both point to disinflation; two-observation trend, not fully confirmed.
why.labor_tightness: Plateau at cycle-low unemployment with no loosening signal; genuinely flat, no directional lean for yields.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI grinding steadily looser; modest magnitude but consistent trend, mildly yield-supportive-lower.
why.balance_sheet: Freshest, most mechanical signal: steady QT/runoff continuing removes duration demand, leaning yields higher; tempers the disinflation pull.
why.term_premium: Own call is flat/neutral and explicitly missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions inputs; discounted in favor of those direct reads per gap flag.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.70
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,192,909) continues the steady decline from the prior release, confirming the previous "down" call was correct. Total assets fell again week-over-week, extending the run-off trend: 4w change is -26,005M, 13w change is -112,582M (-2.61%), and 52w change is -262,752M, all consistently negative with no sign of deceleration. The series has moved further below both its 13w average (4,243,144) and 52w high (4,469,723), and the pace of decline (~26B in the latest 4 weeks) is in line with or slightly above the recent trailing average pace, showing no evidence of tapering. There is no statement language this period, so there is no new "why" to weigh against the "what" — but absent any language announcing a pause or slowdown, the mechanical, cap-driven runoff evidently continued uninterrupted. The falsifier from last time (a rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a pause) did not occur. Everything points to continued balance-sheet runoff at a steady pace into next month-end, so I maintain a confident down call, essentially unchanged from before, since the trend is clean and monotonic with no countervailing signal.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The spread series (…0.25, 0.27, 0.23, 0.23, 0.24) shows continued chop near cycle lows rather than a decisive move either way. My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was essentially wrong in direction terms: the last print (0.24) is below the peak of 0.27 seen two readings ago, and the 63-day change is -0.09pp, confirming a modestly flattening medium-term trend even though the very latest 21-day change is +0.02pp. The spread sits just 0.06pp above its 12-month low and only 9% into its 12-month range (0.09), underscoring how compressed and flattened the curve remains historically (range 0.18-0.86). The 252-day change of -0.60pp shows the dominant trend over the past year has been aggressive flattening, and the last few weeks are just noise around a low plateau near 0.23-0.27. Policy language shifted to an actual hike (to 2-2.25%) with continued "further gradual increases" guidance, which typically pressures front-end yields up and can keep the curve flattening pressure alive. Given the tiny 21d uptick but negative 63d/252d trends and rock-bottom range position, I don't see convincing steepening evidence. I'll call this closer to flat-to-slightly-down, with low-moderate conviction, acknowledging my prior call missed the continued flattening bias.
Would change its mind if: A clear push above 0.30pp with positive 21d/63d changes would confirm steepening; a drop back toward/below 0.18-0.20pp would confirm resumed flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially flattened at the front end: the last four readings are all -0.60, and the 4-week change is -0.00, meaning the easing trend that dominated the 13-week window has stalled. The 13-week change of -0.05 confirms conditions did loosen over the past quarter, but nearly all of that move happened earlier in the window — the most recent weeks show no further movement. My prior call was for continued easing (down) with modest conviction; that call is now only partially validated — the level did drift slightly lower earlier, but the latest four readings show stabilization exactly as I flagged as a possible reversal signal. With the index sitting at -0.60, near its 52-week low (-0.62) and well below the zero average, conditions remain loose in absolute terms, but momentum has died. Range position at 0.20 confirms we're near the loose end of the recent band, but the plateau in the last month suggests the easing impulse has exhausted itself for now, absent new drivers. Given no fresh policy language addressing this driver, I see no basis for expecting a fresh leg of easing or a reversal to tightening. The most defensible near-term call is flat, with low-moderate conviction given the flat 4-week trend dominates over the stale 13-week momentum.
Would change its mind if: A move away from -0.60 in either direction over 2+ consecutive weeks, or fresh policy language explicitly addressing credit/financial conditions, would revise this view.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call of "down" (0.35 conviction) was wrong: the series has since pushed further up, not down, breaking well above the 2.11-2.12 level I flagged as the negation threshold. The last 10 readings show a plateau near 2.14-2.16, essentially flat over the most recent stretch (21d change +0.02pp, 63d change +0.03pp) after a larger run-up over the past year (+0.27pp over 252d). Breakevens are sitting near the top of their 12-month range (range_position 0.89, only 0.02-0.04pp off the 12m high of 2.18), and are +0.14pp above the 2% target — a modest but persistent overshoot. Policy language describes expectations as "little changed, on balance," consistent with the recent flattening in the short-term data, though the level itself reflects the earlier upward drift. Given the near-term data show stabilization rather than continued acceleration or reversal, and the series has been oscillating in a narrow 2.14-2.16 band for the last several readings, I don't see strong evidence for a directional move in either direction over the next month. The elevated range position argues against further upside being the base case, but there's no sign of a rollover either.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.18 (new 12m high) would signal renewed uptrend; a drop below 2.10 would confirm a reversal lower.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline CPI y/y fell from 2.85 to 2.64 to 2.33 over the last two prints, a substantial deceleration, and my falsifier condition (mom>0.30% plus core PCE clearly above 2.0%) did not trigger — outgoing mom was just 0.08% and core PCE actually ticked down to 1.90 from 2.00. The 3-month annualized rates confirm the cooling: headline running at only 1.87% annualized and core PCE at a soft 0.93% annualized, both well below the trailing y/y levels, meaning the y/y figures likely have further room to fall or at least stabilize lower before mean-reverting. Core PCE sits essentially at target (1.90, -0.10pp from 2%) and has been flat-to-softening over 3 months, arguing against any acceleration narrative. Policy language shift from "moved close to" to "remain near" 2 percent is consistent with a stabilization rather than a reacceleration story. Given the sharp two-month drop in headline y/y and soft recent monthly prints, I lean toward continued softness or flat readings next month rather than a rebound, though the pace of the recent decline (0.31pp in one month) may partially reflect base effects that could attenuate.
Would change its mind if: A headline mom print above 0.30% combined with core_pce_yoy rising back above 2.0% would signal renewed acceleration and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.30) undershot the direction of travel: the newest print fell to 3.70%, a fresh 12-month low, extending the series' gentle downtrend rather than plateauing at 3.8%. Over the trailing observations the rate has moved from 3.80/3.80 to 3.70, and the 3m/6m/12m changes are all negative (-0.30, -0.30, -0.60 pp), with the 3-month average (3.77%) still above the latest print, confirming continued month-over-month tightening rather than stabilization. The Sahm-style gap is trivial (+0.07pp) and range position is at 0 (sitting at the 12-month low), so there is no evidence of the rapid-rise dynamic that would flag loosening. Distance from an assumed ~4% natural rate is now -0.30pp, indicating the labor market is arguably tighter than normal, not looser. Policy language reinforces this: the updated FOMC statement upgrades activity from "solid" to "strong" and changes "the unemployment rate has declined" to "has stayed low" — consistent with a labor market holding firm at a tight level rather than deteriorating. Given the unbroken low-water mark and no directional inflection, I lean toward continued tightness with unemployment holding near or slightly below 3.70% next month, though the move size is small enough to keep conviction moderate.
Would change its mind if: A print back above 3.8-4.0% would signal the plateau/reversal; continued readings at or below 3.7% confirm ongoing tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y sits at 3.05, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.93; only 0.77pp above the 12m low, and just 0.06pp below the 12m high of 3.11). Momentum has cooled slightly over the very short run (last two prints 3.06→3.05) after peaking at 3.10, but the broader trend remains firmly up: +0.16pp over 21d, +0.20pp over 63d, and a large +0.74pp over 252d. My prior "flat" call was wrong in direction of magnitude — the breakout above the 2.90-2.95 zone I flagged as the trigger for an upward call has clearly happened and persisted, with yields now consolidating at cycle highs rather than reversing. The 30s10s slope at +0.14pp is modestly positive, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening that would signal a top. With yields pinned near the 12-month high and no sign of a flight-to-quality reversal, the path of least resistance is still a modestly rising or at least persistently elevated term premium. The last two observations show a slight pullback, tempering conviction, but this looks like consolidation near highs rather than a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below ~2.95 with 30s10s flattening/inverting would confirm the up-move has reversed into a falling term premium.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_21d, slope_30s10s, dgs10_12m_high
