user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.80
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest observation (3,851,444) continues the steady decline from the prior series, confirming the previous down call was correct. Total assets fell ~$9.0B in the last week alone, and the 4-week change (-38,247) and 13-week change (-123,146, -3.10%) both show consistent, uninterrupted runoff. There is no sign of deceleration: each of the last several prints is at or near new cycle lows, well below the 13w average (3,919,230) and 473,462 below the 52w high. The pace of decline (~$30B/month over the recent stretch, consistent with roughly the announced monthly cap) shows no inflection. With no statement language provided this period to signal any change in reinvestment policy or cap adjustment, the base case is simple extrapolation of the current trend: continued balance sheet runoff into month-end. The absence of statement commentary is itself unremarkable here — it does not indicate a pause, and the measurement trend is unambiguous and monotonic across all recent windows (4w, 13w, 52w all negative). I maintain a high-conviction down call, slightly tempered from before only because the missing statement language removes one confirming data point I'd normally weight.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread ended the series at 0.19pp, matching the prior peak in the sequence, after dipping to 0.14 mid-series. The last print shows a bounce (0.16→0.19), but the short-run trend measures remain net negative: slope_change_21d and slope_change_63d are both -0.02pp, and slope_change_252d is a much larger -0.26pp, indicating the year-long trend has been toward flattening even though the very latest tick ticked up. My prior "up" call was only partially vindicated — the spread did rise off its low, but it merely returned to a level already seen two readings ago rather than breaking out, and the 21d/63d changes are still negative, which was my stated falsifier condition for abandoning the bullish-steepening view. The spread sits at only 0.24 of its 12-month range (very close to the low), and remains just 0.08pp above the 12-month low and 0.19pp away from flat — still deeply compressed. This is a choppy, range-bound pattern within a longer flattening trend, not a clear steepening. Given the negative 21d/63d changes persist despite the single-point uptick, I'm downgrading conviction in "up" and leaning toward a more neutral-to-cautious stance, since the evidence is genuinely mixed: last-point bounce vs. persistent negative medium-term trend.
Would change its mind if: A firm break above 0.20pp sustained for 2+ readings with 21d/63d changes turning positive would confirm renewed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_above_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series bottomed around -0.61 several readings ago and has since risen steadily to -0.58, the highest level in this 13-observation window. The 4-week change is +0.03 (tightening momentum), while the 13-week change is essentially flat at -0.01, indicating the move is recent and has accelerated over the last month rather than being a longer trend. Conditions remain deeply below the zero average (-0.58 distance from zero), so this is still an accommodative financial-conditions regime in absolute terms, but the direction of travel has clearly shifted from easing to modest tightening. My prior flat call is now looking wrong: the latest print (-0.58) breaches my own stated threshold of -0.55 only partially, but the consistent multi-period uptick from -0.61 to -0.58 is a genuine reversal signal, not just noise. Range position (0.14 of 52-week range) confirms we're still near the loose end historically, but the momentum over the last four readings is unambiguously upward. Given the string of consecutive increases (-0.60, -0.59, -0.58) after a long flat/declining stretch, I lean toward continued modest tightening into next month, though conviction is capped by the fact we're not yet near the upper threshold and absolute level remains very loose.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.60 would signal the recent uptick was noise and easing has resumed; a print above -0.55 would confirm firmer tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the 21d change is now -0.19pp and 63d -0.22pp, with the series drifting from ~1.81 down to ~1.74 over the last ten readings. This is exactly the kind of sharply negative 21d move I said would overturn a flat call, so I was wrong to hold flat last time — the drift is now consistent enough to call directional. Breakeven sits near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.12, only 6bp above the 12m low of 1.68) and 26bp below the 2% objective, having widened its distance from target over the past year (252d change -0.35pp). The last few prints (1.76, 1.73, 1.75, 1.74) show the decline decelerating and possibly stabilizing just above the 12m low, which tempers conviction that the slide continues sharply lower. Policy language notes market-based measures "have remained low," consistent with no near-term catalyst for a reversal. Given the loss of downward momentum in the very latest observations but the still-negative multi-horizon trend and proximity to lows, I lean toward continued softness rather than a rebound, but not with high confidence given signs of flattening.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 1.80 sustained for several readings, or be_change_21d turning positive, would overturn this soft-drift call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (up, 0.45) was wrong: the newest headline_mom printed 0.02%, well below my own 0.15% falsifier threshold, and headline_yoy fell from 2.00 to 1.80. The two strong 0.38% prints that drove that prior "up" call look like a transitory pop rather than a new trend, and the latest data confirms it fading sharply. Core PCE also continues to soften (1.62→1.64, down from a 2.04 peak three prints back), with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.21pp confirming decelerating underlying momentum, and core sitting 0.36pp below target. The 3m annualized core rate (+1.54%) is comfortably below the 12-month yoy print, reinforcing that the recent trend is disinflationary. Policy language corroborates this, now stating both headline and core "have declined and are running below 2 percent" — an explicit downgrade from the prior "core remains near 2 percent" language. Given the very weak outgoing_mom (0.02%) and softening core trend, I expect next month's headline yoy to drift lower or at best hold near current levels, not push back toward 2%. Base effects could cause some noise, but the momentum and policy language both point down.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom rebound above 0.25% next release combined with core_pce_yoy stabilizing or rising would suggest disinflation has stalled and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_mom, headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 3.60%, the trailing 12-month low, down from 3.70% prior and continuing a run that had been oscillating in a narrow 3.70-4.00% band. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in retrospect — this print confirms further tightening rather than stabilization, meeting the threshold I set (at/below 3.60%) for revising toward continued tightening. The 3m/6m/12m changes all show -0.20pp, and the Sahm gap is a negligible +0.10pp, indicating no loosening signal whatsoever. Distance from the natural rate is now -0.40pp, meaning the market is running notably tighter than a 4% anchor. Policy language reinforces this: the updated FOMC statement upgrades the growth assessment from "slowed" to "rose at a solid rate" while maintaining "labor market remains strong" and "unemployment rate has remained low," consistent with a market that is if anything tightening further rather than turning. Given the level is already at a cycle low and momentum remains modestly negative (falling rate), the most likely next print is either another low reading or a flat hold near 3.60-3.70%, with genuine downside risk to unemployment given the momentum, but mean-reversion off multi-year lows also plausible. I lean toward a modest further tightening or flat outcome, not a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 3.80% (back toward range top, sahm_gap rising above 0.3pp) would signal a reversal toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: 10y fell further, from 2.22 to 2.14, sitting at the very bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.00, at the 12m low). Momentum remains firmly negative across all horizons (-0.38pp/21d, -0.62pp/63d, -0.79pp/252d), with no sign of stabilization in the last three prints (2.25→2.22→2.14). The 30s10s slope at 0.44pp is modest and not signaling any acute long-end steepening pressure from issuance/supply concerns; if anything the persistent grind lower suggests continued demand for duration/flight-to-quality dynamics dominating over any term-premium-widening forces. There's no policy language suggesting inflation expectations are driving this (they're described as low and stable), which is consistent with the move being a term-premium/duration-demand story rather than an inflation-compensation story. Given the yield is pinned at its 12-month low with unabated negative momentum, the path of least resistance is still lower into month-end, though the pace of decline could moderate given how compressed the level already is relative to the range floor. I'll maintain a "down" call but only modestly firmer than before given the yield is now testing fresh lows with limited room before mean-reversion risk increases.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close above ~2.60 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of QT/runoff changes would clarify supply pressure on long-end term premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-off/flight-to-quality signals would confirm whether duration demand persists.

arbitrated.notes:
Weighing the panel on the level axis: inflation (down 0.45, formed 16 days ago but corroborated by policy language shift and both headline/core softening), inflation_expectations (down 0.35, breakevens near 12m lows though decelerating), and term_premium (down 0.40, 10y pinned at 12m low with unabated negative momentum across all horizons) all point the same direction — lower yields. Labor tightness is flat-to-slightly-tighter (0.35 conviction, cycle-low unemployment) which is a mild offset since tight labor could support yields, but the analyst's own call is "flat," not up, so it's a weak counterweight rather than a real opposing force. Financial conditions shows a fresh, accelerating tightening signal (up 0.45) — this is the freshest read in the panel and the most direct challenge to the down thesis, since tightening financial conditions historically coincide with higher, not lower, yields, but here the level remains deeply accommodative and the move is only 4 weeks old, capping its weight. Balance sheet is unambiguously down (runoff continuing, 0.80 conviction) but its curve-shape/term-premium relevance is limited for the outright level call, and I discount it because it speaks primarily to supply/duration technicals rather than macro direction, and the panel flags it lacks financial-conditions context to know whether the pace is deliberate policy tightening (yield-negative for risk assets, ambiguous for level) or plain mechanical drift.

Net: three independent, converging drivers (inflation, inflation expectations, term premium) all say down, with decent but not extreme conviction, against one moderate tightening signal (financial conditions) that is early-stage and still accommodative in absolute terms, and one flat labor read. This is a real signal, not a coin-flip, given the multi-driver directional agreement — I lean down on the level, sized moderately given financial conditions is a live disagreeing voice.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (3,826,817) continues the steady runoff trend confirmed at the prior release. Total assets fell again from 3,844,016 to 3,826,817, a drop of ~17,199 over the latest week and consistent with the 4-week change of -24,627 and 13-week change of -128,800 (-3.26%). The series has declined in nearly every observation across the 13-week window, well below both the 13w average (3,881,489) and the 52w high (4,291,615), with assets now 464,798 below that peak. There is no sign of deceleration or pause — the pace of decline (~$6-9B/week average) looks consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff at an ongoing monthly cap. My prior call (down, 0.80) was correct: the balance sheet did indeed fall further. No statement language is available this period to indicate any change in reinvestment policy or a slowing/pausing of caps, so I have no reason to expect a reversal. Absent any new language suggesting a taper of runoff pace or a shift to reinvestment, the trend of steady contraction should persist into next month-end. I maintain a confident down call, keeping conviction slightly below prior levels only because the absence of any statement text removes one confirming data point about intent, though the price action itself is unambiguous.
Would change its mind if: A clean multi-week rise back above the 13w average, or statement language confirming a paused/slowed runoff cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three readings (0.29, 0.28, 0.27, 0.25) show the spread rolling over after peaking at 0.30, giving a clear three-reading pullback from the recent high. My prior flat call anticipated a possible break higher that did briefly occur (spread reached 0.30, well above the 0.20 threshold I cited), so that call was too cautious - the curve did steepen meaningfully over the period versus the prior low of 0.20. However, the most recent trend is now flattening again: the last four prints are a steady decline (0.30→0.29→0.28→0.27→0.25), a fairly consistent short-term reversal. The 21d and 63d changes are still positive (+0.09, +0.11), reflecting the earlier steepening impulse, but the newest data point is the lowest in five readings, suggesting that impulse is fading. Range position at 0.58 is mid-range, not extreme in either direction. The 252d change is negative (-0.08), indicating the longer-run trend is still toward flatter/more inverted, consistent with the recent pullback. Given the newest observations point down while medium-term momentum is still positive, I read this as a modest flattening bias going into month-end, though not high conviction given mixed signals across horizons.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.28 sustained for 2+ readings with 21d change re-accelerating would indicate steepening resumed, not flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has drifted gradually upward from -0.61 to -0.57 over the last 13 weeks, with the most recent prints flattening at -0.57 for three consecutive readings. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.03) both confirm a mild tightening trend, but the pace has clearly decelerated — the last three observations are identical, suggesting the move is stalling rather than accelerating. My prior call of "up" was directionally correct: conditions did tighten modestly from the trough, consistent with the +0.03 13-week change, though the magnitude was small and my falsifier threshold of -0.55 was not reached. Index remains deeply negative (-0.57), well below the zero average and near the 52-week low, meaning conditions are still historically loose overall even if the marginal direction has been toward tightening. Given the plateau in the last three prints and the absence of new policy language pointing toward tightening (the quoted language is dovish/patient, arguably supportive of continued easing bias), I see limited momentum for further tightening into month-end. The most likely outcome is a continuation of the flat plateau, with only a slight residual upward bias from the multi-week trend.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.59 would signal renewed easing; a break above -0.55 would confirm resumed tightening momentum.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out further: breakeven_10y has drifted from ~1.74 to 1.69 over the recent window, with be_change_21d at -0.05pp and be_change_63d at -0.19pp, both negative, confirming the soft-drift-lower thesis rather than overturning it. The last three prints (1.70, 1.68, 1.69) show some stabilization/chop rather than accelerating decline, but no reversal signal — my falsifier condition (sustained rise above 1.80 or positive 21d change) was not met. The policy language shift from "have remained low" to "have declined" is a hawkish-on-disinflation-expectations acknowledgment, confirming the market has genuinely priced lower inflation compensation, not just noise. Breakeven sits 0.31pp below the 2% objective and only 0.08pp above its 12-month low, in the bottom 14% of its 12-month range — a soft, still-negative backdrop. Given the recent flattening in the last three readings, I lean toward continued mild softness or stabilization near current levels rather than a sharp further leg down, but the balance of evidence (medium and long-term momentum both negative, confirmed by policy language) still favors down over flat.
Would change its mind if: A rise back above 1.75 sustained for several readings, or be_change_21d turning clearly positive, would overturn this call.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_distance_from_target, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "down" call was correct: headline_cpi_yoy fell from 1.80 to 1.67, and the newest monthly print (-0.03% m/m) was the softest in the series, well below the 0.25% threshold I flagged as a reversal trigger. Core PCE also continued its slide, from 1.64 to 1.56, extending a clear downtrend over the last several months (1.84→1.74→1.62→1.64→1.56). Both headline and core 3-month annualized rates (1.48% and 1.59%) sit comfortably below the 12-month y/y levels and below 2%, confirming momentum is disinflationary, not just base effects. The policy language shift is notable: it now states both headline and core inflation "have declined and are running below 2 percent," upgraded from the prior statement that only flagged headline weakness while core was "near 2 percent" — this is a clear acknowledgment of broadening softness. With mom_gap_vs_outgoing only +0.04pp and the latest print negative, there is no sign of a rebound. I see no evidence of stalling disinflation; the trend remains intact and if anything has reasserted itself after a couple of stronger prints mid-series. I'll call another modest step down for headline CPI y/y next release.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print above 0.25% combined with core_pce_yoy stabilizing or rising would suggest disinflation has stalled and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% matches the prior month, holding at the 12-month low, so my previous "flat" call was directionally correct in that no reversal materialized — the rate did not rise back toward 3.80%+ as I flagged would change my mind. Unemployment sits 0.40pp below the natural-rate anchor of ~4%, and the trend over 3/6/12 months is modestly negative (-0.20, -0.30, -0.40 pp respectively), indicating a still-tightening or at-worst stable labor market. Sahm gap is negligible (+0.03pp), well below the 0.3pp threshold that would signal loosening momentum. Range position is at 0 (the floor of the 12-month range), and the two consecutive prints at 3.60% suggest the series may be stabilizing at this lower level rather than continuing to fall further, but there's no evidence of loosening either. Policy language reinforces this: the FOMC's updated statement upgraded language from labor market "remains strong" amid slowing growth to labor market "remains strong" with activity rising at a "solid rate" — a more confident characterization, consistent with continued tightness rather than deterioration. With no rise in unemployment and strengthening qualitative language, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-tight, not loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print at/above 3.80% (sahm_gap rising above 0.3pp) would signal a reversal toward loosening; two months of increase would confirm a trend change.
Leaned on: sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year sits at 2.00%, right at its 12-month low (range position 0.00), confirming last cycle's down call was correct. Momentum remains negative across all horizons: -0.22pp over 21d, -0.41pp over 63d, and -0.87pp over the full year. The yield has been grinding lower with no signs of stabilization — the last print (2.00) matches the cycle low exactly, and the recent bounce to 2.07 quickly reversed. The 30s10s slope at +0.52pp is not unusually steep, suggesting the entire long end is being pulled down together rather than a curve-shape story, consistent with a genuine decline in term premium (falling term/duration compensation) rather than just front-end repricing. The updated policy language noting market-based inflation compensation has "declined" (versus merely "remained low") reinforces that long-end inflation risk premium embedded in the 10y is compressing, which is a direct term-premium signal. There is no evidence of a reversal: no close above prior resistance levels, no positive momentum, no steepening. The bias remains toward further downside or at least stabilization at cycle lows, though the pace of decline has moderated slightly (21d change less severe than 63d/252d annualized pace), so I temper conviction slightly from pure momentum extrapolation.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close back above ~2.30-2.40 with positive 21d momentum and a steepening 30s10s slope would signal the downtrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broad easing/tightening in financial conditions would clarify if flight-to-quality is driving the decline.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace of Fed balance-sheet runoff or issuance changes would clarify supply-side pressure on long-end yields.

arbitrated.notes: Duration seat: net direction of nominal yields over the ~1-month horizon. The two drivers most directly tied to yield levels — term_premium (10y at cycle lows, -0.22/-0.41/-0.87pp across 21/63/252d, no stabilization) and inflation/inflation_expectations (headline CPI 1.80→1.67, core PCE still sliding, breakevens 1.74→1.69) — all point the same direction: down. Labor_tightness is flat-to-tight (unemployment pinned at 12-month low, no loosening), which if anything supports continued low yields rather than a reversal — it does not contradict the down call, it just isn't pushing it further. Financial_conditions is flat/plateauing at a still-loose level with only marginal tightening bias, low conviction (0.30) and mixed momentum — a weak, non-actionable signal I largely set aside for the level call. Balance_sheet is confidently down (assets shrinking, QT continuing), which is a hawkish/tightening supply-side force that could in isolation push yields up via reduced demand for duration absorption, but it's explicitly flagged as missing a link to financial_conditions and to term_premium's own read — and the term_premium analyst, who actually observes the 10y directly, sees no upward pressure from any supply story, just relentless downward momentum. I weight the direct-yield-observing analyst (term_premium) and the inflation complex most heavily, since they carry the freshest and most consistent read, and treat balance_sheet as a secondary, running counter-narrative that hasn't yet shown up in actual yield behavior. Net: modest-to-moderate conviction that yields drift lower into month-end, led by the long end but with the front end anchored by a still-tight labor market limiting how far the curve overall falls.

trade.rationale: Long duration bias skewed to the long end where term-premium and inflation-compensation compression is clearest, with a smaller 2y leg since labor tightness caps front-end downside.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (3,803,436) extended the decline from 3,808,110 the prior week, a 4-week drop of -23,381 and 13-week drop of -124,837 (-3.18%), consistent with ongoing runoff. My prior "down" call was correct. However, the key new development is policy language: the Committee will conclude the runoff two months earlier than previously indicated, i.e., balance sheet reduction ends in [date] . This is a clear signal of an approaching halt to QT, but it explicitly confirms the caps remain in effect until that stated end date — it announces a stopping point, not an immediate pause or reversal. arbitrated.notes: Treating this as a level call on nominal Treasury yields, I set aside curve_slope entirely (out of mandate, curve-shape seat) and note it only as corroborating context that front-end/belly pressure is soft. The core level signal comes from term_premium (down, 0.40) and balance_sheet (down, 0.55), both fresh, both pointing the same direction and mutually reinforcing: the 10y sits near 12-month lows with strong 63d/252d downtrends, and the earlier-than-expected end to QT removes a forward source of duration supply pressure — a dovish, yield-suppressing signal for the belly-to-long end. Financial conditions are flat/pinned (loose but static), giving no incremental push either way. Inflation is a genuine cross-current: realized inflation (up, 0.35, formed 16 days ago) shows core PCE stalling its decline, while inflation_expectations (flat/slight-down, 0.30) shows breakevens plateauing rather than falling further — net, inflation is roughly neutral-to-mildly-firming, a modest offset to the term-premium/balance-sheet downside case but not enough to flip it. Labor is flat with a one-tick uptick that's within noise — no clear directional push, and stale-ish (23 days) relative to the balance-sheet and term-premium reads formed at this meeting. Weighing freshness and agreement: two independent, fresh drivers (term premium, balance sheet) point down; financial conditions is neutral; inflation and labor are mixed/flat with modest, offsetting convictions. This nets to a modest-conviction downward level view — yields drift lower into month-end, but not with high confidence given the inflation stall and the fact that the QT-end signal explicitly softens (rather than reverses) the balance-sheet drag. I lean the trade toward duration length concentrated more in the 10y where the term-premium and balance-sheet signals are most directly relevant, with a smaller matching 2y leg to keep it a clean level (non-curve) trade.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two weekly prints show a sharp reversal: assets jumped from 3,769,673 to 3,844,695 then to 3,857,715, a +97.8bn move over 4 weeks, after roughly a year of gradual QT decline (52w change still -335,194). This is the flat-or-above signal I said would change my mind. My prior "down" call was wrong for this most recent period. Additionally, policy language has changed materially: the Committee will end balance sheet runoff in [date] two months earlier than previously planned. Combined with the concrete evidence of assets already rising in the most recent prints (likely reflecting bill purchases to offset reserve scarcity, or technical factors like coupon reinvestment resuming), this points to an inflection from runoff to stabilization/modest growth. Given the announced end of QT and the actual measured increase in the two latest weekly readings, I expect fed_assets to be flat-to-up at the next month-end print. The magnitude of the recent jump is large enough that even with some mean reversion, the balance sheet likely holds near or above current levels rather than resuming meaningful decline within a single month.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing a sharp drop back toward the 3,760-3,770k range would indicate the recent jump was technical/temporary and runoff has resumed.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread remains extremely compressed, currently at 0.05pp versus a 12-month range of -0.04 to +0.35pp (range position 0.23 — still in the lower quartile). My prior "up" call was correct in direction: the spread rose from 0.04 to 0.05-0.06 over recent prints, and the 21-day change is now +0.08pp, confirming a modest steepening impulse. However, this is set against a much larger 63-day change of -0.20pp and 252-day change of -0.22pp, meaning the medium and longer trend remains firmly flattening; the recent uptick looks like a stabilization/bounce off a low rather than a durable reversal. The last ten observations show the spread oscillating narrowly between 0.03 and 0.09 with no clear trend — essentially noise around a flat, historically inverted-adjacent level. The 10s30s slope at +0.44pp shows the long end is comparatively steep, suggesting the compression is concentrated in the 2s10s segment specifically. Given the tiny absolute moves (a few basis points) and the choppy pattern, I don't see strong evidence for continued steepening into month-end; more likely the spread hovers near current pinned levels. I'm downgrading conviction from the prior steepening call toward a flat/marginal-up stance given the mixed short vs. long-term trend signals.
Would change its mind if: A break below 0.00pp with 21d change turning negative again would confirm flattening has resumed; a push above 0.10pp sustained would confirm real steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has continued its slow, steady drift toward tighter conditions. The last two prints held flat at -0.51 after a long uninterrupted climb from -0.58, so the prior "up" call was correct: the tightening drift did not stall or reverse below -0.55, nor did it drop below -0.58. Momentum indicators confirm this: nfci_change_4w (+0.03) and nfci_change_13w (+0.06) are both positive, and range_position at 0.55 shows the index is now past the midpoint of its 52-week range, closer to its high (-0.42) than its low (-0.61). That said, the pace has clearly decelerated — the index went flat for the last two readings after five straight weeks of steady increases, suggesting the tightening impulse may be losing momentum even though it hasn't reversed. Conditions remain looser than the zero average (distance_from_zero -0.51), so this is a mild normalization from very loose conditions rather than a shift to genuinely tight conditions. Given the flattening in the most recent two prints, I lean toward continued mild tightening but with reduced conviction versus last time, since the momentum that justified the prior call is now weakening.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.53 would signal the tightening drift has stalled; a drop below -0.55 would confirm renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints are flat at 1.53, following a decline from 1.66. That looks like stabilization after a leg down, but 1.53 also sits just 0.03pp above the 12-month low of 1.50, and be_range_position of 0.04 shows breakevens are pinned near the bottom of their year-long range. The 21d and 63d changes remain negative (-0.06, -0.16), and the 252d change (-0.63) confirms a strong disinflationary trend in market pricing over the year, though the trend's pace has clearly decelerated at the margin — the last two observations show no further downward movement. My prior flat call was reasonably validated: the series did not break to a new low, and the most recent two readings are identical, consistent with stabilization rather than resumed decline. However, sitting this close to the 12-month low with distance from target still large (-0.47pp) means the balance of risk is not symmetric — a resumption of the downtrend is more likely than a bounce back toward 2%, given no policy language suggesting re-anchoring higher. I'll maintain a flat call but shade slightly toward downside risk given the proximity to range floor, rather than calling flat with confidence.
Would change its mind if: A print below 1.53 (new 12-month low) or be_change_21d turning clearly negative again would confirm renewed downward momentum rather than stabilization.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release's outgoing_mom (0.23) came in just under my 0.25 reversal threshold, and headline yoy ticked down slightly (1.74→1.68), so the "down" call was directionally correct though the move was small. The newest print, however, shows headline_mom rising to 0.15 from 0.09, with the 3m average mom at 0.16 — a mild pickup in momentum versus the prior month, though still below the 12-month outgoing pace. Core PCE yoy has actually turned up over the last two prints (1.64→1.75), and core_yoy_change_3m is +0.18pp, indicating underlying persistence is firming, not fading. Headline yoy is still low in its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.20) and core sits 0.25pp below target, so there's no overheating, but the momentum signal has flipped from decelerating toward stabilizing/reaccelerating. Policy language shift (rate cut, "muted inflation pressures," inflation compensation "remain low") suggests the Committee itself was responding to soft inflation, consistent with the low base—but that's a lagging signal already priced into these prints. Given core's recent uptick and headline's mom re-acceleration, I no longer see confident evidence for continued deceleration; the data now looks more like stabilization with a slight upward tilt than a clean "down" call.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print below 0.05% combined with core_pce_yoy resuming its decline would confirm renewed disinflation and support a down call again.
Leaned on: headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom_3m_avg, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 3.50%, a new 12-month low, down from 3.60% in the prior two readings. My previous "flat" call was too cautious — the labor market has continued to tighten modestly rather than stall. Over 3/6/12-month windows the rate has fallen (-0.10, -0.30, -0.20 pp respectively), and the level sits a full 0.5pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor. Sahm-gap is a negligible +0.10pp (rate at its own 12-month low, so no Sahm signal at all — unrate_above_12m_low is 0.00). Range position is at 0 (i.e., at the low end of the 12-month range), reinforcing that this is a tight, still-tightening labor market, not one showing early loosening. Policy language changes are cosmetic (May→[date] reference) and both describe the labor market as "remains strong," consistent with continued tightness. There is no evidence here of any turn upward in unemployment. Given the string of low/falling prints and no sign of a reversal, I expect the next report to hold near this low level or tick down slightly rather than rise. I don't have strong conviction of a further decline given the rate is already very low and low unemployment rates tend to be sticky at the bottom, but "up" is clearly wrong and "flat to down" is the right characterization.
Would change its mind if: A print of 3.70%+ (two-tenths jump) would signal a genuine turn toward loosening and flip this view.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior flat call was roughly right in direction terms — dgs10 has stayed in a tight low band since, not breaking above the 1.90-2.00 threshold I set. Looking at the last 10 prints, yield has drifted down to 1.68 from 1.81, though the 21d change is actually +0.18pp (a recent bounce from a 1.64 trough), while 63d and 252d changes remain sharply negative (-0.35pp, -1.42pp). Range position is just 0.12 — near the 12-month low, well off the 3.24 high — indicating a persistently low term premium environment. Slope_30s10s at +0.44pp is modest, not signaling a steepening long-end selloff. The one new piece of policy language — ending SOMA runoff two months earlier than planned — is a mild negative for long-end supply pressure, i.e., it should if anything cap or slightly lower the term premium going forward by reducing the pace of balance-sheet-driven duration supply hitting the market. Combined with the yield still sitting near cycle lows and no confirmed reversal in momentum, I lean toward a modest downward or flat bias rather than a reversal higher. The recent 21d uptick keeps me from high conviction on 'down', but the earlier-than-expected runoff conclusion is a directional negative for long-end premium, so I lean slightly down rather than purely flat.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close back above ~1.90-2.00 with positive 21d momentum and steepening 30s10s slope would signal reversal higher.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on pace/composition of runoff conclusion would clarify long-end supply impact.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could show flight-to-quality driving further yield declines.

arbitrated.notes:
Reconciling the panel for a level view on nominal yields over roughly a one-month horizon. The clearest, freshest signals are: labor market still tight and tightening (unemployment at a 12-month low, no Sahm signal, sticky-low), which is disinflationary/dovish for yields at the margin; and financial conditions still drifting tighter (NFCI at range midpoint-plus, momentum positive though decelerating), which is a modest yield-supportive (higher) force via less accommodative conditions. Inflation itself is genuinely mixed — headline still low in its range but core PCE momentum has turned up over two prints — so I read inflation as flat with a slight upward tilt rather than a clean directional driver; I downweight it given the internal conflict (headline soft, core firming) and its 15-day staleness.

The dominant, freshest story is balance sheet: a sharp two-week reversal (+97.8bn) after a year of QT decline, paired with a concrete policy announcement ending runoff two months early. This is a strong, event-driven signal that directly bears on duration supply/demand dynamics — an early end to QT and actual reserve growth is a mechanical support for the front-end and reduces net Treasury supply pressure, which the term_premium analyst explicitly flags as a gap in their own read (they didn't have the balance-sheet detail). Term premium's own call (down) is thus reinforced, not contradicted, by balance_sheet's fresher, more concrete read — both point the same direction structurally (less supply pressure, lower term premium), even though term_premium's own momentum data showed a recent bounce.

Curve slope is explicitly out of scope for this level call. Netting the direction: labor tightness and easier net supply (balance sheet ending QT + lower term premium) argue for yields drifting lower to flat, while financial conditions' continued tightening argues modestly higher. Given the balance-sheet/term-premium alignment is the freshest and most structurally clear story, I lean modestly toward yields drifting lower to flat, with low-to-moderate conviction given the genuine cross-currents.

Stale (15 days) and internally conflicted between headline and core momentum; low weight.

arbitrated.falsifier: A weekly balance-sheet print reversing sharply back toward 3,760-3,770k, or a sustained DGS10 close above 1.90-2.00 with positive momentum, would flip this toward a higher-yield read.

risks.text: Balance-sheet reversal could prove technical/temporary, resuming QT and removing the key dovish input.
Financial conditions tightening could accelerate again, overpowering the supply-relief narrative and pushing yields up.
Core inflation reacceleration could firm further, undercutting the labor-market disinflationary case.

trade.rationale: Modest duration-long bet: early QT end and reduced supply pressure plus persistent labor tightness lean yields lower to flat, tempered by still-tightening financial conditions.

why.balance_sheet: Sharp two-week reversal plus early QT-end announcement; fresh, concrete signal of reduced duration supply pressure ahead.
why.term_premium: Kept analyst's own down call; reinforced by balance_sheet's fresher supply-side read the analyst lacked.
why.labor_tightness: New 12-month low unemployment, no Sahm signal, sticky-tight; modest disinflationary/dovish pull on yields.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI drift toward tighter continues but momentum decelerating two prints running; small upward yield pressure only.
why.inflation: Headline soft but core PCE momentum turning up; internally conflicted and 15 days stale, so treated as near-flat.

=== balance_sheet ===

The 10s30s slope at +0.48pp remains comfortably positive, consistent with a normal-shaped long end, but that's context only. Given the recent 3-week downtick outweighs the older drift, I lean toward continued easing (lower nfci) over the next month, but the move is small and the range is narrow, so conviction is modest.

Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 1.60 or be_change_21d turning clearly positive would indicate stabilization rather than renewed decline.

Report:
  The latest print rose to 3.60% from 3.50% the prior month — a one-tenth uptick, not the two-tenths threshold I set as my falsifier, so my prior "down" call was directionally wrong for this single print but the broader trend it rested on is still intact. Looking at the full series, the rate has oscillated in a narrow 3.50-3.60 band for the last several months after falling from 3.80-4.00 earlier in the window. The 3m and 6m changes are both -0.10pp, and the 12m change is -0.20pp, all still pointing toward a gently tightening or at-worst-flat labor market. The Sahm gap is a negligible +0.07pp, far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turning point, and the level itself sits 0.40pp below the assumed natural rate — consistent with a tight, not loosening, market. Policy language is unchanged, still describing the labor market as "strong" with "solid" job gains. Given the noise inherent in a one-tenth move within a persistent low band, and no acceleration signal, I read this as essentially flat with a slight tightening bias, not a new loosening trend. The single-month uptick is worth watching but doesn't yet constitute a break.
Would change its mind if: A print of 3.70%+ (another two-tenths jump) sustained over two months would confirm a genuine turn toward loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call is validated: the 10y fell sharply into the latest print (1.78 → 1.69), the steepest single-step drop in the series, pulling dgs10_change_63d to -0.21pp and dgs10_change_252d to a large -1.39pp. The yield now sits at just 0.22pp above its 12-month low and only 12% into its 12-month range (dgs10_range_position 0.12), confirming the long end is pinned near cycle lows rather than reversing. The 21d change is barely positive (+0.04pp), reflecting the earlier mid-series bounce to 1.85 that has now fully unwound. None of my reversal conditions (sustained close above 1.90-2.00, positive momentum, steepening 30s10s) are met — in fact the move is accelerating lower, not higher. The 30s10s slope at +0.48pp is unremarkable and doesn't signal fresh long-end-specific pressure from issuance/supply; the drop looks more like a broad-based richening consistent with flight-to-quality or duration demand than a term-premium repricing higher. Given the fresh low-range position and negative short-term momentum, I lean toward continued softness or at best stabilization near these lows into month-end, with modest conviction given the size of the recent move could partially mean-revert.
Would change its mind if: A sustained close back above ~1.85-1.90 with positive 21d momentum and steepening 30s10s slope would signal reversal higher.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The net directional signal for nominal Treasury yields over the ~1-month horizon points modestly lower, but with real dispersion across drivers that argues for sizing down rather than a high-conviction call. The clearest, freshest signal is term_premium: the 10y has fallen sharply (1.78→1.69), sits near 12-month lows, momentum is accelerating lower, and no reversal conditions are met — this is a live, well-evidenced "down" read on yields directly. Inflation_expectations reinforces this from a different angle: breakevens have dropped decisively in the most recent prints, near 12-month lows, which — while nominally a real/breakeven-split issue outside my mandate — corroborates that market-implied inflation compensation is falling, consistent with lower nominal yields absent a real-rate offset. Financial_conditions has reversed from tightening to a mild easing bias, a second-order but consonant signal (easier conditions can accompany/reflect lower yields via duration demand). Labor_tightness and headline inflation are essentially flat/noisy — labor shows a one-tenth uptick within a stable band (not a break), and inflation's "up" call is 16 days stale, resting on a single bounce that core measures don't confirm; I discount it. Balance_sheet shows unambiguous multi-week expansion (higher assets), which is Fed-liquidity-supportive and modestly yield-suppressive for duration demand, adding a small corroborating tilt lower rather than a supply-driven push higher — the panel's own gap-flag (lacks financial_conditions context) tempers how much weight I give it as a standalone driver of yields. Curve_slope is explicitly out of my mandate and roughly flat/range-bound anyway, so it doesn't move my level view. Weighing the two freshest, most decisive reads (term_premium, inflation_expectations) against noisier/staler ones, I land on a modest-conviction "down" call for the level of nominal yields.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (4,052,875) extends the rising trend seen over the last several months. The 4-week change is still positive (+33,052 $M) and the 13-week change is strongly positive (+292,929 $M, +7.79%), confirming that the balance sheet has been expanding rather than merely stabilizing. The 52-week change is now essentially flat-to-slightly-negative (-44,295 $M), which shows that this expansion is a relatively recent, sharp reversal from a longer period of runoff or flat holdings — the series troughed roughly mid-window and has climbed steadily since. Assets are now only ~35,439 $M below the 52-week high, effectively at cycle highs. There is no fresh statement language to explain the "why" (no mention of reinvestment caps, purchase programs, or balance-sheet policy), so I cannot confirm whether this reflects a deliberate QE-style expansion, technical factors (e.g., repo operations, bill purchases to offset reserve drains), or temporary funding operations. Absent contrary language and given the strength and persistence of the recent 4-week and 13-week trends, my prior "up" call was correct and the trend has continued into this new print. I maintain the same directional view, with conviction unchanged given the consistency of the recent measurements, but tempered by lack of any confirming policy narrative this period.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets falling back toward or below ~3.95T, or 4-week change turning negative, would indicate the expansion has stalled.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (0.17) reversed the prior dip from 0.14 back up, but this is noisy chop rather than a clear trend: the series has been oscillating 0.14-0.19 for six readings after an earlier steepening burst (63d change +0.20pp) that has since stalled (21d change -0.03pp, 252d change -0.06pp). Slope sits at 0.62 of its 12-month range, comfortably off both the 12m low (-0.04) and high (+0.30), so neither extreme is in play. My prior "flat" call was roughly right — the spread has stayed in a tight band around 0.15-0.17 rather than resuming a clear steepening or flattening trend since that call. The Fed cut rates again with dovish-leaning language (further insurance cut, muted inflation, one dissent now preferring to hold rather than cut further), which is consistent with front-end cuts anchoring the short end while the long end drifts with growth/inflation expectations — but the net effect on 2s10s recently has been essentially a wash, with dgs2 and dgs10 both near 1.6-1.8%, keeping the spread compressed and rangebound. Without a break above ~0.20 sustained or a drop toward zero, I don't see enough evidence to call a directional move.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 0.20pp for several readings would confirm steepening; a drop back toward 0.00-0.05pp with negative 21d change would confirm flattening resumed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_12m_low, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has continued to ease modestly, moving from -0.51 down to -0.57 over the last several readings, with the most recent three prints flat at -0.57. My prior call of "down" (easing) was correct in direction and magnitude was small but consistent — no print came close to the -0.51/-0.49 threshold I flagged as a reversal signal. Momentum indicators confirm this: 4-week change is -0.03 and 13-week change is -0.04, both indicating continued loosening, though the pace has decelerated noticeably (the last three observations are identical at -0.57, suggesting the easing trend may be stalling). The index sits well below the zero average at -0.57, near its 52-week low (-0.61) and in the loose portion of its range (range_position 0.21 of 1). Given the plateau in the last three prints, I lean toward continued flatness-to-slight-easing rather than a sharp move either way. The lack of new momentum and the flattening trajectory tempers conviction. Absent any tightening catalyst, the path of least resistance is sideways-to-slightly-looser, but the flattening pattern reduces confidence that this trend will extend meaningfully further by month end.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.54 would signal stalling reversing into tightening; a print at or above -0.51 would strongly confirm renewed tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year breakeven has been essentially pinned in a narrow 1.60-1.63 band for the last ten readings, with the most recent print at 1.61 — down slightly from 1.63 but still within the recent range rather than breaking new lows. My prior "down" call anticipated further deterioration, but be_change_21d is flat (+0.00pp) and be_change_63d is actually positive (+0.06pp), which is evidence of stabilization rather than renewed decline. The single-month move to 1.61 looks like noise within a tight band, not a trend continuation. That said, the longer 252-day change (-0.35pp) and the low position in the 12-month range (0.26) confirm breakeven remains well below the 2% target (-0.39pp distance) and near the bottom of its yearly range, so there's no case for calling this "up." Policy language reiterates that market-based inflation compensation remains low with little change in survey measures, consistent with a stalled-out but not accelerating decline. Given the flat 21-day and positive 63-day changes, my prior down call looks slightly overdone in the very near term — the series seems to have found a floor around 1.60-1.63 for now.
Would change its mind if: A clear break below 1.58 or be_change_21d turning sharply negative would confirm renewed decline; a push above 1.65 would suggest recovery.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.35) was validated: headline CPI y/y jumped to 2.09% from 1.73%, a +0.36pp move, driven by two consecutive strong prints (0.28% m/m, 0.28% m/m) versus the prior outgoing pace of 0.07%. The 3m annualized headline rate (2.91%) is now running well above the 12-month y/y print, confirming upward momentum rather than a one-off. However, core PCE tells a different story: it sits at 1.62%, near its 12-month low, with 3m annualized core running just 1.36% — decelerating, not accelerating. The core_yoy_change_3m of -0.02pp shows underlying persistence is flat-to-soft, and core sits 0.38pp below target. This divergence (core_minus_headline = -0.47pp) suggests the headline pop is concentrated in volatile components (food/energy) rather than broad-based price pressure. For the next CPI release specifically, headline momentum has been building for two straight months (0.28, 0.28) with a 3-month average of 0.24% m/m — well above the 12-month trend — so base effects and recent momentum both point to headline staying elevated or ticking higher. But given core's softness, I'd temper conviction versus a scenario where core was also firming. Policy language reiterates "muted inflation pressures" and inflation "running below 2 percent," consistent with the core read, reinforcing that this is a headline-specific uptick.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print reverting below 0.10% alongside continued core_pce_yoy softness below 1.60% would signal the recent uptick was transitory, flipping me to flat.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_minus_headline

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.60%, matching the prior month and confirming the flat call from last time. The series shows a very tight labor market: unemployment sits 0.40pp below the ~4% natural-rate anchor, near its 12-month low (3.50%), and well within a narrow 0.50pp range for the year. Both 3m and 6m changes are exactly zero, and the 12m change is -0.20pp, indicating a market that has if anything tightened slightly over the past year before stabilizing. The Sahm-style gap (unrate vs 3m-low) is a negligible 0.07pp, far from any threshold suggesting a turning point. Policy language continues to describe the labor market as "strong" with "solid" job gains, unchanged in tone from the prior statement. There is no evidence of loosening momentum — no consecutive rises, no acceleration off the 12-month low, and the level itself remains historically low. With flat 3m/6m changes and a rate pinned at its recent low, the most defensible call for the next report is continued flatness, perhaps with a slight tightening bias given the low level, but not enough directional evidence to lean firmly toward further declines.
Would change its mind if: A print of 3.80%+ sustained over two months, or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.5pp, would signal genuine loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_width

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y sits at 1.78, essentially flat versus the prior reading of 1.77 and within a tight 1.73-1.84 band over the last 10 observations. My prior "down" call is only partially validated: the 21d change is -0.06pp (mildly down) but the 63d change is +0.31pp, showing the longer trend has actually been upward, not downward. The yield is still in the lower fifth of its 12-month range (range_position 0.19), well off the 3.06% high and close to the 1.47% low, which argues against a strong new upward premium impulse. The 30s10s slope at +0.43pp is modestly positive but not steepening sharply, and 10s2s at +0.17pp doesn't speak to the long-end premium directly. There's no fresh catalyst in the policy language — inflation compensation measures remain low and stable, suggesting no fresh term-premium repricing from that channel. Given the yield has stabilized in a narrow 1.73-1.78 range for the last several prints after the initial decline, the evidence now points to consolidation rather than continued decline or a fresh rise. I'm revising toward flat, with low-moderate conviction, since the 63d uptrend is real but has stalled in the most recent weeks.
Would change its mind if: A break above ~1.85 with renewed positive 21d momentum and steepening 30s10s would signal resumed upward premium; a drop toward 1.65 or below would confirm renewed decline.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Weighing the panel for a net nominal Treasury yield level view over roughly the next month. The two seats I'm barred from trading directly on curve/real-nominal still inform the level: inflation shows a genuine headline CPI acceleration (0.45, fresh) but core PCE is soft and decelerating, and breakeven inflation expectations are pinned flat in a tight range with no confirmation of the headline pop — this is a real disagreement within the inflation complex itself, not just across drivers, so I discount the inflation upside call rather than let it dominate. Labor stays flat at a tight, low level with zero 3m/6m momentum — no tightening or loosening impulse, essentially neutral for yields. Financial conditions continue to ease modestly but the trend is visibly flattening (three flat prints at -0.57), a stale-ish, low-conviction signal that argues mildly for looser conditions capping yields, not a strong driver either way. Term premium is flat/consolidating after a real 63d uptrend stalled — no fresh catalyst, low conviction, and explicitly missing balance-sheet and financial-conditions context that could tilt it either way, so I lean on it lightly. Balance sheet is the freshest, most confidently reported signal: assets at cycle highs, strong 4-week and 13-week expansion, no reversal in sight. Historically sustained balance-sheet expansion (whether QE-style or technical reserve management) tends to compress term premium and cap long-end yields, partially offsetting any inflation-driven upward pressure. Netting a real but contested inflation upside impulse against a strong, fresh balance-sheet expansion signal and flat labor/financial-conditions/term-premium readings, I land on a mild, low-conviction upward bias in yields — inflation's headline momentum narrowly outweighs the balance-sheet dampening effect, but the panel is too split and too many drivers are flat/stale to lean hard in either direction.

discounted.why: Out of scope for a pure level/duration call; explicitly a curve-shape seat.
Breakevens flat/pinned, contradicts headline CPI story; low signal for level direction.
Low conviction, missing key inputs (balance sheet, financial conditions) per gap flags.

arbitrated.falsifier: A core PCE reacceleration alongside continued headline CPI strength, or a stall/reversal in balance-sheet growth back toward flat, would flip this to a clearer up or down call.

risks.text: Balance-sheet growth could be technical/repo-driven rather than deliberate, misreading its dampening effect on yields.
Inflation complex is internally split (headline vs core vs breakevens), risking a false directional read.
Financial conditions plateau could reverse quickly given three flat prints, understating tightening risk.
Term premium consolidation could break either way once missing balance-sheet/FC context resolves.

trade.rationale: Mild net upward bias from fresh headline inflation momentum outweighing balance-sheet dampening; low conviction given flat labor, stalling FC easing, and split inflation signals.

why.inflation: Fresh headline CPI acceleration is real, but core PCE softness and pinned breakevens temper how much this should move nominal yields.
why.balance_sheet: Strong, fresh expansion at cycle highs typically dampens term premium/long-end yields, offsetting inflation upside; no policy narrative to confirm intent.

why.financial_conditions: Easing trend stalling but still net looser; mild downward pressure on yields, low conviction given plateau.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series shows a clear, sustained expansion. Total assets rose from 3.9458T to 4.1656T over the 13-week window, a +307.9B (+7.98%) increase, with the most recent 4-week change still strongly positive at +112.7B. The trend is not decelerating — the last few prints (4095491 → 4137052 → 4165591) show the pace of weekly gains actually accelerating rather than tapering, and the level is sitting at a fresh 52-week high (assets_below_52w_high = 0). My prior 'up' call at 0.65 conviction was correct and the evidence has only strengthened since: this is no longer a modest lean but a clear, unambiguous expansionary pace. There is no statement language this period to explain the 'why', which is a mild limitation, but the magnitude and consistency of the measured increases (four consecutive weekly rises accelerating in pace) speak for themselves under my mandate to weight recent pace heavily. Absent any sign of deceleration or reversal (no falling weekly print, no negative 4-week change), the balance sheet is expanding at a brisk clip and this pattern should carry into next month-end absent a change in policy. I am raising conviction given the acceleration confirms and extends the prior call.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets falling, or the 4-week change turning negative or near zero, would signal the expansion has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has extended its steepening trend, printing 0.34pp, a fresh 12-month high and now sitting at the top of its 12-month range (range_position 1.00). The prior call of flat (conviction 0.25) was wrong to the extent it doubted follow-through; the falsifier condition for steepening — a sustained move above 0.20pp for several readings — has now been clearly met and then some, with the last three prints (0.29, 0.32, 0.34) showing consistent upward momentum after a brief stall mid-series. Momentum measures confirm this: 21d change +0.17pp and 63d change +0.28pp are both solidly positive, and even the 252d change is positive at +0.13pp, indicating the steepening is not just a short-term blip but part of a longer trend. The curve has moved from near-flat/slightly inverted (12m low -0.04pp) to a full 0.38pp above that low. The 10s30s slope at +0.47pp, wider than 2s10s, suggests the long end continues to steepen more broadly, consistent with the pattern. With the spread pinned at its range high and monetary policy language shifting toward holding rates steady after cuts, there's no evidence in the slope data itself of stalling. I expect further modest steepening or at least consolidation at these elevated levels, not reversal.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.25pp with negative 21d change would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series bottomed at -0.57 and has since ticked up to -0.55 over the last three prints, consistent with the +0.03 4-week change. This confirms the mild reversal I flagged last time: conditions eased steadily from -0.51 to -0.57 over the 13-week window (nfci_change_13w -0.04), but the most recent momentum has turned modestly upward, with the index now above its 13-week average and sitting at 35% of its 52-week range from the low. This is a small but real stalling/reversal signal — my prior flat call was directionally reasonable but slightly underweighted the reversal that has since continued for three consecutive prints rather than one. The level remains deeply negative (-0.55, distance from zero -0.55), so conditions are still far looser than average and this is not a regime shift — just a modest loss of easing momentum. Given the -0.54 threshold I set previously has now been breached (current -0.55, close to it) and the trend of three consecutive upticks, I lean toward continued mild tightening (rising NFCI) into month-end, though conviction is limited given the move is small in absolute terms and the last two prints were flat.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to -0.56 or below would signal renewed easing and invalidate the reversal thesis.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_13w_avg, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last ten readings show breakeven_10y drifting in a narrow 1.73-1.77 band, closing the series at 1.77, matching the series high. My prior 'flat' call referenced thresholds (1.58/1.65) that appear stale relative to this data's actual range (1.48-1.98 12m low/high), suggesting my prior read was calibrated to a different regime or I mis-set the bar too low - the market has clearly been operating well above those levels throughout. Judged on the actual data: be_change_21d (+0.16pp) and be_change_63d (+0.24pp) both show consistent upward momentum over the past one-to-three months, while be_change_252d (+0.03pp) shows the longer trend is flat, meaning this is a fairly fresh, short-term uptick rather than a sustained multi-quarter climb. be_range_position at 0.58 shows expectations are now above the middle of the 12-month range, and the latest two prints (1.75→1.77) mark a second consecutive move up after a small dip. Still below the 2% objective by 0.23pp, so there's room to rise further before it becomes a concern, but the recent trajectory is modestly upward. Policy language describes market-based measures as 'low' but that commentary looks stale relative to the recent uptick. I'll shift to a mild 'up' call given the consistent short-term momentum, tempered by the flat 252-day trend.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 1.73 or be_change_21d turning negative would signal the uptick was noise rather than a trend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.32% from 2.09%, extending the climb from the 1.68% trough three months earlier. The recent monthly prints have been firm (0.28, 0.28, 0.29), giving a 3-month annualized headline pace of 3.48%, well above the trailing y/y rate, and the mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.37pp) confirms momentum is building rather than fading — the falsifier condition (mom back below 0.10%) did not materialize. That said, this headline strength is not corroborated by core: core_pce_yoy continues to grind lower, now 1.50%, a fresh 13-observation low, with 3m annualized core running only 1.14% — well below target and decelerating. The core_minus_headline gap (-0.82pp) is unusually wide, suggesting the headline pickup is being driven by volatile components (likely energy) rather than persistent underlying price pressure. Policy language still describes inflation as "near" 2% symmetric objective with "muted inflation pressures" and low market-based compensation, consistent with core softness rather than broadening inflation. Given three consecutive firm monthly headline prints, I lean toward another modest headline increase next month, but conviction is tempered by the core divergence, which argues the underlying trend is not accelerating.
Would change its mind if: A headline_mom print reverting below 0.10%, or headline_cpi_yoy pulling back toward 2.0%, would flip me to flat given core's continued softness.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_minus_headline, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.60%, matching my prior "flat" call - and that call held up: the rate has now been flat at 3.60% for three of the last four readings after ticking down to 3.50% one month ago. The 3-month change is a marginal +0.10pp, 6-month change is exactly 0.00pp, and 12-month change is still -0.30pp, meaning the broader trend remains one of gradual tightening, not loosening. The Sahm-gap (0.10pp) is far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn, and the level itself (3.60%) sits 0.40pp below the ~4% natural rate, historically tight. Range position (0.2 of a narrow 0.5pp band) confirms the rate is hugging its cycle lows, not breaking out. Policy language is essentially unchanged, still describing the labor market as "strong" with unemployment "remained low" — no shift in tone that would suggest emerging slack. There's nothing here — no acceleration, no sustained rise off the 12-month low, no Sahm trigger — to support calling a loosening turn. I expect the unemployment rate to print flat to marginally higher next month, but not enough to break from the flat call with any real strength.
Would change its mind if: A print of 3.80%+ sustained over two months, or a Sahm-gap exceeding 0.5pp, would signal genuine loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y yield has been rangebound at the newest print (1.92%), essentially flat over the last 10 observations after ticking modestly higher in recent sessions (1.88→1.90→1.92). My prior flat call was directionally fine: yields did not break decisively either way, though the 21d (+0.14pp) and 63d (+0.23pp) changes show mild upward drift consistent with my stated trigger for renewed upward premium (break above ~1.85 with positive momentum), which has technically been satisfied. The 252d change of -0.85pp shows the longer trend is still down, but that's stale relative to the recent stabilization/uptick. Range position at 0.34 (of 1.47-2.79) keeps the yield in the lower third of its 12-month range, arguing against a strong directional call. The 30s10s slope (+0.47pp) is steady, not signaling fresh steepening pressure from issuance/duration supply beyond what's already priced. Inflation compensation and expectations are described as low/stable, removing one upside catalyst for term premium. Given the modest but real recent upward drift (21d/63d positive) against a low position in the 12-month range and no strong slope steepening, I lean slightly toward continued mild upward pressure on the yield into month-end, but conviction is limited given the choppy, narrow recent moves.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back toward 1.85 or below with negative 21d momentum, or a flattening/inversion of 30s10s, would confirm renewed decline rather than drift up.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: The panel converges more than it disagrees, but the mix of stale, fresh, and thin-conviction reads argues for a moderate, not maximal, up-in-yields call. Term premium (the most direct duration read) is fresh but weak conviction (0.30): DGS10 is rangebound with only mild 21d/63d upward drift, still in the lower third of its 12-month range, and 252d trend is down — this is a modest lean, not a break. It also lacks the balance-sheet and financial-conditions inputs that would clarify supply and risk-sentiment pressure. Balance sheet itself is the loudest, freshest, most confident signal (0.75, formed this meeting): assets at a 52-week high with accelerating weekly gains. Historically, sustained reserve/balance-sheet expansion coincides with easier financial conditions and can cap upward yield pressure, but here it's framed as expansionary momentum without clear linkage to Fed's securities holdings direction (could reflect repo/temporary operations) — I treat it as a secondary support for mild upward drift given the panel's own directional framing (up), while flagging the gap that term_premium doesn't get this input directly. Financial conditions shows a small, real tightening reversal (0.35) — mild NFCI uptick off cycle lows, corroborating the term premium and balance-sheet direction, but the move is small in absolute terms. Inflation and inflation-expectations both lean modestly up (0.40 each) on genuine but short-term/headline-driven momentum, with core PCE softening — not a strong embedded-inflation story yet. Labor tightness is flat, offering no directional push either way, and curve_slope is explicitly out of scope for a duration read (curve shape, not level) though I note its steepening backdrop is consistent with a mild bear-steepening bias supporting higher yields on net. Taken together: several small, same-signed drivers (term premium, financial conditions, inflation, balance sheet) each lean modestly up, none screaming, none stale. This nets to a mild-to-moderate upward drift in yield levels into month-end, not a high-conviction move.
