user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was correct: fed_assets rose again to a fresh 52-week high (8,556,181, essentially at the 8,564,943 high), continuing the pattern of expansion. Over the last 13 weeks assets are up +334,708M (+4.07%), and the 4-week change is still solidly positive at +108,200M, consistent with the stated pace of at least $80B/month Treasury and $40B/month MBS purchases. The most recent single observation ticked down slightly from the prior week (8,564,943 → 8,556,181), but this is a single-week wobble within a clear multi-week uptrend, not two consecutive declines, and the policy language shows no tapering announcement — purchases continue at the same explicit pace "until substantial further progress" is made. Given the mandate to weight recent pace over level, and that the 4-week and 13-week trends are both strongly positive with no change in language, I maintain that the balance sheet is expanding. Risk is only that this one-week dip is the start of a stall, but one data point is insufficient to reverse the call given the unchanged purchase commitment.
Would change its mind if: An explicit tapering announcement reducing monthly purchase amounts, or two consecutive weekly declines in fed_assets, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at 0.30 conviction was wrong in direction terms: the curve broke down from ~1.17-1.18 to 1.04-1.07, a sustained move below my stated 1.15 trigger for a flattening resolution. The last two readings (1.04, 1.07, 1.07) show the slope stabilizing at a lower level after the drop, not continuing to fall further. slope_change_21d is -0.18pp (recent flattening), but slope_change_63d is roughly flat (+0.02pp), meaning the move happened sharply and then leveled off. The newest three points are essentially flat to each other (1.04→1.07→1.07), suggesting the flattening impulse from taper signaling has been absorbed and the curve is consolidating at a new, lower plateau. slope_range_position at 0.45 shows we're mid-range over 12 months, not at an extreme, so there's no strong pull in either direction from range positioning. Given the recent stabilization after the flattening shock, and 63d change near zero, I lean toward flat to slightly down for the coming month — the taper-signal flattening looks like it has largely played out, but the trend beta remains negative (252d still positive/steepening) so there's no clear steepening catalyst either.
Would change its mind if: A break below 1.00pp or a fresh push above 1.15pp sustained over 2+ readings would clarify whether flattening resumes or reverses.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The index has been pinned at -0.65 for eleven straight readings before ticking up one hundredth to -0.64 in the latest print. That is a print above -0.65, which per my prior falsifier condition confirms marginal tightening rather than renewed easing. However, the magnitude is trivial: 4-week change is +0.02 and 13-week change is +0.03, both essentially noise against a 52-week range of 0.19 (from -0.69 to -0.51). The series sits at range_position 0.31, still comfortably in the loose half of its own recent history, and remains -0.64 below the zero average — deeply accommodative in absolute terms. Policy language is unchanged and explicitly describes conditions as accommodative, consistent with no shift in stance. My prior flat call was directionally correct in that nothing material moved; the tiny uptick doesn't yet constitute a trend, but it is the first move off a long plateau, so it merits a very slight lean toward continued mild tightening (or at least not further easing) into next month rather than a full reassertion of flat. Conviction remains low given the size of the move relative to historical noise and an unchanged policy stance.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back to -0.65 or lower would confirm the plateau/easing bias resumes; a further rise above -0.63 would confirm tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position, nfci_13w_avg

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last two prints show a clear reversal: breakeven_10y fell from a peak of 2.69 to 2.57 then 2.51, a drop of 0.18pp in the last two observations after a steady climb over the prior year (be_change_252d +0.81pp). My prior "flat" call was wrong in retrospect — the series was still grinding higher toward the top of its 12-month range (be_range_position 0.83) at the time, and I should have flagged the topping risk given the level was already 0.51pp above the 2% objective, an elevated distance historically associated with mean-reversion. Now the 21d and 63d changes (+0.14, +0.11) are still positive on net but are being dragged higher by the earlier surge — the most recent two prints are unambiguously negative, down from 2.69 to 2.51. This looks like the start of a down-move after an extended overshoot. With breakeven still well above target and near the top of its range, there is room to fall further before re-anchoring near 2%. The policy language of tolerating above-2% inflation doesn't itself move market pricing but caps upside conviction. I'll call this down, with moderate conviction given only two data points confirm the reversal.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.60 in the next print, with 21d change turning clearly positive, would signal the downturn was noise, not trend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  My prior "up" call is confirmed decisively: headline CPI y/y jumped from 5.35% to 6.24% (+0.89pp), and the m/m print spiked to 0.96%, well above the 3m average of 0.57% and far above the 0.3% threshold I set as a reversal condition. Momentum is accelerating, not pausing — headline 3m annualized is running at 7.01%, above the y/y rate itself, meaning the trend is still building. Core PCE y/y also rose again, 3.96%→4.01%, continuing an unbroken ascent for a full year, though its pace of increase has slowed (core_yoy_change_3m only +0.15pp vs +0.99pp for headline), suggesting core may be closer to plateauing even as headline reaccelerates on likely energy/food swings. Given the mandate to weight core for persistence, the deceleration signal in core's slope is worth noting, but it's still rising in level terms and nowhere near flattening or turning over. Headline sits at the top of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position=1.00), and the single-month acceleration is too large to dismiss as noise. Policy language shift from "risen" to "elevated" also signals the committee acknowledging a higher, more persistent inflation state. I expect the next headline print to be higher again, though with less certainty than this move given core's slowing second derivative.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below ~0.3% with core PCE y/y flattening or declining would confirm a turning point and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (down/tightening, conviction 0.45) was correct: unemployment fell again to 4.50%, right at my "confirm continuation" threshold of 4.60% or below. The trend remains firmly one-directional — 13 straight months mostly declining, with the rate now at its 12-month low and only 0.50pp above the ~4% natural-rate anchor. The pace of improvement has moderated somewhat (3m change -0.90pp vs 6m -1.60pp vs 12m -2.40pp), indicating the labor market is still tightening but at a decelerating rate as it approaches a level consistent with maximum employment. The Sahm-style gap is trivial (+0.27pp, essentially at the low), confirming no loosening signal. With the rate this close to the natural-rate zone, further declines of the same magnitude become mechanically less likely, but momentum and the string of consecutive declines suggest continued mild tightening rather than a reversal is still the more probable outcome. Policy language remains unchanged, offering no fresh signal about direction. I lean toward another modest decline or flat print, but with reduced conviction given the market is now near equilibrium and the pace of declines has been slowing, raising the odds of stabilization.
Would change its mind if: A print at or above 4.70% would signal stalling/reversal of the tightening trend; a print at 4.40% or below confirms continuation.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y has drifted down over the last three prints (1.63→1.54→1.57→1.55), pulling back from the recent local high near 1.68. My prior "up" call was partly right over the 63-day window (+0.31pp) and clearly right over 252 days (+0.76pp), but the most recent 21-day change is flat and the last few observations show a mild retracement rather than continued ascent. Range position remains elevated at 0.80 (yield sits in the upper portion of its 12-month range), which still argues the broader trend of the past year has been higher yields/term premium, and 30s10s slope of +0.38pp shows a still-positive but not steepening long-end curve. However, the immediate momentum has stalled and even reversed slightly over the last few days. With no fresh policy language pointing to accelerated issuance or balance-sheet changes (the note is boilerplate, unchanged), I don't see a strong catalyst for a fresh leg up or down. I'll moderate my prior conviction: the multi-month uptrend is intact but has lost short-term momentum, so the near-term (one month) directional signal is weaker than before. I lean toward continued modest upward drift given the elevated range position, but with reduced conviction given the recent flattening/pullback.
Would change its mind if: A sustained break below 1.50 with range_position falling toward 0.6 and 30s10s flattening further would confirm the uptrend has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  curve_slope lacks financial_conditions — Would clarify whether tightening conditions are driving further flattening beyond taper signal.
  curve_slope lacks inflation_expectations — Shifts in long-run inflation expectations could move the 10y leg and alter slope direction.
  inflation_expectations lacks inflation — Recent realized CPI surprises would clarify whether the breakeven drop reflects softer inflation data.
  inflation_expectations lacks financial_conditions — A broad risk-off tightening episode could explain breakeven declines via liquidity/technical effects rather than genuine repricing.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Any change in Fed Treasury purchase pace would directly affect long-end term premium.
  term_premium lacks curve_slope — Fuller curve context (2s10s, 5s30s trends) would clarify if steepening is broad-based or isolated.

arbitrated.notes: Building the level view from the drivers actually in my remit (inflation, labor tightness, term premium, financial conditions, balance sheet — curve shape is excluded). The strongest, freshest signal is inflation: headline CPI accelerated sharply (+0.89pp to 6.24%) with core PCE still rising, and this call is only 16 days old — high conviction and directly relevant to nominal yields via the inflation-compensation channel. Labor tightness also points the same way (unemployment at a 12-month low, still declining, 23 days old) though decelerating pace and proximity to the natural rate tempers conviction — this reinforces the case for a Fed that stays on a tightening/less-accommodative path, consistent with higher nominal yields. Term premium's own read is a weak "up" — multi-month uptrend intact (63d +0.31, 252d +0.76) but recent momentum has stalled/reversed over the last few prints, and it explicitly lacks the balance-sheet input, which is one gap I can close myself: balance-sheet expansion continues at pace with no taper language, an unambiguous suppressive force on long-end term premium that likely explains the term-premium stall. So I lean on balance_sheet's fresh, high-conviction "up" (expansion continuing) as a moderating, not reversing, force against the inflation/labor push higher. Financial conditions is barely moving off an 11-print plateau — real but trivial, a very slight tightening lean, adding a small confirming nudge rather than a driver of its own. Netting these: inflation and labor momentum push nominal yields modestly higher, balance-sheet expansion caps the move, term premium's own signal is soft and stalling. I land on a modest net "up" for the level, concentrated more in the belly/front-to-intermediate given labor/inflation are the freshest drivers, with dampened conviction reflecting the balance-sheet offset and term premium's own hesitation.

risks.text: Fed could announce taper or shift language, directly reversing the balance-sheet offset assumption.
Inflation momentum could be a one-month energy/food spike rather than durable acceleration.
Term premium's own stall could presage a broader reversal not yet visible in the data.

trade.rationale: Modest net-up level view driven by fresh inflation/labor tightening signals, tempered by ongoing balance-sheet expansion and a stalling term-premium signal; both legs long yield, weighted toward the belly/long end given inflation persistence.

why.inflation: Fresh (16d), sharp CPI acceleration and still-rising core PCE; strongest evidence for higher nominal yields.
why.labor_tightness: Fresh-ish (23d), unemployment at 12-month low but pace decelerating near natural rate; softer confirmation of tightening-supportive higher yields.
why.term_premium: Own call up but momentum stalled/reversed recently; discounted for missing balance_sheet input which explains the stall.
why.balance_sheet: Fresh, high-conviction expansion continuing at pace, no taper — suppresses long-end term premium, offsetting inflation/labor push.
why.financial_conditions: Trivial move off 11-print plateau; slight tightening lean, low weight given magnitude is noise-level.

arbitrated.notes: Sum of forces points modestly higher in yields, but with real caveats. Inflation is the strongest, freshest, highest-conviction signal (CPI y/y 6.24→6.90, core PCE still climbing, no inflection) — this is a genuine upside driver for nominal yields. Financial conditions have tightened to a 52w high on NFCI, a genuine live signal, but the absolute level is still deeply accommodative — a modest, second-order upward push on yields via tightening-pressure logic, not a dominant one. Balance sheet is still expanding (tapering pace, not runoff), which is a mild support for lower yields (continued Fed bid on long end) — this is a real headwind to the inflation/labor case, not to be waved away. Term premium itself is the direct measurement of the long end and it just broke down sharply (10y 1.63→1.43, 21d -0.14), and importantly flags it's missing the balance_sheet and financial_conditions context — the analyst suspects flight-to-quality/risk-off explains the drop, which financial_conditions' tightening reading does partially corroborate. Curve slope and inflation_expectations are explicitly excluded from a duration-level call (curve is a shape signal; breakevens are the real/nominal split), though breakevens falling does lean against an aggressive nominal-up view. Net: mild net upward tilt on yield levels, low-moderate conviction given the direct long-end print (term_premium) just reversed hard and the balance sheet still expanding to cap upside.

Term premium's own call (10y up modestly) is consistent with this but weaker and explicitly missing the balance_sheet input it should have \u2014 and balance_sheet itself is decelerating (single weekly pullback, shrinking purchase pace) even though its multi-week trend is still expansionary, so it's a soft \"up\" that could flip. arbitrated.notes: Reconciling for a nominal Treasury duration call: the panel is not split so much as layered — the strongest, freshest, most decisive reads (inflation up 0.68, inflation_expectations up 0.55) point firmly higher, while the more mechanical/market-based drivers (term_premium up 0.35, balance_sheet up 0.40, financial_conditions flat 0.35) either agree modestly or are genuinely directionless. Inflation is the standout: hot, accelerating, un-decelerating CPI/core PCE with explicit hawkish policy language (dropped "transitory," accelerating taper) is about as strong a level signal as this panel produces, and it is corroborated independently by breakeven inflation expectations also grinding to new highs. Term premium's own call (10y up modestly) is consistent with this but weaker and explicitly missing the balance_sheet input it should have — and balance_sheet itself is decelerating (single weekly pullback, shrinking purchase pace) even though its multi-week trend is still expansionary, so it's a soft "up" that could flip. Financial conditions are flat/stalled, offering no counter-pull. Labor tightness continues to fall (unemployment at new lows) which is a hawkish labor read but is being treated here as a labor-market driver, not directly a rate-level driver except insofar as it reinforces the same hawkish inflation-taper thread. Netting these: inflation and inflation expectations are the freshest and most decisive drivers, term premium and balance sheet lean the same way but softer, financial conditions is neutral. This adds up to a modest-to-moderate net upward drift in nominal yields over the coming month, with more conviction at the front-to-belly given the labor/inflation/taper linkage than a dramatic long-end repricing, but no analyst here contradicts a broad-based upward yield drift, so I apply it across both tenors with a same-sign duration trade.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: fed_assets rose again to 8,860,485 from 8,867,834 (near the 52w high, only 7,349 below it), and the 4w/13w/52w changes are all positive (+103,025 / +304,304 / +1,455,559 $M respectively). However, the last single observation actually ticked down slightly from 8,867,834 to 8,860,485 - a marginal, one-observation dip after a strong run-up, not yet a confirmed reversal. More importantly, the policy language shows continued tapering: purchases were cut from $40B/$20B to $20B/$10B per month, following an earlier cut from $80B/$40B pace. This is QE at a rapidly shrinking pace, not QT - the Fed is still adding Treasuries and MBS to its balance sheet every month, just less each time. Net asset growth should continue for the next month at a reduced clip until purchases reach zero, at which point the balance sheet would plateau rather than shrink. Given the still-positive purchase pace announced for the upcoming month ($20B+$10B minimum), I expect total assets to be modestly higher at month end, though the magnitude of increase will be smaller than in prior months given the taper trajectory.
Would change its mind if: A statement announcing purchases reduced to zero or reversed into runoff, or two consecutive weekly declines in fed_assets, would flip this to flat/down.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_52w_high, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the spread has fallen from 0.76 to 0.61pp over the last several readings, breaching my own falsifier threshold (sustained sub-0.75) decisively. The curve is now at its 12-month low (slope_range_position 0.00), and momentum is uniformly negative across horizons: -0.18pp over 21d, -0.46pp over 63d, -0.39pp over 252d. This is a clear, persistent flattening trend, not noise. Policy language confirms the driver: the Committee has shifted from patient/accommodative language to signaling imminent liftoff ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range") alongside accelerating taper completion. This is classic front-end-led flattening as the market prices near-term hikes while the long end lags, compressing 2s10s. With dgs2 rising faster than dgs10 (dgs2 +1.18% vs dgs10 +1.79% are cumulative levels, but the 2s10s spread compression confirms front-end repricing dominance), and no sign of stabilization in the last two prints (0.63, 0.63, 0.61), I see continued downward pressure into month-end. The 10s30s context (0.32pp, still positive and not collapsing) suggests this is specifically a front-end-driven flattening rather than a broader long-end inversion dynamic, but that doesn't change the 2s10s trajectory itself.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 0.70pp sustained over 2+ readings, or stabilization at current level for 3+ readings, would signal flattening has paused.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has essentially plateaued over the last several readings, oscillating narrowly between -0.54 and -0.56 after rising from -0.62 thirteen weeks ago. The 4-week change is a negligible -0.01, indicating no meaningful near-term momentum in either direction. The 13-week change of +0.08 shows the broader move over the quarter was toward tightening (off the 52w low of -0.69), but that move has clearly stalled — the last several prints are flat within a tight 0.02 band, and range_position at 0.90 confirms we're sitting near the top of the recent 52-week range with no further push higher. My prior flat call was correct: nfci did not resume its uptrend past -0.54, nor did it drop below -0.60; it simply hovered. Conditions remain deeply accommodative (-0.55, well below zero), consistent with the unchanged policy language about accommodative conditions and ongoing support for credit flow. With no fresh momentum signal and levels stable near a plateau, the most defensible call for month-end is continued flatness, though the slight uptick in the very latest observation (-0.56 to -0.55) keeps a small tightening lean alive.
Would change its mind if: A break above -0.54 with positive 4w change would confirm renewed tightening; a drop below -0.60 would confirm renewed easing.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation is 2.44, unchanged from the prior print and up modestly from the recent local low of 2.33. Looking at the last four readings (2.41, 2.38, 2.40, 2.44) there's a mild stabilizing/firming pattern after the dip from 2.46 to 2.33 earlier in the window. My prior call of "up" at 0.55 conviction was premature — the 21d and 63d changes are still negative (-0.14pp and -0.13pp respectively), meaning breakevens are net lower than a month/quarter ago despite the recent tick-up. The 252d change (+0.31pp) shows the longer trend is still up, but the near-term momentum has clearly stalled and reversed from where it was. Range position at 0.51 (roughly mid-range between 12m low 2.11 and high 2.76) reflects genuine indecision rather than trend continuation. Given the flat final two prints (2.44, 2.44) and negative short-term momentum, I don't see strong evidence for continued near-term upside. The distance from the 2% target (+0.44pp) argues expectations remain anchored above objective, but that's a level statement, not a directional one. I'm revising toward flat, acknowledging my prior up call has not been validated by subsequent data — the series stalled rather than extended.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above 2.50 with positive 21d change would confirm renewed upward momentum; a drop below 2.35 would confirm renewed downtrend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.62
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is confirmed: headline CPI y/y rose again to 7.56% from 7.17%, and core PCE y/y climbed to 5.21% from 4.89%, both continuing an unbroken accelerating trend across the full 13-month series. Momentum remains strong: headline 3m annualized is 8.95%, well above the trailing y/y print, and core 3m annualized (6.64%) still exceeds core y/y, meaning the recent quarter is running hotter than the year-ago comparison — no sign of the deceleration my prior falsifier condition required. The most recent monthly print (0.60% m/m) did dip from the prior two months (0.86%, 0.69%) but remains far above the 0.3% threshold that would have signaled a turn, and the 3-month average m/m (0.72%) is still elevated versus the outgoing print. Policy language changes reinforce this: the Fed dropped "transitory" framing, escalated to "elevated levels of inflation" from imbalances, and announced further taper acceleration — consistent with persistent, broadening inflation pressure rather than a peak. Given headline is at a 12-month high (yoy_range_position =1.00) and core PCE distance from target is now 3.21pp, both level and momentum argue for continued acceleration into the next release, though the mild sequential moderation in m/m prints tempers full conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.3% together with core PCE y/y flattening or declining would indicate inflation has peaked and reverse this call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, core_distance_from_target

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The previous call (down/continued tightening) was wrong: the newest print rose to 4.00% from a 12-month low of 3.90%, a 0.10pp uptick that snapped the long declining trend. That said, this is a small, single-month move within noise range, and the broader trends remain strongly disinflationary for slack: -0.50pp over 3m, -1.40pp over 6m, -2.40pp over 12m, with the level now sitting essentially at the ~4% natural rate (distance_from_natural ~0.00). The Sahm-gap is trivial at +0.10pp, far below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Policy language confirms the backdrop: "job gains have been solid... unemployment rate has declined substantially," and the Fed is tapering asset purchases in response to labor market improvement — consistent with a market that reached maximum-employment territory rather than one that is deteriorating. Given the level is now at the natural rate after a long tightening run, further mechanical declines are less likely; some stabilization or modest give-back is plausible. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up given the single-month reversal, but conviction is low since one month off a cycle low is not yet a trend change.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.90% would reaffirm the tightening trend; a print above 4.20% would confirm a genuine loosening reversal.
Leaned on: unrate_12m_low, sahm_gap, unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call was directionally correct but modest given magnitude: the 10y has recently pulled back from 1.85 to 1.79 over the last two prints after rising sharply through the year. Level sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.90, only 0.70pp above the 12m low, 0.08pp below the 12m high), and 21d/63d/252d changes are all solidly positive (+0.27, +0.22, +0.68), confirming a sustained uptrend in the long end. The pace of tapering (further reduction in purchases) continues to reduce Fed absorption of duration, a structural support for higher term premium via increased net supply to the private market. The 30s10s slope remains a healthy +0.32pp, showing no flattening/inversion signal that would suggest the uptrend is stalling — my prior falsifier condition (drop below 1.45 with range_position toward 0.5) is nowhere close. The small two-tick pullback (1.85→1.81→1.78→1.79) looks like consolidation near cycle highs rather than a reversal. Given persistent upward momentum, elevated range position, steady slope, and continued balance-sheet runoff pressure, I maintain an up call, though tempered slightly given the recent flattening in the last few observations.
Would change its mind if: A sustained drop below 1.60 with range_position falling toward 0.5 and 30s10s flattening/inverting would signal the uptrend has stalled.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk-sentiment or flight-to-quality flows could explain recent pullback in yields.

arbitrated.notes: The panel points net higher in yield level, but with real dispersion in conviction and some staleness to respect. Inflation is the strongest, freshest, highest-conviction signal: headline CPI and core PCE both accelerating, momentum confirmed across multiple windows, policy language dropping "transitory" — this is a genuine driver of higher nominal yields and I weight it heavily. Term premium corroborates from the rates side: 10y near cycle highs, positive multi-horizon momentum, steady 30s10s slope, and a plausible supply-side story (taper reducing Fed absorption of duration). I note the flagged gap — term_premium wasn't given balance_sheet or financial_conditions — but balance_sheet's own read (still-positive net purchases, just slower) actually reinforces rather than contradicts term_premium's supply logic, and financial_conditions is flat/stale-ish itself, so the gap doesn't invalidate the term_premium call, it just means its magnitude claim on "structural support" should be treated cautiously rather than as confirmed fact. Labor tightness is flat with low conviction and a stale-ish single-month wobble — not enough to lean on either way, so I treat it as a mild non-signal, consistent with an economy near full employment rather than deteriorating (no drag on yields). Financial conditions are genuinely flat/plateaued — no directional push. Balance sheet is a modest, still-QE (not QT) tailwind for continued absorption, which is a mild upward pressure on yields at the margin as accumulation slows, but it's a second-order effect compared to inflation and term premium. Curve slope and inflation expectations are explicitly out of scope for me (curve shape, real/nominal split respectively) though I note the CPI-vs-breakeven divergence: realized inflation running hot while inflation expectations sit flat suggests the real yield component may be doing more work than breakevens — but that's not my call to make. Net: nominal yields lean higher, driven predominantly by realized inflation acceleration and long-end term premium momentum, with labor and financial conditions contributing no meaningful drag.

discounted.why: Flat call, low conviction, single-month reversal within noise — no basis for a level view.
Plateaued series with negligible momentum — no directional signal for yield level.

risks.text: Fed could over-taper into a growth scare, capping long-end yields despite hot inflation prints.
Term premium's supply narrative is unconfirmed by balance_sheet/financial_conditions inputs directly, per flagged gap.
Consolidation near cycle highs in 10y could resolve as a pullback rather than continuation if inflation data softens.

trade.rationale: Net higher-yield view driven by accelerating inflation and firm term premium momentum; weighted more to DGS10 given term premium's direct read, with DGS2 reflecting inflation-driven policy repricing.

why.inflation: Fresh, high-conviction, multi-metric confirmation of accelerating CPI/core PCE; strongest single input to a higher-yield read.
why.term_premium: 10y near cycle highs with positive multi-horizon momentum; tempered slightly for gap vs balance_sheet/financial_conditions inputs.
why.balance_sheet: Still net QE, just slower taper — mild tailwind for yields via shrinking absorption, but second-order vs inflation/term premium.
why.labor_tightness: Flat, low-conviction, single-month wobble near natural rate — no meaningful directional signal, treated as near-neutral.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI plateaued with negligible 4w change — genuinely no directional push to lean on.

Distance from flat is only 0.39pp, meaning the curve is closing in on inversion territory. Policy language confirms the driver: forward guidance shifted from patient/tapering to signaling imminent liftoff ("expects it will soon be appropriate to raise the target range"), which typically pressures the front end (2yr) higher faster than the long end, compressing 2s10s. The 10s30s slope (0.34) is still positive but modest, consistent with long-end anchoring while front-end yields rise on hawkish repricing. There's no evidence of stabilization — the last two readings (0.42, 0.42, 0.39) show a slight uptick then renewed decline, not a basing pattern. My prior falsifier conditions (rebound above 0.70pp or 3+ readings of stabilization) were not met. Given the consistency of the climb across all 13 observations with no reversals, I expect this drift to continue into month-end, likely printing modestly higher again (toward -0.45 to -0.42), unless something abruptly reverses the trend. The 21-day change (+0.22pp) is strong and accelerating relative to the 63-day change (+0.07pp), indicating the move is recent and gaining momentum rather than fading. The 252-day change (+0.47pp) confirms a sustained uptrend over the past year, not just noise. Breakevens now sit at 0.77 on the 12-month range position (near the top of the year's range) and 0.46pp above the 12-month low, with the latest print (2.62) the highest in the series. Distance from the 2% target is now +0.62pp, a meaningful premium that reflects market pricing of above-target inflation over the next decade. With the series making a fresh high on the last observation and short-term momentum outpacing medium-term momentum, the evidence supports continued upward drift into month-end rather than stabilization. I'm revising up from my prior flat stance given the confirmed breakout.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.55 or a negative 21d change would signal the breakout is failing and revert my call toward flat.

Headline 3m annualized (8.25%) sits well above the y/y rate, meaning the trailing three months are running hotter than the year-ago comparison, which mechanically implies further upward pressure on y/y in the near term. Core PCE y/y also continues its steady climb (5.21% → 5.36%), showing the underlying/persistent component of inflation is still broadening and has not rolled over — this reinforces confidence that the headline move is not purely energy/food noise. yoy_range_position at 1.00 confirms we are at a fresh cycle high with no evidence of deceleration in either series. Policy language shift ("well above 2 percent," ending asset purchases, moving toward rate hikes) reflects the Committee's own assessment that inflation is not transitory and is broadening, consistent with the data. Given twelve straight months of higher y/y readings and no momentum evidence of a turn, I maintain a confident up call for next month's headline release.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below ~0.3% combined with core PCE y/y flattening or declining would signal a peak and reverse this call.

The broader trend remains a labor market that has tightened substantially over the past year: -2.30pp over 12 months, -1.20pp over 6 months, and -0.20pp over 3 months, with the unemployment rate now essentially at the natural-rate proxy (distance -0.10pp) and at the bottom of its 12-month range (range position 0.00). The Sahm-gap is negligible (+0.03pp), showing no recession-signal acceleration in the rate — quite the opposite, this is a market still historically tight. There is no evidence here of loosening momentum; job gains have been described as solid and unemployment has declined substantially. Absent a fresh shock, the most likely near-term outcome is another flat-to-slightly-lower print, with the tight labor market persisting rather than reversing. I lean toward flat given how close to the floor this rate already sits, with a slight tilt toward continued tightness rather than any loosening.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.10-4.20% would signal a genuine loosening reversal; continued readings at/below 3.90% reaffirm the tightening trend.

The Fed's continued taper of asset purchases (reducing to $20bn/mo Treasury purchases) is a structural reduction in official-sector demand for long bonds, which should keep upward pressure on term premium over the coming month even if there's short-term noise. The single-observation drop to 1.83 could reflect a flight-to-quality bid or temporary positioning, but without confirmation via low position or slope inversion, I don't yet see this as a trend change. I'm lowering conviction from the prior call given the recent softness, but the tapering backdrop and elevated range position still lean toward higher yields by month-end.

arbitrated.notes: The panel is dominated by a hawkish policy backdrop: inflation is accelerating and broadening (headline CPI 7.94% y/y, core PCE still climbing, no sign of peak), breakevens have broken out to fresh highs, and the Fed's own language has shifted from patient/tapering to signaling imminent liftoff. Labor is at/near cycle-tight levels with no loosening signal. These are the freshest, most consistent, least contradicted reads and they all point the same direction: upward pressure on nominal yields via both the inflation/expectations channel and the policy-reaction channel. Term premium is more equivocal — dgs10 just printed a sharp one-observation pullback (1.97→1.83) even though the medium-term trend (63d/252d) and range position still lean up; conviction there is low (0.25) and flagged as a possible pause. Financial conditions have tightened in trend (NFCI at 52w high) but remain accommodative in absolute level, reinforcing rather than driving the yield-up case. Balance sheet is still expanding (still QE, just tapering), a modest offsetting force keeping duration demand present, but decelerating — not enough to fight the inflation/policy impulse. Curve slope's forecast of front-end-led flattening actually corroborates the story: 2yr should rise faster than 10yr on hawkish repricing, but both legs still move up in absolute level per term_premium's medium-term trend. Netting it out: the level view is up, driven mainly by inflation and inflation-expectations, with labor tightness reinforcing and term-premium/balance-sheet only weak, noisy modifiers. Given the flattening dynamic, I'd size the 2yr leg somewhat larger than 10yr while keeping both same-signed (no curve trade), since front-end repricing to Fed hikes is the most immediate transmission channel, with the 10yr also drifting up per breakevens/term premium medium-term trend.

discounted.why: Curve-shape call, out of scope for a duration/level seat; used only qualitatively to size legs.
Still expanding, direction opposes the dominant hawkish signal; treated as minor offsetting drag only.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI print with m/m below 0.3% and core PCE flattening, combined with NFCI reversing below -0.54 and dgs10 confirming a break below 1.75 with falling range position, would flip this to a lower-yield call.

risks.text: A dovish surprise or pause signal from the Fed could quickly unwind the hawkish repricing driving this call.
Term premium's single-print pullback to 1.83 could be the start of a genuine reversal, not noise.
Breakeven breakout could partly reflect liquidity/premium effects rather than real inflation expectations, per the flagged gap.
Front-end-led flattening means 2yr and 10yr could diverge more than assumed, complicating the level read.

trade.rationale: Inflation, expectations, labor tightness and tightening financial conditions all point to higher yields; front-end weighted heavier given hawkish repricing, but both legs same-signed for a level (not curve) view.

why.inflation: Fresh, unbroken accelerating trend in both headline and core, strongest and least contested signal on the panel.
why.labor_tightness: Near-floor unemployment, no loosening evidence; reinforces tightening/hawkish backdrop, kept modest given flat call and low conviction.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI trend clearly tightening (52w high) even though absolute level still accommodative; supports higher-yield drift.
why.term_premium: Medium-term uptrend (63d/252d, range position) intact but latest print pulled back sharply; downweighted for staleness/noise.
why.balance_sheet: Still net QE, just tapering; modest offsetting dovish force but decelerating, so barely nudges level down.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "up" call was correct: fed_assets rose again to 8,962,474 from 8,954,306, marking the 52-week high, with assets_change_4w (+34,345) and assets_change_13w (+171,979) both positive. The series shows steady week-over-week gains over the last 13 observations, consistent with ongoing net purchases. However, the policy language has now shifted decisively: the old language committing to at least $20B/month Treasury and $10B/month MBS purchases has been struck through (removed), replaced by explicit forward guidance that the Committee "expects to begin reducing its holdings... at a coming meeting." This is a clear signal that the expansion phase is ending and runoff is imminent, even though it hasn't shown up in the measured data yet — balance sheet levels are a lagging confirmation of a policy stance that has already pivoted. Given the mandate to weight recent pace of change but also treat statement language as the "why," I read this as still up for the immediate month-end (purchases likely continue through transition before runoff mechanics start), but with much lower conviction than before since the growth impulse is clearly terminating and could stall or reverse within the horizon.
Would change its mind if: A weekly decline in fed_assets, or statement confirming runoff caps have started, would flip this to down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_52w_high, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55

Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: the spread continued collapsing from 0.11 to 0.04pp over the last three readings, and has now essentially flattened at the zero-bound of its 12-month range (range position 0.00, distance from flat only 0.04pp). The last two readings are identical at 0.04, which could suggest stabilization, but this is far below my stated threshold for a pause (0.55pp) and the trend into this floor was steep (21d change -0.32, 63d -0.75). Policy language has shifted decisively hawkish: liftoff has begun (funds rate now 1/4-1/2%), with explicit guidance for "ongoing increases," and one dissent favoring a larger 50bp hike. This kind of front-loaded tightening cycle historically presses hardest on the 2-year, which should continue to compress or invert the 2s10s spread further as the front end reprices faster than the long end. The 10s30s at 0.12pp offers no offsetting steepening signal. Given the spread is already near zero and hawkish repricing is intensifying, I lean toward continued flattening/inversion risk rather than a bounce, though the two flat prints introduce some uncertainty about the pace.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 0.20pp sustained over 2+ readings, or clear deceleration in the pace of front-end hikes reducing hawkish repricing pressure.

The index sits at the top of its 52-week range (range_position 1.00), meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in a year, even though the absolute level (-0.38) remains well below the zero average, so conditions are still accommodative in absolute terms. My prior "up" call at 0.55 conviction was correct: the trend continued and did not reverse below -0.54, nor did the 4w change flatten toward zero — it actually held steady. Momentum shows no sign of stalling; each of the last several prints has moved higher by similar increments, with no deceleration evident in the last 3 observations (-0.41, -0.39, -0.38). Policy language still describes conditions as accommodative, which is a lagging/backward-looking description and does not contradict the ongoing measured tightening trend. Given the consistency and lack of any reversal signal, I maintain a tightening call for month-end, though I temper conviction slightly since the pace of increase per period is modest and the series remains far from zero, limiting how much further near-term tightening is plausible before mean-reversion pressures build.

Labor tightness confirms: unemployment at cycle lows, still falling, reinforcing a "tight economy, hawkish policy" backdrop — also yield-positive via expected policy tightening, even though its own axis is unemployment falling. Financial conditions have tightened steadily for 13 weeks (NFCI up to top of 52-week range) — this is itself partly a consequence of rising yields/hawkish repricing, so I read it as corroborating rather than independent, though it does argue further tightening is already underway rather than choking off the move. Term premium gives a tepid "up" (0.20) but flags its own staleness concern: the last two 10y prints reversed lower off range highs, and it's missing the balance_sheet and financial_conditions cross-reads. Balance sheet still shows expansion in the data but with clearly terminating policy language (runoff imminent) — the analyst itself downgraded conviction to 0.35 and flagged the growth impulse as ending; I read this as a wash to mildly yield-supportive once runoff begins (less duration absorption support). Netting these: the hot, high-conviction, fresh inflation and labor readings dominate over the softer, partially-reversing term premium and the transitioning balance sheet series. I lean toward higher nominal yields into month-end/next CPI cycle, but temper conviction given term premium's own recent pullback and balance sheet's ambiguous transition.

discounted.why: Own analyst flagged recent reversal and missing cross-reads; too stale/uncertain to anchor the level call.
Analyst itself downgraded conviction; transitioning policy stance makes the signal ambiguous, not directional.

arbitrated.falsifier: A soft CPI print (m/m below ~0.3%) with core PCE flattening, plus unemployment ticking back above 3.9%, would flip this to flat/down.

risks.text: Fed pivot on pace of hikes or runoff timing could quickly reverse the hawkish repricing driving this call.
Term premium's own recent pullback off range highs could presage broader long-end yield stalling.
Financial conditions tightening may itself cap further yield rises if it triggers risk-off flows into duration.

trade.rationale: Hot, fresh inflation and labor data outweigh softer/stale term premium and transitioning balance sheet; net level view is modestly higher yields across the curve, sized down for internal disagreement.

why.inflation: Fresh, high-conviction acceleration in both headline and core with no peak signal; dominant driver for higher yields.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment at fresh cycle low, still falling; corroborates tight-economy/hawkish-policy case for higher yields.
why.financial_conditions: 13-week tightening trend at top of range, but likely a symptom of the same hawkish repricing, not independent force.
why.term_premium: Downgraded per analyst's own recent pullback off range highs and flagged gaps vs balance_sheet/financial_conditions.
why.balance_sheet: Analyst cut conviction sharply; expansion still measured but policy language signals imminent runoff, muting support.

Note runoff hasn't formally started (no caps announced yet), so the pace of decline should be modest initially, but the direction of travel — both in the newest data point and in the stated intent — has flipped from expansion to contraction.

The prior call (up, 0.75) was directionally correct as headline rose from 8.23 in the second-to-last reading... wait, actually the newest print (8.23) is LOWER than the prior (8.57), meaning headline_cpi_yoy already turned down last month, contradicting the up call. However, core PCE yoy is still climbing (5.59, barely off 5.60 high) and core_3m_annualized (5.48%) remains well above target, indicating underlying persistence despite the headline dip — likely energy-driven given the policy language citing "higher energy prices" and geopolitical (invasion) upward pressure. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing is only +0.04pp, so momentum isn't clearly breaking down; one soft month could easily reverse given cited new upward pressures on energy from the war shock. Given core is still rising and policy language explicitly flags fresh upward pressure from an energy/geopolitical shock, I expect headline to resume upward or at minimum not sustain the decline. But the recent single-month deceleration and range position near top-of-range warrants tempering conviction versus last time.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive headline m/m prints below 0.4% alongside core PCE yoy turning down would confirm a genuine peak and reversal.

The Sahm gap is a negligible +0.07pp, well below any recession-signal threshold, and the range position is at 0 (at the low), indicating no loosening impulse at all. Policy language reinforces this: "unemployment rate has declined substantially" and job gains "strong," with the labor market expected to "remain strong." There is no evidence of turning upward — no rise off the low, no acceleration in softening. At the same time, with unemployment already 0.3pp below my 4% natural-rate anchor and momentum flattening, further declines look limited; a plateau near current levels is the most probable outcome. I don't see strong evidence for either a fresh leg down or an upturn — the data argue for stability at a tight level.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.80-3.90% would indicate the flat plateau is breaking into a genuine upward/loosening turn.

My prior "up" call at 0.20 conviction was directionally correct and, if anything, understated the persistence of the move. The 30s10s slope has steepened modestly to +0.07pp, consistent with continued long-end pressure rather than curve flattening that would signal stalling. Policy language has shifted toward acknowledging balance-sheet runoff ("reducing its holdings of Treasury securities... at a coming meeting"), which historically adds term premium pressure at the long end via reduced official-sector demand. With yields sitting at/near 12-month highs, some mean-reversion risk exists, but nothing in the measurements (range position near 1.0, positive slope, strong multi-horizon momentum) supports calling a top yet. I lean toward continued upward pressure into month-end, though conviction is moderate given how stretched the level already is relative to the 12-month range, which raises reversal risk.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65

  The last print fell sharply to 8,914,281 from 8,945,898, a drop of ~31,617 in one observation and confirming the down call from last time. The 4-week change is now -24,918 and 13-week change -13,848, both negative, with the series clearly rolling off its 52w high (now -51,206 below it). The recent path was choppy through the middle of the series (oscillating in the 8.94-8.96mm range) but the last two prints show a decisive downward break, consistent with runoff finally biting. Critically, the policy language has flipped from merely "expects to begin reducing holdings" to "decided to begin reducing its holdings... on [date] as described in the Plans for Reducing the Size of the Balance Sheet" — i.e., QT has been formally launched with an announced caps-based runoff plan. This is a strong confirmation that the balance sheet is now in active, mechanical runoff mode rather than passive drift. Given the announced monthly caps framework, the pace of decline should persist or even accelerate toward the caps in the coming month. I raise conviction that this is a genuine regime shift into QT rather than noise.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above the 52w-high, or reversion to reinvestment/purchase language, would flip this to up.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has risen to +0.32pp, the highest print in the series and above the +0.25pp threshold I set previously for confirming steepening resumption, with the last three readings (0.27, 0.29, 0.27, 0.32) holding above 0.25 for four consecutive observations. My prior "flat" call was too cautious in hindsight — the 21-day change is now +0.13pp, a clear positive momentum signal, even though the 63-day (-0.09pp) and 252-day (-1.15pp) changes still show the spread well off its highs and in a longer flattening trend. The spread sits at only 0.24 of its 12-month range (low -0.05, high +1.47), so this remains a historically compressed/inverted-adjacent curve, but the short-term direction is unambiguously steepening. Policy language shows a shift from a 25bp hike with a dissent favoring 50bp to a larger 75bp-equivalent step (3/4 to 1%), consistent with front-end rates catching up and would typically pressure the curve flatter — yet the realized spread has widened, suggesting long-end yields are also moving up or the market is pricing terminal rate proximity. Given the confirmed break above my threshold, I'm shifting to a modest steepening call for month-end, though conviction is tempered by the still-negative 63d/252d trend.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.25pp for 2+ readings, or resumption of the 63d/252d flattening trend, would reverse this call.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, steady uptrend over the past 13 weeks, moving from -0.44 to -0.28 — a +0.19 move over 13 weeks and +0.07 over the most recent 4 weeks. This is consistent, monotonic tightening with no reversals in the last several prints (each newest observation is higher than or equal to the prior one). The index sits at its 52-week high and at range_position 1.00, meaning conditions are the tightest they've been in the observed window, though still notably below the zero average (distance_from_zero -0.28), so conditions remain looser than historical norm in absolute terms. My prior "flat" call at 0.30 conviction was wrong — the 4w change of +0.07 was already above my own stated threshold of +0.03-0.05 for confirming tightening resumed, and I should have caught that. The momentum here is unambiguous and persistent, not noisy: every single one of the last 9 readings has ticked up or held flat, no down-ticks. That is a strong, low-ambiguity trend. Absent any new information reversing it, the most probable near-term path is continuation of this gradual tightening drift toward zero.
Would change its mind if: A 4w change turning negative or a drop back toward the 13w avg (-0.36) would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.

Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three prints (2.62, 2.63, 2.64) show a modest but consistent uptick after the sharp 21-day decline to the 2.55-2.56 trough. My prior "flat" call was reasonable given the choppy path, but the most recent three observations show a small, steady grind higher that deserves acknowledgment — not a reversal call, but a mild upward lean rather than pure flat. The 21-day change (-0.24pp) still reflects the earlier drop from 2.71-2.72, but the 63-day change is now essentially flat-to-positive (+0.02pp) and the 252-day change is clearly positive (+0.22pp), suggesting the longer-trend drift is up even as the short-term move was volatile. Breakeven sits mid-range (0.53 range position) between 12-month low (2.22) and high (3.02), and remains 0.64pp above the 2% target — a level that has now persisted with the recent inching-up rather than falling back toward the range low. With three consecutive higher closes and 63d/252d trends turning positive, I lean toward a modest continuation higher over the next month, though the magnitude of recent moves is small and could stall.

Call: up, conviction 0.55

Status: formed 16 days ago.

  The prior "up" call played out: headline CPI y/y rose from 8.23% to 8.54%, driven by a hot 0.95% m/m print. That confirms the near-term acceleration I flagged, though the trajectory is not clean — mom prints have been volatile (0.31% then 0.95%), so the falsifier condition (two consecutive sub-0.4% prints) was clearly not met. Momentum context: headline 3m annualized is running at 9.91%, well above the 12-month trend, and headline y/y is at range position 0.99 — essentially the cycle high. Core PCE y/y, my preferred read on persistence, ticked down slightly (5.60→5.59→5.35), a hint of deceleration in the 3-month change (-0.01pp) but still far above target (+3.35pp) and not a clean turn given only one month's pullback. Policy language reinforces the acceleration narrative: the statement now asserts (not hedges) that the invasion is "creating additional upward pressure on inflation," and adds "highly attentive to inflation risks" — language tightening, not softening. Given the outgoing 0.95% m/m print, headline y/y is very likely to rise again next month absent a sharp deceleration in monthly prints, even as core shows tentative early softening. I weight headline's own momentum here since the horizon is the headline print itself.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive headline m/m prints below 0.4% alongside core PCE yoy turning down would confirm a genuine peak and reversal.

Given the rate is already at cycle lows with no sign of momentum reversal, and the small recent moves are still negative (tightening), I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-tightening conditions, but with limited room to fall further given proximity to structural lows, so I don't expect a sharp additional decline either.

arbitrated.notes: The level call for nominal yields is up, but with moderate rather than high conviction. Five drivers relevant to my mandate (curve_slope and inflation_expectations are out of scope — real/curve seats) point mostly the same direction: term_premium (up, 0.50, fresh, backed by dgs10 level/63d/252d trend and confirmed QT onset), balance_sheet (down, 0.65, fresh, decisive break lower in assets with formal QT language), financial_conditions (up, 0.65, fresh, clean monotonic NFCI tightening at 52w high), inflation (up, 0.55, 16 days old but corroborated by fresh policy language and still-hot headline momentum), and labor_tightness (flat, 0.35, slightly stale, unemployment ticked down to cycle lows but with limited room to fall further). Balance sheet running off is itself a term-premium/duration-supply story that reinforces the term_premium call rather than opposing it — less Fed buying of duration is a yield-up force even though the "driver" convention here signals down on the stock. Financial conditions tightening and inflation still running hot both push nominal yields higher via risk premium and expected-policy-response channels. Labor tightness at cycle lows offers no counter-signal — it's flat, not loosening, so no offsetting rate-cut expectation buildup. The panel is genuinely converged on the up side across financial conditions, term premium, inflation, and effectively balance sheet (via duration supply), with only labor market conditions neutral. This gives me a real, non-thin level signal, not a split panel. I size the trade meaningfully but not maximally given inflation's staleness and labor's flatness tempering full conviction, and keep it as a pure duration-up (yields rise, prices fall) position across both tenors, weighted a bit more to the long end where term premium and balance-sheet supply effects concentrate.

arbitrated.notes:
Reconciling for the Duration/level seat only (nominal Treasury yields), excluding curve_slope which is out of scope for me. The panel is genuinely split. Pulling yields higher: inflation (headline CPI accelerating sharply, up 0.55) and financial_conditions (NFCI tightening monotonically and accelerating, up 0.68, the freshest and cleanest trend in the set). Pulling yields lower: inflation_expectations (breakeven_10y falling hard and fresh, down 0.55, directly contradicting the headline CPI call) and term_premium (topping/reversing after a strong run, flat-to-soft, 0.30). Labor_tightness is genuinely flat/pinned (0.45 but signed flat) and balance_sheet is a weak, hedged flat-to-up call (0.30) sitting inside a still-active QT regime — I don't let it drive level on its own per the staleness/one-driver rule.

The key tension is inflation (realized, up) vs inflation_expectations (market-priced, down) — a classic realized-vs-expected divergence. The panel flags this gap explicitly: inflation lacks the expectations read and vice versa. Market-based breakevens are forward-looking and already falling sharply and freshly, which is a more relevant signal for where yields go over the next month than a lagged CPI print already priced in. Financial conditions tightening (a real, accelerating, high-conviction trend) argues for higher yields through the risk-premium/tightening channel, but term premium's own reversal and the NFCI's absolute level still being loose complicates a clean "yields up" call.

Net: modest, low-conviction tilt toward yields drifting lower to flat, driven by the fresh breakeven collapse and term premium's reversal, only partially offset by tightening financial conditions and firm headline inflation. This is a weak-signal environment — not a place for a large duration bet.

Weighing the sharp deceleration in the latest month against still-elevated y/y levels and a core print that ticked up, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for next month: the disinflationary impulse from this release is real but core stickiness and one soft month don't yet confirm a durable trend reversal.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

Report:

The 4-week change is -38,568M and the 13-week change is -62,845M, both negative, confirming ongoing runoff. The 52-week change remains positive (+518,693M) but that reflects the earlier QE/reinvestment period and is far less relevant than the recent trend per mandate. Assets sit 114,051M below the 52-week high, and the 13-week average (8,895,808.77) is above the latest print, showing the recent observations are consistently below trend — consistent with steady balance-sheet contraction. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per the announced caps, with no hint of tapering or pausing runoff. The latest single-week uptick (8849762→8851436) is tiny and well within normal noise around auction/settlement timing, not a reversal. Absent any language change signaling a slowdown, the trajectory should continue lower into next month-end.
Would change its mind if: A sustained multi-week rise in fed_assets alongside explicit language pausing or slowing the reduction plan would flip this to expansion.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_13w_avg

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/flattening) played out over the medium term: slope_change_63d is -0.58pp and slope_change_252d is -1.39pp, confirming the multi-month flattening trend, and the curve remains deeply inverted at -0.30pp, sitting near the bottom of its 12-month range (slope_range_position 0.10, only +0.18pp above the 12-month low of -0.48pp). However, the most recent short-term signal has turned: slope_change_21d is now +0.01pp, essentially flat, and the last two prints (-0.35, -0.30) show a small bounce off the recent trough rather than continued deterioration. This is not the sustained re-steepening move above -0.10/0.00pp I set as my falsifier, so I don't think a trend reversal is confirmed yet. The continued Fed tightening language (rate hikes "anticipated to be appropriate," moving to 2.25-2.5%) keeps front-end pressure alive, which argues against a strong steepening move at month end. On balance, the flattening trend has stalled but not reversed — the near-term change is negligible and the level remains deeply inverted near range lows. I'll shift from a confident "down" to a much weaker lean, since the 21d momentum no longer supports further flattening, but nothing signals genuine steepening either.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move back above ~-0.10 to 0.00pp for 2+ consecutive readings, with 21d change turning clearly positive, would indicate re-steepening.

Given the flattening at -0.24 for two consecutive weeks, I lean toward continued mild easing or stabilization near this level rather than a sharp move either way. The signal is not strong enough for high conviction, but the recent 4w trend and lack of any tightening catalyst support a modest continuation of easing or flat conditions.

Policy language shift (adding "food" to energy price drivers) suggests broadening price pressure concern, a mild hawkish nuance, but not decisive for near-term print direction. Given base effects dominate next month's y/y print and the trend, however uneven, is toward deceleration, I lean down again, though with the mom_gap flattening I temper conviction versus last time.

arbitrated.notes:
Synthesizing across drivers relevant to the nominal level (excluding curve_slope, which is off-limits as a shape call): the picture is mixed but tilts toward stable-to-modestly-lower yields with an important offsetting force from term premium/long-end supply dynamics.

Inflation and inflation expectations both lean down, with decent freshness — CPI call is 16 days old but still within horizon, and breakevens (formed this meeting) show a clean four-of-five-reading decline with breaking momentum. These two agree directionally and are mutually reinforcing on the disinflation narrative, which argues for downward pressure on nominal yields via the inflation compensation channel. Labor tightness is flat/plateaued, offering no directional push either way — a genuine non-signal given six readings in a narrow band. Financial conditions lean down (easing) but conviction is low and the easing pace is described as decelerating/flattening, so this is a weak, decaying signal rather than a trend driver. Balance sheet is the freshest, most mechanically clear driver: QT continues on autopilot with no policy language change, which is a structural, non-cyclical upward pressure on term premium/long-end yields via reduced official-sector demand — this is corroborated independently by term_premium's own reading (dgs10 near 12-month highs, positive 21d/63d momentum, QT cited as a structural support).

Term premium is the standout dissenting voice: it flipped from down to up this meeting with high conviction and multiple corroborating windows (21d, 63d, range position), and its causal logic (QT-driven duration supply) is consistent with the balance_sheet call rather than contradicting it. Given two independently-sourced, fresh, high-conviction readings (term_premium and balance_sheet) both point to upward pressure on nominal yields via the long end, while the disinflation drivers argue for a more moderate downward pull, I weight the level view toward a mild upward tilt, tempered by the disinflation drag. This is a net small positive lean on yields, low-to-moderate conviction given the split panel.

Panel is genuinely split between disinflation (down) and term-premium/QT (up) drivers, weakening conviction in either direction.

why.balance_sheet: Continued QT runoff, unchanged policy language, corroborates term_premium's upward long-end pressure via reduced demand.

why.labor_tightness: Plateaued in narrow band for six readings; genuine non-signal, no directional lean on yields.

arbitrated.notes:
The level call here is dominated by term_premium and financial_conditions, both fresh, both pointing the same direction: nominal yields higher. Term_premium shows DGS10 grinding to a 12-month-range high (0.95 position) with strong 21d/63d/252d momentum, and financial_conditions confirms a genuine, accelerating tightening impulse (NFCI at 52-week high, 4w change outpacing 13w change) — a fresh cross-read that corroborates rather than contradicts the long-end rise, and answers the gap term_premium flagged about "idiosyncratic vs systemic": this is systemic tightening, not a duration-only quirk. Balance_sheet's modest, steady runoff (down conviction 0.6) reinforces the term_premium supply story and fills the other named gap — ongoing caps still work against duration, adding a modest steepening-of-term-premium bias rather than reversing it.

Against this, inflation and inflation_expectations pull toward lower yields, but they are split: breakeven-based inflation_expectations shows a sharp, fresh acceleration in disinflation (fresh 12m lows, accelerating 21d decline), while realized inflation (formed 15 days ago, staler) argues underlying core is NOT falling — core PCE reaccelerating, wide core-headline gap. This is an internal disagreement on the inflation axis itself, which weakens its pull on the level call; I don't fully trust the breakeven decline as clean disinflation signal given the flagged gap (breakevens could be reflecting technical/liquidity distortion given financial_conditions tightening, not real inflation view). Labor_tightness is flat/plateaued, offering no directional push either way and is moderately stale.

Netting it out: two fresh, corroborating drivers (term_premium, financial_conditions) pushing yields up, with balance_sheet reinforcing on the supply side, against a mixed and partially self-contradicting inflation signal and a neutral labor read. I lean modestly bullish-yields (i.e., yields up), sized down from full conviction given the inflation-axis split and the flagged data gaps.

Curve-shape view, outside this seat's mandate; not a level signal.

risks.text: A dovish Fed pivot or pause signal could rapidly reverse both term premium and financial conditions tightening.
Breakeven collapse toward 2% could presage real disinflation that eventually caps nominal yields despite current term premium momentum.
NFCI tightening could itself trigger risk-off flows into Treasuries, pushing yields down against this call.
10Y near 12-month highs invites mean-reversion/consolidation as term_premium analyst itself noted.

trade.rationale: Both legs same-sign short (yields up) reflecting a level call; weight tilted to DGS10 since term_premium/financial_conditions evidence is long-end-centric.

why.financial_conditions: Fresh reversal to tightening, corroborates term_premium's long-end rise as systemic not idiosyncratic.
why.balance_sheet: Steady runoff reinforces supply-driven upward pressure on long-end term premium; modest, not accelerating.
why.inflation: Core reacceleration argues against clean disinflation but call is stale (15d) and low conviction; small residual downward pull discounted.
why.labor_tightness: Plateaued at 12m low with no directional signal; no basis for a level view.

Curve slope is out of scope for a level call and its own conviction is thin (0.35, flattening) — a curve-shape read shouldn't be used to infer level direction since flattening can happen with yields up or down.

Netting these: balance-sheet runoff continuing, financial conditions tightening, term premium already elevated with positive momentum, and both inflation gauges leaning up together produce a coherent, multi-driver case for a modest but real rise in nominal yield levels over the next month. None of these reads are stale — all are current or near-current (16-23 days for inflation/labor, same-meeting for the rest) — so I'm not discounting for staleness, but conviction is moderate given several individual drivers sit at only 0.35-0.45 and labor gives no confirming push. I lean into a modest up call across the curve, sized down from any single analyst's number to reflect the moderate conviction levels and the reliance on inferred cross-driver support rather than a single dominant, high-conviction read.

discounted.why: Curve-shape signal out of scope for a level call; flattening is directionally ambiguous for yield levels.
Flat/no-trend read adds no directional information to the level call.

arbitrated.falsifier: A 10y print back toward 3.80-3.90 with range_position under 0.70, paired with balance-sheet runoff pausing or NFCI reversing back below -0.15, would flip this to neutral/down.

risks.text: A dovish pivot or pause in runoff/hikes would remove the two structural supports (balance sheet, financial conditions) behind this call.
10y yield already near 12-month highs (range_position 0.95); further upside may be limited without fresh catalysts.
Several drivers sit at only moderate conviction (0.3-0.45); a single surprise print could flip the net read.

trade.rationale: Multi-driver case (balance sheet runoff, tightening financial conditions, firm inflation/breakevens, elevated term premium) supports a modest broad-based rise in nominal yields; weighted toward the long end where term premium and supply pressure are most direct.

why.term_premium: 10y near cycle highs with positive momentum; corroborated by balance-sheet runoff and tightening financial conditions it lacked as inputs.
why.balance_sheet: Runoff continuing steadily per own high-conviction call; feeds upward pressure on long-end supply/term premium, not a standalone level driver.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI ticking up, top of 52w range; moderate but consistent tightening supports higher yields.
why.inflation: Core PCE reaccelerating despite headline base effects; leans yields up but conviction modest and horizon distinct.
why.labor_tightness: Genuinely flat/no-trend per own report; contributes no directional push either way.

The policy language shows the Committee stepping down the pace of hikes rhetorically (referencing lags and cumulative tightening, considering pace of future increases) even while still hiking, which historically is consistent with a stabilizing-to-mildly-steepening front-end/back-end dynamic as terminal rate expectations firm up. Given the tiny two-observation bounce lacks confirmation and the broader trend remains firmly flattening with the spread pinned near its 12-month low, I don't see strong evidence for a reversal yet, but the deceleration signal in policy language plus the modest bounce tempers a strong "down" call. Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
