The latest print (2.34) is a bounce off two prints at 2.25-2.26, but the broader trend is negative: be_change_21d (-0.17pp), be_change_63d (-0.24pp), and be_change_252d (-0.21pp) all point down over multiple windows, showing sustained cooling in market-implied inflation expectations rather than noise. The series has oscillated in a fairly narrow band (2.25-2.34 in the last ten prints, 12-month range 2.15-3.02) with be_range_position at just 0.22 - solidly in the lower quartile of the annual range. That said, the last single print did tick up 0.09pp from the prior observation, which tempers a purely bearish read. Distance from the 2% target remains positive at +0.34pp, so expectations are still anchored above objective, but the trajectory over the past year and past quarter is clearly one of receding breakevens, not renewed inflation concern. Given the multi-horizon negative changes dominate a single-point uptick, I lean toward continued softening or at best sideways consolidation near current lower-range levels rather than a resumption of the up move I previously expected.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 2.40 over multiple consecutive prints would signal renewed upward momentum rather than range-bound consolidation.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  My previous "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong: headline y/y fell from 7.76 to 7.12, a sizable drop, continuing the decline from the 8.98 peak. I should have weighted the falling base-effect trajectory more heavily. Looking at the current data, headline y/y has now declined for several consecutive months and sits at the 12-month low, with headline_yoy_change_3m at -1.10pp — clear deceleration. The latest m/m print of 0.26% is below the 3m average of 0.42% and well below the outgoing 0.69%, suggesting continued softening momentum rather than reacceleration. However, core PCE tells a more mixed story: it ticked up to 5.46 from 5.11 two months prior, and core_yoy_change_3m is +0.35pp, indicating underlying persistence has not broken down despite headline's favorable base effects (energy/food likely driving the divergence, given core_minus_headline of -1.66pp is unusual — core running above headline). Given headline is the metric being asked about, and both the y/y trend and the low recent m/m print point the same direction, I lean toward continued headline deceleration next month, though the core stickiness tempers my confidence that this becomes a durable trend break.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.6% or a re-acceleration in core PCE y/y above 5.6% would signal the disinflation trend is stalling or reversing.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m

Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate has been flat at 3.5-3.6% for the last several prints (3.50, 3.60, 3.50, 3.60, 3.60), with 3m and 6m changes both at 0.00pp. This confirms the prior flat call was correct: no genuine loosening turn has materialized. The rate sits 0.4pp below the 4% natural-rate anchor, indicating a still-tight labor market by level. The 12m change of -0.50pp reflects the earlier decline from 4.0-4.1% into this plateau, but that move is now stale relative to the last several flat months. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is a negligible +0.07pp, well below any threshold that would signal an accelerating loosening trend. Range position of 0.20 shows the rate sits near the low end of its 12-month range, consistent with tightness rather than loosening. Policy language reinforces this: job gains described as robust and unemployment as low, with no acknowledgment of labor market deterioration. With no directional momentum in either direction over three or six months, and the level still comfortably below the natural rate, the most defensible call is continued flatness. I see no evidence pointing to an imminent uptick or further decline.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.20
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior "up" call (0.35 conviction) was contingent on avoiding: a pullback toward 3.80-3.90 with negative 21d change and range_position under 0.70. That is roughly what happened — the level fell from a 3.83 peak to 3.68, the 21d change is now -0.34pp, and range_position sits at 0.80 (still elevated but off the highs). So the prior call was wrong in the near term; the long end eased rather than continuing to rise. That said, the picture is mixed for the month-ahead: the 63d and 252d changes remain strongly positive (+0.56pp, +2.20pp), meaning the level is still historically high and near the top of its 12-month range (0.80), and balance-sheet runoff continues to exert upward pressure on term premium structurally. The 30s10s slope is modestly positive (+0.12pp), consistent with normal term-premium compensation rather than stress. The most recent print (3.68) is a sharp one-observation drop from 3.75, which could be noise or the start of a further retracement. Given the tension between recent short-term softening and the still-elevated range position plus ongoing balance-sheet runoff, I lean toward a modest further pause/drift rather than a strong directional call.
Would change its mind if: A break below 3.60 with range_position under 0.65 and continued negative 21d change would confirm a genuine down move; a rebound above 3.80 would reaffirm the up call.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, slope_30s10s

=== balance_sheet ===

The 52-week change (-206,291) confirms this is a sustained trend, not noise, and assets are now -414,318 below the 52-week high. The policy language remains unchanged, explicitly confirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS/debt holdings per the announced runoff plan — no language of pausing or slowing. My prior call (down, 0.75) was correct: assets declined further as expected. There is no evidence of any deceleration in pace — the 4-week pace (~-33.4k) is roughly consistent with recent monthly runoff caps, and the 13-week trend is steady and monotonic downward with only two minor upticks in the 13-observation series. With unchanged policy language reaffirming continued runoff, the base case for month-end is a further decline in total assets of similar magnitude to recent weeks. I maintain a confident down call, though conviction is not raised further since the pace itself hasn't been accelerating.

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print pulled back to -0.53pp from -0.43pp two readings prior, breaking a string that had touched -0.43pp. Over the last 10 observations the spread moved from -0.69 to -0.53, a net steepening of ~0.16pp, but the most recent two readings (-0.51, -0.53) show a modest re-flattening after the local high. The 21d change is still positive (+0.17pp), consistent with steepening over the past month, but the 63d change is negative (-0.18pp) and the 252d change is sharply negative (-1.28pp), showing the dominant trend over the past year has been flattening/inversion. The spread sits at 0.18 on the 12-month range (near the inverted end), still -0.53pp from flat, well within the 12m band of -0.84 to +0.89. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate — the spread has oscillated in a narrow -0.43 to -0.57 band for the last six readings, not sustaining a clear breakout above -0.55 as my falsifier required, though it briefly poked above that. The oscillation without a decisive breakout suggests continued near-term consolidation rather than a strong directional move. The 25bp hike continuing the tightening cycle keeps front-end anchored, limiting steepening room. I lean toward flat with a slight downward tilt given the two most recent prints reversing off the local high.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move below -0.60pp for 2+ consecutive readings with 21d change turning clearly negative would confirm renewed flattening; a clean break above -0.45pp would confirm steepening.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.50
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady grind lower: -0.10 → -0.20 over 13 weeks, with the latest print (-0.20) making a fresh low for the window. The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.08) are both negative, confirming continued easing momentum rather than stalling. My prior "down" call was correct — the falsifier (print back above -0.14 or 4w change turning positive) did not trigger; instead conditions kept loosening. The index sits at -0.20, well below the zero average, and at 0.77 range position (77% of the way from the 52-week low toward the high, i.e., closer to the loose end historically for this window), reinforcing that financial conditions remain accommodative and are still loosening at the margin, not just historically loose. There is no sign of deceleration in the last few prints (-0.18, -0.18, -0.19, -0.19, -0.20) — the pace is modest but consistently negative. Absent any policy language shift signaling renewed tightening intent, the trend favors continued mild easing into month end. I maintain a "down" call, with slightly higher conviction than before given the consistent confirmation of the trend and lack of any reversal signal.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.17 or 4w change turning positive/flat would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "flat" call holds up reasonably well: breakeven_10y has moved from 2.27 to 2.30 over the last few prints, staying within the recent range and well short of the 2.40 threshold I set as a falsifier for renewed upward momentum. Looking at the full series, the last three readings (2.26, 2.28, 2.27, 2.30) show a mild grind higher after a dip to 2.19-2.20 mid-series, but the 21d and 63d changes are both slightly negative (-0.04, -0.03 pp), indicating the very recent trend is flat-to-softening even though the latest single print ticked up. The 252d change of -0.20 pp confirms the broader trajectory has been gently disinflationary in market pricing over the past year, and the range_position of 0.19 (near the low end of the 12-month band) suggests expectations remain closer to the low than the high. Distance from the 2% target is +0.30 pp, a modest but not alarming premium. With no consecutive multi-print break above 2.40, and short-term derived changes negative despite the latest tick, I don't see enough evidence for a strong directional shift. This looks like continued range-bound consolidation rather than a new trend, so I maintain a flat call but with slightly higher conviction given the negative 21d/63d changes reinforcing the range-bound read.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above 2.40 or below 2.10 over multiple consecutive prints would signal a genuine breakout from the current range.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target, breakeven_10y

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct and the newest data confirm continued disinflation. Headline CPI y/y fell sharply to 6.40% from 7.12%, a full percentage point drop, and the outgoing m/m print was just 0.02%, the softest in the 13-month window. The 3-month annualized headline pace is now 3.39%, well below the y/y rate, signaling the trend is decelerating, not just base effects. My falsifier (m/m above 0.6% or core PCE y/y above 5.6%) was not triggered — m/m came in near zero and core PCE y/y sits at 5.21%, below its recent peak of 5.61%. Core PCE remains sticky in the 5.1-5.6% band with no clear downtrend, still 3.2pp above target, so persistence risk remains — but I weight headline more here since the question is specifically about headline CPI y/y, and its momentum (3m annualized, falling mom_gap) points firmly downward. The softened policy language ("contributing to" vs "creating additional") also suggests officials see inflation pressure easing modestly. With headline already down five consecutive months from its peak and the freshest print near flat, I expect the next release to continue lower, though the sticky core keeps me from very high conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5% or core PCE y/y jumping back above 5.6% would signal disinflation stalling.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

  The latest print (3.50%) matches the 12-month low and confirms the previous "flat" call was correct: the unemployment rate has been oscillating in a narrow 3.50-3.60% band for the last several months with no directional break. The 3m change is exactly 0.00pp, the 6m change is a negligible -0.10pp, and the Sahm-rule gap is a trivial +0.07pp, far below the ~0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn. The rate sits 0.50pp below the assumed natural rate of 4%, indicating a still-tight labor market, and the 12-month trend of -0.40pp reflects earlier tightening now fully plateaued rather than any fresh loosening impulse. Policy language reinforces this picture, describing job gains as robust and the unemployment rate as remaining low, consistent with a stable, tight labor market rather than one deteriorating. There is no evidence of acceleration in either direction — the range position sits at the very bottom of its 12-month band (0.0), meaning the market is if anything at its tightest point recently, not loosening. Absent a break above 3.70% or below 3.30%, I see no basis for a directional call beyond continuation of the current plateau.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.70% would confirm a genuine loosening turn; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening beyond this plateau.

Call: up, conviction 0.55

The 21d (+0.20pp) and 63d (+0.16pp) changes are both positive, and the 252d change is a large +2.39pp, showing sustained upward momentum over the past year. Range position sits at 0.86, near the top of the 12-month range, indicating the yield is pressing toward cycle highs rather than mean-reverting. The 10s30s slope remains modestly positive (+0.09pp), consistent with continued steepening pressure at the long end rather than curve inversion pulling long yields down. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff (continued Treasury/MBS reduction) is a structural tailwind for term premium, reducing price-insensitive demand and requiring the market to absorb more duration — consistent with the upward drift observed. There is no flight-to-quality signal evident in the data. Given the confirmed break above 3.80, elevated range position, and structural runoff backdrop, I am revising toward a clear up call for month-end, correcting my previous underweighting of the recent uptrend.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 3.75 with range_position falling under 0.70 and negative 21d change would signal the up move has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Duration is a level call, so I aggregate across the drivers actually on my axis: inflation, labor tightness, financial conditions, balance sheet, and term premium (curve_slope is explicitly off-limits to me). The strongest, freshest signal is term_premium: 10Y at 3.88%, range position 0.86, positive 21d/63d/252d momentum, and a plausible structural link to ongoing balance-sheet runoff reducing price-insensitive demand for duration. That runoff link is corroborated directly by balance_sheet's own high-conviction call — assets still falling steadily, policy language unchanged, no deceleration — so the two drivers reinforce each other rather than one depending blindly on an unconfirmed input. That gap is now closed by cross-reading: term_premium's missing balance_sheet input is supplied and confirms upward pressure on yields via reduced Fed absorption of duration.

Working against this: inflation is falling clearly (headline CPI decelerating sharply, though core PCE still sticky), and financial conditions continue to ease (NFCI grinding to fresh loose lows), both of which are typically consistent with lower, not higher, yields on a multi-factor view. Labor tightness is genuinely flat/plateaued, offering no directional pull either way. inflation_expectations (breakevens) is also flat/range-bound, reducing the odds that inflation is the marginal driver of nominal yields right now.

So the panel is split: a live, corroborated upward pressure from term premium/balance-sheet supply dynamics versus a live, if modestly-easing, disinflation and financial-conditions signal pulling the other way. Given the term-premium move is the most proximate driver of nominal Treasury yields specifically, and it's freshly formed and cross-confirmed by balance-sheet runoff, I lean modestly toward higher yields, but size down given the genuine two-sided disagreement from inflation and financial conditions.

discounted.why: Flat plateau, no directional signal for yield level.
Out of scope for a duration-level call; shape belongs to another pod.

arbitrated.falsifier: A 10Y drop back below 3.75 with falling range position, or a re-acceleration in CPI/NFCI reversing toward tightening, would flip this toward flat-to-lower yields.

risks.text: A dovish pivot in Fed language could stall term-premium rise and reinforce the disinflation/easing case for lower yields.
Balance-sheet runoff pace could decelerate, removing the structural supply tailwind supporting the up call.
Term premium's own range position near cycle highs raises mean-reversion risk if momentum stalls.

trade.rationale: Modest net long-duration-yield (short bonds) tilt weighted toward the long end where term premium/supply pressure is most direct, sized down given inflation and financial-conditions pulling the other way.

why.term_premium: 10Y at cycle-high range position, positive 21d/63d/252d momentum, cross-confirmed by balance-sheet runoff closing its own input gap.
why.balance_sheet: Runoff continuing steadily reduces Fed duration absorption — a yield-raising force via term premium channel, opposite sign to headline 'assets falling'.
why.inflation: Headline CPI decelerating sharply on fresh data; pulls against higher yields even though core PCE stays sticky.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI at fresh multi-week lows, steady easing trend, modestly supportive of lower yields, no reversal signal.
why.labor_tightness: Plateaued in a narrow band with no directional break; no basis for a level view from this driver.

arbitrated.notes:
The level call here is more coherent than the panel's individual conviction levels might suggest, because several drivers point the same direction while term_premium — the one that most directly proxies the long-end level — is itself flat with low conviction and stale relative to today's inflation/financial-conditions readings. Financial conditions are easing sharply (NFCI at 13-week lows, accelerating), balance-sheet runoff continues unabated (a structural drain that should, if anything, add term premium pressure but is being outweighed in the data by falling yields), inflation expectations are breaking down (breakevens down 0.09 in two prints, accelerating), and headline/core inflation continues to decelerate in y/y terms despite a single hot MoM print. Labor tightness is genuinely flat with high conviction and low volatility, offering no directional push either way. Curve slope's own call (down, low conviction) is a slope signal, not directly usable for a level view but its context (Fed holding front-end high while long end doesn't chase) is consistent with a bull-flattening backdrop, which nets out as lower yields overall given the long end is the softer side.

The critical read is term_premium's own report: DGS10 has fallen over the last 21d and 63d, consolidating below recent highs, even as balance-sheet runoff (a term-premium positive) continues. That means the actual price action in 10y yields is already net negative despite the structural QT headwind — the market is telling us the disinflation/easing-financial-conditions story is currently dominating the QT supply story. Given financial conditions and inflation expectations are both live, accelerating, and pointing the same way, while term_premium's own analyst undersells the move by calling flat, I override modestly toward down. Labor tightness truly has no signal to contribute either way. Net: a modest but multi-driver-corroborated downward drift in yields into month-end, not a sharp move.

Policy language dropped the explicit "public health" reference from the list of factors the Committee monitors, a subtle simplification that doesn't change the inflation-expectations framing itself but suggests reduced emphasis on pandemic-era disinflationary/deflationary risk factors, consistent with modestly firming expectations. Taken together, the short-term momentum is clearly up, breakeven is at a local high, and there's no offsetting signal in the recent data. I weight core PCE for persistence, and it too is grinding lower, just more slowly than headline. The policy language shift — from "elevated" to "eased somewhat but remains elevated" — corroborates the disinflation narrative without declaring victory. Base effects (the prior year's high comparisons rolling off) plus a modest recent m/m of 0.34% suggest headline y/y should keep drifting down next month, absent a shock. Distance from target remains large (core ~2.94pp above 2%), so this is deceleration, not disinflation danger — the direction is still down but the pace of decline may slow given the still-firm 3m annualized core figure.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.0% would signal disinflation has stalled.

arbitrated.notes:
Synthesizing across the panel for a level (not curve, not real/nominal split) call on nominal yields over the next ~month: the dominant, freshest, and most directly yield-relevant signals point modestly higher, but with real disagreement that argues for sizing down rather than a high-conviction directional bet.

Term premium (10y yield itself) is the most direct read on the level and shows clear upward momentum (+0.40pp/21d, +0.24pp/63d, near top of 12-month range), though momentum is decelerating and the analyst flags a real gap: it wasn't given balance_sheet or financial_conditions context. Balance_sheet data (fresh, high conviction) confirms uninterrupted QT runoff, which structurally supports higher term premium via supply — this closes part of that gap and corroborates the term_premium call rather than contradicting it. Financial_conditions has reversed from easing to a mild "up" (tightening) lean, but conviction is low and it's a noisy single-period reversal — this is a modest headwind to further yield increases (tightening financial conditions can either reflect or dampen yield rises) so I weight it lightly.

Inflation (CPI, formed 13 days ago, is by report status not fully fresh but still recent) says down — clear disinflation trend, momentum consistent. Inflation_expectations (breakeven, fresh) says up — a sharp reversal in market-implied inflation risk. These two are in tension but cover different concepts (realized vs expected); the breakeven move is fresher and the CPI call's own author admits deceleration is slowing. Labor_tightness is flat/no-view on rates — sits out of the level call.

Netting it out: balance-sheet-driven supply pressure and rising term premium/breakeven expectations outweigh the disinflation pull and the tentative FC tightening signal, producing a modest "up" level lean, sized down given the panel's real splits and the term_premium/FC data gap.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last three observations show a sharp jump in total assets: from ~8.342T to 8.639T to 8.734T to 8.706T — a rise of roughly +366B over 4 weeks, reversing the prior steady runoff pattern (which had ground from ~8.51T down to ~8.34T over the preceding ten weeks). This is a clear inflection: my prior "down" call was correct through the runoff phase but the most recent prints show the level has snapped back sharply, now sitting only ~260B below the 52w high, with 13w change flipping positive (+1.81%). The policy language is unchanged, still describing continued reduction of Treasury/agency holdings under previously announced plans — so this is not an announced QE program. That mismatch (rising assets vs. runoff-only language) suggests the jump is likely a technical/liquidity event (e.g., repo operations, bill settlement effects, or balance sheet mechanics unrelated to a policy shift) rather than a deliberate reversal of QT. However, since my mandate is to weigh the recent pace of change most heavily, and the last three prints are unambiguous large increases, I have to respect the measurement even though the "why" doesn't support sustained expansion. The latest print (8705942) did tick down from the peak (8733787), suggesting the spike may already be fading.
Would change its mind if: A continued decline back toward the pre-spike ~8.34T level over coming weeks, or explicit language confirming this was a temporary technical add, would confirm reversion to runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35

Report:
  The prior call (down/flattening, 0.45 conviction) was wrong: the last two readings show the spread deepening further to -0.55 then -0.58, essentially matching the trough of this run. However, the 21d change is now +0.23pp — a notable stabilization/steepening impulse versus the 63d (-0.07) and 252d (-0.69) trends, both negative. The series itself is choppy: -0.45→-0.58→-0.48→-0.38→-0.38→-0.41→-0.47→-0.51→-0.55→-0.58, oscillating in a band roughly -0.38 to -0.58 with no clean trend, ending back at the cycle-recent low. The spread sits at -0.58, only 0.49pp above the 12m low (-1.07) and well below the 12m high (+0.44), giving a range position of 0.32 — still in the lower third, i.e. meaningfully inverted with more room below than above. Fed language continues to signal further hikes ("additional policy firming may be appropriate"), which historically presses front-end yields up faster than the long end, a flattening/inverting force. Given the latest print re-tests the recent low despite the positive 21d change, I read this as still biased toward flattening, but the mixed short-term signal tempers conviction.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45pp for 2+ consecutive readings with 21d change staying positive would indicate genuine steepening reversal.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series shows a clear, sustained rise from -0.31 (trough) to -0.15 over the last several prints, with the most recent readings (-0.20, -0.17, -0.15) continuing the uptrend. The 4-week change is now +0.11 (strongly positive) and even the 13-week change has flipped positive (+0.05), confirming the tightening move has broadened beyond just near-term noise. Range position at 0.79 shows the index sitting in the upper part of its 52-week range, well off the -0.37 low. My prior "up" call was correct: conditions have continued tightening steadily, not reversed. The pace of increase per observation has been fairly consistent (roughly +0.02 to +0.03 per period), suggesting momentum is intact rather than decelerating sharply. Policy language reinforces this, explicitly flagging that recent developments are likely to tighten credit conditions further and weigh on activity — consistent with continued upward pressure on the index. There is no sign of the 4w change turning negative or a fresh low, which was my stated falsifier. Given the consistency of the trend across multiple windows (4w, 13w, range position all pointing the same way) and confirming policy language, I maintain a tightening call, though the index remains below the zero average, so this is tightening from a still-loose base.
Would change its mind if: A print reversing below -0.20 (retracing the recent gains) or 4w change turning negative would signal the tightening trend has stalled or reversed.

arbitrated.notes:
Weighing the panel for a level (duration) view: the strongest, most current signal is financial conditions tightening steadily (NFCI up across 4w/13w/range-position, conviction 0.55) and inflation still decelerating (down 0.45, though momentum slowing as base effects fade). Labor stays flat/tight with high conviction (0.68) but is a range-bound, non-directional input for rates — it argues against a dovish pivot but doesn't push yields either way on its own. Term premium reversed to flat/slightly-down (0.30, weak) after its prior "up" call broke down on negative 21d/63d momentum; this is the most rate-proximate driver but it's low-conviction and self-admittedly conflicted (long-term trend up, recent momentum down). Balance sheet's technical asset-spike is likely noise (analyst's own read), and conviction is modest (0.35) with explicit disclosure that policy language doesn't support real QE reversal — I discount this toward flat/no-view for level purposes. Curve and inflation-expectations are out of scope for this seat (curve shape, breakevens).

Netting it out: financial conditions tightening is a genuine, broad, sustained impulse that historically coincides with elevated real yields/curbed risk-taking, but its effect on nominal Treasury yields is ambiguous — tightening credit conditions can either push yields up (term premium, supply) or down (flight to quality, growth deceleration expectations). Given term_premium's own directional call is now flat/down and its analyst flags dependency on financial_conditions to explain the yield pullback, I read the FCI tightening here as more consistent with a flight-to-quality/growth-slowing channel pressuring yields down rather than up. Combined with continued (if decelerating) disinflation and a stable, non-loosening labor market that removes any near-term hawkish surprise risk, my net level view is mildly lower yields, with low-to-moderate conviction given the genuine cross-currents and several stale/reversed calls.

Flat, range-bound signal; informs policy risk but not level direction directly.
Breakevens are a real/nominal-split signal, out of this seat's mandate.

arbitrated.falsifier: A term-premium print reclaiming >3.57%/range-position>0.75, or NFCI reversing back below -0.20, would flip this toward flat-to-higher yields.

Term premium call just flipped directions twice in two meetings; signal is noisy and low-conviction.

trade.rationale: Mild bull-flattening-neutral duration long: tightening financial conditions and decelerating inflation argue for modestly lower yields across the curve, tempered by stale/reversed term-premium and balance-sheet noise.

why.financial_conditions: Sustained NFCI tightening across 4w/13w/range-position; freshest, most consistent driver, but ambiguous sign for nominal yield level.
why.inflation: Deceleration continues but momentum slowing as base effects exhaust; downgraded slightly from analyst's 0.45 given fading pace.
why.term_premium: Own call reversed from up to flat/down on negative 21d/63d momentum; low conviction, flagged as needing FCI/balance-sheet context.
why.balance_sheet: Asset spike judged technical/noise by its own analyst, contradicted by unchanged runoff policy language; set aside for level view.
why.labor_tightness: Stable, range-bound plateau; removes hawkish surprise risk but gives no directional push to yield level on its own.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.62
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four readings show a clean, steady decline: 8632384 → 8614797 → 8593263 → 8562768, a run-off of about -69.6B over the last 4 observations and -143174 ($M) over the 4-week derived window. This confirms the reversal I flagged as a condition last time: the mid-series spike (peaking near 8733787, the 52w high) has been unwinding steadily for several consecutive readings, not just one blip. The 13w change is still slightly positive (+92211) only because the spike is inside that window, but the trend within the window is now unambiguously downward and monotonic in the last four prints. The policy language confirms this is consistent with continued runoff: the Committee statement explicitly reaffirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS/debt holdings per previously announced plans — no language of reinvestment expansion. Given the pace (~-20 to -35B per weekly-equivalent step) and the confirmed multi-period consecutive decline, I now think my prior "flat" call was too cautious — this looks like renewed/continued QT runoff. Absent any new liquidity-facility or emergency-lending shock, the trajectory should continue lower into month-end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above ~8.6T over the next few readings, or new language suspending/pausing runoff, would overturn this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print shows slope_2s10s falling back to -0.60pp after a brief pop to -0.46/-0.47, reversing the steepening seen in the middle of this window. My prior "down" call is now validated: the move above -0.45 did not sustain for 2+ readings and the 21d change has turned negative again (-0.05pp), confirming the reversal I flagged as the falsifier condition did not materialize. The 63d change is still positive (+0.10pp) but that's stale relative to the newest print showing renewed flattening. The curve remains deeply inverted at -0.60pp, sitting only 0.31 of the way up its 12-month range (low -1.07, high +0.44), i.e., closer to its most inverted levels than to flat. The 252d change of -0.83pp shows the dominant trend over the past year has been flattening/deepening inversion. Policy language shows continued rate hikes (funds rate raised twice in the shown sequence, from 4.50-4.75% to 4.75-5.00%) with guidance for "additional policy firming," which via front-end pressure (dgs2 4.04% vs dgs10 3.44%) is consistent with continued inversion pressure rather than steepening. Given the snap-back to -0.60 and hawkish continuation language, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for month end, not a resumption of steepening.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move above -0.45pp for 2+ consecutive readings with 21d change staying positive would indicate genuine steepening reversal.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, dgs2, dgs10

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call ("up"/tightening) has been overtaken by the data. After rising steadily from -0.30 toward -0.15 over the middle of the series, NFCI has now ticked back down for three consecutive prints: -0.15, -0.17, -0.18. The 4-week change is -0.02, i.e. mildly negative, which is exactly the reversal condition I flagged as a falsifier. The longer 13-week change (+0.11) still shows conditions net looser than three months ago in absolute tightening terms (recall: rising NFCI = tightening, so +0.11 over 13w actually means conditions tightened over that window from a very loose base), but the recent momentum has clearly stalled and inflected downward. The index remains well below zero (-0.18), meaning conditions are still looser than historical average, and range position (0.66) shows we're in the tighter half of the past year's range but pulling back from the highs. Policy language noting "recent developments" likely tightening credit conditions suggests some exogenous stress, but the actual index has not confirmed further tightening—it's easing over the last month. Given the 3-print pullback and negative 4w change, I now lean toward continued easing (down) rather than further tightening, reversing my prior stance.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.15 or 4w change turning positive again would signal the tightening trend has resumed.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print fell sharply to 2.18 from 2.27, a -0.09pp single-observation drop that breaches the 2.20 level I flagged as the trigger for a renewed downtrend. This follows a series that had been drifting sideways-to-down (2.28→2.28→2.26→2.28→2.28→2.25→2.25→2.27→2.18), so my prior "flat" call was reasonable given the range-bound behavior through most of the window but is now invalidated by this latest breakdown. The 21-day change (-0.16pp) and 63-day change (-0.11pp) both confirm the drop is not just a one-day noise blip but a real shift lower, and the 252-day change (-0.67pp) shows this is part of a larger, sustained disinflationary repricing in market expectations. The breakeven is now sitting near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.09, only 0.08pp above the 12m low of 2.10), suggesting further downside is plausible if the trend continues, though proximity to the 12m low could also invite some stabilization or mean-reversion. Distance from the 2% target is still +0.18pp, so expectations remain anchored above target, giving some room to fall further without dramatic dislocation. Given the clear break below my downtrend threshold and multi-horizon confirmation, I now lean toward continued softening.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.25-2.27 sustained over the next print would suggest the 2.18 reading was noise rather than trend continuation.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release confirmed the "down" call: headline CPI y/y fell to 4.95% (essentially flat vs 4.92% prior, but down sharply from the high-8s a year earlier), and core PCE y/y ticked further down to 4.78%, a new low in the series and continuing its slow grind lower. Neither of my stated reversal triggers fired: headline m/m came in at 0.34%, below the 0.5% threshold, and core PCE stayed under 5.0%. So the disinflation trend, while decelerating in pace, remains intact. That said, momentum is not uniformly friendly: headline_mom_3m_avg (0.27%) implies a 3-month annualized headline pace of 3.27%, still above the 2% target, and the two most recent monthly prints (0.12%, 0.34%) show some bounce after a very soft 0.02% reading three months back — the disinflation is decelerating in speed, not reversing. Core remains the more important read given headline's flatness and past energy swings; core's slow but steady decline (5.61%→4.78% over the window) is the dominant signal. With headline y/y sitting at a 12-month low and core also easing, but base effects from year-ago prints likely to be less favorable going forward, I expect next month's headline y/y to be roughly flat to slightly up, not a continuation of sharp declines.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5% or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.0% would signal disinflation has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print came in at 3.40%, a new 12-month low, down from 3.50% and squarely within the tight range this cycle. My prior "flat" call held up: the print stayed within the plateau band and did not breach 3.70%. If anything the market tightened further rather than loosening. The unrate_change_3m (-0.10pp), 6m (-0.20pp) and 12m (-0.30pp) all point modestly downward, and the sahm_gap is a negligible +0.10pp, well below the 0.5pp threshold that would signal a genuine turn. Distance from natural rate is -0.60pp, meaning the labor market is tighter than the 4% longer-run anchor, not looser. Policy language reinforces this: job gains have "picked up" and are "running at a robust pace," with unemployment "remained low" — language that got more emphatic on strength, not less. There is a forward-looking caveat about tighter credit conditions weighing on hiring going forward, but that is anticipatory, not yet visible in the data. Given the level is at a 12-month low and momentum is neutral-to-tightening, I lean toward continued flat-to-slightly-tighter conditions next month, with modest conviction since a print this low leaves limited room to fall further and asymmetric risk of a bounce.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would suggest the low has passed and loosening is beginning; a print at/below 3.20% would signal further tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_low, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_range_position

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield sits at 3.44, near the middle of its 12-month range (range_position 0.51) and down slightly over both the 21d and 63d windows (-0.11pp each), while still up sharply over the past year (+0.67pp). The last print (3.44) is a pullback from the prior local high (3.53), and the series has been choppy without a clear trend within the last two weeks. My prior flat call was reasonably accurate — yields oscillated in a narrow band and did not break the 3.57 threshold I set as a trigger for an upward revision. The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive (+0.23pp), consistent with steady but unremarkable term-premium compensation at the long end; no steepening acceleration is evident. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff continues to be a background upward pressure on term premium, but there's no fresh signal of accelerating issuance concerns or flight-to-quality flows in this data. With the yield roughly mid-range and recent momentum mildly negative, I see no strong case for a directional move by month-end. The evidence continues to support a flat call, perhaps with a very slight bias toward down given the recent 21d/63d softness, but not enough to override the mid-range positioning and stable slope.
Would change its mind if: A move back above 3.57% with range_position climbing over 0.75 and positive 21d change would revive the upward term-premium case; a break below 3.40 with steepening would confirm downward.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing runoff and net Treasury issuance would clarify supply pressure on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment shifts could signal flight-to-quality demand for long bonds.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.68
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last several readings confirm continued balance-sheet runoff. Total assets fell from 8,503,994 to 8,436,255 over the most recent observations, with assets_change_4w at -126,513$M — a clear acceleration of decline versus prior weeks. Assets are now -498,091$M below the 52-week high (8,934,346), and the 52w change is -478,026$M, consistent with steady QT. The 13w change shows a small positive (+54,065$M, +0.64%) only because it spans an earlier bump in the series (a jump from ~8.34T to ~8.64T-8.73T well before the recent declines); the trend since that bump has been consistently downward for seven straight readings. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued runoff of Treasury and MBS holdings with no signal of pause or reinvestment. My prior call of "down" was correct — the rebound-above-8.6T scenario that would have overturned it did not materialize; instead assets kept falling to fresh cycle lows. With the 4-week pace of decline actually steeper than before and no policy language change, the most likely path over the next month is continued gradual decline, consistent with the announced runoff caps.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above ~8.55-8.6T over the next few readings, or new language pausing/suspending runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call played out: the spread fell from -0.55 to -0.76pp over the ten readings, deepening inversion. However, the most recent three prints (-0.67, -0.74, -0.76) show the pace of flattening decelerating — the 21d change is -0.21pp (still negative, confirming near-term flattening pressure) but the 63d change is now +0.05pp, meaning the flattening has essentially reversed over the medium window. The curve sits at -0.76pp, near the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.22, only 0.31pp above the 12m low of -1.07), which is itself a stretched, historically inverted level. Policy language shifted from "additional firming may be appropriate" to softer language about "extent to which additional policy firming may be appropriate," dropping the more forward-leaning tightening bias — consistent with a Fed nearing the end of hikes, which in past cycles has coincided with curve stabilization or modest re-steepening as front-end expectations cap out. Given the mixed signal — recent 21d still flattening but 63d flat-to-slightly-steepening, and rate hikes appearing near a pause — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down call rather than continued sharp flattening. Conviction is modest given the conflicting short vs medium-term signals.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive readings above -0.65pp with positive 21d change would confirm a genuine steepening reversal; renewed move below -0.85pp would confirm continued flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (-0.21) continued the easing-then-mild-retightening pattern: after bottoming near -0.15 mid-series, NFCI has ticked down (tighter) for four consecutive readings (-0.17→-0.18→-0.19→-0.20→-0.20→-0.21), giving a 4w change of -0.03 (further easing... wait, more negative = looser). Actually the last four prints show the index moving more negative, i.e., financial conditions loosening further, not tightening. My prior call was "down" (looser) and this materialized: the index fell from -0.17 to -0.21, a continuation of easing. The 13w change (+0.08) reflects the earlier bottoming-out rebound from -0.15, but the most recent 4-5 observations show renewed softening in conditions, pushing the index back toward its 52w low (-0.31) and away from the recent high (-0.10). Range position at 0.47 shows we're mid-range, but the momentum (4w change -0.03) points to continued loosening. Policy language shift removed explicit reference to "recent developments" causing tighter credit, softening the tightening narrative, consistent with easing financial conditions. This supports a continuation of the easing trend at a modest pace.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.17 or 4w change turning positive would signal tightening has resumed and reverse this easing call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: breakeven_10y fell further to 2.18 from 2.22, extending the decline rather than rebounding above the 2.25-2.27 range that would have signaled noise. The trend is now well-established across horizons: -0.05pp over 21d, -0.30pp over 63d, and -0.44pp over 252d, showing consistent, accelerating downward momentum in market-implied inflation expectations. The series sits near its 12-month low (only 0.08pp above the 2.10 floor) and in just the 12th percentile of its 12-month range, indicating this is not a minor fluctuation but a sustained repricing lower. While the breakeven remains 0.18pp above the Committee's 2% longer-run objective, the trajectory suggests convergence toward or even below target-implied levels if the trend continues. With no rebound signal materializing and the most recent print (2.18) being the lowest in the series, momentum favors continued softening. I'll maintain a down call, with slightly higher conviction given the confirmation of trend continuation and proximity to the 12-month low, though the magnitude of further decline may moderate given how compressed levels already are relative to the range floor.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 2.22-2.25 sustained over the next print would suggest 2.18 was a trough rather than continued decline.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call was too cautious: headline y/y actually fell sharply, from 4.95 to 4.13 (-0.82pp in one month), extending a clear disinflationary trend (3m annualized headline at just 2.52%, and the yoy series is sitting at its 12-month low with yoy_range_position of 0.00). The latest print (0.16% m/m) was well below both the outgoing print (1.26%) and the 3m average (0.21%), so my falsifier condition (m/m above 0.5%) did not trigger — disinflation continued, it did not stall. Core PCE is stickier: yoy sits at 4.79%, barely down from 4.86-4.94% a few months back, and 3m annualized core is running hot at 4.25%, well above target and above the recent yoy pace, suggesting core momentum is not decelerating as fast as headline. Given base effects (a very high year-ago comparison of 8.98% two years back rolling off) and continued soft monthly headline prints, I expect headline yoy to fall again next release, though the size of the base-effect tailwind is diminishing as the high-inflation comparison base is now mostly cycled through. Core's stickiness argues against a large further headline drop, but the immediate direction for headline yoy is still down.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.5%, or core PCE y/y rising back above 5.0%, would signal disinflation stalling/reversing.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.50
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 3.60% sits at the top of the trailing 12-month range (3.40–3.60%), matching prior peaks seen earlier in the series rather than breaking above them. My prior falsifier threshold (a print above 3.60%) was not breached, so the "flat" call from last period was correct: the market has oscillated in a narrow 0.2pp band for over a year with 3m/6m/12m changes all at 0.00pp. The Sahm-gap of 0.10pp is trivial and well below the 0.50pp threshold that would signal recessionary loosening. Policy language continues to describe job gains as "robust" and unemployment as "remained low," reinforcing that this uptick to 3.60% is noise within a tight-labor-market plateau rather than a trend break. Distance from the natural rate remains solidly negative (-0.40pp), meaning the market is still tighter than long-run normal. Given the flat multi-horizon changes and unchanged qualitative language, I see no basis for a directional call — the print has simply returned to the upper edge of its established range, not exceeded it. I maintain flat, with slightly lower conviction than before given the print sits exactly at the range ceiling, raising modest odds of a genuine break next month.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% would suggest the low has passed and loosening is beginning; a print at/below 3.20% would signal further tightening beyond this plateau.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has pulled back over the last few observations, closing at 3.64% after peaking near 3.83%. My prior "flat" call held reasonably well: the 21d change is only +0.05pp, essentially flat, though the level did briefly spike above my 3.57% trigger before retreating. Range position at 0.63 shows the yield still sits in the upper half of its 12-month range (2.60-4.25%), but the recent 3-observation decline (3.83→3.80→3.69→3.64) suggests some cooling in long-end pressure after the run-up. The 63d change of -0.44pp is notably negative, indicating the longer-run trend within the quarter has actually been one of falling yields, even though the 252d change of +0.89pp shows a much higher level than a year ago. The 30s10s slope at +0.21pp is modestly positive but not steepening dramatically, and continued balance-sheet runoff remains a persistent mild upward force on term premium that hasn't disappeared. On net, the recent turn lower in yields plus flat 21d momentum argues against calling this "up" with conviction, but the range position still in the upper two-thirds and ongoing QT keep me from calling a confident "down" either. This looks like consolidation after a peak, with modest downward drift in the immediate data.
Would change its mind if: A renewed move above 3.80% with range_position back over 0.75 and positive 21d/63d change would revive the upward case; a break below 3.50% would confirm the downward drift.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Reconciling for a duration/level call on nominal Treasuries: inflation and inflation_expectations both give fresh, well-evidenced "down" reads — CPI headline decelerating sharply and breakevens grinding to 12-month lows with accelerating momentum across 21/63/252d windows. These are the two freshest, most internally consistent signals and both point the same direction, so they anchor the call. Financial conditions also reads "down" (looser), reinforcing the disinflationary, lower-rate-pressure backdrop, though the analyst's own writeup is somewhat self-contradictory about sign convention, so I discount its weight modestly. Labor tightness is flat/plateau — unemployment sitting at range ceiling but not breaking out — a genuine no-view driver rather than a counterweight. Term premium is genuinely mixed (down-leaning but weak conviction, explicitly flagged as missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions context) — the analyst admits QT is a persistent upward force it isn't pricing, and financial-conditions easing could be driving the yield pullback rather than a term-premium retreat itself. Balance sheet continues firm, accelerating QT (down calls on reserves, a structural upward pressure on term premium/duration supply), which is the one driver pulling against the level-down thesis. On net: two fresh, strong disinflationary drivers (CPI, breakevens) plus loosening financial conditions outweigh a persistent but incremental QT drag and a hedged term-premium read. I read the net level direction as modestly lower, driven by the inflation/growth-expectations channel rather than duration-supply dynamics, with QT capping how much conviction I put on the low end.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.72
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: total assets fell from 8,362,060 to 8,340,914 in the latest reading, extending an uninterrupted downtrend across all 13 observations. The 4-week change (-44,940) and 13-week change (-365,028) confirm runoff is proceeding at a consistent pace, with no deceleration visible in the last few prints (changes of roughly -26k, -22k, -1k, +3k, -23k over recent weeks show some week-to-week noise but the multi-week trend is unambiguously down). Assets sit $558B below the 52-week high, and the policy language explicitly reaffirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS holdings per previously announced plans — no hint of tapering the runoff pace or pausing. My prior call of 'down' was correct: assets continued to fall as expected. Given the mechanical, cap-driven nature of QT and the unchanged reaffirming language, the same dynamics should persist into next month-end. There's no evidence of a rebound toward the 8.55-8.6T threshold I set as a falsifier, nor any suspension language. The trend is clear and consistent, warranting continued conviction in further runoff, albeit tempered slightly by the occasional single-week flat/positive print suggesting the pace isn't perfectly linear.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above ~8.55-8.6T over the next few readings, or new language pausing/suspending runoff.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was wrong: the spread has continued grinding more inverted, falling from -0.93 to -1.06pp over the ten readings, with slope_change_21d at -0.30pp and slope_change_63d at -0.48pp both negative and accelerating rather than stabilizing. The last three prints (-0.97, -1.02, -1.06) show a fresh leg lower, pushing the spread to a new 12-month low (slope_12m_low -1.07, slope_range_position 0.01 — essentially at the bottom of its range). This is a clear continuation of flattening/inversion, not noise. The Fed holding rates steady (skip after a long hiking cycle) while signaling further tightening remains possible ("additional policy firming that may be appropriate") keeps the front end anchored high (dgs2 4.87%) while the long end lags (dgs10 3.81%), consistent with the deepening inversion. The 10s30s slope at +0.04pp shows the long end itself is roughly flat, so the inversion pressure is concentrated in the belly/front-end relationship, not a broad long-end selloff. Given the persistent negative momentum across both short and medium windows and the position at the extreme of the 12-month range, I expect the spread to stay negative and likely inch lower or hold near these lows rather than reverse within a month.
Would change its mind if: A print above -0.90pp with positive 21d change would signal reversal; continued closes below -1.10pp confirm ongoing deepening flattening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" (easing) call was correct: NFCI has continued to drift lower, from -0.21 to -0.22 over the last few prints, with 4-week change at -0.01 and 13-week change at -0.07. The index sits well below the zero average at -0.22, near the middle-lower portion of its 52-week range (range_position 0.42), and momentum remains modestly negative rather than reversing. The pace of easing has slowed noticeably versus the prior 13-week trend (-0.07 over 13 weeks vs. essentially flat over the most recent 4 weeks), suggesting the easing trend is decelerating and flattening out rather than accelerating. Policy language shows only a minor wording tweak (removing "the extent to which" in favor of "the extent of") with no substantive change in tightening bias, and the credit-conditions language is unchanged, so there's no new signal there to alter the trajectory. Given the flattening momentum but continued negative sign and no reversal above -0.17, I lean toward continued mild easing or flat conditions into next month, with lower conviction than before given the deceleration.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.19 or 4w change turning clearly positive would signal tightening resuming and reverse this easing call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.60) is now looking wrong: the 10y breakeven bounced from the 2.18 trough to 2.22 in the latest print, matching the earlier high in the series and sitting at the top of the recent 2.18-2.24 range. The 21-day change is now positive (+0.04pp) even though 63-day and 252-day changes remain negative (-0.10pp, -0.26pp), indicating the longer downtrend has stalled and reversed over the last two prints. My stated falsifier—a sustained rebound back above 2.22-2.25—has essentially been met with this print. Breakevens remain modestly above the 2% objective (+0.22pp) and well off the 12m high (2.62%), sitting only 23% into the 12-month range, so there's no evidence of an inflation-expectations scare; rather this looks like stabilization after a decline, with the last two observations (2.20, 2.22) suggesting a modest upward drift. Given the whipsaw pattern in the last 10 observations (2.18-2.22 repeatedly), I don't have high confidence in a strong continued rise, but the immediate momentum and the falsifier condition being met argue for reversing from "down" to a modest "up" lean rather than continuing the prior thesis.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.18-2.20 next print would confirm the range-bound/declining pattern rather than a genuine reversal.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: headline y/y fell sharply from 4.13% to 3.07%, a decline consistent with the disinflation trend, and my falsifier conditions (m/m above 0.5% or core PCE back above 5.0%) were not triggered — outgoing m/m was actually negative-to-flat (-0.01%) before the latest 0.22% print. However, the picture for the next release is more mixed than the y/y number suggests. The 3-month annualized headline pace (2.94%) is running near target, but this reflects a very favorable base a few months back; the most recent m/m prints (0.34, 0.16, 0.22) are firmer than the near-zero prints from earlier in the window that are about to roll off the y/y calculation. Core PCE has been essentially flat for three months (4.86→4.78→4.79→4.73), still well above the 2% target (distance +2.73pp) and decelerating only marginally — core_yoy_change_3m is just -0.12pp, indicating stalling progress on the persistent-inflation measure. Because a low base month is exiting the 12-month window and recent m/m readings are modestly above the very low prints being replaced, headline y/y is more likely to tick up or hold flat than continue its steep decline. I weight core PCE's stalling here as a caution against extrapolating further headline improvement.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.1% or a fresh drop in core PCE y/y below 4.5% would confirm continued disinflation rather than stalling.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate printed 3.60% again, matching the top of its trailing 12-month range and consistent with my prior "flat" call — the print did not exceed 3.60%, so my falsifier condition for a loosening call was not triggered. The series has oscillated tightly between 3.40% and 3.60% for over a year, with no sustained trend: 3m and 6m changes are only +0.10pp, and 12m change is exactly 0.00pp. The Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) is a modest 0.13-0.20pp, well below the ~0.50pp threshold that historically signals recession-onset loosening. Level remains 0.40pp below the assumed natural rate of 4%, indicating the labor market is still tight in absolute terms. Policy language continues to describe job gains as "robust" and unemployment as "remained low," with no shift in characterization of labor conditions themselves — the changed language concerns credit conditions' future effects on hiring, not a present labor-market observation. Given the rate is sitting at the high end of its range but has been there before (compare obs 1,5,6,9,12,13 all at 3.60) without breaking out, this looks like noise within a stable plateau rather than a new trend. I maintain flat, though I'd flag the print is at the upper bound, keeping conviction moderate rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% (new 12m high) would confirm loosening has begun; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_width, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.30) was wrong: the 10-year has pushed higher, closing at 3.81% after touching 3.85%, with range_position at 0.73 (near the upper third of the 12-month band) and positive momentum across all horizons (+0.17pp/21d, +0.33pp/63d, +0.61pp/252d). This is exactly the scenario I flagged as reviving the upward case. The trend over the past year has been persistently higher yields, not a reversal, and the recent bounce off 3.71 back to 3.81 suggests renewed upward pressure rather than consolidation. The 10s30s slope is only +0.04pp, essentially flat, so the long end isn't showing a distinct steepening signal beyond the general drift higher — but combined with ongoing balance-sheet runoff (continuing Treasury/MBS reduction per the policy language), the supply-side pressure on the long end remains a persistent tailwind for higher yields. Given the level is now in the upper part of its range and momentum is positive across all three windows, I'm revising to a modest up call, though conviction is capped since the move from 3.71 to 3.81 is only a few basis points beyond noise and the 10s30s slope isn't confirming a strong steepening dynamic.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 3.70% with range_position under 0.60 and negative 21d/63d change would revive the downward case.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/details of runoff would clarify supply pressure magnitude on long end.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Would show if flight-to-quality flows are offsetting supply pressure.
