arbitrated.notes:
The panel is split on level direction, and I need to separate a genuine duration/level view from what are curve-shape and real-rate seat calls. Term premium (nominal 10y yield itself, up 0.40) is the most direct and freshest read on the long-end level: DGS10 has pushed to 3.81%, near the upper third of its 12-month range, with positive momentum across 21d/63d/252d windows — a real, un-stale signal of higher long yields. Balance sheet reinforces this from the supply side: QT continues mechanically and unambiguously (down 0.72, fresh), which is a structural tailwind for higher term premium/long yields, and the panel itself flags that term_premium's own call was made without seeing this balance-sheet input, so the two should be read together rather than independently — this strengthens rather than dilutes the up-yield case. Financial conditions are only mildly easing (down 0.30, decelerating pace) — not a strong offsetting force. Inflation is flat/stalling-in-core (0.35 conviction, slightly stale at 15 days) and inflation_expectations (breakevens) are curving up modestly but that's a real-rate/breakeven story, out of my seat. Labor tightness is genuinely flat, plateaued, no lean either way. Curve_slope and inflation_expectations are excluded from my level view as they are curve or real/nominal-split seats.

Netting this out: the freshest, most directly relevant drivers (term premium, balance sheet) both point toward higher nominal yields, particularly concentrated in the long end, while financial conditions offer only a weak, decelerating offset and labor/inflation are neutral-to-flat. This supports a modest-to-moderate net "yields up" level view, more pronounced at the long end given the supply/term-premium linkage, with lower conviction at the front end given labor and inflation are flat and front-end policy is on hold.

discounted.why: Curve-shape seat, not a level signal; deepening inversion doesn't resolve net direction.
Breakeven/real-nominal split is out of scope for a pure nominal level call.

arbitrated.falsifier: A DGS10 drop back below 3.70% with negative momentum, or balance-sheet runoff pausing/reversing toward 8.55-8.6T, would flip this to neutral or down.

risks.text: Term-premium move is only a few bp beyond noise; could reverse quickly if long-end demand resumes.
Fed could signal QT tapering or pause, removing the balance-sheet tailwind for higher long yields.
Flight-to-quality flows (not observed here) could offset supply pressure and cap yields despite runoff.

trade.rationale: Net long-duration short (higher yields expected) skewed to the long end where term-premium and balance-sheet drivers concentrate, with smaller front-end exposure given flat labor/inflation.

why.term_premium: Fresh, direct nominal 10y signal: higher level, positive momentum across all windows, upper-range position.
why.balance_sheet: QT unambiguous and ongoing; reinforces term-premium upside via long-end supply pressure, per flagged gap.
why.financial_conditions: Mild, decelerating easing; weak headwind against the up-yield case, not enough to offset it.
why.labor_tightness: Plateaued at range top with no breakout; genuinely flat, no level signal.
why.inflation: Core PCE stalling suggests slight upside risk to next CPI print, but conviction is low and view is 15 days stale.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: total assets fell from 8,503,994 to 8,243,344 over 13 weeks, a drop of ~319B (-3.73%), with the most recent 4-week change of -97,570M showing no deceleration — if anything the pace looks slightly brisk versus the 13w average pace (~24.6k/wk vs recent 4w ~24.4k/wk, essentially matching). Assets remain well below the 52w high (-635,794M) with no sign of a floor or rebound. My prior call (down, 0.72) was correct: the falsifier condition (rebound above ~8.55-8.6T) did not occur; instead assets fell further. Policy language is unchanged, confirming the Committee continues balance-sheet runoff via the previously announced plans, with no indication of tapering the cap or pausing. Given the consistent, uninterrupted week-over-week decline across all horizons (4w, 13w, 52w all negative and of similar per-week magnitude), the trend is clearly intact. There is no evidence of any technical adjustment, reinvestment change, or halt language that would suggest a near-term inflection. I maintain a confident down call for month-end, expecting assets to be lower than the last print of 8,243,344, continuing the established runoff pace.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back above ~8.5T over the next few readings, or new language pausing/suspending runoff.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was wrong: the last two prints (-0.91, -0.91) actually mark a modest recovery from the recent low, not further deepening. The 21d change is now +0.11pp (mild steepening), even as the 63d (-0.31pp) and 252d (-0.73pp) changes confirm the dominant trend over the past year has been flattening/inversion deepening. The spread sits at -0.91pp, well inside the 12-month range (position 0.19 of 0-1), still closer to the deeply inverted end (-1.08 low) than the least-inverted point (-0.17 high). The Fed just hiked another 25bp (5.25-5.50%), which typically pressures the front end (2y) more than the long end, a flattening impulse; yet the curve has stabilized rather than inverted further, suggesting the hike was largely priced in. With slope_10s30s flat at +0.05pp, there's no strong long-end signal either way. Near-term, the short-term uptick (+0.11pp over 21d) is a mild steepening signal, but it's small relative to the multi-month flattening trend and the level remains deeply inverted. I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call for month end, but with low conviction given the mixed signals and the still-hawkish policy tone.
Would change its mind if: A fresh close below -1.00pp would confirm renewed flattening; a push above -0.85pp with continued positive 21d change would confirm steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady grind lower, from -0.19 to -0.26 over 13 weeks, with the most recent print (-0.26) being the lowest in the entire window and below the 52w range midpoint, sitting at only 0.23 of the way up the 52-week range. The 4-week change (-0.04) and 13-week change (-0.08) are both negative and consistent in direction, showing no sign of stalling or reversing. My prior "down" call at 0.30 conviction was correct — conditions have continued to ease, not reverse, and the falsifier condition (a print back above -0.19 or a clearly positive 4w change) did not trigger. The index remains well below zero, meaning conditions are looser than historical average, and the trend of increasingly negative prints each period shows persistent, gradual easing momentum without deceleration. There's no evidence in the measurements of a turning point — each of the last several prints has set a fresh low relative to the window. Given the consistency and lack of any offsetting signal, I maintain the down call and modestly increase conviction given the confirmed trend continuation.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.20 or 4w change turning positive would signal tightening resuming and reverse this easing call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.35) was correct: breakeven_10y rose from 2.35 toward 2.38 before settling at 2.37, confirming the reversal off the 2.22 low rather than a fade back into the 2.18-2.20 zone I flagged as the falsifier. The series has now stabilized in a tight 2.36-2.39 band for the last five prints, well above the 2.10 12m low and roughly mid-range (0.52 range position). The 21d and 63d changes remain solidly positive (+0.17, +0.19 pp), but the very latest prints (2.36, 2.37, 2.38, 2.37) show the momentum has largely leveled off after the initial jump from 2.22-2.23. The 252d change is still slightly negative (-0.05 pp), meaning breakevens are only marginally above where they were a year ago, and remain 0.37 pp above the 2% target — not extreme. Given the plateau in recent prints, I don't see strong evidence for continued near-term ascent, but there's no sign of reversal either. This looks like consolidation at a modestly elevated level rather than a fresh leg up or down.
Would change its mind if: A break below 2.30 would signal the plateau is rolling over; a push above 2.42 would confirm renewed upward momentum.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_above_12m_low

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release headline CPI y/y rose to 3.29% from 3.07% — a genuine uptick, not the "flat" I called. In hindsight the falsifier I set (core PCE below 4.5%) did trigger (4.38%), which would have argued disinflation continuing, but headline itself moved up on base effects, showing the two can diverge over a single month. Looking at momentum now: headline m/m has been 0.16, 0.22, 0.20 over the last three prints — modestly above the prior trough (outgoing_mom 0.06) and above the 3m average (0.19), so sequential momentum is stabilizing rather than falling further. Headline 3m annualized (2.35%) is still below the current y/y rate, implying some further disinflationary pull, but the last two m/m prints (0.22, 0.20) are firmer than earlier in the year, so the pace of deceleration is clearly slowing. Core PCE y/y continues to grind down slowly (4.73→4.38), consistent with gradual, not stalled, disinflation, but the gap between core and headline (core_minus_headline +1.10pp) and firming headline momentum both point toward headline y/y likely ticking up again next print due to base effects (last year's comparison base was falling then). Net: modest upward pressure on headline y/y is more likely than a fresh leg down.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% alongside a renewed drop in core PCE y/y below 4.2% would signal disinflation resuming rather than stalling/reversing.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, outgoing_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print (3.50%) sits right in the middle of the 13-month range (3.40-3.60%), and my prior flat call was borne out: the series has oscillated within a tight 0.20pp band for over a year with no net drift (12m change = 0.00pp). The 3m change of +0.10pp is trivial noise given the historical bounce between 3.40 and 3.60. Sahm gap of 0.17pp is well below the 0.50 threshold that would signal recession-onset loosening. The level itself (3.50%) is 0.50pp below the assumed 4% natural rate, indicating a still-tight labor market by any historical standard. Policy language confirms this: job gains "robust," unemployment "remained low," with tighter credit conditions cited as a future (not yet realized) drag on hiring. There is no evidence of a turn — no consecutive rises, no breakout above the 12m high. The market looks essentially flat, oscillating in a tight band with a persistent low level. I maintain a flat call, keeping conviction modest since a single-month reading within this band carries limited information, and the range is narrow enough that even small moves could tip toward either boundary next month.
Would change its mind if: A print above 3.60% (new 12m high) would confirm loosening; a print at/below 3.30% would signal renewed tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_width, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_low, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.40) was correct: the 10y has continued climbing, from 3.86 to 3.97 over the last observations, with 21d change +0.12pp and 63d +0.53pp confirming persistent upward momentum. The 252d change of +1.19pp shows this is a sustained, not transient, move. The yield sits at 0.83 of its 12-month range (near the high end, 1.37pp above the 12m low, only 0.28pp from the 12m high of 4.25%), indicating the long end is pressing toward recent highs rather than reverting. The 10s30s slope is modestly positive (+0.05pp), consistent with a still-steepening long end rather than curve inversion at the back end, which supports continued term premium buildup. Continued balance-sheet runoff (as referenced in the unchanged policy language) keeps a structural supply/duration pressure on the long end, reinforcing the upward bias. There's a slight moderation in the very latest print (3.97 vs 4.01 the observation before), which could hint at consolidation near highs, but the broader trend and range position argue for continuation rather than reversal. I maintain an up view, similar conviction, given no contradicting evidence has emerged and the falsifier condition (drop below 3.70 with range_position under 0.60) is far from being met.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 3.70% with range_position under 0.60 and negative 21d/63d change would revive the downward case.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing runoff would clarify magnitude of structural long-end supply pressure.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader tightening/loosening context would confirm whether long-end pressure reflects risk appetite or flight-to-quality.

arbitrated.notes: Level view for Treasury yields over the next ~1 month: net modestly higher, driven mainly by term_premium's confirmed uptrend at the long end (10y climbing from 3.86→3.97, near 12m highs, sustained 63d/252d momentum) reinforced by ongoing balance-sheet runoff providing structural duration supply. Financial_conditions is easing (NFCI grinding lower, looser conditions), which is a modest offsetting force pulling against higher yields, but its conviction is moderate (0.40) and the linkage to nominal level is indirect (looser conditions can support risk-on curve steepening without necessarily capping long yields when supply/term-premium dynamics dominate). Inflation posted a genuine upside surprise in headline CPI with firming momentum, adding a modest additional upward nudge to the level view, though inflation_expectations (breakevens) have plateaued, suggesting the market isn't yet pricing a fresh inflation leg. Labor_tightness is flat/range-bound, offering no directional signal for the level. Curve_slope is explicitly off-limits for this seat and in any case reports low-conviction mixed signals, so I don't lean on it. Netting these: term_premium (clear, fresh, high-conviction up) is the dominant, best-informed signal, partially reinforced by balance-sheet runoff and firming headline inflation, partially offset by easing financial conditions. This tilts the level modestly higher rather than sharply — conviction is capped by the offsetting FC signal and by inflation_expectations' plateau.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.78
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: total assets fell further, from 8,145,727 to 8,139,066 over the latest reading, continuing an unbroken decline across all 13 observations. The 4-week change (-104,278M), 13-week change (-297,189M), and 52-week change (-712,370M) all confirm steady, ongoing runoff with no deceleration evident in the most recent print. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-693,693M) and below the 13-week average, consistent with mechanical QT proceeding at (or near) the announced monthly caps. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings per previously announced plans — no hint of tapering the runoff pace or pausing balance-sheet reduction. Given the consistency of the trend (13 consecutive readings without reversal) and the reaffirmed reinvestment/runoff language, the highest-probability outcome for month-end is another decline of roughly similar magnitude to the recent 4-week pace, i.e., continued QT. There is no evidence in front of me of any slowing, pause, or reversal signal — the falsifier condition (rebound above ~8.5T or paused-runoff language) has not been met.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~8.3-8.5T, or new statement language pausing or slowing the runoff pace.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread remains deeply inverted (-0.76pp last print), essentially unchanged from the prior reading (-0.78 → -0.76) and near the midpoint of its 12-month range (range_position 0.35). The 21-day change is a modest +0.04pp (mild steepening pressure), but the 63-day change is -0.04pp and the 252-day change is -0.46pp, showing no durable trend either way — the last ten readings have oscillated in a narrow -0.63 to -0.78 band with no clear breakout in either direction. My prior flat call was correct: neither the -1.00pp flattening threshold nor the -0.85pp steepening threshold was breached, and the spread stayed range-bound. The Fed's rate hike (to 5.25-5.50%) keeps front-end anchored high, and with dgs2 (4.85%) still well above dgs10 (4.09%), inversion persists but isn't deepening. The 10s30s slope at +0.11pp shows the long end itself isn't inverted, suggesting the inversion is concentrated in the front-to-belly relationship tied to policy rate expectations, not a broad flattening impulse. Absent a clear catalyst or range breakout, I expect the spread to stay near current levels into month-end, with modest chop within the recent band.
Would change its mind if: A close below -1.00pp would confirm renewed flattening; a sustained push above -0.60pp with positive 21d/63d change would confirm steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady, monotonic decline, now at -0.32, a fresh 52-week low, from -0.21 thirteen weeks ago. Momentum is intact and even accelerating slightly: the 4-week change (-0.06) is close to the average pace of the 13-week change (-0.11 over ~13 weeks), showing no deceleration in the easing trend. Range position is at 0.00, meaning current level sits at the very bottom of its 52-week range, confirming this is the loosest financial conditions have been in a year. Distance from zero (-0.32) shows conditions are well below the historical average, i.e., looser than normal, and getting looser each week without a single uptick in the last 13 prints. My prior call (down, 0.40) was correct — the print has moved further below -0.20 and the 4w change remains negative, so the falsifier condition was not triggered. Given the unbroken monotonic run of declines across all 13 observations with no sign of stabilization or reversal, I am increasing conviction that this trend continues into month end. There is no policy language suggesting any shift; the cited language is generic and unchanged, offering no new signal either way.
Would change its mind if: A print rising back above -0.25 or 4w change turning positive/flat would signal the easing trend is stalling or reversing.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call has been overtaken by events: the falsifier I set was a break below 2.30, and breakevens have now printed 2.29, 2.26, 2.24 — a clean, accelerating decline through that level. The last four readings (2.30, 2.29, 2.26, 2.24) show consistent downward momentum, not noise. be_change_21d is -0.12pp and the most recent three prints alone account for most of that move, suggesting the decline is fresh and ongoing rather than stalling. The 63d change (+0.08) is stale, reflecting the earlier plateau near 2.32-2.35 before the recent breakdown; the 252d change (-0.34) confirms the broader trend has been down over the year, and the recent action looks like a resumption of that trend after a mid-period pause. be_range_position at 0.29 shows breakevens sitting in the lower third of the 12-month range, and falling further. Distance from the 2% target is still +0.24pp, giving room for further compression before expectations would look anchored at target. With no sign of stabilization in the last three prints, I expect the move to continue or at least hold, not revert, over the next month.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 2.30 over the next few readings would signal the recent decline was noise rather than a new trend.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release headline CPI y/y rose from 3.29 to 3.72 (+0.43pp), driven by a firm 0.48% m/m print — well above the 3m average of 0.30% and above the falsifier threshold I set. My prior "up" call was correct: disinflation had stalled and reversed, not resumed. The trend since the trough (3.07%) is now two consecutive increases, and the m/m readings (0.16, 0.22, 0.20, 0.48) show acceleration in the most recent month specifically. Core PCE y/y continues to grind down (4.38→4.30), and core 3m annualized (2.80%) is much cooler than headline's recent pop, suggesting the headline jump may partly reflect volatile components (energy/food) rather than broad-based re-acceleration. Still, headline is the metric being asked about, and its momentum is clearly upward now: outgoing m/m of 0.48% versus a 3m average of 0.30% implies base effects will push y/y higher again next month unless the comparable month a year ago was unusually high. Given the sharp jump and the base effect arithmetic (a weak base print rolling out is likely, given the sequence), I lean toward another increase in headline y/y, though core's continued deceleration tempers full conviction.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.20% combined with core PCE y/y falling below 4.2% would indicate the reversal was a one-off, not renewed acceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_mom_3m_avg, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print rose to 3.70%, a new 12-month high, up from 3.50% the prior month and 0.10pp above the previous 3-month/6-month/12-month range. This confirms the loosening scenario I flagged as my falsifier last time — my prior "flat" call was too cautious given the data was already sitting at the top of its range. The Sahm-rule-style gap (current rate minus 12m low) is now 0.30pp, still below the classic 0.50pp recession-signal threshold, but the move is not noise: it's a fresh cycle high following a sequence that had been rangebound 3.40-3.60% for a year. The level itself (3.70%) remains close to estimates of the natural rate (near 4%), so this is not an alarming absolute level, but the direction and the fact that this is the highest print in the window matters more than the level alone per my mandate. Policy language still describes the labor market as robust with low unemployment, which is backward-looking and doesn't capture this print. One month's uptick could be noise, so I won't go to high conviction, but the balance of evidence points to a modest loosening trend continuing rather than reversing sharply.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.50% would suggest this was noise; a print above 3.80% would confirm accelerating loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_12m_high, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y has drifted down over the most recent readings (4.34 → 4.09), giving back some of the run-up that justified my prior "up" call. The 21-day change is now essentially flat (+0.01pp), showing the recent uptrend has stalled, even though 63d and 252d changes remain solidly positive (+0.48pp, +0.97pp), reflecting the larger multi-month rise. Range position at 0.80 still shows yields sitting in the upper portion of the 12-month range, well above the 12m low, which argues against a genuine reversal in term premium — this looks more like a modest pullback within an elevated range than a new downtrend. The 30s10s slope is a modest +0.11pp, not signaling aggressive long-end cheapening pressure but not flagging flight-to-quality either. Continued balance-sheet runoff remains a background force keeping term premium biased upward structurally, even as short-term momentum has cooled. My prior "up" call was too confident given that the last several prints show yields easing, not extending. Given the flat 21d change and the pullback from the 12m high, I lean toward a smaller, more moderate upward bias rather than a strong one — the level remains elevated in-range, but momentum has faded enough that a continued grind higher is less assured than last time.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 3.70% with range_position under 0.60 and negative 21d/63d change would revive the downward case; a fresh push to new 12m highs would confirm renewed upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Rising expectations would corroborate persistence of the headline acceleration rather than a one-off energy/food shock.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirm whether runoff pace is accelerating, which would firm up the structural upward pressure on long yields.

arbitrated.notes: Netting the panel: the clearest, freshest, high-conviction reads point toward lower nominal yields, while the pro-yield forces are stale, contradicted, or thin. Balance sheet runoff (conviction 0.78, formed this meeting, 13 straight confirming prints) is the strongest single signal and is mechanically a modest structural upward pressure on term premium/long yields via supply, but term_premium's own analyst — who should own the level call on the long end — has already downgraded from "up" to a soft +0.25 precisely because dgs10 has fallen 4.34→4.09 with flat 21d momentum, and explicitly lacks the balance_sheet and financial_conditions inputs that might have kept conviction higher. Financial conditions are at fresh 52-week lows and easing monotonically (0.55 down), which historically coincides with lower long yields via lower risk premia, and this directly undercuts the term-premium analyst's "upward bias" framing since they were missing this input. Inflation expectations (breakevens) are breaking down through the analyst's own falsifier level, three prints running, fresh and accelerating — a genuine down signal for nominal yields via lower expected inflation compensation. Labor is loosening (unemployment at a 12m high, up 0.55) which is disinflationary/dovish for the front end. The lone up-pressure driver, headline inflation, is 16 days stale, contradicted by falling breakevens and continuing core PCE deceleration, and the analyst itself flags it may be a one-off energy/food pop rather than persistent — I discount it. Curve slope is not my seat. On net: balance-sheet supply pressure is real but is outweighed by fresher, more numerous signals (financial conditions, breakevens, labor) all pointing to lower yields, and the term-premium analyst — best positioned to synthesize the long end — has already moved toward a weaker upward call given the same data. I read a modest net-down bias in nominal yields into month end.

discounted.why: 16 days stale, contradicted by fresher breakeven decline and continuing core PCE deceleration.
Out of scope for a level call; range-bound with no directional breakout anyway.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in NFCI back above -0.25, breakevens reversing above 2.30, and/or fresh CPI acceleration with core PCE turning up together would flip this to neutral/up.

risks.text: Balance-sheet runoff could accelerate or Fed could signal a hawkish hold, reversing the easing-conditions narrative.
A one-off inflation print could prove the start of renewed persistent acceleration, not noise.
Term premium analyst missing key inputs; a corrected read could show stronger upward pressure than assumed.

trade.rationale: Modest net-down level view: easing financial conditions, falling breakevens, and loosening labor outweigh balance-sheet-driven supply pressure and stale inflation call.

why.balance_sheet: Fresh, high-conviction, unbroken 13-print QT trend; structurally pushes term premium/long yields up via supply, but is only one force.
why.financial_conditions: Fresh 52w-low, monotonic easing with no deceleration; looser conditions typically coincide with lower yields/risk premia.
why.inflation: Stale (16d), contradicted by fresher breakevens and core PCE deceleration; analyst flags possible one-off — heavily discounted.
why.term_premium: Analyst's own call softened to near-flat given stalled 21d momentum and pullback from highs; missing balance_sheet/FC inputs, so I do not lean on it much.
why.labor_tightness: Fresh cycle-high unemployment print signals loosening labor market, dovish/disinflationary for front-end and overall yield level.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.80
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series continues its steady decline: from 8,298,312 to 8,002,064 over 13 observations, with no rebound at any point. The most recent 4-week change (-119,252) is actually larger in magnitude than the trailing 13-week average pace (-338,850/13 ≈ -26,065/wk vs -119,252/4 ≈ -29,813/wk), indicating the runoff has not slowed and may have modestly accelerated in the latest month. The 52-week change (-793,503) confirms this is a sustained, large-scale, multi-quarter runoff, not noise. Assets sit $756,989M below the 52-week high with no sign of basing. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury, agency debt, and agency MBS holdings per previously announced plans — no mention of tapering the cap or pausing runoff. My prior call (down, 0.78) was correct: assets fell further, and the falsifier condition (rebound toward 8.3-8.5T or dovish language change) did not occur. Given the consistent monthly cap-driven decline and unchanged reinvestment language, I expect continued balance sheet contraction into next month-end, likely another decline in the $100-150B range based on recent pace.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~8.3T, or new statement language pausing, slowing, or ending the runoff pace.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has continued its steepening trend, moving from -0.45 to -0.44pp, the least inverted print in the series. This confirms the steepening move flagged last time: 21d change +0.34pp and 63d change +0.62pp both remain strongly positive, and the spread has now cleared my prior -0.60pp threshold for confirming steepening, sitting at the 0.77 percentile of its 12-month range (near the -0.25pp high, well above the -1.08pp low). My previous "flat" call was too cautious — the steepening trend was already well underway and has persisted for multiple consecutive prints without reversal. The pace of steepening has slowed somewhat in the last two observations (-0.45 to -0.44, only +0.01pp) versus the sharper moves earlier in the window, suggesting the move may be decelerating near the top of its range, but the trend direction remains intact. The 10s30s slope at +0.14pp (positive, long end still normally sloped) is consistent with broad curve normalization rather than a long-end-specific distortion. Given the deceleration near the range highs, I'll temper conviction slightly, but the directional evidence still favors continued steepening over the next month.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.55pp or a sustained negative 21d change would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this steepening call.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down/easing continuation) has been contradicted: NFCI bottomed at -0.34 and has risen for two consecutive prints to -0.32, with nfci_change_4w now +0.01 (positive) versus the deeply negative 13-week change of -0.10. This is exactly the reversal condition I flagged as a falsifier. The index remains well below zero (-0.32, distance_from_zero -0.32), so conditions are still looser than historical average and near the 52-week low (only +0.01 above it), but the momentum has clearly shifted from easing to a mild tightening/stabilization in the most recent two readings. The longer 13-week trend still shows substantial net easing, so this could be noise around a trough rather than a durable turn. Policy language is unchanged and generic, offering no new signal on financial conditions specifically. Given the short two-print uptick against a much larger multi-week easing move, I read this as a modest tightening lean for the next month rather than a strong reversal call, acknowledging my prior down call was wrong for this most recent stretch.
Would change its mind if: A print falling back below -0.34 (new 52w low) or 4w change turning negative again would signal the uptick was noise, not a turn.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.55) was wrong: breakeven_10y has since printed 2.34, 2.39, 2.35 — the exact reversal back above 2.30 I flagged as my falsifier. The last 10 readings show a choppy but modestly rising pattern (2.36→2.35 net, but with the 21d change +0.09pp and 63d change +0.13pp both positive), while the 252d change is nearly flat (+0.02pp), indicating the recent upward drift is a short-term move within a longer stable range rather than a durable regime shift. The series sits at 0.51 of its 12-month range (2.10-2.59), squarely in the middle — no extreme positioning either way. Distance from the 2% target remains a notable +0.35pp, keeping breakevens elevated versus objective, which caps how much further downside conviction is warranted since the level itself doesn't look like it's collapsing toward target. Given the latest print (2.35) is a pullback from the recent local high (2.39), and the short-term momentum is positive but noisy with no clear trend acceleration, I read this as most consistent with a flat-to-slightly-up near-term path. I don't have enough evidence of a decisive breakout to call a strong directional move.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move outside the recent 2.33-2.39 band, especially a break above 2.45 or below 2.30, would confirm a new trend rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y ticked down slightly (3.72→3.69) despite a firm 0.39% m/m print, and core PCE y/y fell to 3.83 from 4.30 — a sizable step down that breaches the falsifier I set (core PCE below 4.2%) even though the m/m print was above the 0.20% threshold I specified. My prior "up" call was wrong: the bounce in headline m/m (0.48, 0.39 in the last two prints) did not translate into renewed y/y acceleration; instead headline y/y is flat-to-down from its recent local low and core continues to decelerate meaningfully (core_yoy_change_3m -0.90pp). The 3-month annualized core rate of 2.05% is right at target, a much better signal than the noisier headline print, and I weight it heavily given the mandate's PCE framing. Headline is being held up by base effects and volatile components while the underlying trend (core) is cooling. Headline's own 3m annualized (4.36%) is still elevated versus core, reflecting energy/food swings, but the y/y series shows headline near its 12-month low with no clear acceleration signal. On balance, momentum favors continued disinflation or flatness in headline y/y at the next print, not acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.45% combined with core PCE y/y rising back above 4.0% would signal renewed acceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, headline_yoy_change_3m, headline_mom, yoy_range_position

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print held at 3.70%, matching the prior month rather than falling back to 3.50% or accelerating above 3.80%. That validates the prior "up" call: the unemployment rate is now at its 12-month high, sitting at the top of the observed range (range_position 1.00), and up 0.20pp over both 6 and 12 months, with a 0.30pp rise off the trailing 12-month low. The Sahm-gap of 0.23pp is approaching but not yet at the classic 0.50pp recession-signal threshold, so this looks like a gradual loosening rather than a sharp deterioration. Still, two consecutive prints at the cycle high, after a period of noisy oscillation between 3.40-3.60%, is a real directional shift rather than noise. The level itself (3.70%) remains only modestly above the ~4% natural rate, so this is not yet a tight-to-loose regime change, but the trend is clearly no longer flat. Absent a pullback, momentum favors continued mild upward drift or at least stabilization at this higher plateau. I lean toward another flat-to-up print, with some chance of a technical retracement given two months already flat at 3.70%.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.50% would signal reversion/noise; a print above 3.80% would confirm accelerating loosening and raise conviction sharply.
Leaned on: unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, unrate_12m_high, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year yield has continued climbing since my last read, moving from the mid-4.4s to 4.59% and sitting at 4.61% two readings back — essentially at the 12-month high (range_position 0.98). My prior "up" call was correct and the fresh data confirms rather than reverses it: 21d change is still strongly positive (+0.47pp), 63d change is +0.78pp, and 252d change is +0.62pp, all pointing the same direction with no sign of reversal. The pace of increase has moderated slightly in the last two prints (4.59, 4.59 — flat), suggesting the move may be losing some momentum near the top of the range, but there is no pullback yet. The 30s10s slope remains modestly positive (+0.14pp), consistent with steepening pressure at the long end rather than curve flattening that would suggest demand-driven richening. Continued balance-sheet runoff (as noted in the policy language) keeps a structural bid against duration, supporting further term-premium pressure or at least a floor under yields. Given the level is pinned at a 12-month high with positive momentum across all horizons, but the last two observations are flat rather than rising, I lean toward continued modest upward pressure into month-end, with reduced conviction given the recent stalling.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 4.40% with range_position under 0.60 and negative 21d/63d change would reverse this; a fresh push to new 12m highs above 4.65% would strengthen it.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Reconciling for a level (duration) call, I set aside curve_slope and inflation_expectations entirely — the first is explicitly a curve-shape signal (out of scope for a level view) and the second is a real-vs-nominal/inflation-market seat, not level. That leaves balance_sheet, financial_conditions, inflation, labor_tightness, and term_premium as inputs to the nominal yield level.

Balance sheet runoff is fresh, high-conviction, and unambiguous: continued QT drains duration-absorbing capacity and is a structural upward pressure on yields — this is the strongest, least stale read on the panel. Term premium (10y itself) is corroborating and fresh: DGS10 sitting at a 12-month high with strong 21d/63d/252d momentum, though the analyst flags recent flattening in momentum, tempering conviction modestly. These two point the same direction and are mutually reinforcing (QT reduces the bid for duration, pushing term premium/yields up), so I weight them heavily.

Financial conditions gives a mild tightening lean but is a fragile 2-print reversal against a much larger 13-week easing trend — I read it as directionally supportive of higher yields but low-conviction and noisy, so I downweight but don't discard it.

Inflation and labor_tightness are the softer, more stale reads (labor formed 22 days ago, inflation 15 days ago) and both point to flat-ish, non-accelerating price/labor pressure — core PCE decelerating meaningfully, headline near 12-month lows, unemployment drifting up but not sharply. Neither argues for higher yields on its own; if anything they lean toward a ceiling on how far yields can rise from the demand side. They don't contradict the supply-side (balance sheet) story enough to override it, but they cap my conviction.

Net: modest upward lean on the level, driven by structural QT and momentum in the 10y, tempered by soft inflation/labor and a fragile financial-conditions signal.

Belongs to the real-vs-nominal split, a different pod's seat, not the level call.

arbitrated.falsifier: A rebound in fed assets toward 8.3T+ or dovish QT-pause language, combined with DGS10 breaking back below 4.40%, would flip this to a lower-yield read.

risks.text: A Fed pivot on QT pace or balance-sheet composition would remove the main upward driver quickly.
Term premium momentum has already stalled over the last two prints, risking a plateau rather than continued rise.
Financial conditions signal is only two prints old and could revert to the larger easing trend, undercutting the tightening lean.

trade.rationale: Structural QT and 10y momentum argue for modestly higher yields across the curve; weight tilted to the long end where the fresh term-premium and balance-sheet signals are most direct.

why.balance_sheet: Fresh, unambiguous, accelerating runoff — I read this as the primary upward pressure on yields (higher assets decline = higher yields), sign flipped to yield direction.
why.term_premium: 10y at 12m high with strong multi-horizon momentum but two flat prints; corroborates balance_sheet, tempered for stalling.
why.financial_conditions: Two-print tightening reversal against a much larger easing trend; directionally supportive but noisy and low conviction.
why.inflation: Core PCE decelerating meaningfully, headline near 12m lows; slightly caps upward yield pressure, mostly a wash.
why.labor_tightness: Unemployment drifting to cycle high but gradual; barely nudges level, stale (22d) so discounted heavily.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.82
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print continues the steady QT trend: total assets fell to 7,907,830M from 7,933,162M, a 4-week decline of ~94,234M, consistent with recent pace. The 13-week change (-335,514M, -4.07%) and 52-week change (-815,260M) confirm a persistent, roughly linear runoff with no deceleration visible in the last several observations — each successive reading is lower than the last, and the pace of decline (roughly 90-100k per 4 weeks) has been remarkably steady. Assets remain ~826,000M below the 52-week high, reinforcing the runoff narrative. The policy language is unchanged, explicitly reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS holdings per previously announced plans — no hint of tapering, pausing, or ending the runoff. My prior call of "down" at 0.80 conviction was correct: the balance sheet continued to shrink exactly as expected, with no rebound toward 8.3T and no dovish shift in language. Given the consistency of the monthly cap-driven runoff and the absence of any statement change, I expect the same trajectory next month: total assets should decline by roughly 80,000-100,000M from the current 7,907,830M level, landing somewhere near 7,810,000-7,830,000M at month-end.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~8.3T, or new statement language pausing, slowing, or ending the runoff pace.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was directionally correct: slope_2s10s printed -0.19 at the most recent reading, near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.94), and remains well off the -1.08 low. However, the last observation ticked down from -0.15 to -0.19, a modest 4bp re-flattening move within an otherwise steepening trend. Looking at the full run, the series has been broadly steepening over medium horizons (63d change +0.68pp, 252d change +0.15pp) even though the most recent few prints (-0.13, -0.16, -0.15, -0.15, -0.19) show stalling/consolidation rather than continued acceleration. The curve remains inverted (-0.19pp) but is near its least-inverted level of the past year, having moved up substantially from the -1.08 low. The FOMC language shift (from "raise" to "maintain" the funds rate, and description of activity from "moderate" to "solid" pace) suggests a pause/hold posture, which historically supports gradual continued steepening as front-end pressure eases, though the immediate print doesn't yet confirm fresh momentum. Given the mixed short-term signal (recent tick down) against the medium-term uptrend and high range position, I maintain a steepening lean but with reduced conviction given the stall.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below -0.35pp or a sustained negative 21d change over the next month would signal renewed flattening and invalidate this steepening call.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_change_21d

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The series bottomed at -0.34 mid-window and has climbed steadily for six consecutive readings to -0.28, matching the series' opening level. The 4-week change is now +0.04, confirming the tightening tilt I flagged last time, though the 13-week change is still slightly negative (-0.02), reflecting the earlier easing phase before the trough. Momentum is clearly positive and consistent (six straight upticks with no reversal), and the level remains well below zero (-0.28), so conditions are still looser than average in absolute terms, but the direction of travel is unambiguously toward tightening. My prior falsifier (a new 52w low or negative 4w change) did not occur — instead the index kept rising, so the prior "up" call was validated. Range position at 0.24 (of 0-1) shows we're still closer to the loose end of the 52-week range, but the steady grind upward from the low is the dominant signal for the next month. Absent any reversal signal in the last three prints, I expect this grind to continue modestly rather than snap back.

arbitrated.notes: My mandate is the net directional level of nominal Treasury yields, drawing on inflation, labor tightness, term premium, financial conditions, and balance sheet — leaving curve shape and breakevens to other seats even though I heard them. The balance-sheet analyst delivers the freshest, highest-conviction, mechanically-grounded read: QT continues on an unbroken linear pace with no policy language change, which is a genuine independent driver of long-end term premium via supply. Term premium's own analyst flags they lack the balance-sheet input, and now that I have it, it confirms rather than contradicts their modest "up" lean — deceleration in dgs10 momentum is real but the structural supply story (continued runoff, positive 30s10s slope) argues against a reversal, just a pause. Financial conditions is also live and freshly trending: six consecutive tightening prints is a real, non-stale signal, and tightening financial conditions historically coincide with or follow higher yields (or at least aren't a headwind to elevated yields). Labor tightness is the loudest call (0.60 "up" on unemployment, meaning labor market loosening), but rising unemployment is disinflationary and dovish for the Fed — pulling the level down, not up. This creates real tension: balance sheet and term premium argue for a firmer/higher level from supply and momentum, while a loosening labor market argues for lower yields on Fed-easing expectations. Inflation itself is soft and falling, reinforcing the labor-market case for lower yields. Weighing it: I read the net as modestly higher yields — the supply/QT/term-premium channel is a slower-moving, mechanically confirmed force with no offsetting reversal, whereas the labor/inflation dovish case, while real, is more about future Fed action than immediate level pressure, and the panel is split enough that I size down rather than lean hard either way.

discounted.why: Curve/breakeven-adjacent driver outside my mandate; also range-bound noise, no trend.
Explicitly out of scope for a level view; shape not level.

arbitrated.falsifier: A reversal in balance-sheet runoff (rebound toward 8.3T or paused-taper language) combined with a labor print back at/below 3.70% would flip this toward lower yields.

risks.text: Fed could pivot dovish on rising unemployment faster than QT-driven supply pressure can offset, pulling yields down.
Term premium momentum has decelerated; a fresh break below 4.70 on DGS10 would reverse the supply-driven thesis.
Panel is genuinely split between supply/tightening forces and labor-driven easing expectations, weakening conviction.

trade.rationale: Modest net-higher-yield view driven by continued QT/supply pressure and tightening financial conditions, tempered by labor-market loosening; long-end weighted higher given term-premium/supply channel.

why.balance_sheet: Fresh, high-conviction, mechanically confirmed QT runoff continuing linearly; supports higher term premium via less demand absorption of duration supply.
why.term_premium: Momentum has stalled but level high in range; balance-sheet gap now filled and confirms continuation, not reversal.
why.financial_conditions: Six consecutive tightening prints is fresh, consistent momentum; modest supportive lean toward higher yields.
why.labor_tightness: Real, high-conviction loosening signal is dovish for Fed path, pulling level down; weighed against supply-side forces.
why.inflation: Disinflation trend firming reinforces case for lower yields, but somewhat stale (16 days) and modest conviction.

Term premium itself calls for down, but explicitly flags it's missing the balance_sheet and financial_conditions reads; balance_sheet analyst is running hot (high conviction, unbroken runoff, no deceleration) which is a structural upward pressure on term premium via supply that term_premium didn't fully price in. This is the classic gap: term_premium's "down" call is undercut by an omitted driver that argues the opposite direction. Netting it out: disinflation and soft labor argue for modestly lower yields, while relentless QT runoff (supply) argues for a term-premium floor or mild upward pressure, and financial conditions easing is a small offsetting force. Given the gap flagged by term_premium itself, I discount its "down" conviction and let the balance-sheet supply story pull the level view back toward flat-to-mildly-lower rather than a confident decline. Net: modest bias toward slightly lower yields, low-to-moderate conviction given the offsetting forces and no driver showing a clean, undisputed directional trend beyond the disinflation grind.

trade.rationale: Modest bias toward lower yields from disinflation grind, tempered by QT-driven supply pressure on term premium; small same-sign duration long across both tenors, sized down given panel disagreement.

why.inflation: CPI y/y and core PCE both grinding lower monotonically, benign momentum, no acceleration signal per analyst.

why.balance_sheet: High-conviction unbroken QT runoff is a structural supply/term-premium upward force, offsetting term_premium's down call.

arbitrated.notes: Synthesizing the level view: term_premium's own read (10Y drifting from 3.91 to 3.88, negative 21d/63d changes, range position near low) is the most direct evidence on nominal yield levels, and it explicitly flags two unmet dependencies — balance_sheet and financial_conditions — both of which point the OPPOSITE direction from what term_premium concluded. So the yield-decline narrative term_premium built partly rests on a channel (risk-off buying of duration) that the fresher financial_conditions read contradicts (conditions are easing/loosening, not tightening/panicking). That weakens conviction in "down" specifically, leaving a mixed picture: mechanical QT argues up, easing financial conditions argue mixed-to-down, and term_premium's own price action (still net negative 21d/63d) argues down but decelerating with a small 3-print bounce. Inflation is flat/uncertain (headline up, core down) and inflation_expectations lean down modestly — a soft disinflation backdrop that caps upward yield pressure. Labor is drifting toward loosening (mild), consistent with a slightly softer growth path, again capping yields. Net: I read this as a modest down lean, but conviction is low given the direct contradiction between the freshest supply-side driver (balance_sheet, up-pressure) and the price-action driver (term_premium, down), with disagreement being the dominant feature rather than a clean signal.

However, the most recent readings show the spread has slipped back to -0.28 from a recent high near -0.14, and the 63d change is now -0.13pp — a clear reversal within the broader trend. The 21d change is still marginally positive (+0.07pp), but the last two prints (-0.30, -0.28) show the curve re-flattening after touching -0.14 a few observations back. My prior falsifier (drop below -0.42 or sustained negative 21d) hasn't technically triggered, so the long-term steepening thesis isn't invalidated, but the near-term momentum has clearly turned against it. The FOMC language shift (from "growth has slowed" to "solid pace," dropping tightening-bias language toward a cut threshold) suggests the market may be pricing a slightly more hawkish near-term hold, which could keep the front end pinned and cap further steepening. Given the recent pullback and mixed short-term signal, I'm lowering conviction on further steepening this month and leaning toward flat-to-slightly-down rather than continued up.

arbitrated.notes: Net read is modestly toward lower nominal yields over the next month, but conviction is capped by genuine disagreement and mixed short-term momentum across drivers. The strongest, freshest signals are: (1) financial conditions (NFCI) grinding to a fresh 52-week low with monotonic, non-stalling weekly prints — a real, live easing-of-conditions signal that historically associates with lower term premia and softer yields; and (2) inflation, where core PCE deceleration is sustained and unbroken even though headline CPI's y/y range position is flat and momentum indicators are mixed. Term premium itself reports a choppy picture — 21d momentum up, 63d down, 252d up, mid-range level — and explicitly flags missing balance_sheet and financial_conditions context. Given financial conditions shows sustained easing and balance_sheet confirms continued QT (a structural upward pressure on long-end supply/term premium), I weight term_premium's own "down" call down-weighted, treating it as partially informed by a driver (financial conditions) that itself argues for lower yields, netting out to a modest net-down lean rather than the analyst's own moderate conviction number. Labor tightness and curve_slope give genuinely flat/no-view level signals (labor's 3m trend reversed, curve's own axis is off-limits anyway). Balance sheet's runoff is a real, structural force that argues for some upward pressure at the long end via increased net Treasury supply, partially offsetting the disinflation/easing-FC forces — hence I don't go full "down" on the level, just modestly so. The overall picture: two live, monotonic, non-stale drivers (financial conditions easing, inflation decelerating) outweigh one structural but well-understood offsetting force (balance sheet runoff), while term premium and labor data are too mixed/reversed to add directional conviction on their own.

The statement language is unchanged, reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency MBS holdings with no mention of slowing, pausing, or ending runoff. That is exactly the reacceleration signal I flagged. The 3-month annualized headline rate (3.71%) now sits above the latest y/y (3.16%), and headline_mom_3m_avg (0.30%) plus the most recent 0.41% print show momentum building after a soft patch (0.14-0.19% in the prior three months). Core PCE y/y did tick up slightly too (3.11→3.16), interrupting its multi-month deceleration trend, though core_yoy_change_3m is still -0.32pp reflecting the broader downtrend over the quarter. Headline sits at the bottom of its 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.05), so the recent uptick is a change in trajectory, not yet a return to prior highs. Given two consecutive months of accelerating m/m prints and a rise in both headline and core y/y readings, I read this as tentative reacceleration rather than noise, though one month of core PCE ticking up could just be residual seasonality. I'll weight the momentum signal (3m annualized, m/m trend) over the still-favorable y/y distance from last year's peak.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below ~0.20% or core PCE y/y resuming its decline would indicate this was noise, not reacceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom_3m_avg, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, core_yoy_change_3m

Call: up, conviction 0.55

Report:
  The latest print rose to 3.90%, matching the 12-month high and up from 3.70% the prior month. That's the second consecutive increase noted as a trigger in my prior falsifier, and it confirms the loosening trend rather than a one-off blip. The 3-month change (+0.20pp), 6-month change (+0.20pp), and 12-month change (+0.30pp) all point the same direction, and the Sahm gap has widened to 0.40pp — approaching but not yet at the classic 0.50pp recession-signal threshold. Range position is now at 1.00 (sitting at the 12-month high), and the rate is only 0.10pp below the natural-rate anchor of ~4%, meaning further modest increases would push it above trend. My prior "flat" call was too cautious; I underweighted the persistence of the drift up. Policy language still describes the labor market as strong with low unemployment, but that language is backward-looking and hasn't yet caught up to the two-month acceleration. Given the confirmed pattern of consecutive increases, proximity to the natural rate, and the Sahm-rule-style gap widening, I now see a clear loosening signal rather than noise. I'll call this an "up" move with moderate-to-clear conviction, though not extreme since the moves are still small in absolute pp terms and one print could reverse.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.70% would confirm this was noise; a further rise toward/above 4.0% or sahm_gap crossing 0.50 would confirm accelerating loosening.

arbitrated.notes: The panel splits into an easing-financial/balance-sheet cluster pointing toward lower yields and a firming labor/inflation cluster pointing toward higher yields, with term premium itself flat/directionless. Balance sheet is the freshest, highest-conviction, mechanically clear signal: steady runoff continues to drain reserves and, all else equal, exerts upward pressure on term premium/long yields via reduced official-sector demand for duration — note this cuts against, not with, the "down" framing the analyst gave it, since balance-sheet shrinkage is a yield-supportive (upward) force on the long end, not a yield-suppressing one. Financial conditions easing (NFCI at 52-week lows) is real and monotonic, but NFCI eases partly *because* long yields have been contained; it's more a symptom than a fresh independent pull lower, and it's not obviously talking about the same transmission channel as the level of nominal yields. Labor tightness (unemployment drifting up, Sahm gap widening) is a genuine, fresh (21-day-old but confirmed two-print) loosening-labor signal that argues for a dovish Fed reaction eventually, but the term-premium and inflation analysts show no confirming break yet — CPI reaccelerated last print (up call, but lacks inflation-expectations corroboration, and breakevens are flat), and the term premium/10y level itself is rangebound, having failed both the up-break and down-break triggers. Given the explicit gap flags (term premium missing balance-sheet and financial-conditions inputs), I weight the mechanical duration-supply logic of continued QT more heavily than the term-premium analyst's own flat call, and I lean toward a modest net higher-yield bias, tempered by clearly stale-ish labor data and directionally opposed FCI/labor signals. Net: mild upward drift, low-to-moderate conviction, not a strong directional call.

arbitrated.notes: Synthesizing across drivers relevant to nominal Treasury level (curve_slope and inflation_expectations are out of scope for this seat). Inflation: the headline CPI print is a genuine acceleration (m/m and 3m annualized both rising), though core PCE is decelerating — the headline read is fresher and directly on the forecast target, so I keep an "up" lean but temper it since the underlying persistence measure (core) argues the opposite. This is a modestly hawkish input for yields. Labor: unemployment has plateaued at cycle high rather than continuing to climb, so the loosening thesis is intact but stalled — a mild disinflationary/dovish-for-yields signal, but with low momentum behind it. Balance sheet: this is the cleanest, least stale, most confirmed signal — QT continues at a steady-to-accelerating pace with unchanged policy language reaffirming no pause. Persistent balance-sheet runoff is a structural technical that removes a buyer and adds duration supply pressure, pushing long-end yields (and by extension the broader curve) modestly higher over the coming month. Term premium's own read is flat/directionless with low conviction, but it explicitly says it lacks the balance_sheet input and financial_conditions input — both of which argue for mild upward pressure (continued runoff = supply pressure; financial conditions loose but stalling, not clearly disinflationary). Financial conditions itself is flat — easing has stalled near multi-year lows, so no strong impulse either way, but the loose backdrop caps how high yields can run. Netting these: balance sheet's steady, confirmed, high-conviction "down" (i.e., shrinking assets = QT continuing) argues for higher yield pressure structurally, inflation's fresh reacceleration modestly reinforces upward yield pressure, while labor's stall and financial conditions' flatness argue for a much more limited overall move. I land on a modest net-up call on the level of yields, low-to-moderate conviction given the genuine cross-currents (core PCE deceleration, term premium's own flat read) tempering the balance-sheet-driven thesis.

=== balance_sheet ===

The prior call (down, 0.78) was correct: assets kept falling as predicted, and the pace did not decelerate meaningfully. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings "as described in its previously announced plans" — no hint of tapering the runoff or pausing. Given the consistency of the trend, the unchanged reinvestment language, and the absence of any rebound signal, I expect assets to be modestly lower again at next month-end, roughly in line with the recent ~$60-80B monthly pace. The latest 4-week change is somewhat smaller than the prior 13-week average pace, but not enough to suggest an inflection — still comfortably within normal QT cadence.
Would change its mind if: A rebound in fed_assets back toward/above ~7.7T, or new statement language pausing, slowing, or ending the runoff pace.

arbitrated.notes:
The level call is dominated by the term-premium analyst, whose read is the freshest, highest-conviction, and directly observable in price (DGS10 at 4.69, near 12m highs, with consistent positive momentum across 21/63/252-day windows). That analyst explicitly flags a dependency on balance-sheet pace as a structural tailwind for duration supply, and the balance-sheet analyst independently confirms steady, uninterrupted QT runoff with no sign of deceleration — the two drivers reinforce rather than conflict, closing the named gap in the same (upward-yield) direction. Financial conditions also lean up (tightening off the trough), which is consistent with — though smaller in magnitude than — the term-premium move; it corroborates rather than drives. Inflation and inflation-expectations are more ambiguous: headline CPI/core PCE are flat-to-stalled (30-35 days horizon, modest conviction), while breakeven inflation has plateaued after an uptrend. Labor tightness is flat, plateaued at 3.90%, offering no directional push either way. Curve slope is a curve-shape signal and outside this seat's mandate, so I set it aside for the level call, though its flattening bias is not inconsistent with front-end anchoring against back-end pressure.

Netting these: the two freshest, most concrete, and mutually corroborating drivers (term premium, balance sheet) point to a modestly higher yield level into month-end, with financial conditions adding a small confirming nudge. Inflation and labor data are stale-ish and non-directional, so I discount them rather than let them cancel the level view. This supports a mild bullish-yield (higher rates) level call, not a strong one — the underlying inflation/labor picture is not confirming acceleration, so I keep conviction moderate rather than high.
