user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: assets fell again to 7,284,319 from 7,299,566, continuing an unbroken multi-quarter decline. The 4-week change (-78,155) and 13-week change (-283,488) confirm runoff is still active, though the pace has moderated versus prior periods, consistent with the new statement language slowing the Treasury redemption cap from $60B to $25B starting in [date] while keeping the agency cap at $35B. This is a slower but still clearly negative trajectory — QT continues, just at a reduced monthly rate. The statement change is exactly the kind of "slowing" language I flagged as a potential reason to soften conviction, and it has materialized. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-1,105,006) with no sign of reversal or pause. Given the mechanical, pre-announced nature of the caps, next month's print should again be lower, but the reduced cap size means the magnitude of decline should shrink versus the prior few months. I'm maintaining a down call but trimming conviction to reflect that the pace of decline — the thing I'm asked to weight most — is now decelerating, even as direction stays negative.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (-0.38) actually ticked up from the prior reading (-0.37→-0.38 is a slight dip, but looking at the full recent path: -0.47, -0.40, -0.35, -0.37, -0.38, the spread has bounced off the recent low of -0.47 and is now oscillating narrowly in the -0.35 to -0.38 band). My prior "down" call was wrong in trend terms — the 21d and 63d changes are modestly negative (-0.05, -0.03pp) but the 252d change is strongly positive (+0.39pp), and the curve sits at 0.74 of its 12-month range (well above the low), consistent with a steepening trend over the past year that has stalled/consolidated recently rather than reversed. The last two prints (-0.37, -0.38) are a marginal flattening but far short of my stated falsifier threshold, and also far from re-testing the -0.47 low. Slowing balance-sheet runoff (redemption cap cut) is a mild technical support for longer-end demand/duration scarcity dynamics, which could lean modestly toward further flattening at the margin, but the effect is subtle. Net: recent action looks like sideways consolidation after a steepening move, not a fresh flattening trend. I'll call this flat with low-modest conviction, acknowledging my prior down call was premature.
Would change its mind if: Two consecutive prints below -0.44 (re-testing the 12m low) would confirm renewed flattening; two consecutive prints above -0.33 would confirm resumed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has been essentially flat-to-marginally-tightening over the past 13 weeks, moving from -0.43 to -0.41. The most recent six readings are pinned at -0.41, showing no momentum in either direction over the last month (nfci_change_4w = +0.00). The longer 13-week change is a negligible +0.01, confirming this is a plateau rather than a trend. Conditions remain well below the zero average (distance_from_zero = -0.41) and near the loose end of the 52-week range (range_position = 0.12), meaning financial conditions are still historically easy despite the tiny uptick from the cycle low.
  
  My prior call of "up" with 0.40 conviction was based on a modest drift off the 52-week low, but the last several weeks show that drift has stalled completely — the index is flatlined at -0.41 with zero week-over-week movement. There's no evidence of renewed tightening momentum, and no evidence of resumed easing either. The series is simply sitting still. Given the flat 4-week change and flat recent prints, I no longer see support for a directional call; the earlier tightening lean should be downgraded to a flat call given the plateau.
Would change its mind if: A clear break above -0.41 sustained for two+ weeks (tightening) or below -0.43 (renewed easing) would revive a directional call.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call has not played out well: since that call, breakeven_10y drifted from 2.36 down to 2.35, and the short-term trend (be_change_21d = -0.03pp) is now negative, though the medium (63d = +0.03pp) and long (252d = +0.13pp) windows remain modestly positive. The series is essentially range-bound in the last few prints (2.32-2.36), sitting at 0.60 of its 12-month range — elevated but not near the highs. There's no fresh breakout above the recent 2.36 ceiling, and the latest two prints (2.36, 2.35) suggest stalling rather than continued ascent. Distance from the 2% target remains meaningfully positive (+0.35pp), so inflation expectations are still anchored above objective, but the near-term momentum has flattened rather than extended. Given the small pullback in the last observation and negative 21-day momentum offsetting the longer-term uptrend, I read this as a plateau with a slight downward tilt in the very near term, not a continuation of the prior uptrend. I don't see enough evidence for a confident directional call either way over the next month — this looks like consolidation just below the recent local high.
Would change its mind if: A sustained move below 2.32 across multiple observations, or a fresh push above 2.38, would clarify the plateau's resolution.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior call (flat, 0.35 conviction) was reasonably vindicated: headline CPI y/y ticked from 3.36 to 3.24, a modest decline rather than the acceleration some of the choppy m/m prints had hinted at. The latest m/m print of 0.05% is notably soft — well below the 3m average of 0.24% and the outgoing 0.22% — which argues for continued deceleration or at least stability near current levels. Headline 3m annualized (2.87%) sits below the y/y rate (3.24%), consistent with disinflationary momentum building. Core PCE also ticked down to 3.01% y/y, its lowest in the window, with core_yoy_change_3m at -0.15pp confirming a gentle downtrend. However, core PCE remains 1pp above target and headline has been rangebound (3.07-3.72%) for over a year with no clear breakout either direction — yoy_range_position of 0.27 suggests we're near the low end of that range, a modestly favorable sign. The policy language shift is notable: new language flags "lack of further progress" on inflation, a hawkish addition suggesting policymakers see stalling disinflation risk, which tempers my read of the soft m/m print. Weighing the soft recent momentum against policymakers' own caution about stalled progress, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for next month's headline print, but without strong conviction given the mixed signals between data momentum and policy framing.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% combined with core PCE reversing upward would indicate reacceleration, overturning this flat-to-down lean.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_yoy_change_3m, yoy_range_position, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The last three prints are flat at 3.90%, so the immediate 3-month trend shows no further deterioration—my prior "flat" call was directionally correct in that unrate did not fall back below 3.70, but it also did not accelerate upward as the alternative scenario in my falsifier described. The 12-month trend, however, remains unambiguously one of gradual loosening: unrate has risen from a 3.50 low to 3.90, a 0.30pp 12-month increase and 0.40pp Sahm gap, sitting at the top of its 12-month range (range_position 1.00). This is off natural-rate territory only slightly (-0.10pp), but the trajectory over the past year is worth taking seriously even though the most recent three readings have plateaued. Plateauing at an elevated (relative to the 12-month low) level is consistent with a market that has already loosened and is now stabilizing at the new, softer level rather than tightening back up. Absent a fresh reversal below 3.70, I do not see strong evidence for renewed tightening (down), but the flatness in the last 3 months argues against calling further loosening (up) with high conviction either. I'll lean flat, modestly acknowledging the plateau, while flagging that the underlying 12-month drift argues for a slight tilt toward continued softening rather than a rebound to tighter conditions.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 3.70% would indicate renewed tightening; a move to/above 4.10% or sahm_gap above 0.50 would confirm renewed loosening acceleration.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.55) was partially right: yields pushed to 4.61 mid-window before pulling back to 4.51, so the last two prints show clear fading rather than confirmation above 4.75. My stated falsifier (drop below 4.55 with follow-through) has now triggered. That said, the broader picture is not a reversal: 63d change is still +0.32pp, 252d change is +0.82pp, and the level sits at 0.66 of its 12-month range — comfortably in the upper half, well above the 12m low. The 21d change is only -0.12pp, a modest cooling rather than a rout. Slope_30s10s at +0.14pp is only mildly positive, not signaling aggressive long-end selling pressure. The policy change to slow balance-sheet runoff (cutting the Treasury redemption cap from $60B to $25B) is a mechanical reduction in net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed's own book, which is a modest tailwind for lower long-end yields (less runoff-driven selling). Combining a recent pullback with reduced QT pressure, I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-down outlook for month-end, but conviction is limited since the longer trend (63d, 252d) still points upward and could resume.
Would change its mind if: A push back above 4.61 with follow-through would revive the uptrend view; a drop below 4.40 would confirm a firmer downside reversal.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  inflation lacks inflation_expectations — Would clarify whether the 'lack of progress' language reflects expectations drift, sharpening the momentum read.
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Confirm whether slower runoff materially reduces net Treasury supply enough to matter for long-end pricing.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment could show flight-to-quality flows affecting long-end demand.

arbitrated.notes:
The panel is dominated by "flat" calls across every rate-relevant driver except balance sheet, which remains directionally down (still-shrinking Fed assets) but at a decelerating pace given the redemption cap cut. Inflation is flat-to-slightly-down (soft m/m CPI, core PCE ticking lower) but tempered by hawkish "lack of progress" language, so I read it as a weak disinflationary lean rather than a clean down call. Labor tightness has plateaued at a softer level after a year of loosening, but the last three prints show no further deterioration — flat with a mild loosening tilt, not tight. Term premium's own analyst walked back an "up" call after a pullback in DGS10, landing flat-to-slightly-down, and explicitly notes the balance-sheet slowdown as a modest tailwind for lower long-end yields, plus an unaddressed dependency on financial conditions (also flat, easy, no momentum). Financial conditions are plateaued at historically loose levels with zero momentum, offering no directional push either way.

Netting this out: the strongest, freshest, most mechanical signal is balance sheet — QT continues (asset shrinkage still active, no pause), which is a genuine if modest steady downward pressure on duration term premium via supply/scarcity dynamics, reinforced by term_premium's own analyst citing the same mechanism as a tailwind for lower long yields. Everything else (inflation, labor, financial conditions, curve slope) is flat with low conviction, offering no counter-force. This tilts the level view modestly lower for nominal yields over the next month — not a strong conviction call, but the balance sheet and term premium reads point the same direction while other drivers are neutral, not contradictory. Curve slope is out of scope for this level view.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (down, 0.65) was correct: total assets fell again, from 7,252,542 to 7,231,163 (-21,379 over the latest week), continuing an unbroken downtrend. The 4-week change (-53,156), 13-week change (-253,576, -3.39%), and 52-week change (-1,109,751) all confirm steady, ongoing runoff with no signs of stalling. Assets remain far below the 52-week high (-1,067,149), consistent with sustained QT. The policy language confirms this trajectory continues, but at a reduced pace: the Treasury redemption cap was cut from $60B/month to $25B/month starting in [date] while the agency MBS/debt cap stays at $35B (with excess reinvested into Treasuries). This is a deceleration of runoff, not a pause or reversal — the Committee explicitly states it "will continue reducing its holdings." Given the announced cap cut, I expect the pace of decline to slow from the ~$50-60B/4wk pace seen recently toward something closer to $25-35B/4wk going forward, but the direction remains firmly down. No indication of reinvestment exceeding runoff or a balance sheet floor being reached yet.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_change_52w, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has steepened steadily over the last several prints, moving from -0.47 to -0.35, a clear break above the prior consolidation range around -0.45/-0.46. This confirms the steepening resumption threshold I set previously (two consecutive prints above -0.33 was the bar; we're not quite there yet at -0.35, but the trajectory and slope_change_63d of +0.04 alongside slope_change_252d of +0.62 both point firmly in the steepening direction). My prior "flat" call was too cautious — the data was already showing a steepening bias building, and it has now extended further. Range position is 0.77, near the top of the 12-month range, indicating the curve has moved well off its most inverted levels toward flat (still -0.35pp inverted, per slope_distance_from_flat). The 10s30s slope at +0.15 (mildly positive) suggests the long end isn't driving inversion, consistent with front-end-led steepening dynamics. The QT taper announcement (reducing redemption caps) reduces the pace of balance-sheet runoff, which structurally supports somewhat more accommodative conditions at the margin and doesn't argue against continued steepening. With three consecutive up-moves in the last three prints and no reversal signal, I lean toward continued steepening into month-end, though the spread remains meaningfully inverted overall.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back below -0.42 for two consecutive prints would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s, slope_distance_from_flat

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has drifted gently upward from -0.43 to -0.39 over the 13-week window, with the most recent two prints both at -0.39 versus -0.41 in the prior several weeks. That confirms the modest tightening drift I flagged as a trigger last time: conditions have now sustained a break above -0.41 for more than two weeks, satisfying my own falsifier for reviving a directional call. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.05) are both positive and consistent in sign, showing gradual, low-magnitude tightening rather than a reversal. Still, the index remains deep in easy territory at -0.39, well below the zero average and near the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position 0.24), so this is a small easing-of-easing move, not a regime shift. With no policy language addressing this driver, I have only the mechanical measurements to go on. Given the consistent, if slight, upward momentum across both short and medium windows, I lean toward continued mild tightening (higher nfci) into month-end, but conviction stays modest given the small magnitude and flat last-two-week reading.
Would change its mind if: A drop back to -0.41 or below, or two straight weeks of decline, would signal the drift has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last ten breakeven readings show a modest but clear uptrend at the margin: 2.17→2.28 over the window, with the most recent three prints (2.26, 2.26, 2.28) making new local highs after a brief mid-series dip. The 21d and 63d changes are still slightly negative (-0.08, -0.04 pp), reflecting the earlier dip, but the 252d change is positive (+0.08 pp) and the very latest tick (2.28) is the highest in the sample, sitting at 0.40 of the 12-month range and only 0.21 pp below the 12m high. My prior "flat" call anchored on a 2.32-2.38 band was miscalibrated to a series that never actually traded there — the actual range (2.14-2.49) and recent action show renewed upward drift after a shallow pullback, not a stable plateau. Breakevens remain 0.28 pp above the 2% objective, and the short-term momentum (last three consecutive higher/equal prints) suggests continuation rather than reversion. This isn't a large move, and the 21d/63d metrics still carry a slight negative sign, so conviction is limited, but the newest data point breaking to a fresh high is the most relevant signal for a one-month-ahead call. I'm shifting from flat to a modest "up" lean.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 2.22 over multiple observations, or resumption of the 21d/63d negative trend, would negate this and revert to flat/down.

=== inflation ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  The previous flat call is superseded by clear disinflationary evidence in the newest print. Headline CPI y/y fell to 2.97% from 3.24%, the lowest in the 12-month window, with the month-over-month print actually negative (-0.04%), the weakest reading in the series and well below the 3-month average (0.07%) and outgoing pace (0.20%). Headline 3m-annualized is just 0.90%, a sharp deceleration signal even allowing for energy/food noise. Core PCE, the better persistence gauge, also fell to 2.77% y/y from 3.01%, continuing a steady downtrend from 4.73% thirteen months ago, and core 3m-annualized (2.81%) is now closer to target than the y/y figure, confirming the disinflation is not just base effects. Policy language upgraded from "lack of further progress" to "modest further progress" toward 2%, corroborating the data turn. Both headline and core are now decelerating in tandem, with core distance from target down to 0.77pp. My prior flat/mixed lean was wrong given today's negative headline print and confirmed core cooling — the momentum is now clearly downward. Given a single negative m/m print can be noisy, I won't go to extreme conviction, but the weight of evidence (core trend, language shift, weak 3m annualized) supports continued softening into the next release.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print rebounding above 0.3% alongside core PCE ticking back up would signal reacceleration and overturn this down call.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m, outgoing_mom, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print jumped to 4.10%, up from 3.90% the prior three months and off the 12-month low of 3.50% by 0.60pp — a meaningful acceleration versus the flat 3.90% plateau that preceded it. My prior "flat" call was wrong: the data broke to a new cycle high rather than holding steady. The 3m change (+0.20pp) and 12m change (+0.50pp) both show clear upward momentum, and the Sahm-style gap (0.47pp) is now just shy of the 0.50 threshold I flagged as confirming loosening acceleration — effectively at the edge of that signal. The level itself (4.10%) is now 0.10pp above my natural-rate anchor of ~4.0%, so this is no longer just "low and stable" but a market that has both risen in level and accelerated in pace. Policy language softened only slightly ("have moved toward better balance over the past year" vs. "are moving into better balance"), which is consistent with acknowledging labor cooling has been underway, not a one-off print. Given the range position is at 1.00 (the high of the 12-month window), the momentum and level both point the same direction. I now lean toward continued loosening (higher unemployment) next month, though not with extreme confidence since a single print can partially reverse.
Would change its mind if: A pullback to 3.90% or below, or sahm_gap receding under 0.30, would indicate the rise was noise, not renewed loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y has drifted up over the last few prints (4.20→4.36), with the last observation the highest in the window. My prior "flat" call held since the level stayed within the 4.40 threshold I set, so it wasn't falsified, though the recent tick up to 4.36 shows some modest firming pressure building rather than pure sideways action. The 21d change is still negative (-0.25pp), but 63d and 252d changes are positive (+0.16, +0.59), suggesting the medium/long trend is mildly up even as the very short-term move has been a pullback. Range position at 0.51 is right in the middle of the 12-month band, offering no strong directional signal on its own. The 30s10s slope is a modest +0.15pp, not steep, suggesting no acute long-end cheapening pressure beyond what's already priced. The key new policy input — slowing the Treasury runoff cap from $60B to $25B — reduces the pace of net Treasury supply being added back to the market via balance-sheet reduction, which is a mild negative for term premium (less technical/supply pressure on the long end going forward). This somewhat offsets the recent modest uptick. On balance I lean slightly toward flat-to-modestly-up, since the last three prints show acceleration (4.23→4.32→4.29→4.36) even though slower QT should cap upside supply pressure over time.
Would change its mind if: A push back above 4.61 with follow-through would confirm renewed uptrend; a drop below 4.15 would confirm downside reversal.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Net level read on Treasury yields is modestly up, but with meaningful internal offsets that argue for a small, moderate-conviction position rather than a large directional bet. The clearest, freshest signals are labor_tightness (up, unemployment accelerating to a new cycle high, 0.55 conviction) and financial_conditions (mild tightening drift, satisfied its own trigger, 0.35) — both point toward higher yields via less easy conditions and a cooling-but-not-collapsing labor backdrop. Term_premium independently corroborates a mild-up drift in the 10y level itself (0.30), though it explicitly lacks the balance_sheet and financial_conditions context that would sharpen it; since balance_sheet's own read (QT taper, slower runoff, still-declining assets) is a modest net negative for term premium/supply pressure, I shade term_premium's "up" call down slightly rather than accept it at full face value — the gap is real and cuts against the loudest part of its story. Inflation is the strongest countervailing force: a clean, multi-metric disinflation signal (headline turning negative m/m, core PCE decelerating, policy language upgrade) argues for lower yields, and it is fresher in evidence quality than term_premium's own mixed short/medium-term signal. Inflation_expectations, however, shows a fresh uptick to new local highs, which complicates a clean disinflation-lowers-yields read — but curve_slope and inflation_expectations are outside my mandate (curve shape, real/nominal split) so I don't lean on them directly, only note the tension. Balance_sheet's steady QT (even decelerating) is a structural, well-evidenced but slow-moving force that modestly supports higher term premium over time despite the taper. On net: labor cooling, mild financial tightening, and continuing (if slower) QT drift outweigh the inflation disinflation signal for the one-month level call, but disagreement across drivers keeps this a small, not large, up-lean.

discounted.why: Weighed heavily but tension with inflation_expectations uptick and labor loosening caps its net contribution.

arbitrated.falsifier: A CPI/core PCE rebound above prior trend, or unemployment pulling back to 3.90% and NFCI reversing below -0.41, would flip this to a down/flat level call.

risks.text: QT taper and inflation cooling could dominate if labor data reverses next print, pulling yields down instead.
Panel is split across drivers; a level bet here rests on a thin net majority, not consensus.
Term premium's own signal is mixed (negative 21d, positive 63d/252d) and missing key cross-driver context.

trade.rationale: Modest up-lean from labor cooling, tightening financial conditions, and continuing QT outweighs disinflation signal; small same-sign duration short across both tenors reflects low-conviction level call.

why.labor_tightness: Fresh, multi-metric confirmation of unemployment rising to cycle high; strong basis for higher yields via looser labor.
why.inflation: Clear multi-metric disinflation with policy language corroboration; strongest evidence quality on panel but opposes the up-lean.
why.financial_conditions: NFCI drift confirmed its own trigger threshold; small magnitude but consistent sign supports mild tightening/higher yields.
why.term_premium: 10y drifting up medium-term but call lacks balance_sheet/FCI context; shaded down from analyst's 0.30 given known gaps.
why.balance_sheet: QT continues, still contractionary for reserves/duration technicals, though taper caps the pace; modest supportive force for higher yields.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print (7,205,455) continues the decline from the prior read (7,208,247), confirming the previous "down" call was correct. Total assets have fallen in nearly every one of the last 13 observations, with the 4-week change at -25,708M and 13-week change at -196,979M, both consistent with steady, ongoing runoff rather than any deceleration or pause. The pace over the last 4 weeks (~-25.7B) is roughly in line with or slightly below the trailing 13-week average pace (~-15.2B/week), so there's no clear reacceleration or deceleration signal — this looks like continued mechanical runoff under the standing caps. The statement language is unchanged, reaffirming that the Committee continues reducing Treasury and MBS holdings, with no hint of tapering the pace or halting runoff. Assets remain over $1 trillion below the 52-week high and continue to grind lower. Absent any change in reinvestment policy language, the trajectory into next month-end should continue in the same direction and at a similar pace. My falsifier condition (a flat/higher print or pause language) was not met — the print was lower and language unchanged — so I maintain the down call with similar conviction.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff entirely.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" (steepening) call has partially stalled but not reversed. Last release the spread was -0.20; the most recent print shows -0.20 again, following -0.19 before that — essentially flat over the last two readings after a run of steepening from -0.26 to -0.09 earlier in the window. The 21d and 63d changes remain positive (+0.09, +0.15), and the 252d change is strongly positive (+0.71), confirming the multi-month steepening trend is intact and the curve sits near the top of its 12-month range (range_position 0.87, only 0.09pp below the 12m high). However, the last three prints (-0.16, -0.19, -0.20) show a mild reversal/stall within that broader uptrend — steepening momentum has clearly slowed and ticked slightly backward from the recent peak of -0.09. This isn't a break of my prior falsifier (-0.42), so the broader steepening thesis isn't invalidated, but the immediate trend is directionless to slightly negative. Given the plateau at -0.19/-0.20 for two straight prints, near-term risk is a continued pause or minor further flattening before resuming the broader steepening trend. I'll moderate conviction and lean toward flat given the stall, while acknowledging the dominant multi-month direction remains up.
Would change its mind if: A print back below -0.30 would confirm renewed flattening; a fresh push above -0.09 would confirm resumed steepening trend.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series has moved steadily from -0.41 to -0.36 over the last 13 weeks, a gradual but persistent tightening drift. The most recent print (-0.36) confirms the prior call was correct: conditions continued to tighten rather than stall or reverse. The 4-week change (+0.02) and 13-week change (+0.05) both point the same direction, and momentum has not shown the two-week decline that would have falsified the view. That said, the index remains well below zero (-0.36, distance_from_zero -0.36), meaning conditions are still much looser than historical average — this is a mild normalization off very loose levels, not a shift to tight conditions. The range_position of 0.46 shows we're roughly mid-range of the 52-week band, not at an extreme. The pace of increase is slow and incremental (roughly +0.01/week average), so while the direction is consistently up, the magnitude is modest. Absent any new policy commentary on this driver, I extend the same tightening drift forward one month, expecting nfci to tick higher again, but I temper conviction slightly given the small absolute magnitude of weekly moves.
Would change its mind if: A drop back toward -0.39 or below, or two consecutive weekly declines, would signal the tightening drift has stalled or reversed.

The 10-year breakeven has drifted from 2.31 to 2.24 over the ten observations shown, a steady, low-volatility decline rather than the stabilization/reversal I had leaned toward. All three change windows are negative and monotonic in magnitude (21d -0.08, 63d -0.17, 252d -0.14), indicating the decline is a persistent multi-month feature, not just short-term noise. The series sits at 2.24, close to the 12m low (2.14) and in the bottom third of its 12-month range (range_position 0.29), reinforcing that downward momentum is intact rather than exhausted. Breakevens remain above the 2% target (+0.24pp), so there's no sign of disinflation expectations overshooting into deflation territory, but the direction of travel is clearly down. The policy language is unchanged boilerplate and offers no new signal to offset the price action. Given the consistency of the decline across all horizons and the lack of any stabilizing tick in the most recent observations, I now expect the breakeven to continue drifting lower or at best flatten slightly by month end, not resume the uptrend I previously anticipated.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 2.28 sustained over multiple observations, or a positive 21d change, would negate this and revert to flat/up.

Given base effects (the y/y comparison next month drops against last year's print) and only mild m/m firming, I lean toward another modest downtick or flat print rather than a reversal, but with less conviction than last time given the mom uptick.

The 3m change (+0.30pp) and 6m change (+0.50pp) both show the pace of loosening picking up rather than stabilizing, and the Sahm-style gap (current vs 12m low) has widened to +0.50pp, with sahm_gap itself at 0.37 — above the 0.30 threshold I set as my falsifier, so the "noise" case is rejected. Policy language still describes the labor market as strong, but that language is backward-looking and hasn't caught up to the recent prints. Given the consistent monotonic rise over the last three readings (3.90→4.10→4.20) with no offsetting pullback, I see this as a genuine, moderately strong tightening-reversal (loosening) signal, not just level-drift. I'll maintain an up call, with somewhat higher conviction than before given the confirmation of trend and breach of my own falsifier threshold.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.00% next month, or sahm_gap falling back under 0.30, would suggest the recent rise is stalling rather than continuing.

Term premium (10y yield itself) has broken down decisively — -0.39pp over 21d, -0.60pp over 63d, five straight lower prints, now near 12-month range lows — this is essentially the level variable itself moving down hard, formed fresh at this meeting. Inflation (CPI/PCE) and inflation expectations (breakevens) both confirm disinflation: CPI at a new 12-month low, breakevens grinding from 2.31 to 2.24, both fresh/near-fresh reads reinforcing lower yields via lower inflation compensation. Balance-sheet runoff continues steadily (down/shrinking assets), which in isolation is a modest upward pressure on term premium via reduced official-sector demand, but the term_premium analyst itself notes no fresh catalyst from this channel and the runoff pace is unchanged/mechanical, not accelerating — so I discount its ability to offset the observed yield decline. Financial conditions are tightening only mildly and off very loose levels — a weak, low-magnitude upward pressure on yields via tighter conditions, but conviction is limited (0.40) and normalization is incremental. Labor tightness is the one driver pointing toward easing-cycle expectations (rising unemployment, breach of Sahm-style threshold) — this argues for lower policy rate expectations feeding into the front end and, with some transmission, the long end, reinforcing the down call rather than opposing it. Given the direct, high-conviction, fresh evidence of falling 10y yields corroborated by disinflation and labor loosening, with only a mild, low-magnitude financial-conditions tightening as pushback, I set a net modest-down duration view, sized down from full conviction given the offsetting balance-sheet/financial-conditions signals and the flagged term_premium gaps (no visibility into whether the drop is durable or flight-to-quality driven).

discounted.why: Curve shape is out of scope for a directional level call.
Overlaps with inflation driver; avoided double-counting the disinflation signal.

arbitrated.falsifier: A push of 10y yield back above ~4.20 with follow-through, or CPI m/m breaching 0.35% with core PCE reaccelerating, would flip this to neutral/up.

risks.text: Term premium drop could be flight-to-quality/event-driven and reverse quickly once risk sentiment normalizes.
If runoff pace or guidance shifts, balance-sheet driver could flip to a stronger upward yield pressure.
Panel is split (financial conditions and balance sheet lean up) — level signal is weaker than a unanimous call would be.

trade.rationale: Net-down level view driven by fresh term-premium breakdown, disinflation, and labor loosening; weighted more to the long end where the freshest evidence sits, tempered by offsetting financial-conditions/balance-sheet signals.

why.term_premium: Fresh, decisive multi-week decline in 10y yield, near 12m range lows; central to the level call.
why.inflation: New 12m low in CPI, core PCE grinding lower; corroborates lower long-end yields via inflation compensation.
why.labor_tightness: Rising unemployment above natural rate implies easing expectations, pulling yields lower despite being framed as 'up' on unemployment.
why.financial_conditions: Mild tightening drift off very loose levels; small magnitude, partial offset to the down view.
why.balance_sheet: Steady runoff is a modest structural upward pressure on term premium, but no acceleration or fresh catalyst.

Given the sharp two-tenths deceleration in headline is unlikely to fully repeat, but the underlying momentum is still soft, I expect headline CPI y/y to be roughly flat-to-slightly-down next month, not sharply higher. The small uptick in core PCE y/y is worth flagging as a minor caution but doesn't overturn the overall soft-momentum picture.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% combined with core PCE y/y rising further (e.g., above 2.9%) would signal reacceleration and overturn this call.

However, the pace of increase has stalled at the margin — the last two prints are both 4.20%, a flat sequential reading rather than a fresh acceleration. Policy language shifted from "remained low" to "moved up but remains low," and now emphasizes moderating job gains, confirming the loosening trend is real and being acknowledged, not a one-off. But since the two most recent prints are identical, the immediate one-month trajectory looks more like a pause at an elevated plateau than continued rapid ascent. Distance from natural rate (~4%) is only 0.20pp, still modest in level terms. Given the flattening of the most recent two months alongside a still-rising trend on 3/6/12m windows, I lean toward a further mild uptick or flat print next month, but with reduced conviction versus last time since the immediate momentum has decelerated.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 4.00%, or sahm_gap falling under 0.30, would signal the loosening trend has reversed rather than plateaued.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has drifted up in the most recent observations, from 3.79 to 3.91, and last release's "down" call did not materialize further—yield sits near the low end of its 12-month range (range_position 0.11, only 0.13pp above the 12m low) but has ticked up over the last several prints. The 21d change is still slightly negative (-0.08pp) reflecting the prior decline, but the last five readings (3.81, 3.82, 3.83, 3.84, 3.87, 3.91) show a clear short-term uptick, suggesting the multi-month downtrend (63d -0.60pp, 252d -0.21pp) has stalled and modestly reversed. The 30s10s slope remains moderately positive (+0.29pp), consistent with continued balance-sheet runoff pressure at the long end, and the Fed's ongoing balance-sheet reduction language is unchanged, which structurally leans toward higher long-end supply pressure. Given the yield is still very low in its range, a full reversal isn't clear, but the recent momentum has shifted from down to modestly up. My prior "down" call was wrong for the most recent leg—yield rose rather than fell. I now lean toward a mild upward drift continuing into month-end, given the recent trend reversal and steady runoff pressure, though conviction is limited by how compressed the range still is.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below ~3.80 with follow-through would confirm the downtrend resumed; a push toward 4.00+ would confirm the reversal higher.
Leaned on: dgs10_change_21d, slope_30s10s, dgs10_range_position

=== balance_sheet ===

  The latest print continues the runoff trend that my prior "down" call anticipated: fed_assets fell to 7,080,059 from 7,109,137, a fresh cycle low and well below the 52w high (-875,723). The 4-week change (-43,179) is actually larger in magnitude than the prior 4-week pace implied, and the 13-week change (-151,104, -2.09%) confirms a steady, uninterrupted decline rather than deceleration. There is no sign of a flat or higher monthly print, so the condition that would have made me reconsider did not occur — the prior call was correct and the evidence still supports it. Statement language continues to affirm ongoing balance-sheet reduction ("will continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency MBS"), with only a minor wording trim (removal of "In addition,") that does not signal any change in substance or pace — no mention of tapering the cap, pausing, or ending runoff. With both the quantitative trend and the qualitative language pointing the same direction, this remains a clear, low-ambiguity QT signal. I expect total assets to be lower again at next month-end, continuing the established monthly decline of roughly $30-45B.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call was too cautious: the spread had actually pushed decisively above the 0.10 threshold I set, and this release confirms further steepening momentum has been present, even as the last two prints (0.20, 0.15) show a modest pullback from the 0.26 high. Looking at the full run, the trend over 21/63/252 days is unambiguously steepening (+0.15, +0.44, +0.64 pp respectively), and the spread sits at 0.86 of its 12-month range — near the top, well above the -0.53 low. The recent dip from 0.26 to 0.15 is a retracement within a steepening channel, not a reversal, especially with the 50bp cut just delivered easing front-end yields more than the long end typically responds, which tends to support further steepening as cuts continue. The 10s30s slope at +0.33 pp corroborates a broadly un-inverted, normalizing curve further out, consistent with continued 2s10s steepening pressure. However, two consecutive lower prints (0.20 then 0.15) warrant some caution against calling for further sharp steepening this month — I'll lean toward continued gradual steepening but with moderate conviction given the recent softening in momentum.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.05 pp would signal renewed flattening; a fresh push above 0.30 would confirm resumed strong steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (continued easing/down) is confirmed by the latest data. NFCI has fallen steadily over the last several readings, from -0.36 down to -0.42, with the most recent three observations (-0.40, -0.41, -0.42) showing accelerating momentum rather than the flattening I had flagged as a risk. The 4-week change (-0.04) and 13-week change (-0.03) both point in the same direction, and the index is now sitting near its 52-week low (-0.44), well below both its 13-week average (-0.38) and its long-run zero average. Range position at 0.11 (out of 0-1) confirms conditions are near the loose end of the recent 52-week band. There is no offsetting policy language to suggest a turn. Given the consistency and slight acceleration of the decline over the past three readings, I have more confidence than last time that this is a genuine easing trend rather than noise. I do not see evidence of stabilization or reversal, so I extend the down call with somewhat higher conviction than previously.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.38 (the 13w average) over 2-3 readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last observation (2.18) sits at the top of the recent range and confirms the modest uptick flagged by be_change_21d (+0.02pp). Over the past ~10 readings, breakeven has oscillated in a tight 2.12-2.19 band, with the most recent print near the high end (be_range_position 0.34 relative to the 12-month range, but near the top of the short-window range shown). My prior "flat" call was reasonable: the series did not break down toward 2.07-2.10 as the falsifier specified, nor did it show renewed negative 21d momentum — instead it edged up slightly. The medium-term declines (63d -0.14, 252d -0.16) still dominate the longer-run picture, keeping the driver below its 12-month high and only modestly above its 12-month low, consistent with a broader cooling trend that has stalled recently. But the very last two prints (2.15→2.18) show a small pop, and the 21d change is positive, suggesting near-term stabilization or slight firming rather than continued decay. With breakeven still +0.18pp above the 2% objective, there's no urgency for the Committee's language to shift, and no strong directional catalyst evident in the data alone. I lean toward flat-to-slightly-up for the next month, given the recent uptick outweighs longer-term softness at this short horizon, but the signal is not strong enough to call a clear reversal.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.13 sustained over multiple observations, or renewed negative 21d change, would confirm a down move instead of flat/up.

  Headline CPI y/y fell to 2.43% from 2.61%, extending a clear downtrend from the 3.1-3.5% range seen in prior months, and the previous "down" call was correct. However, the underlying momentum is now more mixed than the headline trend suggests. The latest headline m/m was 0.21%, up from -0.04% and 0.17% in the prior two months, and headline 3m annualized (2.17%) is now below core 3m annualized (2.46%), indicating the recent disinflation has been aided by softer non-core components. Core PCE y/y has ticked up three months in a row (2.75 → 2.81 → 2.87), a +0.10pp change over 3 months, suggesting core persistence is not fully resolved even as headline y/y continues to benefit from base effects (headline was 3.24-3.36 a year ago, a high comparison base still rolling off). Policy language shows only incremental easing ("modest" → "some" further progress), consistent with deceleration continuing but decelerating in pace. Given base effects are largely exhausted (headline now at 12-month low) and core momentum has firmed, I expect headline y/y to be roughly flat to marginally lower next month, not a continuation of the sharp decline seen recently.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.35% or core PCE y/y rising further above 2.9% would signal reacceleration; a headline m/m below 0.1% would confirm continued deceleration.

Report:
  The latest print ticked down to 4.10% from 4.20%, a small pullback rather than continued deterioration. My prior "up" call was directionally too aggressive for this release: the last observation actually fell 0.1pp. That said, the broader trend since the 12-month low of 3.70% is still one of net loosening — unrate_change_12m is +0.40pp, sahm_gap is +0.47pp (above the 0.30 threshold I set as a reversal signal), and unrate_range_position sits at 0.80, meaning we're near the top of the past year's range. The 3m change is flat (0.00pp) and the 3m average (4.17%) is still elevated versus the 12m low, suggesting the level has plateaued at a higher plateau rather than reversing back toward 3.70-3.90%. Policy language also shifted to acknowledge "job gains have moderated" and unemployment "moved up," confirming the committee sees the same drift. Given the single-month decline, I don't think the loosening trend is over, but the immediate one-month momentum is ambiguous — a plateau with one down-tick is not strong evidence of continued monthly increases. I'll moderate conviction and lean toward flat-to-slightly-up rather than confidently up.
Would change its mind if: A print back at or below 3.90%, or sahm_gap falling under 0.30, would confirm reversal; a fresh rise above 4.20% would confirm renewed loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_3m_avg

=== term_premium ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (3.81) is up from 3.75 and near the top of the recent short run, but the 10Y remains deep in the bottom of its 12-month range (range_position 0.13, only 0.18pp above the 12m low of 3.63). The 21d change is essentially flat (-0.06pp) while 63d and 252d changes are sharply negative (-0.67, -0.80pp), meaning the dominant trend over the past quarter and year has been falling yields/premium, with only a modest recent uptick. My prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was wrong in direction over the near term — the series continued to hover/drift rather than confirming a reversal toward 4.00+. The last two prints (3.75, 3.81) show some bounce, and the 10s30s slope steepened modestly to 0.33pp, consistent with some long-end cheapening pressure (continued balance-sheet runoff language reaffirmed, unchanged in substance). But this is a small bounce within a still-depressed range, not a confirmed trend reversal. Given the mixed signal — recent uptick but still low in range and longer-term trend down — I lean toward a flat-to-slightly-up call with low conviction, acknowledging the prior miss.
Would change its mind if: A clean break below 3.75 with follow-through confirms downtrend resumed; a sustained push through 3.90-4.00 confirms reversal higher.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, dgs10_above_12m_low, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes: Synthesizing the level-relevant drivers (excluding curve_slope, which is explicitly off-limits as a shape call): financial_conditions shows the clearest, freshest, and most internally consistent signal — NFCI declining with accelerating momentum toward 52w lows, no offsetting language. That's a genuine easing-in-conditions signal, which on its own is more a risk-appetite/liquidity variable than a direct yield-level driver, but sustained loosening financial conditions historically coincide with less urgency for the Fed to ease further via rates and can keep long-end yields supported-to-higher via term premium and growth expectations, while also reflecting looser front-end conditions post-cut. term_premium itself is genuinely torn — a small recent bounce (3.75→3.81) within a still-depressed 12-month range, with 63d/252d trends firmly negative. This is a stale-ish, low-conviction call (0.25) that I read as closer to flat, not a reversal. inflation and inflation_expectations both land at flat with modest conviction (0.35 each): headline CPI disinflation is decelerating but core PCE has firmed three months running, and breakevens have ticked up slightly off a stable band — together these lean flat-to-marginally firm, not a disinflationary impulse that would pull yields down. labor_tightness is flat/plateaued (unemployment plateaued at a higher level after one down-tick), a weak, low-conviction signal that doesn't clearly push yields either way. balance_sheet is the strongest, freshest, highest-conviction call (0.72, down) — continued QT is a structural drain on Fed holdings but its direct read-through to nominal yield *level* is ambiguous (less duration absorption by Fed could mean modestly higher term premium/yields, offsetting the "down" framing of the driver itself, which describes assets not yields). Netting these: modest core inflation persistence, plateaued labor slack, continued QT-driven duration supply pressure, and stabilizing-not-reversing term premium suggest a slight upward tilt to yield levels over the month, but with real disagreement (term premium and labor near-flip-flops, financial conditions pulling a different direction) that argues for sizing down rather than a confident directional call.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.72
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: fed_assets fell again, continuing the steady QT trajectory. Total assets declined from 7,039,284 to 7,029,408 in the latest print, a ~$9.9B weekly drop, with 4-week change of -$50,651M and 13-week change of -$176,047M (-2.44%). The pace looks slightly slower than some earlier weeks in the series but is still a clear, consistent monthly runoff of roughly $50B, in line with announced caps. Assets remain $837B below the 52-week high with no sign of deceleration or reversal. The statement language change is cosmetic (dropped "In addition,") and still explicitly affirms continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings — no softening, no pause, no taper signal. There is no evidence of a slowing cap or reinvestment change. Given the consistent trend across the full 13-observation window and unchanged runoff commitment in the language, I maintain that the balance sheet will be lower at next month-end. Conviction remains high but not maximal, since the pace of decline has been mildly uneven week to week and could moderate.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, fed_assets

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has pulled back to +0.12pp from +0.17pp two readings ago, and the last print of 0.12 is the lowest in the recent 10-observation window aside from the 0.13 starting point. The 21-day change is essentially flat (-0.01pp), while the 63-day (+0.29pp) and 252-day (+0.27pp) changes still show a large steepening impulse over the medium term — the curve has moved from deeply inverted (12m low -0.53pp) to modestly positive, sitting at 0.82 of its 12-month range. That said, my prior call for continued steepening was only partially right: the trend stalled and reversed slightly over the last few observations, dipping from 0.17 to 0.12. The just-announced 50bp rate cut with dovish easing language (front-end anchor) typically supports steepening as short rates fall faster than long rates in anticipation of an easing cycle, but the immediate print shows compression, not expansion. Absent a clear catalyst for renewed steepening and given the recent chop within a narrow 0.12-0.17 band, I lean toward the curve holding roughly flat to modestly re-steepening as rate cuts continue to pull the front end down, but conviction is tempered by the recent stall.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 0.05pp would signal renewed flattening; a push back above 0.20pp would confirm resumed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_low, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady grind lower, now at -0.44, a fresh 52-week low, down from -0.43 and -0.42 in the prior two readings. The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.08) both confirm consistent, unbroken easing momentum with no signs of stalling — every single one of the last 13 readings is flat-to-lower than the one before it, an unusually monotonic sequence. Range position sits at 0.00, meaning current conditions are at the loosest point of the entire observed window, and distance from zero (-0.44) shows conditions are now firmly and increasingly looser than historical average. My prior "down" call was correct: the falsifier condition (a rebound above -0.38) did not occur — in fact the index moved further away from that threshold, strengthening rather than weakening the easing thesis. There is no policy commentary to suggest a change in driving forces. Given the consistency of the trend, lack of any inflection or deceleration in the pace of change, and the absence of any offsetting information, I maintain that conditions are easing and likely continue to do so into the next reading, absent an exogenous shock not visible in this data.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above -0.40 (13w avg) sustained over 2-3 readings would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last print (2.33) sits just below the recent local peak of 2.34 and above the prior flat band of 2.29-2.31 that persisted for six of the last ten readings. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate through most of the window but the last two observations (2.34, 2.33) show a modest upward break from that range. The 21d (+0.14pp) and 63d (+0.13pp) changes are now both positive and of similar magnitude, suggesting the move is not just short-lived noise but a small sustained drift higher. The 252d change is still negative (-0.11pp), so the longer trend remains flat-to-down, but the recent momentum has clearly turned. Breakeven is now at the 76th percentile of its 12-month range and 0.33pp above the 2% target — not extreme, but on the higher side of recent experience. Given the two-observation uptick and consistent positive short/medium-term changes, I lean toward a continued mild upward drift into month-end rather than reversion to the 2.29-2.31 band. Conviction is moderate given the small sample of the recent uptick and the offsetting negative annual trend.
Would change its mind if: A pullback below 2.30 sustained over two or more observations, or 21d change turning negative, would confirm reversion to flat/down instead of up.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.45
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline m/m printed 0.29%, above the 0.1% deceleration threshold I flagged but just under my 0.35% reacceleration trigger. Headline y/y actually rose to 2.58% from 2.43% — the first uptick in this series after six months of decline from 3.49% to 2.43%. My prior "flat" call undersold this: the trend broke upward, not sideways. The 3m annualized headline (2.66%) and core (2.60%) rates sit modestly above the 2% target but below the recent m/m run-rate, and the mom_gap_vs_outgoing (+0.07pp) plus the 0.29% latest print (above the 0.22% 3m average) both point to mild upward momentum building into the next print. Core PCE y/y has also ticked up two months running (2.81→2.87→2.84), though it remains near its 12m low. I weight core PCE for persistence, but the recent CPI momentum — outgoing_mom rising through 0.16, 0.21, 0.29 — is the more timely signal for the next CPI release specifically. This isn't a strong reacceleration case, but the direction of travel over the last two prints is unambiguously up rather than flat, and I was wrong last time to call it flat. Given genuine but modest momentum, I lean up with moderate conviction rather than high.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15% or a drop in headline y/y back toward 2.4% would indicate the uptick was noise, not a turn.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, mom_gap_vs_outgoing, outgoing_mom

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The last print held at 4.10%, unchanged from the prior month—so the "flat" call was correct this time, and the falsifier conditions I set (a drop to ≤3.90% or a fresh push above 4.20%) were not triggered. The series now shows three consecutive months at 4.10-4.20% after stepping up from a 3.70-3.90% range, which looks like a plateau rather than continued deterioration or a reversal. The 3-month change is actually slightly negative (-0.10pp), while 6m and 12m changes are modestly positive (+0.20pp each), and the Sahm gap sits at 0.43pp, above the classic 0.50 recession trigger only marginally below it. The range position (0.80) confirms the rate is sitting near its 12-month high, but the flattening of the 3-month trend suggests the rise has paused rather than accelerated. Policy language continues to describe gains as "moderated/slowed" with unemployment "moved up but remains low," consistent with gradual cooling rather than sharp deterioration. Absent a new acceleration signal, the most defensible call is another flat, though the elevated level versus the natural rate keeps this a close call rather than a confident stable read.
Would change its mind if: A print at or below 3.90%, or sahm_gap falling under 0.30, would signal reversal; a fresh rise above 4.20% would confirm renewed loosening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10Y has drifted higher over the past month, sitting at 4.28 vs 4.19-4.20 a few observations back. The 21d change of +0.54pp shows recent upward momentum, though the 63d change of +0.29pp is milder and the 252d change is still -0.60pp, so the longer-term trend remains down even as the near-term trend has turned up. The yield now sits at 0.52 in its 12-month range (roughly the midpoint), having moved off the 12m low (+0.65pp above it) but well below the 12m high. My prior flat call was too cautious given the clear +0.54pp 21-day move; the level has effectively stabilized in the upper-4.2s for the last several prints (4.25-4.29), suggesting the sharp rise has largely leveled off already at a plateau near recent highs. The 30s10s slope is a modest +0.19pp, not showing aggressive long-end cheapening pressure, and the policy language change is a minor rewording, not a substantive shift in balance-sheet runoff pace. Given the flattening-out at a higher level after a real move up, I lean toward continued modest upward drift or stabilization, but conviction is limited since the last 4 readings are essentially flat around 4.28.
Would change its mind if: A move back below 4.10 would confirm the recent rise has reversed; a sustained push above 4.40 would confirm renewed upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Weighing the drivers relevant to my level-of-yields seat (excluding curve_slope, which is out of scope): balance_sheet gives the freshest, most consistent signal — QT continuing on trend with no policy softening, which is a modest structural pressure toward higher term premium/yields via reduced Fed demand for duration. Term_premium itself reports a real 21d move up in DGS10 (+0.54pp) that has since plateaued near 4.28, consistent with — not contradicted by — the balance_sheet read; the flagged gap (term_premium lacks balance_sheet) actually resolves in the same direction, so I lean on both together rather than discounting either. Inflation and inflation_expectations both lean up with moderate conviction, momentum building but not a strong reacceleration — mutually reinforcing but modest. Labor_tightness is flat/plateau, a genuine no-view input rather than a pull in either direction. Financial_conditions is the strongest, freshest single read (NFCI monotonic 52-week low, no offsetting signal) but it operates on risk-asset conditions, not directly on the level of nominal yields — sustained easing can coexist with or even accompany higher yields if it reflects looser policy expectations, so I weight it as a secondary confirming force rather than the primary lever, and I don't let it dominate the duration call given its indirect read-through on yield level per se.

Netting these: balance_sheet (down assets, hawkish-ish for long-end supply/term premium), term_premium (up drift, plateauing), and inflation/inflation_expectations (mild up drift) all point modestly higher for yields. Labor is neutral. Financial conditions easing is a modest offsetting force (would normally accompany lower yields if driven by rate-cut expectations) but the panel's own dovish rate-cut framing suggests NFCI easing here is from lower short rates/spread compression, not necessarily long-end pull-down. Net view: modest upward drift in yields, driven more by long end (term premium, supply, mild inflation upside) than short end. Conviction moderate given multiple drivers agree but each individually is only moderate-conviction.

arbitrated.notes: The level call for nominal yields tilts modestly higher, built mainly on term_premium's direct read of DGS10 itself (level at 4.58, near 12-month highs, strong positive momentum across 21/63/252d windows) corroborated by curve_slope's independent finding that the 2s10s spread is steepening to fresh highs — consistent with long-end yields rising even as the front end eases with Fed cuts. Labor_tightness is genuinely flat/plateaued (low conviction, oscillating band), so it neither confirms nor denies the direction and is set aside as a non-signal. Balance_sheet's continued runoff is a structural duration-supply headwind that aligns with, and helps explain, the term_premium's rise — the gap flagged (term_premium lacks financial_conditions/curve_slope) is address-able: curve_slope's steepening actually corroborates term_premium rather than contradicting it, so I lean on it despite the flagged gap. Financial_conditions is the one dissenting driver — NFCI still easing (looser conditions, which historically can coincide with lower long yields via risk-on Treasury demand or could just reflect credit-spread compression) — but its momentum is decelerating sharply and sits at a 52w low with limited room to ease further, so I discount its pull on the level call. Net: multiple independent, live drivers (term premium, curve steepening, inflation, breakevens, balance-sheet runoff) point the same direction — up — with only a decelerating financial-conditions read pulling the other way.
