user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print continues the runoff trend I called last time: total assets fell to 6,818,186 from 6,831,760, a 4-week decline of -34,305M, consistent with recent weekly declines (-2,310, -19,484, -2,310, -13,574 in the last few obs). The 13-week change of -195,304M (-2.78%) and 52-week change of -811,938M confirm a steady, ongoing QT pace with no deceleration signal in the data — the last four weekly deltas (-1,317, -19,484, -2,310, -13,574) are noisy but still net negative, and assets sit -815,688M below the 52-week high with no bounce. Policy language is unchanged: the Committee reiterates continued reduction of Treasury and agency holdings, with no mention of slowing caps or pausing. This is exactly the scenario my prior falsifier would need to see reversed, and it hasn't — no flat/higher print, no dovish language shift. I therefore maintain a down call. Conviction is high given multiple consecutive confirming data points and unchanged reinvestment language, though the very last weekly delta was smaller in magnitude, suggesting the pace may be modestly decelerating within the same directional trend, which tempers conviction slightly from the prior reading.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_change_52w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread's latest print is +0.36pp, matching the recent plateau (0.34-0.36 range over the last several readings) after ticking up from 0.34 to 0.36 in the most recent observation. My prior call for further steepening (up) was directionally correct in that the spread held near/at its 12-month high rather than reversing toward +0.25pp, but the move has been essentially flat for the last 4-5 readings rather than continuing to steepen — the 21d change is a mere +0.03pp, showing momentum has stalled even as the 63d (+0.19pp) and 252d (+0.57pp) changes confirm the broader steepening trend remains intact. Slope_range_position at 0.94 shows the curve sitting near the top of its 12-month range, which increases asymmetry risk — more room to fall back than to extend further. The Fed holding rates steady (versus the prior cut) removes a recent steepening catalyst from front-end easing expectations, but also removes downside pressure on the front end. With the curve near a range high and short-term momentum flat, I lean toward continued steepening being less likely near-term than consolidation or mild pullback, but the multi-month trend still has some pull toward up.
Would change its mind if: A break below +0.30pp would confirm stalling/reversal near the range high; a fresh push above +0.40pp would confirm renewed steepening.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_change_252d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its slow, grinding decline: latest reading -0.50, matching the 52-week low, with the index sitting at the bottom of its 52-week range (range_position = 0.00). The 4-week change (-0.02) and 13-week change (-0.06) are both negative, confirming continued easing momentum, though the pace has decelerated somewhat versus prior periods (the last few weekly increments are only -0.01 each). Conditions remain well below the zero average, indicating looser-than-average financial conditions, and there is no sign of stabilization or reversal — no reading has ticked up, and the 4-week change has not turned flat or positive as would be needed to invalidate the easing call. My prior "down" call was correct: the index did continue falling from -0.48ish toward -0.50. Given the persistent (if modest) negative momentum and the index printing fresh lows, I expect the grind lower to continue into next month-end, though the shrinking magnitude of weekly moves (-0.01) tempers conviction that this trend has much further room before flattening out.
Would change its mind if: A reading back above -0.43 sustained over 2-3 releases, or a flat/positive 4w change, would signal the easing trend has stalled or reversed.
Leaned on: nfci, nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: up, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is holding up reasonably well. The last observation (2.42) sits at the high end of the recent range, matching the prior peak. The series has been choppy (2.41→2.38→2.40→2.42→2.43→2.40→2.42→2.40→2.38→2.42) but the 21d, 63d, and 252d changes are all positive (+0.08, +0.12, +0.12 pp), confirming a mild upward drift rather than reversal. The falsifier I set previously (sustained break below 2.30, or negative 21d change) was not triggered — 21d change remains clearly positive and the series never approached 2.30. Range position at 0.93 (near the 12m high of 2.45) reinforces that breakevens are firming, not falling. Distance from the 2% target (+0.42 pp) is elevated and roughly stable, suggesting the market has priced in a persistent modest overshoot rather than an accelerating one. Given the oscillation within a narrow band (2.38-2.43) over the last several prints, I don't see strong momentum for a big move higher, but the drift and position near range highs favor continued mild upside over flat. Conviction remains moderate given the choppiness and lack of decisive breakout.
Would change its mind if: A drop below 2.35 sustained over two or more observations, or 21d/63d changes both turning negative, would reverse this view.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  The prior "up" call is confirmed by the newest data. Headline CPI y/y rose again to 2.99% from 2.87%, its fourth consecutive monthly increase off the 2.43% trough, and the outgoing print's m/m gain was 0.43%, the strongest in the 13-month window and well above my 0.15% threshold for stalling. Headline 3m annualized momentum sits at 4.29%, more than double the 2% target and running hot relative to core's 2.39% annualized pace — a divergence that flags energy/food (or other volatile components) as the current driver of headline strength rather than broad-based persistence. Core PCE y/y, the better persistence gauge, is essentially flat (2.99% vs 2.98%, 2.99% three months prior), suggesting underlying inflation is stable, not accelerating. Still, headline is what this call is scored on, and three straight months of accelerating m/m prints (0.28, 0.34, 0.43) with no sign of deceleration argues for continued upward pressure into the next release. I weight the momentum in headline_mom over the core stability for this specific horizon call, since the trend in m/m prints has been consistently positive and accelerating. Policy language remains unchanged, unhelpful for timing.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print below 0.15%, or headline_3m_annualized falling back toward core's ~2.4% pace, would signal the firming has stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print fell to 4.00% from 4.10%, exactly at the level I flagged as confirming a tightening reversal. This is the lowest reading in three months and pulls the rate back toward the 12-month low of 3.90%. The 3m and 6m changes are now both negative (-0.10pp and -0.20pp respectively), reversing the drift higher that had persisted for several months. The 12m change is still positive (+0.30pp) showing the market is somewhat looser than a year ago, and sahm_gap at 0.20pp is well below any recession-signaling threshold. Range position at 0.33 shows the rate sitting in the lower third of its 12-month range. My prior flat call was reasonably positioned given the choppy 4.10-4.20 plateau, but this print breaks that plateau to the downside, suggesting a modest re-tightening rather than continued loosening. Absent a clear driver of the improvement in the narrative provided, I'll treat this as a genuine but modest turn rather than noise, given it's a decisive move off the recent range. One data point on the downside after several months of gradual increases doesn't yet establish a strong trend, so I lean toward flat-to-down with modest conviction, leaning down given the magnitude and directionality of the move.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back to 4.10%+ would suggest this was noise; a further drop toward 3.90% would confirm sustained re-tightening.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_12m_low, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10yr has been essentially flat to modestly higher over the last month (21d change ~0.00pp), but sits well up over 63d (+0.30pp) and 252d (+0.50pp), and remains high in its 12-month range (0.82 range position, only 0.95pp above the 12m low, near the 4.79 high). My prior "up" call at 0.55 conviction played out roughly correctly in trend terms — yields have held near recent highs (4.52-4.65 band) rather than reversing, though the most recent print (4.58) is not a fresh high and the last 10 readings show choppy sideways action with no clean breakout. The 10s30s slope at +0.25pp is positive but not steepening dramatically, consistent with continued but not accelerating term premium pressure. Balance-sheet runoff continues per policy language, a structural tailwind for higher long-end yields/term premium. Given the flat 21-day change and lack of a new high, I'll moderate conviction from the prior call — the uptrend has stalled somewhat but hasn't reversed. No signal of flight-to-quality or a drop toward the low end of the range. I lean toward continued modest upward pressure into month-end, given elevated range position and ongoing supply/runoff dynamics, but the flat near-term momentum tempers confidence.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.40 with the 10s30s slope flattening or inverting would signal upward pressure has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/schedule changes to runoff would directly affect long-end supply pressure and term premium.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk appetite shifts could trigger flight-to-quality flows into long-end Treasuries.

arbitrated.notes: Five relevant drivers speak to the level (curve_slope is out of scope for this seat). Inflation shows headline CPI accelerating for a fourth straight month (2.87%→2.99%, 3m annualized 4.29%) with decent conviction (0.55) and only 16 days stale — this is the strongest, freshest upward pull on nominal yields, even though core PCE is flat, suggesting the inflation risk premium embedded in nominals should stay firm. Inflation expectations (breakevens) corroborate this with a fresh, moderate-conviction (0.40) up call near range highs. Term premium also leans up (0.35) with the 10y holding near range highs on 63d/252d momentum, and explicitly cites balance-sheet runoff as a structural tailwind — this is a real, named dependency I honor by leaning on balance_sheet's own down call as confirming evidence for term premium's argument, not undermining it: continued QT means duration supply keeps pressure on long-end yields upward. Working against this: financial_conditions is easing (down, -0.35, matching 52w lows) which is a modest offsetting force pulling yields lower via looser conditions, but its momentum is decelerating and stale-ish in trend terms. Labor_tightness ticked down to 4.00% (down, -0.35) which is a modest labor-market-easing signal that would typically pull yields lower, but it's a single data point off a plateau, 23 days stale, and low conviction. Balance_sheet's own down call (0.65, fresh) is about the level of Fed assets, not yields directly, but its policy implication (continued runoff, no pause) reinforces upward term-premium pressure via increased net Treasury supply absorption needs. Netting these: inflation and term premium/breakevens dominate on freshness and multi-driver corroboration, partially offset by financial conditions and labor easing. I land modestly bullish on yields (nominal rates rising), concentrated more in the long end given term premium and supply dynamics, with a smaller add on the front end reflecting the inflation and labor tension.

Core PCE stability could reassert itself, stalling headline CPI momentum and undercutting the inflation-driven yield rise.
Financial conditions and labor easing could accelerate together, overwhelming the modest upward drivers.

trade.rationale: Modest net-higher yield view driven mainly by fresh inflation acceleration and term-premium/supply dynamics at the long end, tempered by easing financial conditions and softer labor.

why.labor_tightness: Single-month unemployment downtick, 23-day stale, low conviction; modest offsetting pull lower.

The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.75
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "down" call was correct: total assets fell again, from 6,782,332 to 6,766,101 ($M), continuing an uninterrupted decline. The 4-week change (-52,085) is actually steeper than the trailing 13-week average pace (-139,039/13w ≈ -10,700/wk vs -52,085/4w ≈ -13,000/wk), suggesting runoff has not slowed and may have modestly accelerated recently. The 13-week percent change (-2.01%) confirms a steady, non-trivial monthly drain rather than a plateau. Assets remain well below the 52-week high (-775,868), with no sign of reversal in any of the last several prints — every one of the last 13 observations is lower than several observations prior, with no up-week in the most recent stretch. Policy language is unchanged: the Committee continues to reduce Treasury, agency debt, and agency MBS holdings, with no mention of slowing or pausing caps. Given the consistent, uninterrupted weekly declines and unchanged reinvestment/runoff language, month-end total assets should be lower again, continuing at a similar or slightly faster pace than the trailing 13-week average. There is no evidence in these measurements of any deceleration, let alone a pause.
Would change its mind if: A monthly fed_assets print flat or higher, or new statement language pausing/ending runoff, or announcement of slowing the monthly caps.
Leaned on: fed_assets, assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The slope series ended at 0.25pp after dipping to 0.20-0.22 the prior two prints, so the latest move is a bounce, not a new high. Last release I called flat (0.35 conviction), citing 0.30/0.40 as break levels; neither was hit — the series has oscillated in a tight 0.20-0.27 band for the last several readings, so that call held up reasonably well. Looking longer, slope_change_252d (+0.66pp) shows the curve has steepened substantially over the past year, and slope_range_position at 0.82 confirms we're sitting near the top of the 12-month range (-0.47 to +0.41). But short-term momentum has faded: slope_change_21d is -0.09pp (mild flattening) while slope_change_63d is +0.16pp (still net steepening over the quarter). The FOMC held rates steady this meeting after cutting previously — a pause in the easing cycle removes a prior source of front-end anchoring/steepening pressure, and with the front end roughly stable (dgs2 near 4%), the 2s10s spread looks more likely to consolidate than to break out further. The 10s30s at +0.27pp is consistent with a normal, mildly upward-sloping long end, not signaling fresh curve distortion. Net: the range-bound, choppy pattern near cycle highs with fading 21d momentum argues for continued consolidation rather than a decisive move either way.
Would change its mind if: A close below +0.18pp would confirm flattening reversal; a sustained push above +0.32pp would confirm renewed steepening breakout.
Leaned on: slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_12m_high, slope_12m_low, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: up, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (continued easing/down) was wrong: NFCI bottomed at -0.51 and has ticked up two consecutive readings to -0.49, giving a +0.01 4-week change. That is exactly the flat/positive 4w change I flagged as a reversal signal, though it's still well short of the -0.43 threshold for a sustained reversal. Conditions remain very loose in absolute terms (distance from zero -0.49, near the 52w low, range position just 0.09) but the momentum has clearly stalled and modestly reversed off the low. The 13-week trend (-0.02) still shows net easing over the quarter, but the most recent two prints are the freshest signal and point to a small tightening impulse. With no policy commentary to corroborate either the recent uptick or the prior easing trend, I read this as a stabilization with a slight tightening lean rather than continued easing. arbitrated.notes:
Weighing the drivers relevant to the level of nominal yields (not curve shape, not real/nominal split): financial_conditions gives the freshest, cleanest, most monotonic signal — NFCI has risen for six straight prints, off its trough, momentum accelerating, no policy-driven noise clouding it. That's a genuine tightening-from-loose-conditions signal, which typically accompanies higher rates as looser financial conditions get priced out. Term_premium (10y itself) shows a decline that has stabilized (21d flat, 63d down) — this is a directly-observed yield read, but it's backward-looking and its own analyst flags flat near-term momentum rather than continued decline; the QT-taper rationale (Fed reducing Treasury supply pressure) is a real but diffuse and slow-moving factor. Balance_sheet confirms continued but decelerating runoff — mixed for yields: less duration supply pressure argues for lower yields at the margin, but the deceleration itself is modest relative to the six-month trend already priced. Inflation is split: headline down but core hot and diverging — genuine ambiguity, low conviction, partially stale at 16 days. Labor_tightness is flat/range-bound, low conviction, no directional lean for rates. Inflation_expectations lean up but modestly and decelerating in the very short term.

Netting these: financial_conditions is the freshest and most confident signal and it points toward higher yields (tightening from loose conditions historically coincides with higher term premia/yields as risk appetite adjusts). Term_premium's own directly observed yield decline is real but stalling, and the QT-taper narrative is a modest offsetting force reducing upward pressure. Given the split — one fresh, monotonic, accelerating signal (up) against a stabilizing-not-continuing decline (down) with inflation genuinely mixed — I lean toward a mild net upward bias in yields but size it down given the disagreement across drivers and the staleness/ambiguity in inflation and labor. This is not a curve call — I keep both legs positively signed, sized modestly reflecting the panel's split.

QT continues in name (language unchanged: "will continue reducing holdings"), but the pace is now very slow, and reinvestment dynamics could even produce a flat or marginally positive month given agency MBS paydown volatility. I lean toward continued mild runoff but at a much reduced pace, bordering on flat.

arbitrated.notes:
Weighing the direct-to-nominal-yield drivers: term_premium reports the 10Y itself has been declining (4.19→4.17), with deceleration in pace but no reversal signal — this is the most directly relevant read for the level call and it's fresh. Financial_conditions shows tightening momentum stalling (two flat prints at -0.38) but not reversing — a mild net tightening lean that would, at the margin, support somewhat higher yields via risk-premium/liquidity channels, but conviction is modest and explicitly described as losing steam. Balance_sheet shows QT decelerating sharply toward near-flat, which is a modestly supportive (yield-lowering) factor for the long end as net Treasury supply pressure from the Fed eases — term_premium's own analyst explicitly cites this same balance-sheet slowing as reinforcing their down call, so there's no independent contradiction, just correlated evidence pointing the same direction.

Inflation and inflation_expectations are not directly in my seat (real/nominal split is out of scope) but they inform the net nominal picture only via level, not composition — inflation is flat/pausing with some upside risk from core momentum, breakevens are softening. These two roughly net to neutral-to-mild-easing on inflation-driven yield pressure, consistent with, not contradicting, the term_premium-led down call. Labor_tightness is flat/sticky-high, a neutral signal for yields. Curve_slope's steepening call is a shape signal I explicitly exclude from a level view, though I note it doesn't have term_premium's input, which is a real gap — but my level call rests on term_premium and balance_sheet directly, not on curve dynamics.

Net: multiple independent, fresh, non-stale drivers (term_premium, balance_sheet) point down, tightening financial conditions push back mildly against that but with decaying conviction. I read the net level call as modestly down, not flat.

discounted.why: Shape/steepening call is out of scope for a level view; excluded by mandate.
Real/nominal split out of scope; breakeven move doesn't isolate nominal direction cleanly.

arbitrated.falsifier: A 10Y rebound above 4.30 with financial conditions resuming clear tightening (two consecutive declines past -0.40) would flip this to flat/up.

risks.text: Fed could resume faster runoff (Waller dissent) if inflation reaccelerates, reversing balance-sheet supportive tailwind.
Term premium deceleration could stall into a reversal if long-end supply/steepening pressures reassert.
Financial conditions plateau could break either way; a resumed tightening print would offset the down call materially.

trade.rationale: Modest bull-flattish level bet: term_premium and balance_sheet both point to lower yields, concentrated on the long end where the direct evidence sits, small DGS2 weight for net duration exposure without a curve view.

why.term_premium: 10Y level actually declining across all horizons, deceleration but no reversal; most direct fresh evidence for level.
why.balance_sheet: QT pace collapsing toward flat reduces net Treasury supply pressure, mildly supportive of lower yields, corroborates term_premium.
why.financial_conditions: Tightening trend intact but stalling in last two prints; modest offsetting upward pressure, reduced conviction.
why.inflation: Headline flat/pausing with core momentum upside risk; near-neutral net effect on nominal level.
why.labor_tightness: Sticky at cycle-high but no breakout; no clear directional push on yields.

arbitrated.notes: Weighing the five drivers relevant to a level (not curve) call: labor_tightness gives the clearest, freshest directional signal — a new 12-month-high unemployment print, Fed language shifting to flag unemployment risk, and a breakout from a tight band — which argues for lower yields as the labor market loosens. Financial_conditions confirms this with four consecutive prints of easing (looser conditions), though NFCI easing itself is a modest yield-lowering force via reduced tightening pressure rather than a strong duration signal on its own. Inflation is flat-to-softer at the margin (headline hugging 12-month lows, core PCE cooling), which is modestly disinflationary and supportive of lower yields, though conviction there is limited (30d-stale, borderline print). Term_premium's own analyst flags flat-to-up, but by its own admission this was driven by past momentum, not fresh catalysts, and it explicitly did not have balance_sheet information showing the Treasury cap cut from $25B to $5B — a dovish supply signal for the long end. Given that gap, I discount the "flat to up" term-premium call somewhat, since a slower QT pace should ease upward pressure on the long end, consistent with the balance_sheet analyst's own read of a slower (but still down) balance-sheet trend. Balance_sheet direction is down but at a much slower pace next month, a modest and continuing supportive (lower-yield) force given reduced Fed asset shrinkage acting alongside labor and financial-conditions easing. Netting these: labor loosening and financial conditions easing are the freshest, most decisive signals; inflation adds a mild tailwind; term premium's "flat/up" read is undercut by the balance-sheet gap. I lean toward a modest downward drift in yields into month-end/next data point, not a sharp move, given genuine disagreement and staleness across drivers.

discounted.why: Curve-shape driver, out of scope for a level/duration call.
Overlaps with inflation and real/nominal split, out of this pod's mandate.

arbitrated.falsifier: A labor print reversing back to 4.10% or below, plus NFCI turning back above -0.40 for two prints, would flip this toward flat-to-up yields.

risks.text: Fed could accelerate QT pace or hawkish surprise if inflation reaccelerates, reversing the easing bias.
Term premium and curve analysts show curve near highs; a steepening breakout could keep long yields elevated despite labor/FC easing.
Slower balance-sheet runoff reduces one downward-yield channel if markets price it as less dovish than assumed.

trade.rationale: Modest parallel duration-long bet: labor loosening and easing financial conditions are the freshest signals, reinforced by slower QT; low conviction given curve/term-premium disagreement and staleness.

why.labor_tightness: Loosening labor market (higher unemployment) is disinflationary/dovish for yields; fresh breakout print, hawkish Fed labor-risk language.
why.financial_conditions: Four straight prints of easing conditions reinforce lower-yield bias, fresh and unambiguous momentum.
why.inflation: Soft headline/core prints near 12-month lows add mild disinflationary tailwind, but stale (16d) and borderline print limits conviction.
why.balance_sheet: Still net QT (down) but sharply slower pace; slower runoff also eases long-end supply pressure, a modest yield-lowering force.
why.term_premium: Analyst's own flat/up call discounted: missed balance_sheet gap (slower Treasury runoff) argues against sustained upward long-end pressure.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print continues the runoff trend: total assets fell to 6,662,200 from 6,681,056, a decline of roughly -18,856 over the most recent single observation, and the 4-week change is -11,044 while the 13-week change is -78,053 (-1.16%). The series is choppy week-to-week (there was a small uptick mid-series from 6,672,885 to 6,681,056), but the net direction over the last month and quarter is clearly down, and assets remain well below the 52-week high (-561,879). My prior call of 'down' was correct in direction — the last print was a meaningful decline, at the higher end of what I'd flagged as consistent with continued runoff rather than a stalled cap. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued balance sheet reduction (QT) via capped, non-reinvested runoff of Treasuries, agency debt, and MBS. There is no language suggesting a pause or reinvestment shift. Given the consistent negative 4-week and 13-week trends, and unchanged policy commitment to reduction, I expect assets to be lower again at next month-end, likely in the range of a typical monthly runoff pace, absent any technical/reserve-driven bounce.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print showing assets flat or rising would indicate runoff has paused or reversed; a decline exceeding ~$25-30B would suggest an accelerated or newly binding cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior call (up, 0.30) partly played out then reversed: the spread rose from 0.56 to a high near 0.56 before pulling back to 0.52 in the latest print, a -0.04pp move off the recent peak. That's a mild pullback, not the "close below +0.50" breakdown threshold I set, but it does show the steepening trend losing momentum right at the top of its range. slope_range_position sits at 0.86 (near 12-month highs) and slope_change_252d (+0.94pp) confirms a large steepening move over the past year, but slope_change_21d (+0.01pp) shows near-total stalling over the last month, and the most recent single observation ticked down from 0.56 to 0.52. This looks like consolidation/mild retracement after a strong steepening run rather than a fresh leg either way. The 10s30s slope (+0.54pp) is roughly in line with 2s10s, suggesting the long end isn't independently driving further steepening. Given the curve is still well off its lows and has not broken any range boundary, but the most recent tick is down and momentum has clearly faded, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down rather than continued steepening. Conviction is modest given the ambiguity between a pause and the start of a reversal.
Would change its mind if: A fresh close above +0.60pp would confirm renewed steepening; a close below +0.45pp would confirm a real reversal into flattening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady easing trend: the latest print of -0.49 extends a consistent decline from -0.44 four readings back, with nfci_change_4w at -0.03 and nfci_change_13w at -0.06, both negative and roughly monotonic. There is no sign of reversal — the last several observations show uninterrupted declines (-0.44, -0.45, -0.46, -0.47, -0.48, -0.49), which is the opposite of my stated falsifier (a print above -0.40 or two consecutive increases). My prior down call was correct and the trend has if anything strengthened slightly, with the index now sitting at its 52-week low (nfci_52w_low is -0.51, only 0.02 above the current reading) and range_position at just 0.12, indicating conditions are near the loosest they've been in the observed window. Distance from zero is -0.49, meaning conditions remain substantially looser than the historical average, and that gap is widening rather than closing. Momentum is consistent and unbroken across at least six consecutive periods. Absent any offsetting catalyst, the most likely one-month-ahead outcome is a continuation of this gradual easing path, though the pace of decline (-0.01 per period recently) is modest and could plausibly flatten given proximity to the 52-week low.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.46 or two consecutive increases would signal easing has stalled or tightening is resuming.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_52w_low, nfci_distance_from_zero

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "up" call has not played out: breakeven_10y has stabilized in a narrow 2.28-2.29 band over the last three observations after peaking at 2.33, and both the 21d (-0.03pp) and 63d (-0.08pp) changes are negative, exactly the falsifier condition I set (sustained sub-2.32 readings plus negative 21d change). The longer 252d change is still positive (+0.07pp), showing the broader trend up from a year ago, but the recent momentum has clearly faded and flattened just under the middle of the 12-month range (range_position 0.61). Breakevens remain 0.29pp above the 2% target, consistent with inflation expectations still anchored somewhat above objective, but the recent flat-to-slightly-down drift near 2.28-2.29 suggests no fresh upward pressure is building. Given the tight clustering of the last four prints (2.29, 2.28, 2.28, 2.29) I read this as consolidation rather than a renewed downtrend — the market seems to have found a local equilibrium. I'm revising off my prior up view since the evidence didn't confirm it; the near-term signal is essentially flat, with a slight residual downward tilt from the 63d change.
Would change its mind if: A break above 2.33 or below 2.26 sustained two+ observations would reassert a clear directional trend.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 15 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline CPI y/y rose to 2.68% from 2.38%, a 0.30pp jump, with headline m/m at 0.25% — inside my prior "flat" range but on the high side. My previous flat call was directionally too cautious given the jump; the print leaned toward reacceleration rather than staying flat, though it did not cross my stated 0.30% m/m threshold. Core PCE y/y actually ticked down to 2.78 from 2.61 the month before but that series has been choppy (2.67→2.61→2.78), not a clean signal either way. The 3-month annualized rates for both headline and core sit at 2.08%, well below the y/y levels, suggesting the y/y jump owes something to base effects rather than a sudden acceleration in monthly momentum — mom_gap_vs_outgoing is exactly 0, so momentum is not building further. Core remains 0.78pp above target, and headline is mid-range of its 12-month band (0.53). Policy language shift ("risks of higher unemployment and higher inflation have risen") flags inflation risk without confirming direction. Given base effects likely fade and monthly momentum is flat, I expect headline y/y to hold near current levels or drift up slightly rather than fall sharply, but conviction is limited by the mixed core signal.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.30% would confirm reacceleration; a sub-0.10% print or core_pce_yoy back below 2.6% would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 22 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print reversed back to 4.10% from 4.30%, exactly the condition I flagged previously as evidence the prior uptick was a one-off rather than a genuine loosening trend. My prior "up" call was wrong for this release: the rate fell 0.20pp month-over-month rather than continuing to climb. Looking at the full series, unemployment has been oscillating in a narrow 4.00-4.30% band for over a year with no sustained trend — 3m change is now -0.10pp, while 6m and 12m changes are both flat at 0.00pp. The Sahm-gap of 0.20pp (current vs 12m low) is well below the ~0.50pp threshold that would signal genuine labor-market deterioration. The level itself (4.10%) sits just 0.10pp above my 4.00% natural-rate anchor, consistent with a still-solid, essentially balanced labor market. Policy language now flags that risks of higher unemployment have "risen," which is a mild forward-looking caution, but the hard data shows no confirmed loosening trend — just noisy chop within a tight range. Given the whipsaw pattern (down-up-down over the last three prints), I don't have conviction in either direction for the next report; the base case is another reading close to 4.10-4.20%.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.30% (new 12m high) or two consecutive increases would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_change_12m, sahm_gap, unrate_range_position, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10-year has drifted lower over the past month, from 4.46 to 4.24, with the 21-day change at -0.19pp while the 63-day and 252-day changes are near zero, indicating the decline is recent and not yet a durable trend break. My prior flat call was too cautious given the persistence of the move: yields have printed lower lows across the last several observations (4.29→4.24 in the final two prints) and range_position at 0.53 is now closer to the middle of the 12-month range, well off the highs, though not yet below the 0.4 threshold I set as a falsifier. The slope_30s10s at 0.54pp remains modestly positive and hasn't visibly flattened, so the long-end curve isn't screaming duress or flight-to-quality distortion — this looks like a grind lower rather than a sharp repricing. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff is a structural headwind to term premium, but it hasn't stopped the recent drift down, suggesting other forces (softer data, demand for duration) are currently dominant. Given the clear direction over 21 days but the lack of a confirmed break below 4.30 with range_position under 0.4, I'll lean down with moderate conviction rather than high conviction.
Would change its mind if: A rebound back above 4.35 with range_position climbing above 0.6 would indicate the downward drift has reversed.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
Weighing the panel toward net Treasury yield direction over roughly one month: the strongest, freshest signals point down. Term premium shows a clear 21-day decline in DGS10 (4.46→4.24) with moderate conviction (0.45 down), and financial conditions confirm an unbroken, six-period easing streak in NFCI now at a 52-week low (0.55 down) — this easing in financial conditions is consistent with (and likely a partial driver of) the term premium compression, addressing the gap the term_premium analyst flagged. Balance sheet runoff continues on trend (0.55 down conviction) but works through the term-premium channel already captured above, so I avoid double-counting it as a fully independent driver of the level while still noting it as a structural headwind that has not stopped yields from falling — meaning other forces (financial conditions easing, softer demand-side pressure) currently dominate over the mechanical QT drag.

Inflation is the main offsetting force: the CPI print reaccelerated y/y (0.35 up) though 3-month annualized momentum is flat and base effects are implicated, while inflation expectations (breakevens) have stalled flat with a slight downward tilt (0.25 flat) — both fresher-vintage but low conviction and roughly cancel. Labor tightness is genuinely flat/range-bound noise with low conviction, giving no directional push. Given two well-formed, high-conviction easing signals (financial conditions, term premium) against a weak, internally-conflicted inflation complex, the net level view leans down, but I size down from the individual convictions given the panel's real split between inflation (up) and financial/term premium (down).

Curve shape is out of scope for a duration-level call; not directional on outright yields.

Balance sheet runoff is a structural offset to easing financial conditions and could reassert itself, pushing yields back up.
Panel is split (inflation up vs financial conditions/term premium down), so conviction on net direction is only moderate.

trade.rationale: Net level view is down on easing financial conditions and drifting term premium; weight DGS10 more heavily since the term-premium signal is most directly a long-end phenomenon.

why.financial_conditions: Unbroken six-period NFCI easing streak at 52wk low, fresh and high-conviction; primary driver of yield decline.
why.balance_sheet: Structural QT headwind confirmed but not currently the dominant force; downweighted to avoid double-counting with term premium.

why.labor_tightness: Range-bound whipsaw with no trend; no directional basis for a level view.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The previous "down" call was correct: total assets continued to decline, though at a modest pace. Over the last 13 weeks assets fell ~$69B (~1.03%), and the most recent 4-week change is -$4.5B, a slower pace than the trailing 13-week average would imply. The series shows a bumpy but persistently negative trend: 6673244→6672885→6677155→6681056 (a brief uptick)→6662200→6659598→6661912→6659273→6657715, netting lower. Assets remain $521B below the 52-week high, consistent with ongoing QT. The unchanged policy language confirms the Committee continues to run off Treasury and MBS holdings — no signal of a pause or reversal. The pace is not accelerating (no move exceeding the $25-30B/month threshold that would signal a newly binding or tightened cap), but it is also not decelerating to flat. This is a continuation of gradual, steady runoff. Given the consistent month-over-month declines and unchanged reinvestment language, I expect total assets to be modestly lower again at month end, roughly in line with the recent ~$4-7B monthly pace of decline.
Would change its mind if: A monthly print showing assets flat or rising would indicate runoff has paused; a decline exceeding ~$25-30B would suggest an accelerated cap.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w, assets_below_52w_high

=== curve_slope ===
Call: down, conviction 0.45
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 2s10s spread has continued to grind lower: from 0.56 down to 0.43pp over the last ten readings, with the most recent print (0.43) below my prior flattening trigger of 0.45pp. The 21-day and 63-day changes are both negative (-0.05, -0.14pp), confirming a persistent, if gradual, flattening trend rather than noise. My prior "flat" call is now falsified by the data — the spread broke below the threshold I set for a real reversal into flattening, so I was wrong to stay neutral last time given the trend was already visible. That said, the spread still sits at 0.72 of its 12-month range (0.63pp above the 12m low of -0.20, out of an 0.87pp range), meaning it remains well within steepener territory relative to the past year's extremes — this is a pullback from a steep regime, not a dive toward inversion. The 10s30s slope at +0.52pp shows the long end still upward-sloping, consistent with continued modest curve compression happening mostly in the belly. Absent a clear catalyst reversing the recent grind, I expect the mild flattening drift to persist into month-end, though the move has been slow and shows no acceleration.
Would change its mind if: A close back above 0.50pp would signal the flattening drift has stalled; a break below 0.35pp would confirm acceleration into deeper flattening.
Leaned on: slope_2s10s, slope_change_21d, slope_change_63d, slope_range_position, slope_10s30s

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: down, conviction 0.30
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI series continues its steady grind lower: the last two prints are -0.51, -0.51, essentially flat but still at the cycle (52-week) low, with the 4-week change at -0.02 and 13-week change at -0.13. My prior call (down) was directionally correct — conditions kept easing since the last release, consistent with the trend of the prior 13 weeks. The falsifier I set (print back above -0.46, or two consecutive increases) has not triggered; if anything the series is pinned at the 52-week low with range_position at 0.00, meaning no bounce at all. That said, momentum has clearly decelerated — the last two observations are identical, and the 4-week change is now much smaller than the 13-week average pace of decline, suggesting the easing trend may be flattening out near this floor. Distance from zero remains solidly negative (-0.51), so conditions are still looser than average by a wide margin, but the rate of further easing looks to be stalling. Absent any new catalyst, I lean toward continued mild easing or flat, but with reduced conviction versus last time given the visible deceleration and the two identical prints at the trough.
Would change its mind if: A print back above -0.48, or two consecutive increases, would confirm the easing trend has stalled or reversed into tightening.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_distance_from_zero, nfci_52w_low, nfci_range_position

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: down, conviction 0.40
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The prior "flat" call at 0.25 conviction was weak and the subsequent data shows more drift than I credited: breakeven_10y has moved from 2.41 down to 2.39 over the last three prints, but the recent trajectory is choppier than trending. Looking at the full 10-print series: 2.41→2.40→2.39→2.37→2.45→2.44→2.44→2.43→2.42→2.39, there was a jump up to 2.45 mid-series, then a steady grind down over the last five observations (2.45→2.44→2.44→2.43→2.42→2.39). That recent leg down is the more relevant signal for a one-month-ahead call. The 21d and 63d changes are both positive (+0.10, +0.16) reflecting the level versus a month/quarter ago, but the most recent three observations show clear deceleration, with the last print (2.39) matching the series low from early in the window. Range position at 0.84 shows breakeven is still elevated within its 12-month range, and distance from the 2% target remains wide at +0.39pp, but the immediate momentum has reversed from the mid-series peak. Given the last three consecutive declines, I lean toward continued softening rather than flat, though the longer-window changes still show net upward drift, keeping conviction moderate.
Would change its mind if: A reversal back above 2.42 sustained for two+ observations, or resumption of the 21d/63d uptrend, would reassert the up-trend and invalidate this down call.
Leaned on: be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_range_position

=== inflation ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.30
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release: headline_cpi_yoy rose to 2.74% from 2.68%, and headline_mom printed 0.23%, within my stated "flat-ish" range (not above 0.30 nor below 0.10), so the prior "up" call at 0.35 conviction was directionally correct but the underlying momentum was moderate rather than a clear reacceleration signal. Core PCE also ticked up to 2.81% from 2.78%, though it remains below several recent readings (2.97, 2.99) earlier in the window, suggesting momentum is not building forcefully in either direction.
  
  Looking at the full picture: headline_yoy_change_3m is +0.42pp, showing recent upward drift from the 2.33% low, and yoy_range_position at 0.63 confirms we're in the upper half of the 12-month range. However, headline_3m_annualized (2.35%) is actually below the current yoy print (2.74%), which points to deceleration in the trend rather than acceleration. The mom_gap_vs_outgoing of only +0.04pp is negligible. Core_3m_annualized (2.75%) also sits below core_pce_yoy (2.81%), reinforcing that the trailing 3-month pace is cooler than the year-over-year figure — a sign the yoy uptick partly reflects base effects rather than fresh momentum. Policy language shift acknowledging inflation risk without characterizing it as accelerating supports a modest, cautious read. Given the mixed signal — yoy drifting up but 3m annualized pointing down — I lean flat with slight upward bias given the two consecutive yoy increases.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print above 0.30% would confirm reacceleration; a sub-0.10% print or renewed drop in core_pce_yoy below 2.6% would confirm deceleration.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, core_pce_yoy, headline_3m_annualized, core_3m_annualized, headline_yoy_change_3m, mom_gap_vs_outgoing

=== labor_tightness ===

Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The latest print of 4.30% ties the 12-month high (previously hit two readings ago at 4.30%, then dipped to 4.10%, now back to 4.30%), confirming the range has not broken decisively but is sitting at its upper edge. My prior "flat" call was reasonably accurate in that the series stayed range-bound rather than trending sharply, but the bounce back up to the cycle high after a dip to 4.10% is a mild loosening signal I should not dismiss. The 3m change (+0.10pp) and 6m change (+0.30pp) show some upward drift, and the Sahm gap of +0.23pp is still well below the 0.50 trigger threshold, so this is not a recession-signal move. The series has been oscillating in a narrow 4.00-4.30% band for over a year with no sustained trend — the last three prints (4.20, 4.10, 4.30) show noise rather than a clear direction. Policy language actually softened toward more sanguine ("remains low... solid") dropping the risk-balance caveat, suggesting the committee reads this as noise too. Given the volatility right at the top of the range but lack of a confirmed breakout, I lean toward a modest reading that another sideways-to-slightly-higher print is plausible, but conviction stays low given the choppy, non-trending pattern.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.30% (new 12m high) or two consecutive increases would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_change_6m, unrate_range_position, sahm_gap, unrate_12m_high

=== term_premium ===
Call: up, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down, 0.45) was wrong: the 10y has held above 4.35 and range_position sits at 0.64, above the 0.6 threshold I set as my own falsifier. The last 10 prints show yields oscillating in a 4.34-4.44 band but the recent trend (21d +0.11, 63d +0.20, 252d +0.17) is modestly upward, and the level sits in the upper 64th percentile of the 12-month range. The 10s30s slope at +0.52pp is still positively sloped, consistent with continued term-premium compensation for duration risk, not a flight-to-quality flattening. Ongoing balance-sheet runoff (continued Treasury/MBS reduction) is a structural source of upward pressure on long-end supply absorption, reinforcing the modest uptrend. The last print (4.37) ticked down from 4.38, so the very latest move is not yet decisive, but the broader trend over 21-252 days is up, and I don't see fresh flight-to-quality evidence to counter it. I'm revising toward a mild upward lean rather than continuing to expect a reversal down — my prior downward call has not been validated by the data.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.35 with range_position falling under 0.5 and 21d change turning negative would signal renewed downward drift.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

arbitrated.notes:
The panel is genuinely mixed on level, which argues for a modest, low-conviction directional trade rather than a bold call. On the "up" side: term_premium (fresh, formed this meeting) shows the 10y holding in the upper part of its 12-month range with positive 21d/63d/252d momentum, and it explicitly ties this to ongoing balance-sheet runoff as a structural supply-absorption force — a linkage the term_premium analyst flagged as missing input but which balance_sheet's own read (continued, unaccelerated QT) actually corroborates directionally. That's a coherent story: steady runoff plus curve behavior consistent with duration-risk compensation, not flight-to-quality. On the "down" side: financial_conditions is easing (though decelerating and pinned at a 52-week low, so more stale/flattening than a strong offsetting force), and inflation_expectations shows a real recent downshift in breakevens over the last several prints. Inflation itself (headline CPI/core PCE) is flat with a mild upward yoy tilt but decelerating 3-month annualized pace — a wash. Labor is flat/noisy at the top of its range, no strong signal either way.

Netting this out: the freshest, most decision-relevant signal for the level of yields is term_premium's actual 10y level and momentum, which is up, reinforced by balance_sheet's continued runoff (a genuine cross-driver corroboration, not overlap-double-counting since balance_sheet was itself formed independently). Financial conditions' easing and breakeven softening are real but of lower conviction and are consistent with a modestly reduced inflation/risk premium rather than a strong bid for duration. I read the net as a mild upward drift in nominal yields into month end, keeping conviction low given how split and staleness-prone several drivers are.

user_prompt:
The panel has reported. Each analyst below covers one driver and nothing else.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: down, conviction 0.60
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The latest print confirms the prior call: total assets fell again, from 6,618,415 to 6,603,384 ($M), a drop of ~$15.0B in the most recent week and continuing the steady QT trend. The 4-week change is -39.2B and 13-week change is -69.9B, both consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff at a pace roughly in line with (or slightly below) the announced caps — no sign of acceleration beyond the ~$25-30B/month threshold I flagged, and no sign of a pause. The series has declined in 9 of the last 12 observations, with only minor upticks, and is now $511.6B below its 52-week high with a persistent negative slope. Policy language is unchanged, reaffirming continued runoff of Treasury and agency holdings with no reinvestment. There is no indication of a pause, taper announcement, or reversal in either the measurements or the stated policy. Given the consistency of the multi-week decline and unchanged guidance, I expect assets to continue falling into month-end, likely by another $15-40B, keeping the trend intact.
Would change its mind if: A weekly print showing assets flat or rising, or a policy statement announcing a pause/taper in runoff, would reverse this call.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w, assets_pct_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My prior call (down/flattening, conviction 0.45) was wrong: the spread has instead steepened, rising from the high-0.50s to 0.64pp, with the last two prints at 0.60 and 0.64. The 21d change (+0.21pp) and 63d change (+0.12pp) both confirm a steepening trend that has accelerated recently, not stalled. The spread now sits at 0.96 of its 12-month range (near the 12m high of 0.67pp), well above the 12m low of -0.04pp, meaning the curve has moved decisively away from inversion toward positive slope. The 10s30s context (+0.69pp) shows the long end also steep, consistent with broad-based steepening rather than a front-end-only move. Policy language shows a dissent (Waller) favoring a 25bp cut, which if anything pressures the front end lower given expectations of easing, supportive of continued steepening as short rates would fall relative to long rates. Given the strong, multi-horizon consistent steepening signal and the spread pushing to a fresh range high, I'm reversing my prior call. The trend has clear momentum, though a move this close to the 12-month high raises some risk of near-term consolidation or pause.
Would change its mind if: A close back below 0.55pp would signal the steepening has stalled; a break below 0.45pp would suggest renewed flattening pressure.

=== financial_conditions ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The NFCI has flattened at the bottom of its recent range: the last four readings are all -0.52, with the 4-week change essentially zero (-0.01) and the 13-week change modest (-0.07). The prior easing trend (steadily falling index) has stalled — the series shows no further declines in the last four prints, sitting exactly at the 52-week low with a range position of 0.00. My previous call was for continued easing (down), but the last several observations show that trend has stopped rather than reversed or continued. There's no evidence of tightening (no increases), but there's also no evidence of further easing momentum — the index is pinned flat. Distance from zero remains meaningfully negative (-0.52), so conditions are still looser than average, but the momentum that would justify a directional call for the next month is absent. Absent any policy language addressing this driver, and with the measurement series showing a clear plateau, the most defensible call is flat: conditions are holding at a loose level rather than easing further or tightening. My prior 'down' call was not wrong in direction over the 13-week window but has clearly run out of momentum in the most recent weeks, which is what matters for the one-month-ahead horizon now.
Would change its mind if: A print below -0.52 (new 52w low) would signal renewed easing; a print above -0.50 would signal tightening resuming.
Leaned on: nfci_change_4w, nfci_change_13w, nfci_range_position, nfci_above_52w_low

=== inflation_expectations ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  My previous "down" call at 0.40 conviction was wrong: the series printed 2.41 twice near the end but before that touched 2.43, and the broader trend since has held near multi-month highs rather than reversing. Looking at the last 10 prints, breakeven_10y rose from 2.38 to a peak of 2.43 before settling to 2.41-2.41, essentially flat over the most recent two readings. The 21d change (+0.02) and 63d change (+0.07) are both still positive, and the 252d change (+0.26) confirms a sustained upward drift over the past year. The series sits at be_range_position 0.89 — near the top of its 12-month range (2.02-2.46) — and is +0.41pp above the 2% target, a meaningful and persistent overshoot in market-implied inflation expectations. The last two observations flattening at 2.41 could be early stabilization, but with the level still elevated and no genuine reversal (no break below prior support, no multi-observation decline), I don't see convincing evidence of a down-trend. Instead this looks like a plateau near cycle highs after a longer uptrend — flat-to-slightly-up is more consistent with the data than continued decline. I'm revising away from "down" given the previous call's failure to materialize.
Would change its mind if: A clear multi-observation break below 2.38 (prior range) or renewed negative 21d/63d change would confirm a genuine downturn rather than plateau.
Leaned on: breakeven_10y, be_change_21d, be_change_63d, be_change_252d, be_range_position, be_distance_from_target

=== inflation ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: the next monthly CPI release (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 16 days ago.
Report:
  Last release delivered headline m/m of 0.35%, above my stated 0.30% reacceleration threshold, and headline yoy rose to 2.94% from 2.68%, near the top of the 12-month range (yoy_range_position 0.92). My prior "flat" call was wrong on the momentum signal — this is a fresh, clear acceleration print, not noise. Core PCE also ticked up to 2.86% from 2.81%, though core's 3m annualized (2.99%) is more muted than headline's (3.37%), and core_yoy_change_3m (+0.25pp) is smaller than headline's (+0.56pp). This divergence suggests some of the recent headline push is coming from food/energy volatility rather than pure underlying persistence, but core is still not decelerating — it's drifting sideways-to-up near 2.8-2.9%, well above target (distance +0.86pp). Given headline sits at a 12-month high with mom_gap_vs_outgoing positive and the 3-month annualized pace clearly outrunning the 12-month trend, the momentum evidence favors another firm or higher print next month rather than a reversal. I weight the headline momentum surprise heavily here since it directly missed my own threshold last time, while treating core's smaller rise as corroborating (not driving) the call.
Would change its mind if: A headline m/m print back below ~0.15% or core_pce_yoy dropping below 2.7% would signal the acceleration stalled.
Leaned on: headline_cpi_yoy, headline_mom, headline_3m_annualized, core_pce_yoy, core_3m_annualized, yoy_range_position, headline_yoy_change_3m, core_yoy_change_3m

=== labor_tightness ===
Call: flat, conviction 0.25
Horizon: the next monthly jobs report (roughly one month ahead)
Status: formed 23 days ago.
Report:
  The unemployment rate sits at 4.30%, matching the prior print and tying the 12-month high, after a choppy range of 4.00-4.30% over the past year. The last observation shows no further increase (4.30 to 4.30), so my prior "flat" call was directionally correct in that no new high was made and there was no two-consecutive-increase confirmation. The 3-month change is exactly 0.00pp, and the series has been oscillating rather than trending — 4.10/4.30/4.10/4.30 in the last four readings looks like noise around a slowly rising centerline rather than a clean breakout. That said, the level is now sitting at the top of its 12-month range (range_position = 1.00), 0.3pp above both the 12-month low and the natural-rate anchor near 4%, and the 6m/12m changes are modestly positive. This is consistent with a labor market that has stopped improving and is drifting sideways-to-slightly-looser, but has not shown the rapid acceleration that would signal a Sahm-rule-style regime shift. Policy language is unchanged and still characterizes conditions as "solid," offering no independent confirmation of deterioration. I lean toward flat with a slight tilt toward up given the range position sitting at its ceiling, but conviction remains low given the choppiness.
Would change its mind if: A print above 4.30% (new 12m high) or two consecutive month-over-month increases would confirm a genuine loosening trend rather than range-bound noise.
Leaned on: unrate_change_3m, unrate_range_position, unrate_12m_high, sahm_gap, unrate_distance_from_natural

=== term_premium ===
Call: down, conviction 0.35
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The 10y yield has drifted down to 4.23 from 4.34 ten observations ago, with 21d change -0.14pp and 63d change -0.18pp both negative, confirming a recent downward drift rather than the modest upward lean I called last time. My stated falsifier has essentially triggered: yield is below 4.35, range_position sits at 0.52 (barely above midpoint, no longer clearly elevated), and 21d change is negative. The longer 252d change (+0.41pp) shows the broader trend over the past year is still up, so this isn't a sharp reversal, but the near-term momentum has clearly shifted lower. The 10s30s slope (+0.69pp) remains steep, consistent with ongoing balance-sheet runoff pressure on the long end, which argues against a large further decline. On net, the level is near the middle of its 12-month range, momentum is modestly negative, and there's no fresh policy language suggesting either acceleration of runoff or flight-to-quality. I was likely wrong last time calling 'up' with the drift now clearly negative over both 21d and 63d windows. Given the balance between persistent supply pressure (steepening slope) and the recent downward drift, I lean toward flat-to-slightly-down for month end, with modest conviction given the mixed signals.
Would change its mind if: A rebound above 4.30 with 21d change turning positive and range_position rising back above 0.55 would signal renewed upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s

  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Pace/size of ongoing runoff would clarify supply pressure on long end going forward.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader risk sentiment would clarify if recent yield decline reflects flight-to-quality.

arbitrated.notes: The level view here is a genuine tug-of-war between two fresh, high-conviction upward pressures (inflation and labor tightness) and a set of flat-to-mixed structural drivers (balance sheet, financial conditions, term premium) with only inflation_expectations leaning modestly down. Inflation is up 0.55 conviction, formed 15 days ago, with headline CPI at a 12-month high and momentum intact — this is a strong, non-stale signal for upward pressure on nominal yields via the inflation compensation channel. Labor tightness is up 0.55, formed 22 days ago, showing unemployment at a 12-month high and accelerating — but this is a two-edged signal for nominal yields: rising unemployment usually argues for lower policy rates and could pull the front end down even as it reflects real economic softening, so I read it as a weaker, more ambiguous contributor to the nominal level than inflation. Term premium and balance sheet are both flat with low-modest conviction and explicitly note stalling/plateauing after prior more directional calls — they don't add net force. Financial conditions is flat and stable, not a friction for either direction. Inflation expectations lean down but at 35 conviction on a 2-3bp move that the analyst themselves call "noise" — weak signal, and it lacks the inflation driver's read which would clarify the move is likely not a demand-story reversal. Netting this out: inflation is the freshest, most decisive, least stale driver pointing toward higher yields, partially offset by the labor print's ambiguous rate implications and a flat term-premium/balance-sheet backdrop. I read the level as leaning modestly higher, not dramatically so, given the offsetting flat/mixed signals and the genuine two-sidedness of the labor data for nominal rates.

  The policy language shift is decisive corroborating evidence: the Committee has moved from "will continue reducing its holdings" to explicitly deciding to "conclude the reduction of its aggregate securities holdings." Combined with the near-zero 4-week change already observed, this strongly supports a flat-to-slightly-up balance sheet over the next month, as no further scheduled shrinkage will occur and any residual maturities/reinvestment could roughly stabilize the total.

arbitrated.notes:
Net level view: modestly higher yields over the next month, but with tempered conviction given a genuinely mixed panel. The two strongest directional pulls are stale-but-not-contradicted upside calls on inflation (CPI y/y at 12-month high, momentum intact) and labor tightness (unemployment at 12-month high, monotonic 3-print uptrend) — both are 46-53 days stale, so I discount their strength somewhat, but neither has a fresher read to override it, and both are internally coherent trend stories rather than noise. These two argue for higher yields via both inflation-risk-premium and Fed-reaction-function channels (a looser labor market normally argues for lower yields via easing expectations, but the panel frames this as a modest, non-recessionary loosening that hasn't yet flipped the Fed's cutting bias into anything urgent — so I weight it as a secondary, weaker upward nudge on term structure via still-firm growth/inflation backdrop rather than a cut-acceleration story).

Working against duration-higher: balance_sheet reports QT explicitly concluding, a clear structural removal of long-end supply pressure — a genuine downward force on term premium, corroborated directly by term_premium's own analyst who cites the same policy language as reason to lean flat-to-down rather than up despite a 3-print uptick. Financial conditions are flat/pinned, offering no independent signal. Term premium itself is flat with low conviction and low range position, and inflation expectations (breakevens) also settled to flat after an initial "down" call fizzled.

So the picture is: two moderately-stale "up" fundamentals (inflation, labor tightness) versus a fresher, structurally-grounded "flat-to-down" long-end supply signal (balance sheet ending QT, echoed by term premium). This is a real split on the rate axis, not a consensus. I lean slightly toward up given inflation's persistence and labor tightness both pointing the same way, but size down materially given the staleness and the structural counter-signal from QT conclusion.

=== balance_sheet ===
Call: up, conviction 0.65
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead
Status: formed at this meeting.
Report:
  The last four weekly prints show total assets rising: 6535781 → 6539303 → 6556861 → 6581231, a cumulative +28812 over 4 weeks, reversing the prior downtrend. My previous "down" call is now clearly wrong — the runoff has ended and the balance sheet is expanding again. This aligns with the policy language shift: the Committee explicitly states reserves have reached "ample" levels and it will "initiate purchases of shorter-term Treasury securities as needed to maintain an ample supply of reserves on an ongoing basis." That is affirmative QE language, not merely halting QT — it commits to active purchases going forward. The 13-week change is still slightly negative (-27164, -0.41%) and assets remain well below the 52-week high (-272323), reflecting the tail of the prior runoff still working through the trailing window, but the recent 4-week trend and the new purchase directive both point the same direction. With an explicit commitment to ongoing purchases now in the statement, the most likely path over the next month is continued modest balance sheet growth as the Fed builds reserves back toward ample levels via Treasury bill purchases. This is a clear regime change from runoff to expansion.
Would change its mind if: A subsequent weekly print showing assets flat or falling again despite the "initiate purchases" language would suggest the purchase program is offset by other runoff (e.g., MBS paydowns) and flip this back toward flat/down.
Leaned on: assets_change_4w, assets_change_13w

=== curve_slope ===
Call: up, conviction 0.55
Horizon: month end, roughly one month ahead

The curve has been steepening steadily, not oscillating. Fed language continues to cut the front end (25bp cut, dissent favoring even larger 50bp cut) while leaving the door open to more cuts data-dependently — this keeps front-end yields anchored/falling relative to the long end, consistent with continued steepening pressure. With dgs2 and dgs10 both rising modestly but the spread still expanding, and no sign of deceleration in the trend, I'd expect the steepening trend to continue or at least hold near these elevated levels into month-end. Risk is a stall right at the 12-month high (0.73), which could produce a plateau rather than further steepening, tempering conviction somewhat.
Would change its mind if: A close back below 0.60pp, or a reversal in slope_change_21d turning negative, would signal the steepening trend has stalled or reversed.

  The 10y closed the last observation at 4.18%, near the top of its recent chop and up from 4.12-4.14 a few prints back. My prior flat call was roughly right through most of the window but the most recent print broke slightly above the 4.15 threshold I'd flagged, with 21d change now clearly positive (+0.09pp) though 63d remains only marginally positive (+0.03pp) — so the break is real but not yet emphatic. Yield sits at 0.26 of its 12-month range, still closer to the low than the high, and the 252d change is still firmly negative (-0.40pp), so the longer-run trend remains disinflationary/lower-yield in character. The policy language shift is notable: the Fed ending balance-sheet runoff and moving toward purchasing shorter-term Treasuries to maintain ample reserves is a technical/liquidity operation concentrated in the front end (bills/short coupons), not direct long-end QE. That should be mildly supportive for the long end (less net supply absorption pressure at the margin) but the effect is indirect. Given the modest uptick in yield and slope_30s10s/10s2s both still positive but unremarkable, I see a slight upward lean but weak conviction — the move is small relative to the 12-month range and could easily reverse.
Would change its mind if: A drop back below 4.10 with 21d turning negative would confirm the range-bound/lower lean; a push above 4.25 with 63d turning clearly positive would confirm sustained upward pressure.
Leaned on: dgs10_level, dgs10_change_21d, dgs10_change_63d, dgs10_change_252d, dgs10_range_position, slope_30s10s, slope_10s2s

=== What the panel says it was never handed ===
  term_premium lacks balance_sheet — Details on scale/duration of shorter-term Treasury purchases to gauge net long-end supply impact.
  term_premium lacks financial_conditions — Broader financial conditions tightness/looseness would clarify flight-to-quality vs. risk-on pressure on long yields.

arbitrated.notes: Reconciling the panel for a net level view on nominal yields over the next ~month. Balance sheet: the Fed has pivoted from runoff to explicit purchases of short-term Treasuries, and four weekly prints confirm assets rising off the trough. This is real and fresh, but it's a front-end liquidity operation (bills), not long-end QE, so its yield impact is asymmetric — modestly reserve-easing but not a strong pull on the long end. Term premium itself is only marginally up (10y broke 4.15 but 63d change is barely positive, 252d still firmly negative) and conviction is weak; it also flags a dependency on balance_sheet detail it doesn't have, which cuts against leaning on it too hard for level direction beyond what balance_sheet already tells us. Inflation is up at moderate conviction (headline at cycle highs, momentum intact) even as core cools — a genuine upside pull on yields via the inflation channel. Labor tightness also leans up (unemployment trending higher over the year despite one soft print), which is a genuine offsetting force since a loosening labor market argues for lower yields via easier policy expectations — this partially cancels the inflation-up push. Financial conditions is flat/stale-ish with weak conviction and no directional signal. Curve slope and inflation-expectations are explicitly out of scope for this seat (curve shape, real/nominal split). Netting it out: balance sheet expansion is a mild easing force concentrated at the front end with limited direct long-end lift; inflation pressure and continued labor loosening roughly offset each other in terms of level pressure, with inflation's fresher, higher-conviction read (0.30, tangible CPI momentum) narrowly outweighing labor's ambiguous signal (one-month reversal not yet confirmed). Net: a slight upward lean on the level, driven mostly by inflation persistence and the modest term-premium uptick, but conviction is low given internal offsets and staleness in financial conditions.

discounted.why: Flat/stale with no directional momentum, sits at range floor, offers no level signal.
Latest print reversed the trend partially; offsets inflation, kept small to avoid overweighting either side.

arbitrated.falsifier: A confirmed break of core inflation below 2.7% alongside two consecutive sub-4.30% unemployment prints would flip this to a downward level call.

risks.text: Balance sheet purchases could expand in scale/duration beyond bills, altering long-end supply dynamics unexpectedly.
Term premium signal is weak and could reverse quickly given it's near a range boundary, not a confirmed trend.
Labor and inflation drivers partially offset; a decisive move in either could flip net direction abruptly.

trade.rationale: Mild net-up level view driven by inflation persistence and modest term-premium uptick, tempered by labor-market loosening and muted long-end pass-through from front-end-focused balance-sheet purchases.

why.inflation: Headline CPI at cycle highs with intact momentum; core cooling caps upside, so modest positive lean retained as reported.
why.labor_tightness: Trend up over year but latest print reversed toward threshold; discounted for noisiness, kept small positive lean.
why.term_premium: 10y broke above 4.15 but 63d change barely positive and long-run trend still down; weak, discounted for missing balance_sheet/FC context.
why.balance_sheet: Genuine regime shift to purchases, but front-end/bills focused, so muted pass-through to overall level; small positive lean only.
why.financial_conditions: Flat, at 52-week loose extreme, no momentum either way; no view contributed to level.
